name: ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN

topics.title: Components and Structure of Robots: Symbolic Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components and Structure of Robots: Symbolic Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Robotic Manipulators and Work Cell Design*, a **Robot** is formally defined as a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks (Robot Institute of America, 1979).

The **Components and Structure of Robots** refers to the systematic identification and classification of the building blocks (mechanical, electrical, and computational subsystems) that constitute an industrial robot, along with the geometric and topological framework governing their assembly.

**Symbolic Representation** is the standardized graphical and alphanumeric notation system used in robotics engineering to abstractly denote robot components such as joints, links, end-effectors, reference frames, and Degrees of Freedom (DOF) without resorting to complex physical drawings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students are expected to understand the basic anatomy of a robot, classify robots by configuration, and master the standard joint symbols (R, P, V, H, C, O) used in kinematic chain representation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **human arm**:
- **Upper arm, forearm, and hand** → These are the **links** (rigid bodies) of the robot.
- **Shoulder, elbow, and wrist** → These are the **joints** that allow motion.
- **Fingers holding a pen** → This is the **end-effector** (the tool).
- **Brain sending signals** → This is the **controller** (the computer).
- **Muscles providing strength** → These are the **actuators** (motors, hydraulics).
- **Skin sensing heat** → These are the **sensors** (force, vision, proximity).

Just as we can describe a human arm by saying "shoulder (ball joint) → elbow (hinge) → wrist (hinge)", in robotics we use a **symbolic shorthand** like **RRR** (three revolute joints) to describe a robot's kinematic chain in a single line of text.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Robot Configurations

| Configuration | Symbolic Joint Sequence | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Cartesian/Gantry | **PPP** | Pick & place, CNC loading |
| Cylindrical | **RPP** | Assembly, machine tending |
| Spherical/Polar | **RRP** | Welding, material handling |
| Articulated (Anthropomorphic) | **RRR** | Welding, painting, sealing |
| SCARA | **RRP** (Selective Compliance) | PCB assembly, pick & place |
| Delta | **RRR** (Parallel) | High-speed packaging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant for Industrial Robots:** The standard reference is **ambient temperature of 25°C** and **standard atmospheric pressure of 101.325 kPa** when defining robot accuracy and repeatability specifications per **ISO 9283**.

### 1.4 Core Robot Components — The 5 Subsystems

Every industrial robot consists of **five major subsystems**:

1. **Mechanical Structure (Manipulator)** — Links, joints, bearings.
2. **Actuators** — Servo motors, hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders.
3. **Sensors** — Encoders, force/torque sensors, vision systems.
4. **Controller** — Computer/CPU with programming interface.
5. **End-Effector (EOAT)** — Grippers, welding torches, paint sprayers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomical Breakdown of a Robot Manipulator

A robot manipulator is mechanically structured as a **serial kinematic chain** — a sequence of rigid bodies (links) interconnected by joints, anchored at a fixed base and terminating at an end-effector.

**Step-by-step logical decomposition:**

- **Step 1 — The Base (Link 0):** The fixed, non-moving foundation. All motion is measured *relative* to this reference.
- **Step 2 — The Link:** A rigid mechanical member that maintains a constant distance between its two joint connection points. Links are numbered sequentially: link 1, link 2, ..., link n.
- **Step 3 — The Joint:** The pivot/sliding interface between two adjacent links. Joints provide the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**.
- **Step 4 — The End-Effector:** Mounted on the terminal link (link n), it performs the actual industrial task.
- **Step 5 — The Wrist:** The sub-chain of joints (usually 2 or 3) closest to the end-effector, responsible for orienting the tool.
- **Step 6 — The Arm:** The sub-chain of joints (usually 3) closest to the base, responsible for positioning the wrist in 3D space.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 3-arm joints handle **positioning** (x, y, z in Cartesian space), while the 2-3 wrist joints handle **orientation** (roll, pitch, yaw). This separation is the **Shoulder-Arm-Wrist Decomposition Principle**.

### 2.2 Joint Types and Symbolic Notation

The international standard notation for joints follows the **Denavit-Hartenberg (D-H) inspired symbolic alphabet**:

| Symbol | Joint Type | DOF | Motion Description | Real-world Example |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|---|
| **R** | Revolute | 1 | Rotation about an axis | Elbow, shoulder |
| **P** | Prismatic | 1 | Linear sliding along an axis | Telescopic arm |
| **V** | Revolute (Cylindrical) | 1 | Continuous rotation | Carousel |
| **H** | Helical (Screw) | 1 | Combined rotation + translation | Lead screw |
| **C** | Cylindrical | 2 | Translation + rotation | Lathe saddle |
| **O** | Planar (Ball) | 3 | Spherical motion | Human shoulder |
| **S** | Spherical | 3 | 3 independent rotations | Gimbal mount |
| **E** | Planar (E-type) | 3 | X-Y translation + rotation | XY-θ table |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degrees of Freedom (Planar Grübler) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ = links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ = 2-DOF joints |
| 2 | Degrees of Freedom (Spatial Grübler) | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | For 3D space; $j_1..j_5$ = joints with 1..5 DOF |
| 3 | Number of Independent Coordinates | $n = \text{number of movable links}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Workspace Volume (Cylindrical) | $V_c = \pi h (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)$ | $m^3$ |
| 5 | Workspace Volume (Spherical) | $V_s = \frac{2}{3}\pi (r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3)$ | $m^3$ |
| 6 | Joint Space ↔ Cartesian Space | $q = (q_1, q_2, ..., q_n)^T$ | $n$-dimensional vector |
| 7 | Pose Definition | $P = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)^T$ | 6 parameters (3 position + 3 orientation) |
| 8 | Repeatability (Industrial Standard) | $RP \leq \pm 0.025 \text{ mm}$ | High-precision class |
| 9 | Payload-to-Weight Ratio | $\eta = \frac{W_{payload}}{W_{robot}}$ | Typical $\eta = 0.05$ to $0.15$ |
| 10 | Number of Joints (Serial) | $n_j = \text{DOF}_{\text{positioning}} + \text{DOF}_{\text{orientation}}$ | $n_j \geq 3$ for spatial positioning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Syntax:** The LaTeX delimiter `\vert` is intentionally used instead of the pipe symbol `\vert x \vert` to preserve markdown table integrity in the KTU 2024 PDF renderer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The symbolic representation of robots is **not merely academic** — it serves three critical engineering purposes:

1. **Communication Standard:** Mechanical, electrical, and software engineers across the globe use a single notation (e.g., **"FANUC M-16iB/20: RRR"**) to unambiguously specify robot architecture.
2. **Kinematic Modeling:** The joint symbol sequence directly determines the choice of **transformation matrices** (e.g., 4×4 homogeneous matrices in D-H convention).
3. **Workcell Layout Design:** Industrial engineers use the workspace geometry derived from the joint sequence to design safe **exclusion zones** and calculate **cycle times**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Grübler's Formula for Degrees of Freedom

The **Grübler-Kutzbach Criterion** is a topological formula that determines the mobility (degrees of freedom) of a kinematic chain. We will derive it for the **planar case** step by step.

---

#### **Step 1: Define the Unconstrained System**

Consider a system with $n$ rigid links (one is the fixed ground) in 2D planar motion.

Each free link in 2D space has **3 degrees of freedom**: 
- Translation along X: $t_x$
- Translation along Y: $t_y$  
- Rotation about Z: $\theta_z$

**Total unconstrained DOF** of the system:

$$
F_{free} = 3n \quad (\text{assuming ground link is also counted, total } n+1 \text{ bodies})
$$

> *Note:* We count only the **movable links** $n$ to be consistent with the formula. Adjusting: if we have $n+1$ total bodies (including ground), the count is $3n$ DOF before constraints.

#### **Step 2: Apply Joint Constraints**

A **1-DOF revolute or prismatic joint** removes **2 degrees of freedom** in 2D (constrains 2 of the 3 planar motions).
A **2-DOF planar joint** removes **1 degree of freedom** in 2D (constrains 1 of the 3 planar motions).

If there are $j_1$ joints of 1-DOF type and $j_2$ joints of 2-DOF type:

$$
F_{constrained} = 2j_1 + 1j_2
$$

#### **Step 3: Subtract Constraints from Free DOF**

The remaining (unconstrained) degrees of freedom represent the **mobility M**:

$$
M = F_{free} - F_{constrained}
$$

$$
M = 3n - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

#### **Step 4: Adjust for the Ground Link**

Since the ground link is fixed and is **not** a moving body, we must subtract 3 DOF (the 3 motions the ground *cannot* perform). This converts $n$ to $(n-1)$:

$$
M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

> **Final Planar Grübler Formula:**

$$
\boxed{M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2}
$$

---

#### **Step 5: Generalization to 3D (Spatial) Case**

In 3D space, each free rigid body has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). A 1-DOF joint in 3D removes **5 DOF**; a 2-DOF joint removes **4**, and so on. The spatial formula is:

$$
M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

$$
\boxed{M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{5} (6-i) \cdot j_i}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: 4-DOF SCARA Robot

**Problem:** A SCARA robot has 4 joints: joint 1 (R), joint 2 (R), joint 3 (P), and joint 4 (R). Find its degrees of freedom using Grübler's formula.

**Given:**
- Number of moving links: $n = 4$
- Number of 1-DOF joints: $j_1 = 4$
- All joints operate in 3D space.

**Solution using Spatial Grübler Formula:**

$$
M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

Substituting $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$, and all higher-DOF joint counts = 0:

$$
M = 6(4-1) - 5(4) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 1(0)
$$

$$
M = 6(3) - 20
$$

$$
M = 18 - 20
$$

$$
M = -2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A **negative result** indicates the structure is a **mechanism**, not a free kinematic chain. For a SCARA, the prismatic joint and a redundancy in design mean special handling is needed. The actual SCARA DOF is 4 (3 for positioning + 1 for orientation about vertical axis), which is verified through physical construction analysis, not just Grübler's formula.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Joint Configuration vs. Workspace

| Robot Type | Joint Sequence | DOF | Workspace Shape | Symbolic Diagram |
|---|---|:---:|---|:---:|
| Cartesian | P-P-P | 3 | Rectangular Box | ⬜ |
| Cylindrical | R-P-P | 3 | Hollow Cylinder | 🟡 |
| Spherical (Polar) | R-R-P | 3 | Spherical Sector | 🔵 |
| Articulated | R-R-R | 3 | Spherical-ish Volume | ⚽ |
| SCARA | R-R-P-R | 4 | Cylindrical Sector | 🟢 |
| Delta (Parallel) | R-R-R (×3) | 3 | Dome | 🔺 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Robot System Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a standard industrial robot, showing how the five major subsystems interact:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HMI[Human Machine Interface HMI Controller Pendant] --> CTRL[Central Controller CPU and Memory]
    CTRL --> ACT[Actuator Drive System]
    CTRL --> SENP[Sensor Processing Unit]
    ACT --> MOT[Servo Motors and Gearboxes]
    MOT --> LINK1[Link 1 and Joint 1]
    LINK1 --> LINK2[Link 2 and Joint 2]
    LINK2 --> LINK3[Link 3 and Joint 3]
    LINK3 --> WRIST[Wrist Assembly Joints 4 5 6]
    WRIST --> EE[End Effector Gripper or Tool]
    SENP --> ENC[Encoders on Joints]
    ENC --> CTRL
    SENP --> FS[Force Torque and Vision Sensors]
    FS --> CTRL
    EE --> WOBJ[Workpiece or Environment]
    WOBJ --> SENP
```

### 4.2 Tree Diagram: Classification of Robots by Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROB[Industrial Robot] --> SER[Serial Kinematic Chain]
    ROB --> PAR[Parallel Kinematic Chain]
    ROB --> HYB[Hybrid Configuration]
    SER --> CAR[Cartesian PPP]
    SER --> CYL[Cylindrical RPP]
    SER --> SPH[Spherical RRP]
    SER --> ART[Articulated RRR]
    SER --> SCA[SCARA RRPR]
    PAR --> DEL[Delta RRR x3]
    PAR --> HEX[Hexapod Stewart Platform]
    PAR --> PLA[Planar XY Parallel]
    HYB --> PAL[Palletizing RRR plus Linear Axis]
    HYB --> WEL[Welding Arm with External Track]
```

### 4.3 Joint Symbol Reference Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Symbolic Joint Notation Alphabet"
        R1[R Symbol Revolute Joint 1 DOF Rotation]
        P1[P Symbol Prismatic Joint 1 DOF Sliding]
        V1[V Symbol Continuous Revolute]
        H1[H Symbol Helical Screw Joint]
        C1[C Symbol Cylindrical Joint 2 DOF]
        O1[O Symbol Planar Spherical Joint 3 DOF]
        S1[S Symbol Spherical Joint 3 DOF]
    end
    R1 --> EX1[Example Elbow of Human Arm]
    P1 --> EX2[Example Hydraulic Piston]
    C1 --> EX3[Example Lathe Compound Slide]
    O1 --> EX4[Example Ball and Socket Joint]
```

### 4.4 Functional Flow: Symbolic Chain to Physical Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1 Identify Task] --> B[Step 2 Choose Joint Types]
    B --> C[Step 3 Write Symbolic Chain e.g. RRP]
    C --> D[Step 4 Apply DH Parameters]
    D --> E[Step 5 Derive Forward Kinematics]
    E --> F[Step 6 Simulate Workspace]
    F --> G[Step 7 Physical Robot Build]
    G --> H[Step 8 Validate with Test Path]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Notes Applied:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (`step1`, `step2`, etc.), all labels are plain text without markdown formatting inside the quoted strings, and no reserved keywords are used as standalone node names.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*Define the term "Degree of Freedom" (DOF) as applied to a robotic manipulator. How many DOF are required to completely specify the position and orientation of an object in 3D space?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot is defined as the **number of independent parameters (coordinates)** required to completely specify the configuration (position and orientation) of the manipulator's end-effector in space.

To fully specify the position and orientation of a rigid body in 3D space:
- **3 DOF** are required for **position** (coordinates $x, y, z$ in Cartesian space)
- **3 DOF** are required for **orientation** (Euler angles: roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$, yaw $\psi$)

**Total DOF required = 3 + 3 = 6 DOF**

A robot with 6 DOF is called a **dexterous** robot and can attain any arbitrary pose in its workspace. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

*List any three types of joints used in robotic manipulators. State one real-world example of a revolute joint.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three common joint types used in robotic manipulators are:

1. **Revolute Joint (R)** — Provides 1 DOF rotation about a fixed axis. *Example: The elbow joint of a human arm, or the hinge of a door.*
2. **Prismatic Joint (P)** — Provides 1 DOF linear sliding motion along a fixed axis. *Example: A hydraulic cylinder piston, or a linear actuator in a Cartesian robot.*
3. **Cylindrical Joint (C)** — Provides 2 DOF (1 rotation + 1 translation). *Example: The compound slide of a lathe machine.* **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note on KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each, with internal choice between the two Part B questions on the question paper.

---

**Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat symbolic diagram, explain the **five major components** of an industrial robot. State the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify robots based on their **mechanical structure / kinematic configuration**. For each type, give the **symbolic joint sequence** and **one industrial application**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The five major components of an industrial robot are:

1. **Manipulator (Mechanical Arm)** — Composed of links and joints in a serial kinematic chain. *Function:* Provides the physical reach and motion of the robot arm from the base to the end-effector.

2. **End-Effector (EOAT — End of Arm Tooling)** — The tool mounted on the wrist, such as a gripper, welding torch, or paint sprayer. *Function:* Performs the actual industrial task on the workpiece.

3. **Actuators** — Servo motors, stepper motors, hydraulic cylinders, or pneumatic pistons. *Function:* Convert electrical/pneumatic/hydraulic energy into mechanical motion to drive the joints.

4. **Sensors** — Encoders (for joint position), force/torque sensors, proximity switches, and vision cameras. *Function:* Provide feedback to the controller about the robot's internal state and external environment.

5. **Controller** — A microcomputer/CPU with memory and software. *Function:* Executes the user program, computes joint trajectories, and sends signals to the actuators.

**(Full 7 marks awarded for: Correct identification of all 5 components + function of each + symbolic link/joint diagram showing manipulator + end-effector)**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

Classification by mechanical structure:

| Sl. No. | Configuration | Symbolic Joint Sequence | Industrial Application |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cartesian / Gantry** | P-P-P | CNC machine loading, 3D printing |
| 2 | **Cylindrical** | R-P-P | Machine tending, foundry work |
| 3 | **Spherical / Polar** | R-R-P | Welding, material handling |
| 4 | **Articulated (Anthropomorphic)** | R-R-R (+ wrist) | Automotive welding, painting |
| 5 | **SCARA** | R-R-P-R | PCB assembly, pick & place |
| 6 | **Parallel (Delta)** | R-R-R (×3 parallel arms) | High-speed packaging, food sorting |

**(Full 7 marks awarded for: All 5-6 configurations correctly listed + correct joint sequence + one valid application per type + neat symbolic diagrams)**

---

**Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **symbolic representation of joints** in robotics with a labeled diagram. List the symbols R, P, V, H, C, O, S with their DOF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-DOF planar robot has 4 moving links connected by 4 revolute joints and 1 prismatic joint. Calculate its degrees of freedom using **Grübler's formula**. Verify the result physically. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

Symbolic representation in robotics is a standardized shorthand notation to denote the type and sequence of joints in a kinematic chain. This allows engineers to communicate robot architecture globally without ambiguity.

**Standard Joint Symbols and Their DOF:**

| Symbol | Joint Name | DOF | Motion Description |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| **R** | Revolute | 1 | Rotation about a single axis |
| **P** | Prismatic | 1 | Linear translation along an axis |
| **V** | Revolute (continuous) | 1 | Unbounded rotation |
| **H** | Helical / Screw | 1 | Combined rotation + translation (e.g., lead screw) |
| **C** | Cylindrical | 2 | Rotation + translation (independently) |
| **O** | Planar / Ball (2D) | 3 | Motion in a plane (2 translations + 1 rotation) |
| **S** | Spherical | 3 | 3 independent rotations about a common point |

**Example Labeled Diagram (to be drawn):** Show a 3-link manipulator with joints labeled R, R, P (in that order from base to tool), with coordinate frames at each joint per DH convention.

**(Full 7 marks = 5 marks for table + 2 marks for labeled diagram)**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- $n = 4$ moving links (planar system)
- $j_1 = 4$ revolute joints (1-DOF each)
- $j_2 = 1$ prismatic joint (1-DOF, but in the planar Grübler formula, a 1-DOF joint = 2D constraint)

**Applying the Planar Grübler Formula:**

$$
M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

Substituting values:

$$
M = 3(4-1) - 2(4 + 1) - 0
$$

$$
M = 3(3) - 2(5)
$$

$$
M = 9 - 10
$$

$$
M = -1
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The negative result indicates a redundant constraint or closed-loop structure** — the system has more constraints than it has freedoms. This is known as a **statically indeterminate** or **pre-stressed** structure.

**Physical Verification:**
- 4 links in 2D plane have $4 \times 3 = 12$ DOF before constraints.
- 5 joints (each 1-DOF) impose $5 \times 2 = 10$ constraints.
- Net mobility = $12 - 10 = 2$ DOF.
- This matches the 2-DOF planar motion (e.g., XY positioning with 2 inputs but 4 links — a parallel-like structure).

**Conclusion:** The negative Grübler result signals a **parallel/redundant mechanism**, and the effective DOF must be confirmed by physical analysis (2 DOF in this case).

**(Full 7 marks = 3 marks formula application + 2 marks for substitution + 2 marks for physical verification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "joints" with "links"** — A serial chain with $n$ joints has $n+1$ links (including the ground). This is a common 2-mark deduction point.
> 2. **Do NOT write "Degree of Freedom" without specifying "in 3D space"** — always clarify whether planar (3 DOF per link) or spatial (6 DOF per link) Grübler is being used.
> 3. **Symbol notation must be uppercase (R, P)** — lowercase is incorrect and may cost 0.5 mark.
> 4. **For a negative Grübler result, do NOT leave the answer as a number** — you MUST comment on what it implies (redundancy or parallel structure).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Robot Definition:** A reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator (RIA 1979) — must be **programmable** and **multitasking**.
- **5 Subsystems:** Manipulator, End-Effector, Actuators, Sensors, Controller.
- **Joint Symbols:** R = Revolute, P = Prismatic, V = Continuous Revolute, H = Helical, C = Cylindrical (2 DOF), O = Planar (3 DOF), S = Spherical (3 DOF).
- **Standard Configurations (must memorize):** Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), Articulated (RRR), SCARA (RRP + R), Delta (parallel RRR).
- **DOF Requirement:** 3 DOF for position + 3 DOF for orientation = **6 DOF total** for full 3D dexterity.
- **Planar Grübler:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- **Spatial Grübler:** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
- **Shoulder-Arm-Wrist Decomposition:** First 3 joints = positioning, last 2-3 joints = orientation.
- **Negative Grübler M** = redundant/parallel mechanism — not a free kinematic chain.
- **Industrial Standard:** ISO 9283 governs robot performance testing; repeatability typically $\leq \pm 0.025$ mm for precision class.
- **Common Pitfall:** SCARA is **NOT** fully 3D positioning — it has 2 planar positioning + 1 vertical (P) + 1 rotation = 4 DOF total.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degrees of Freedom and Workspace
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Degrees of Freedom and Workspace

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robotic manipulator is defined as the **number of independent parameters (variables)** that are required to completely specify the configuration of the manipulator (i.e., the position and orientation of every link of the robot with respect to a fixed reference frame).

Mathematically, the DOF of a mechanism is given by the mobility of the mechanism, denoted by **M**. For a spatial mechanism, **M** represents the number of independent ways in which the mechanism can move in 3D space.

A single **rigid body in 3D space** possesses **6 DOF**:
- **3 Translational DOF** → movement along the X, Y, and Z axes (surge, sway, heave).
- **3 Rotational DOF** → rotation about the X, Y, and Z axes (roll, pitch, yaw).

A **planar rigid body** (constrained to move in a single plane) possesses only **3 DOF**: 2 translations (along X and Y) and 1 rotation (about Z).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

Imagine you are asked to place your fingertip at a specific point in space. To do this, your brain commands your **shoulder (3 DOF)**, **elbow (1 DOF)**, and **wrist (3 DOF)** — a total of **7 DOF** in the human arm. However, to reach a specific *point* in space, you need only **6 DOF** (3 for position + 3 for orientation). The extra DOF is called **redundancy**, which is what allows you to reach the same point with your elbow up *or* down, or while keeping your wrist stiff or loose. Robots are designed with the same idea in mind — **just enough DOF for the task, plus redundancy only if needed for obstacle avoidance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DOF matters in KTU exams:**
> The DOF question is almost always a 7 or 14 mark question in KTU ESE. The examiner expects: (1) definition, (2) the Grübler formula, (3) joint DOF table, and (4) a numerical calculation on a given robot configuration.

---

## 1.2 Workspace — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The **Workspace** of a robot manipulator is the **set of all points in space that the end-effector (or the tool centre point, TCP) can reach**, given the geometric constraints of the links and the mechanical limits (joint limits) of the joints.

The workspace is broadly classified into two categories:

| Workspace Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Reachable Workspace** (RWS) | The total volume that **at least one orientation** of the end-effector can reach. |
| **Dexterous Workspace** (DWS) | The sub-volume within the reachable workspace where the end-effector can reach the point with **all possible orientations (360° roll, pitch, yaw)**. |

### Conceptual Analogy — The Lighthouse

Think of a **lighthouse beam**. The beam can sweep in a circle, lighting up a full ring of points — that ring is the **reachable workspace**. But the *brightest central spot* of the beam, where the lighthouse keeper can stand and be seen clearly from every direction (i.e., the camera can be rotated freely), is the **dexterous workspace**. The dexterous workspace is always a **subset** of the reachable workspace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:**
> For a robot with **n ≤ 6 DOF**, the dexterous workspace is typically empty or very small. The dexterity **peaks** when the robot is in a specific "elbow-up" or "elbow-down" singular configuration. The boundary of the reachable workspace for a 2-DOF planar arm is an **annulus** (a ring-shaped region).

---

## 1.3 Joint Types and Their DOF

The DOF of a robot is built up by chaining together **joints**, each contributing a certain number of DOF. A joint in robotics is a kinematic pair that constrains the relative motion between two adjacent links.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lower Pair Joints (Surface Contact):** Most robot joints are *lower pairs*, where the surfaces are in conformal contact. These are:
> - **Revolute (R)** → 1 DOF (rotation about an axis) — *e.g., shoulder of a robot arm*
> - **Prismatic (P)** → 1 DOF (linear translation along an axis) — *e.g., telescopic column of a crane*
> - **Cylindrical (C)** → 2 DOF (rotation + translation along the same axis)
> - **Spherical (S)** / **Ball-and-Socket** → 3 DOF (rotation about 3 axes)
> - **Planar (E)** → 3 DOF (2 translations + 1 rotation in a plane)
> - **Helical/Screw (H)** → 1 DOF (coupled rotation + translation)
> - **Universal (U)** → 2 DOF (two perpendicular revolute axes)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Grübler's Formula — The Heart of DOF Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grübler's Criterion (Kutzbach–Grübler Criterion):**
> This is the **standard, examiner-preferred method** for calculating the DOF (mobility, M) of any planar or spatial mechanism. It is a KTU high-yield topic — expect a direct 7-mark question on it.

### 2.1.1 Planar Mechanism Grübler's Formula

For a mechanism moving in a single plane:

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Where:
- $M$ = mobility (DOF) of the mechanism.
- $n$ = total number of links (including the ground link).
- $j_1$ = number of **lower pairs** (1 DOF joints) — revolute, prismatic.
- $j_2$ = number of **higher pairs** (2 DOF joints) — e.g., cam-follower in contact.

### 2.1.2 Spatial Mechanism Grübler's Formula

For a mechanism in 3D space:

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

Where:
- $j_i$ = number of joints with $i$ degrees of freedom.

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Grübler's Formula

The formula is built on three pillars:

1. **Total freedom without constraints:** A free-floating rigid body in 3D has **6 DOF**. So $n$ links (with one as the fixed ground) would have a theoretical maximum of $6(n-1)$ DOF.
2. **Constraint removal by joints:** Each joint of type $j_i$ removes $6 - i$ degrees of freedom. For example, a revolute joint ($j_1 = 1$ DOF joint) removes $6 - 1 = 5$ DOF.
3. **Net DOF:** Subtract the total constraints from the total theoretical freedom.

> **Intuition:** Think of building a kinematic chain. Start with unconnected links (lots of freedom), and each joint you add *restricts* some of that freedom. The remaining free parameters are your DOF.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{\text{planar}} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Grübler's formula for planar mechanisms | dimensionless |
| 2 | $M_{\text{spatial}} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | Grübler's formula for spatial mechanisms | dimensionless |
| 3 | DOF of a free rigid body in 3D | $M = 6$ | 3 translations + 3 rotations |
| 4 | DOF of a free rigid body in 2D | $M = 3$ | 2 translations + 1 rotation |
| 5 | Workspace of 2-DOF arm | Inner radius: $r_{\min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert$ ; Outer radius: $r_{\max} = l_1 + l_2$ | length (m or cm) |
| 6 | Workspace of 2-DOF arm (area) | $A = \pi \left( (l_1 + l_2)^2 - (l_1 - l_2)^2 \right) = 4 \pi l_1 l_2$ | area unit |
| 7 | Reachable Workspace | $W_R = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid \exists q : f(q) = p \}$ | set of points |
| 8 | Dexterous Workspace | $W_D = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid \forall R \in SO(3), \exists q : f(q) = (p, R) \}$ | set of points |
| 9 | Jacobian Singularity | $\det(J(q)) = 0 \Rightarrow$ loss of DOF at boundary | condition |
| 10 | Total DOF rule for a robot | $M = \sum (\text{DOF of each joint})$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Tip:** The pipe symbol `\vert` in formula 5 is written as `\vert` deliberately — never use a raw `\vert x \vert` inside markdown tables, as it breaks the table rendering. Always use `\lvert` and `\rvert` or `\vert` for absolute value inside tables.

---

## 2.3 Joint DOF Reference Table (For Quick Recall)

| Joint Symbol | Joint Name | DOF | Type | Typical Robot Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R | Revolute | 1 | Lower pair | Shoulder, elbow, wrist joints |
| P | Prismatic | 1 | Lower pair | Linear gantry, Cartesian Z-axis |
| C | Cylindrical | 2 | Lower pair | Radial drilling machine |
| S / B | Spherical / Ball | 3 | Lower pair | Hip joint of humanoid robot |
| U | Universal | 2 | Lower pair | Between wrist and forearm |
| E | Planar | 3 | Lower pair | XY plotter table |
| H | Helical / Screw | 1 | Higher pair | Lead screw, ball screw |
| (Cam) | Cam-follower | 2 | Higher pair | Indexing mechanisms |

---

## 2.4 Workspace — Engineering Utility

The workspace calculation is **not just academic**. In real-world engineering:

- **Automotive welding:** Engineers compute the workspace of a 6-DOF welding arm to verify that it can reach every spot on the car body.
- **Surgical robotics (Da Vinci):** Workspace must be carefully designed so the surgeon can reach all incision points inside the patient.
- **CNC Machining:** Workspace of the end-mill must cover the full envelope of the workpiece.
- **Disaster-response robots:** Workspace must be maximized to navigate cluttered rubble.

A robot with **insufficient DOF** cannot perform a task; a robot with **excess DOF** is expensive and harder to control. **DOF and workspace go hand-in-hand** in robot selection.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Grübler's Formula (Spatial)

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation is the **complete step-by-step logical build** that an examiner would expect on a 7-mark derivation question.

### Step 1: Define the Degrees of Freedom of an Unconstrained Rigid Body

A single rigid body in 3D Euclidean space has **3 translational** degrees of freedom (displacement along the $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ axes) and **3 rotational** degrees of freedom (rotation about $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ axes). Therefore, the total DOF of a free rigid body in 3D is:

$$
\text{DOF}_{\text{free, 3D}} = 3 + 3 = 6
$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Unconstrained DOF of a Multi-Body System

Consider a system of $n$ links, with one link designated as the **ground (fixed reference)**. The remaining $n - 1$ links are free to move. If there were **no joints** between any of the links, each of the $n - 1$ moving links would independently have 6 DOF. Hence, the total unconstrained DOF is:

$$
\text{Total Unconstrained DOF} = 6 \times (n - 1)
$$

### Step 3: Determine the Number of Constraints Imposed by Each Joint

When two links are connected by a joint, the joint **restricts** the relative motion between them. A joint of type $j_i$ that provides $i$ degrees of freedom (i.e., allows $i$ independent relative motions) therefore removes $6 - i$ degrees of freedom.

For example:
- A **revolute joint** ($i = 1$) removes $6 - 1 = 5$ DOF.
- A **universal joint** ($i = 2$) removes $6 - 2 = 4$ DOF.
- A **spherical joint** ($i = 3$) removes $6 - 3 = 3$ DOF.
- A **planar joint** ($i = 3$) removes $6 - 3 = 3$ DOF.
- A **cylindrical joint** ($i = 2$) removes $6 - 2 = 4$ DOF.
- A **helical joint** ($i = 1$) removes $6 - 1 = 5$ DOF.
- A **prismatic joint** ($i = 1$) removes $6 - 1 = 5$ DOF.

> **Interpretation:** A 1-DOF joint between two 6-DOF bodies leaves only **one** relative motion, but eliminates **five** of the six possible relative motions.

### Step 4: Sum the Constraints

The total number of constraints imposed by all joints in the mechanism is:

$$
\text{Total Constraints} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (6 - i) \cdot j_i = 5 j_1 + 4 j_2 + 3 j_3 + 2 j_4 + 1 j_5
$$

where $j_i$ is the number of joints with $i$ DOF.

### Step 5: Net Mobility Formula

The **net DOF (mobility)** of the mechanism is the unconstrained DOF minus the constraints imposed by the joints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n - 1) - \left[ 5 j_1 + 4 j_2 + 3 j_3 + 2 j_4 + 1 j_5 \right] \\
\therefore \quad M &= 6(n - 1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2 - 3 j_3 - 2 j_4 - j_5
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **spatial Grübler–Kutzbach formula**.

### Step 6: Reduction to the Planar Case

For a planar mechanism, the unconstrained DOF of a single body is $3$ (not 6). The constraints are:
- A 1-DOF planar joint (R or P) removes $3 - 1 = 2$ DOF.
- A 2-DOF planar joint (cam) removes $3 - 2 = 1$ DOF.

So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - 1 j_2 \\
M &= 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The same logic must be applied **rigorously** for the KTU derivation question. The examiner awards 1 mark for each logical step.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — DOF of a 6-DOF Articulated Robot (PUMA-style)

A 6-revolute articulated robot (PUMA 560) has:
- 1 ground (fixed base) link.
- 6 moving links (upper arm, forearm, and 3 wrist links).
- 6 revolute joints.

Therefore:
- $n = 7$ (1 ground + 6 moving links).
- $j_1 = 6$ (all revolute, each providing 1 DOF).
- $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$.

Applying the spatial Grübler's formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n - 1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2 - 3 j_3 - 2 j_4 - j_5 \\
M &= 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 1(0) \\
M &= 6 \times 6 - 30 \\
M &= 36 - 30 \\
M &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The PUMA 560 has **6 DOF**, as expected. The 6 revolute joints are sufficient to position and orient the end-effector anywhere in its 3D workspace.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Workspace of a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

Consider a 2-revolute planar arm with link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$ (no joint limits for now).

### Step 1: Position of the End-Effector

The position $(x, y)$ of the end-effector as a function of joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ is:

$$
x = l_1 \cos(\theta_1) + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = l_1 \sin(\theta_1) + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Squared Distance from Origin

The squared distance of the end-effector from the base (origin) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^2 &= x^2 + y^2 \\
&= (l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2))^2 + (l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2))^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding using the identity $\cos^2 \alpha + \sin^2 \alpha = 1$ and the cosine subtraction formula $\cos(A - B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^2 &= l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos(\theta_1 - (\theta_1 + \theta_2)) \\
&= l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos(\theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Boundary Conditions on $r$

The maximum value of $r$ occurs when $\cos(\theta_2) = +1$, i.e., when the arm is fully extended:

$$
r_{\max} = \sqrt{l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2} = l_1 + l_2
$$

The minimum value of $r$ occurs when $\cos(\theta_2) = -1$, i.e., when the arm is fully folded back on itself:

$$
r_{\min} = \sqrt{l_1^2 + l_2^2 - 2 l_1 l_2} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert
$$

### Step 4: Workspace Area (Annulus)

Since $r$ varies continuously between $r_{\min}$ and $r_{\max}$, the reachable workspace is an **annulus** (a ring-shaped region). The area of the annulus is:

$$
A_{\text{workspace}} = \pi r_{\max}^2 - \pi r_{\min}^2 = \pi \left[ (l_1 + l_2)^2 - (l_1 - l_2)^2 \right]
$$

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{workspace}} &= \pi \left[ l_1^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 + l_2^2 - l_1^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 - l_2^2 \right] \\
&= \pi \cdot 4 l_1 l_2 \\
&= 4 \pi l_1 l_2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The reachable workspace of a 2-DOF planar arm is an **annulus** with outer radius $l_1 + l_2$, inner radius $\vert l_1 - l_2 \vert$, and area $4 \pi l_1 l_2$.

> **Dexterous Workspace:** The dexterous workspace for a 2-DOF arm is **a single point** (the origin) only when the arm can fold onto itself. In a practical 2-DOF arm, the dexterous workspace is **empty** because the arm cannot achieve all orientations at any point other than the base.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DOF vs Workspace for Common Robot Configurations

| Robot Type | Configuration | Number of Joints | DOF | Workspace Shape |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (PPP) | 3 prismatic | 3 | 3 | Rectangular box |
| Cylindrical (RPP) | 1R + 2P | 3 | 3 | Hollow cylinder |
| Spherical (RRP) | 2R + 1P | 3 | 3 | Section of a sphere |
| Articulated (RRR) | 3 revolute | 3 | 3 | Section of a sphere (complex) |
| SCARA (RRP) | 2R + 1P (selective compliance) | 3 (or 4) | 3 (or 4) | Cylindrical, vertical axis |
| PUMA 560 | 6 revolute | 6 | 6 | General 3D |
| Stanford Arm | RRR + 3R wrist | 6 | 6 | General 3D |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term 'Degrees of Freedom' as applied to a robotic manipulator. State the number of DOF possessed by a rigid body in 3-dimensional space.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robotic manipulator is the **number of independent parameters** (joint variables) that completely specify the configuration of the manipulator — i.e., the position and orientation of every link with respect to a fixed reference frame.

A single **rigid body in 3-dimensional space** possesses **6 DOF**, comprising:
- 3 translational DOF (motion along X, Y, Z axes — *surge, sway, heave*).
- 3 rotational DOF (rotation about X, Y, Z axes — *roll, pitch, yaw*).

A planar rigid body (constrained to a plane) has **3 DOF** (2 translations + 1 rotation).

**Examiner's Note:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for 6 DOF in 3D, 1 mark for the breakdown (3T + 3R).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the 'Reachable Workspace' and the 'Dexterous Workspace' of a robot manipulator.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Reachable Workspace (RWS) | Dexterous Workspace (DWS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Set of all points reachable by the TCP with **at least one** orientation of the end-effector. | Subset of RWS where the TCP can be reached with **all possible** orientations. |
| **Size** | Larger; covers the full envelope of the robot. | Smaller; often empty for low-DOF arms. |
| **Shape (2-DOF planar)** | Annulus (ring). | Empty or a single point at the base. |
| **Mathematical Set** | $W_R = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid \exists q : f(q) = p \}$ | $W_D = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid \forall R \in SO(3), \exists q : f(q) = (p, R) \}$ |
| **Relation** | $W_D \subseteq W_R$ | Always contained in RWS. |

**Examiner's Note:** Award 1 mark for the definition of each workspace, 1 mark for the correct set notation / shape, and 1 mark for stating the inclusion $W_D \subseteq W_R$.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the different types of joints used in robotic manipulators, with neat sketches and a statement of the degrees of freedom provided by each joint. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Robot manipulators are constructed by connecting **rigid links** through **joints** (kinematic pairs). The choice of joint type determines the DOF of the robot. The standard joints used in robotics are classified into **lower pairs** (surface contact) and **higher pairs** (point or line contact).

**1. Revolute Joint (R) — 1 DOF:**
- Provides **rotational motion** about a fixed axis.
- Constrains 5 DOF (3 translations + 2 rotations).
- **Examples:** Shoulder, elbow, wrist joints of an articulated robot; hinges.

**2. Prismatic Joint (P) — 1 DOF:**
- Provides **linear sliding motion** along a fixed axis.
- Constrains 5 DOF (2 translations + 3 rotations).
- **Examples:** Z-axis of a Cartesian gantry; telescopic arm of a crane.

**3. Cylindrical Joint (C) — 2 DOF:**
- Combines a revolute and a prismatic joint along a common axis.
- Allows independent rotation and translation.
- **Examples:** Radial drilling machine column.

**4. Spherical (Ball-and-Socket) Joint (S) — 3 DOF:**
- Allows 3 independent rotations (no translation).
- **Examples:** Hip joint of a humanoid robot; shoulder of a 7-DOF arm.

**5. Universal (Hooke) Joint (U) — 2 DOF:**
- Two perpendicular revolute axes, often used in wrists.
- **Examples:** Drive shafts in cars; between forearm and wrist of a robot.

**6. Planar Joint (E) — 3 DOF:**
- Allows 2 translations and 1 rotation, all in a single plane.
- **Examples:** XY plotter table.

**7. Helical / Screw Joint (H) — 1 DOF:**
- A coupled rotation and translation (e.g., lead screw).
- **Examples:** Ball screw in CNC machines.

> **Examiner's Note:** Award 1 mark per joint (1 for name + DOF + 1 short application), totalling 7 marks. The student must give the *number* of DOF, *not* just the name.

---

#### (b) Using Kutzbach–Grübler's formula, determine the degrees of freedom of a 6-revolute articulated robot (PUMA-style) with a spherical wrist. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Number of revolute joints $j_1 = 6$ (each contributing 1 DOF).
- Number of links: $n = 7$ (1 ground + 6 moving links: upper arm, forearm, 3 wrist links, end-effector mount).
- All other joint types: $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$.

**Spatial Grübler's Formula:**

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2 - 3 j_3 - 2 j_4 - j_5
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 1(0) \\
&= 6 \times 6 - 30 \\
&= 36 - 30 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The PUMA-style articulated robot has **6 DOF**, sufficient to position and orient the end-effector at any reachable point in 3D space (3 for position + 3 for orientation).

> **Examiner's Note:** 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for correctly counting $n$, 2 marks for substituting the joint counts, 1 mark for the arithmetic, 1 mark for the correct final answer, and 1 mark for the interpretation/validation comment.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Discuss in detail the concept of workspace analysis for a robotic manipulator. Compare the reachable and dexterous workspaces, with the help of a 2-DOF planar manipulator example. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction to Workspace:**
The **workspace** of a robotic manipulator is the **3D region in space that the end-effector (tool centre point, TCP) can physically reach**, given the link lengths and the joint mechanical limits (range of motion of each joint). Workspace analysis is one of the most important design-time checks for any robot cell, as it determines whether the manipulator can physically perform the assigned task.

**Classification of Workspace:**

**1. Reachable Workspace (RWS):**
- The set of all points reachable by the TCP with **at least one orientation** of the end-effector.
- For a 2-DOF planar arm with links $l_1$ and $l_2$, the RWS is an **annulus** (ring) with:
  - Outer radius $r_{\max} = l_1 + l_2$ (arm fully extended).
  - Inner radius $r_{\min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert$ (arm fully folded back).
  - Area $= 4 \pi l_1 l_2$.

**2. Dexterous Workspace (DWS):**
- The sub-volume of the RWS where the TCP can be reached with **all possible orientations** of the end-effector (full roll, pitch, yaw).
- For a 2-DOF planar arm, the DWS is **a single point** at the base (origin), because with only 2 DOF the arm cannot achieve arbitrary orientation at any other point.
- For a 6-DOF arm, the DWS is a meaningful sub-volume of the RWS, typically centered around the *elbow-up singular configuration*.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Reachable Workspace | Dexterous Workspace |
|---|---|---|
| Cardinality | Larger; full envelope | Smaller; subset of RWS |
| Orientation requirement | At least one | All possible |
| 2-DOF planar example | Annulus with area $4 \pi l_1 l_2$ | A single point (origin) |
| 6-DOF example | Roughly spherical region | Sub-volume inside RWS |
| Set relation | $W_R$ | $W_D \subseteq W_R$ |

**Engineering Significance:**
- A task that requires arbitrary orientation of the end-effector (e.g., **welding a complex 3D curve**) needs a robot whose task volume lies inside the DWS.
- A task that requires only one orientation (e.g., **pick-and-place**, **vertical insertion**) needs only the RWS.
- **Workspace holes** and **singularities** must be identified before deployment.

> **Examiner's Note:** 2 marks for the definition and classification, 2 marks for the 2-DOF annulus derivation, 2 marks for the comparison, and 1 mark for the engineering significance.

---

#### (b) A 2-DOF planar RR manipulator has link lengths $l_1 = 10$ cm and $l_2 = 8$ cm. Determine the inner and outer radii of the reachable workspace and the total workspace area. State whether the dexterous workspace is non-empty. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $l_1 = 10$ cm
- $l_2 = 8$ cm
- Joint type: 2 revolute (RR) — no joint limits assumed for ideal case.

**Step 1: Outer Radius of Reachable Workspace**

The outer radius is achieved when the arm is **fully extended** ($\theta_2 = 0$):

$$
r_{\max} = l_1 + l_2 = 10 + 8 = 18 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2: Inner Radius of Reachable Workspace**

The inner radius is achieved when the arm is **fully folded back** ($\theta_2 = \pi$):

$$
r_{\min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert = \vert 10 - 8 \vert = 2 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 3: Workspace Area (Annulus)**

$$
A_{\text{workspace}} = \pi \left( r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2 \right)
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{workspace}} &= \pi \left( 18^2 - 2^2 \right) \\
&= \pi \left( 324 - 4 \right) \\
&= 320 \pi \text{ cm}^2 \\
&\approx 1005.31 \text{ cm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using the simplified formula** $A = 4 \pi l_1 l_2$:

$$
A = 4 \pi \times 10 \times 8 = 320 \pi \approx 1005.31 \text{ cm}^2
$$

Both methods agree. ✓

**Step 4: Dexterous Workspace**

A 2-DOF RR planar arm has only **2 DOF**, which is sufficient to specify a point in the plane (2 coordinates) but **not** sufficient to specify the orientation of the end-effector (which requires 1 more DOF — the roll angle $\phi$). Therefore, the dexterous workspace is **empty** (or a single trivial point at the origin where the arm can fold onto itself). In practice, the dexterous workspace is **non-existent** for a pure 2-DOF RR arm.

> **Conclusion:**
> - Inner radius: **2 cm**.
> - Outer radius: **18 cm**.
> - Workspace area: **$320 \pi \approx 1005.31$ cm²**.
> - Dexterous workspace: **Empty** (zero area).

> **Examiner's Note:** Award 1 mark each for $r_{\max}$ and $r_{\min}$, 2 marks for the area calculation, 1 mark for the alternative formula verification, 1 mark for the dexterous workspace analysis, and 1 mark for the concluding statement.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ground link in $n$:** Students often count only the moving links, missing the fixed ground. If the robot has $k$ moving links, $n$ must be $k + 1$. Forgetting the ground gives $M = 6(k - 1) - 5 j_1$, which is **wrong by 6 DOF**.
> 2. **Mixing up joint types:** A **cylindrical joint** has 2 DOF, not 1. A **spherical joint** has 3 DOF, not 1. Misclassifying these gives an incorrect $j_1, j_2, j_3$ count and loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Using the planar formula on a 3D robot:** Always check whether the mechanism is planar (use $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$) or spatial (use the full 6-DOF formula). Applying the planar formula to a 3D PUMA arm is a common, **fatal** error.
> 4. **Confusing reachable vs dexterous workspace:** The dexterous workspace is **always** a subset of the reachable workspace. Drawing them as equal, or as the dexterous workspace being *larger* than the reachable, costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the interpretation step:** After computing $M$, the examiner expects a **sentence of interpretation** (e.g., "*M = 6, so the robot can position and orient its end-effector anywhere in 3D space*"). Skipping this costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Units missing:** Workspace areas and radii must carry units (cm, m, cm², m²). A numerical answer without units is marked partially incorrect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Degrees of Freedom and Workspace**
>
> - [x] **DOF** = number of independent parameters to fully specify the manipulator configuration.
> - [x] A free rigid body in **3D → 6 DOF**; in 2D → 3 DOF.
> - [x] **Planar Grübler's formula:** $M = 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - j_2$.
> - [x] **Spatial Grübler's formula:** $M = 6(n - 1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2 - 3 j_3 - 2 j_4 - j_5$.
> - [x] **Always include the ground link** in the count of $n$.
> - [x] Joint DOF reference: R = 1, P = 1, C = 2, S = 3, U = 2, E = 3, H = 1.
> - [x] **Reachable Workspace (RWS)** = all points reachable with **at least one** orientation.
> - [x] **Dexterous Workspace (DWS)** = all points reachable with **all possible** orientations; $W_D \subseteq W_R$.
> - [x] For a 2-DOF RR planar arm, **RWS is an annulus** with $r_{\max} = l_1 + l_2$, $r_{\min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert$, area $= 4 \pi l_1 l_2$.
> - [x] The **DWS of a 2-DOF arm is empty** (the arm has insufficient DOF to orient the end-effector).
> - [x] A robot needs **6 DOF** for general 3D positioning + orientation; **redundant** arms have **> 6 DOF**.
> - [x] **Singularities** occur at workspace boundaries when $\det(J(q)) = 0$ — DOF is locally lost.
> - [x] **KTU 2024 Scheme:** DOF + Grübler's formula + Workspace types are **assured questions** in Part A or Part B of Module 1.
> - [x] Always state **units** and provide a one-line **interpretation** of the final numerical result.
> - [x] Remember: $\lvert x \rvert$ uses `\lvert` and `\rvert` in LaTeX — avoid raw `|` inside markdown tables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Robots - Power Source
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Robots - Power Source

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of industrial robotics, **power source** refers to the primary energy supply mechanism that drives the actuators responsible for producing motion and force in a robot's joints and end-effector. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCRAT303 – Module 1)**, robots are broadly classified into three principal categories based on the nature of the power source employed to actuate their motion-producing elements:

1. **Electrically Powered Robots**
2. **Hydraulically Powered Robots**
3. **Pneumatically Powered Robots**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of robots by power source is a foundational concept. It is **directly linked to Course Outcome CO1** of PCRAT303 — *"Understand the basic concepts, anatomy, and classification of industrial robots."* This topic carries a typical mark weightage of **3 to 7 marks** in End Semester Examinations.

Each power source category directly determines the **load-handling capacity, speed of operation, accuracy, working environment compatibility, and overall cost** of the robotic system. The selection of a power source is the first critical decision in the design of any robotic work cell.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Understanding It Intuitively

Imagine the human body as a robot:

- The **muscles** of the human body generate force and movement. Different muscles use different "energy sources" — your **arm muscles** use chemical energy, but if we think of machines:
  - **Electric Power →** Like a precise, quiet watchmaker's tool. Smooth, accurate, and clean. (e.g., A robot arm assembling a circuit board)
  - **Hydraulic Power →** Like a powerful construction crane. Uses pressurized liquid (oil) to lift massive loads. (e.g., A robot handling a car chassis)
  - **Pneumatic Power →** Like a balloon releasing air. Uses compressed gas (air) for quick, light pushes. (e.g., A robot picking a biscuit from a conveyor)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of three vehicles — an **electric car** (smooth, silent, accurate), a **hydraulic excavator** (powerful, slow, heavy-duty), and a **pneumatic air gun** (fast, simple, light tasks). Each represents a robotic power source's personality.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Industrial Air Pressure for Pneumatic Systems:** **0.5 to 1.0 MPa (5 to 10 bar)**
- **Typical Hydraulic Operating Pressure:** **7 to 21 MPa (70 to 210 bar)**
- **Standard Electric Supply (Industrial):** **400V AC, 3-phase, 50 Hz** (India) or **24V/48V DC** for low-power servo systems
- **Repeatability Classification (linked to power source):**
  - Electric: **±0.01 mm to ±0.05 mm**
  - Hydraulic: **±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm**
  - Pneumatic: **±0.5 mm to ±2.0 mm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Electrically Powered Robots

Electric robots use **electrical energy** converted into mechanical motion through motors. They dominate modern industrial robotics (over **70%** of installed industrial robots globally).

**Sub-classifications:**

**(a) DC Servo Motor Driven Robots**
- Use brushed DC servo motors with tachogenerators and encoders
- Excellent speed control over a wide range
- Older generation; gradually being replaced

**(b) AC Servo Motor Driven Robots**
- Use brushless AC servo motors (most common today)
- Higher power-to-weight ratio
- Maintenance-free (no brushes)
- Used in precision applications (assembly, welding, CNC tending)

**(c) Stepper Motor Driven Robots**
- Open-loop control (no feedback required for basic operation)
- Position determined by pulse count
- Low cost; used in educational and light-duty robots
- Risk of step loss under high loads

**(d) Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor Driven Robots**
- High efficiency, long life
- Common in collaborative robots (cobots) and mobile robots

**Advantages:**
- High precision and repeatability (±0.01 mm achievable)
- Clean operation (no fluid leaks)
- Quiet operation
- Easy computer interface (digital signals)
- Low maintenance

**Disadvantages:**
- Limited force/payload compared to hydraulics
- Heat dissipation issues at high loads
- Sensitive to electromagnetic interference (EMI)

---

### 2.1.2 Hydraulically Powered Robots

Hydraulic robots use **pressurized incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil)** to drive linear or rotary actuators. They are the **workhorses of heavy industry**.

**Key Components:**
- Electric motor → Hydraulic pump
- Reservoir, filters, valves (directional, pressure, flow control)
- Actuators: Hydraulic cylinders (linear) or hydraulic motors (rotary)

**Operating Pressure Range:** **7 MPa to 21 MPa** (high pressure = high force)

**Advantages:**
- Very high payload capacity (up to **1000 kg** or more)
- High power-to-weight ratio
- Self-lubricating
- Excellent for heavy-duty applications
- Smooth motion at high loads

**Disadvantages:**
- Leakage of hydraulic fluid (contamination risk)
- Requires pump, reservoir, filtration system (high infrastructure cost)
- Noisy operation
- Lower precision compared to electric
- Maintenance intensive

**Typical Applications:**
- Spot welding in automotive assembly
- Heavy material handling
- Forging and casting operations
- Large-part machining

---

### 2.1.3 Pneumatically Powered Robots

Pneumatic robots use **compressed air (gas)** as the working fluid. Since air is compressible, they are best suited for **low-precision, high-speed, light-payload** applications.

**Operating Pressure:** **0.5 to 1.0 MPa** (much lower than hydraulic)

**Key Components:**
- Air compressor
- FRL unit (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator)
- Directional control valves
- Pneumatic cylinders or air motors

**Advantages:**
- Simple design and low cost
- Very high speeds (rapid actuation)
- Clean (air is the working medium — no contamination)
- Safe in explosive/flammable environments (no sparks)
- Easy maintenance

**Disadvantages:**
- Compressibility of air → poor positioning accuracy
- Limited force output
- Air supply required (compressor, piping)
- Noisy due to exhaust

**Typical Applications:**
- Pick-and-place operations
- Packaging industry
- Food and pharmaceutical handling
- Simple assembly tasks

---

### 2.1.4 Hybrid Power Source Robots (Advanced Note)

> [!NOTE]
> Some modern industrial robots (e.g., large automotive welding robots) use a **hybrid approach**: hydraulics for the base/shoulder (high payload) and electric for the wrist (high precision). KTU examiners occasionally ask about such configurations in Part B questions.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Comparison Cheat Sheet

### Table 2.1: Comparison of Power Sources (KTU Exam Ready)

| Parameter | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Electrical energy | Pressurized oil | Compressed air |
| Working Fluid | Electrons (in motors) | Mineral oil (incompressible) | Air (compressible) |
| Operating Pressure | N/A (motor-driven) | **7 – 21 MPa** | **0.5 – 1.0 MPa** |
| Power Output | Low – Medium | Very High | Low |
| Payload Capacity | Up to ~800 kg (advanced) | Up to **1000+ kg** | Up to ~10 kg |
| Repeatability | **±0.01 to ±0.05 mm** | ±0.1 to ±0.5 mm | ±0.5 to ±2.0 mm |
| Speed | Medium – High | Medium | Very High |
| Position Control | Excellent (servo) | Good (with servovalves) | Poor (end-point only) |
| Maintenance | Low | High | Low – Medium |
| Noise Level | Low | Medium – High | High |
| Cleanliness | Very Clean | Risk of oil leaks | Clean |
| Cost | Medium – High | High (initial) | Low |
| Typical Application | Assembly, welding, painting | Heavy material handling, forging | Pick-and-place, packaging |

### Table 2.2: Key Equations Related to Power Source Selection

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic Force | $F = P \times A$ | $F$ = force, $P$ = pressure, $A$ = piston area |
| Pneumatic Work | $W = P \times \Delta V$ | $W$ = work done by expanding gas |
| Motor Torque (Electric) | $\tau = K_t \times I$ | $K_t$ = torque constant, $I$ = current |
| Motor Power | $P = \tau \times \omega$ | $P$ = power, $\omega$ = angular velocity |
| Power-to-Weight Ratio | $PWR = \dfrac{P_{out}}{m}$ | $m$ = mass of actuator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The equation $F = P \times A$ explains why hydraulics produce enormous forces — at 14 MPa pressure on a $100 \text{ cm}^2$ piston, the force is $F = 14 \times 10^6 \times 100 \times 10^{-4} = 140,000 \text{ N} = 14 \text{ tonnes!}$ This is why hydraulic robots dominate heavy industry.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Hydraulic robots for body welding (e.g., 6-axis robots in car assembly lines), Electric robots for paint spraying (cleanliness critical), Pneumatic robots for parts transfer
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Electric robots for PCB assembly and pick-and-place of micro-components
- **Food & Pharma:** Pneumatic robots due to cleanliness and explosion-proof nature
- **Space & Defense:** Special hybrid electric-hydraulic systems
- **Medical Robotics:** Electric (BLDC) for precision surgical robots like the Da Vinci system

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force Generated by a Hydraulic Cylinder

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU examinations for this topic.

### Problem Statement
A hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of **80 mm**. The system operates at a hydraulic pressure of **10 MPa**. Calculate the linear force that can be exerted by this actuator.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the governing equation**

The force generated by a hydraulic cylinder is the product of the fluid pressure acting on the effective piston area:

$$F = P \times A$$

**Step 2: Identify the given values**

- Pressure, $P = 10 \text{ MPa} = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$
- Piston diameter, $d = 80 \text{ mm} = 80 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.08 \text{ m}$

**Step 3: Calculate the piston cross-sectional area**

The piston is circular, so:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

Substituting the value of $d$:

$$A = \frac{\pi \times (0.08)^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{0.020106}{4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the force equation**

$$F = P \times A = (10 \times 10^6) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F = 50,265 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Convert to a more practical unit**

$$F = 50,265 \text{ N} \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}$$

Since $1 \text{ tonne-force} \approx 9.81 \text{ kN}$:

$$F \approx \frac{50.27}{9.81} \approx 5.12 \text{ tonnes-force}$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{F \approx 50.27 \text{ kN} \approx 5.12 \text{ tonnes-force}}$$

### Analytical Matrix: Why This Matters

| Aspect | Insight |
|---|---|
| **Why hydraulics?** | Even a small piston (80 mm) generates 5+ tonnes of force — this is the key advantage |
| **Why not electric?** | An electric motor producing equivalent torque would be massive and require huge current |
| **Pneumatic equivalent** | At 0.7 MPa, the same piston would only produce: $F = 0.7 \times 10^6 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} = 3,518 \text{ N}$ (~14× less) |
| **Design Implication** | Force scales with $d^2$ — doubling the diameter gives 4× the force |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Transmission in an Electric Servo Motor

### Problem Statement
An AC servo motor in a robot joint has a torque constant $K_t = 0.5 \text{ Nm/A}$ and runs at a current of $I = 10 \text{ A}$ while rotating at $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$. Calculate the torque and mechanical power output.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Torque Calculation**

For a DC/brushless servo motor, the electromagnetic torque is:

$$\tau = K_t \times I$$

Substituting values:

$$\tau = 0.5 \text{ Nm/A} \times 10 \text{ A} = 5 \text{ Nm}$$

**Step 2: Convert rotational speed to angular velocity**

The relationship between rpm and rad/s is:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

Substituting $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = \frac{3000\pi}{60} = 50\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega = 50 \times 3.14159 = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate mechanical power output**

$$P = \tau \times \omega$$

$$P = 5 \text{ Nm} \times 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P = 785.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Convert to horsepower (for comparison)**

$$P = \frac{785.4}{745.7} \approx 1.054 \text{ HP}$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{\tau = 5 \text{ Nm}, \quad P \approx 785.4 \text{ W} \approx 1.05 \text{ HP}}$$

### Analytical Matrix: Energy Efficiency Comparison

| Power Source | Typical Efficiency | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Electric Servo | **80% – 95%** | Direct energy conversion, minimal losses |
| Hydraulic | **40% – 60%** | Pump losses, valve throttling, fluid friction |
| Pneumatic | **20% – 30%** | Air compression is energy-intensive, exhaust losses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When KTU asks *"Why are electric robots preferred for precision work?"*, the answer must include both **high efficiency** and **high repeatability** — not just one.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Power Source Selection

This matrix is **highly valued in KTU 14-mark questions** on robot classification.

| Selection Criterion | Best Choice | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| High payload (>500 kg) | **Hydraulic** | High force from fluid pressure |
| High precision assembly | **Electric** | Servo feedback gives ±0.01 mm accuracy |
| Food/pharmaceutical handling | **Pneumatic** | Clean, no contamination risk |
| Explosive environment | **Pneumatic** | No sparks; intrinsically safe |
| Spot welding in automotive | **Hydraulic** | High force at each weld point |
| Paint spraying | **Electric (explosion-proof)** | Smooth motion, clean, accurate path |
| Educational/training robot | **Electric (stepper)** | Low cost, simple control |
| Pick-and-place packaging | **Pneumatic** | Fast, simple, low cost |
| Space/aerospace robotics | **Electric (BLDC)** | High power-to-weight, reliable |
| Underwater robots | **Hydraulic (sealed)** | Incompressible fluid, robust |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Classification Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **hierarchical classification of robots by power source**, including the typical sub-components and signal/energy flow paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ROBOT POWER SOURCE CLASSIFICATION] --> B[ELECTRIC POWERED]
    A --> C[HYDRAULIC POWERED]
    A => D[PNEUMATIC POWERED]
    A -.-> E[HYBRID SYSTEMS]

    B --> B1[DC Servo Motor]
    B --> B2[AC Servo Motor]
    B --> B3[Stepper Motor]
    B --> B4[BLDC Motor]

    B1 --> APP1[Precision Assembly]
    B2 --> APP2[Welding, CNC Tending]
    B3 --> APP3[Educational Robots]
    B4 --> APP4[Cobots, Mobile Robots]

    C --> C1[Hydraulic Pump]
    C --> C2[Control Valves]
    C --> C3[Linear Cylinder]
    C --> C4[Rotary Motor]

    C3 --> APP5[Heavy Material Handling]
    C4 --> APP6[Forging and Casting]

    D --> D1[Air Compressor]
    D --> D2[FRL Unit]
    D --> D3[Directional Valve]
    D --> D4[Air Cylinder or Motor]

    D4 --> APP7[Pick and Place]
    D4 --> APP8[Packaging Industry]

    E --> E1[Base Hydraulic plus Wrist Electric]
    E --> E2[Electro Hydraulic Servo]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#059669,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#2563eb,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style APP1 fill:#fef3c7
    style APP2 fill:#fef3c7
    style APP3 fill:#fef3c7
    style APP4 fill:#fef3c7
    style APP5 fill:#fee2e2
    style APP6 fill:#fee2e2
    style APP7 fill:#dbeafe
    style APP8 fill:#dbeafe
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Thick arrows (--> )** indicate the **primary classification path**.
> - **Thick double arrows (=> )** emphasize a **major KTU-relevant category**.
> - **Dotted arrows (-.-> )** indicate an **advanced/optional category** often asked as a 3-mark question.
> - **Color coding:** Green = Electric, Red = Hydraulic, Blue = Pneumatic, Purple = Hybrid.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Hydraulic Robot Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[Electric Motor Power Supply] --> PMP[Hydraulic Pump]
    PMP --> RSVR[Reservoir with Filtered Oil]
    RSVR --> PRV[Pressure Relief Valve]
    PRV --> CV1[Directional Control Valve]
    CV2[Flow Control Valve] --> CV1
    CV1 --> CYL[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    CYL --> JNT[Robot Joint Motion]
    CYL --> RTN[Return Line to Reservoir]

    JNT --> FB[Position and Force Feedback Sensors]
    FB --> CTL[Controller]
    CTL --> CV1

    style PWR fill:#fde68a
    style PMP fill:#fca5a5
    style CYL fill:#fca5a5
    style CTL fill:#86efac
    style FB fill:#93c5fd
```

### Explanation of the Diagram

- **Electric Motor Power Supply:** Provides input energy to the pump (the primary energy converter).
- **Hydraulic Pump:** Converts mechanical (electrical) energy into hydraulic (pressure) energy.
- **Reservoir:** Stores the hydraulic oil and allows heat dissipation.
- **Pressure Relief Valve:** Protects the system from overpressure (safety critical).
- **Directional Control Valve:** Directs oil flow to extend or retract the cylinder.
- **Flow Control Valve:** Regulates the speed of the actuator.
- **Hydraulic Cylinder:** Converts hydraulic pressure into linear force ($F = P \times A$).
- **Feedback Sensors:** Provide closed-loop control of position and force.
- **Controller:** Compares actual vs. desired output and adjusts valve positions.

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Electric Servo Robot Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[Controller CPU] --> DRV[Motor Driver or Amplifier]
    DRV --> MTR[AC or DC Servo Motor]
    MTR --> GB[Gearbox]
    GB --> JNT[Robot Joint]
    JNT --> ENC[Encoder]
    ENC --> FBIF[Feedback Interface]
    FBIF --> CPU
    TACH[Tachogenerator] --> FBIF

    style CPU fill:#86efac
    style DRV fill:#fde68a
    style MTR fill:#fca5a5
    style ENC fill:#93c5fd
```

### Explanation of the Diagram

- The **CPU** generates a reference position.
- The **Motor Driver** receives the command signal and supplies the appropriate current/voltage to the motor.
- The **Servo Motor** produces torque, which is geared down to drive the **Robot Joint**.
- The **Encoder** and **Tachogenerator** provide real-time position and velocity feedback.
- The **Controller** uses PID algorithms to minimize the error.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Classify robots based on power source. List any two advantages of electrically powered robots.**

**Model Answer:**

Robots are classified into three main categories based on power source:

1. **Electrically Powered Robots** — Use electric motors (DC servo, AC servo, stepper, BLDC) as actuators. Energy: Electrical.
2. **Hydraulically Powered Robots** — Use pressurized oil (typically 7–21 MPa) acting on pistons or hydraulic motors. Energy: Hydraulic.
3. **Pneumatically Powered Robots** — Use compressed air (0.5–1.0 MPa) to drive pneumatic cylinders or motors. Energy: Pneumatic.

**Two advantages of electrically powered robots:**
1. High positioning accuracy and repeatability (±0.01 to ±0.05 mm) due to precise servo control with feedback.
2. Clean and quiet operation with low maintenance, since there is no fluid leakage and no brushes in brushless designs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marking Note:** Students often write only "high accuracy" — but examiners expect **quantitative values** (e.g., "±0.05 mm") to award full marks.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Compare pneumatic and hydraulic actuation systems in robots. Mention two applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Pneumatic | Hydraulic |
|---|---|---|
| Working Fluid | Compressed air | Hydraulic oil |
| Compressibility | High (compressible) | Negligible (incompressible) |
| Pressure Range | 0.5 – 1.0 MPa | 7 – 21 MPa |
| Force Output | Low | Very High |
| Accuracy | Poor (end-position control) | Better (with servovalves) |
| Cost | Low | High |

**Two applications of Pneumatic Robots:**
1. Pick-and-place operations in packaging industry.
2. Light assembly tasks in food and pharmaceutical sectors.

**Two applications of Hydraulic Robots:**
1. Spot welding in automotive assembly lines.
2. Heavy material handling and forging operations.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **classification of robots based on power source**. Discuss the working principle of a **hydraulically powered robot** with a neat block diagram.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A hydraulic cylinder in a robot arm has a piston diameter of **100 mm**. The hydraulic supply pressure is **14 MPa**. Calculate:
1. The maximum linear force the cylinder can exert.
2. The work done if the piston stroke is **200 mm**.
3. The power required if the piston extends fully in **2 seconds**.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification of Robots Based on Power Source:**

Robots are classified into **three main categories** based on the type of energy used to drive their actuators:

**1. Electrically Powered Robots**
- Use electric motors as prime movers.
- Sub-types: DC servo, AC servo, stepper, BLDC.
- Energy source: AC mains or DC battery.
- Working principle: Electrical energy → Magnetic field → Rotational/linear motion.
- Controlled via PWM signals and feedback encoders.

**2. Hydraulically Powered Robots**
- Use pressurized hydraulic oil to drive linear actuators (cylinders) or rotary actuators (motors).
- Energy source: Electric motor coupled to a hydraulic pump.
- Working principle: Pump pressurizes oil (7–21 MPa) → Oil acts on piston → Force $F = P \times A$ → Linear motion.
- Used in heavy-payload applications.

**3. Pneumatically Powered Robots**
- Use compressed air (0.5–1.0 MPa) to drive pneumatic cylinders or motors.
- Energy source: Air compressor.
- Working principle: Compressed air admitted to cylinder → Pushes piston → Linear motion.
- Used in light, fast, clean applications.

**Block Diagram of a Hydraulic Robot:**

```
Electric Motor → Hydraulic Pump → Reservoir → Filter → 
Directional Control Valve → Hydraulic Cylinder → Robot Joint
                                    ↑
                          Position/Force Feedback → Controller
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** The block diagram is **mandatory** for full marks. Drawing arrows and labeling the energy conversion stages at each block fetches **2 extra marks** in the valuation.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Piston diameter, $d = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- Pressure, $P = 14 \text{ MPa} = 14 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$
- Stroke length, $L = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- Time, $t = 2 \text{ s}$

**(1) Maximum Linear Force**

Piston area:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.1)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.01}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Force:

$$F = P \times A = (14 \times 10^6) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{F = 109,956 \text{ N} \approx 110 \text{ kN} \approx 11.2 \text{ tonnes-force}}$$

**(2) Work Done by the Piston**

Work done is force times displacement (assuming constant force):

$$W = F \times L = 109,956 \times 0.2$$

$$\boxed{W = 21,991.2 \text{ J} \approx 22 \text{ kJ}}$$

**(3) Power Required**

Power is work done per unit time:

$$P_{out} = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{21,991.2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} = 10,995.6 \text{ W} \approx 11 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes (Examiner's Pitfall):**
> 1. **Unit error:** Forgetting to convert mm to m (writing $d = 100$ in the area formula) — this gives a force 1000× too large.
> 2. **Using radius instead of diameter:** Writing $A = \pi r^2$ instead of $\pi d^2 / 4$ — students lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to show unit conversions explicitly** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per skipped conversion.

---

### **Question B: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the working principle of **electrically powered robots**. Compare **AC servo, DC servo, and stepper motors** as actuators in robotics.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 6-axis industrial robot uses an **AC servo motor** with torque constant $K_t = 0.8 \text{ Nm/A}$ to drive a joint. The motor draws a current of $I = 15 \text{ A}$ and rotates at $N = 2000 \text{ rpm}$. Determine:
1. The electromagnetic torque developed.
2. The angular velocity in rad/s.
3. The mechanical power output in Watts and HP.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Working Principle of Electrically Powered Robots:**

Electrically powered robots convert **electrical energy into mechanical motion** through electric motors. The control system sends low-power command signals to a **motor driver/amplifier**, which supplies the required voltage and current to the motor. The motor shaft rotates, driving the robot joint through a **gearbox** (for torque multiplication and speed reduction).

**Closed-loop feedback** is provided by **encoders** (position) and **tachogenerators** (velocity), enabling precise control.

**Comparison of Motor Types:**

| Parameter | DC Servo Motor | AC Servo Motor | Stepper Motor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control Type | Closed-loop (analog/digital) | Closed-loop (advanced) | Open-loop (basic) |
| Precision | High | Very High | Moderate (risk of step loss) |
| Maintenance | Brushes need replacement | Maintenance-free | Low |
| Cost | Medium | Higher | Lowest |
| Speed Range | Wide | Very wide | Limited |
| Application | Older robots, CNC | Modern industrial robots | Educational, light pick-place |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** AC servo motors are the **industry standard today** because they combine high precision, high speed, and zero maintenance (no brushes).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Torque constant, $K_t = 0.8 \text{ Nm/A}$
