- Current, $I = 15 \text{ A}$
- Speed, $N = 2000 \text{ rpm}$

**(1) Electromagnetic Torque**

$$\tau = K_t \times I = 0.8 \times 15$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 12 \text{ Nm}}$$

**(2) Angular Velocity**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 2000}{60} = \frac{4000\pi}{60} = \frac{200\pi}{3}$$

$$\omega = \frac{200 \times 3.14159}{3} = \frac{628.318}{3} = 209.44 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{\omega \approx 209.44 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**(3) Mechanical Power Output**

$$P = \tau \times \omega = 12 \times 209.44$$

$$P = 2,513.28 \text{ W}$$

In horsepower:

$$P_{HP} = \frac{2,513.28}{745.7} = 3.37 \text{ HP}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 2,513.3 \text{ W} \approx 3.37 \text{ HP}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\pi$ in angular velocity conversion** — a very common mistake; costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $K_t$ (torque constant) with $K_e$ (back-EMF constant)** — these are numerically equal in SI units but conceptually different.
> 3. **Not converting final answer to HP** — KTU often asks for both SI and imperial units; missing one loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing the unit of angular velocity as "rpm"** — KTU strictly expects "rad/s"; rpm is wrong unit system for the formula $P = \tau \omega$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PCRAT303 Module 1**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- [ ] Robots are classified into **3 main power source types**: Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic
- [ ] **Electric** = Highest precision (±0.01 mm), clean, quiet, computer-friendly
- [ ] **Hydraulic** = Highest force/payload, uses oil at 7–21 MPa, heavy industry
- [ ] **Pneumatic** = Highest speed, lowest cost, uses air at 0.5–1.0 MPa, light-duty
- [ ] **Hybrid systems** exist in modern large robots (electric wrist + hydraulic base)

### Formulas to Memorize
- [ ] Hydraulic force: $F = P \times A$
- [ ] Piston area: $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$
- [ ] Motor torque: $\tau = K_t \times I$
- [ ] Motor power: $P = \tau \times \omega$
- [ ] RPM to rad/s: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$

### Numerical Values to Remember
- [ ] Hydraulic pressure: **7 – 21 MPa**
- [ ] Pneumatic pressure: **0.5 – 1.0 MPa**
- [ ] 1 tonne-force = **9.81 kN**
- [ ] 1 HP = **745.7 W**

### Common KTU Exam Traps
- [ ] Always convert mm → m before substituting in $F = P \times A$
- [ ] Always convert rpm → rad/s before using $P = \tau \omega$
- [ ] In part (a) of 14-mark questions, **always draw a block diagram** for full marks
- [ ] Comparison tables fetch **extra marks** — use them liberally
- [ ] Mention **quantitative values** (e.g., "±0.05 mm") — not just qualitative words like "high accuracy"

### Application-Based Memory Hooks
- [ ] **Automotive welding** → Hydraulic
- [ ] **PCB assembly** → Electric (precision)
- [ ] **Food packaging** → Pneumatic (clean, safe)
- [ ] **Paint spraying** → Electric explosion-proof
- [ ] **Forging/casting** → Hydraulic (high force)
- [ ] **Educational robot** → Stepper motor (low cost, simple)

### Final Exam Strategy
- [ ] For **3-mark questions** — give definition + 2 advantages + 1 application
- [ ] For **7-mark sub-parts** — show all formulas, all unit conversions, and a final boxed answer
- [ ] For **14-mark questions** — use **headings, sub-points, tables, and block diagrams** — presentation matters for 10–15% of the marks

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application Area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application Areas of Robotic Manipulators** refer to the specific industrial, commercial, medical, and exploratory domains where robotic manipulators (articulated arms, Cartesian robots, SCARA, delta robots, etc.) are deployed to perform tasks traditionally executed by human operators or dedicated hard-automation machinery.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRAT303)**, an *Application Area* is classified based on the following four KTU-defined parameters:

1. **Task Nature** — Material handling, processing, assembly, inspection, or service.
2. **Operating Environment** — Structured (factory floor) vs Unstructured (field, underwater, space).
3. **Degree of Human Collaboration** — Coexistent, Cooperative, or Collaborative (Cobots).
4. **Payload & Reach Specification** — Micro (≤1 kg), Small (1–10 kg), Medium (10–100 kg), Heavy (>100 kg).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of robotic manipulators like **specialized power tools in a craftsman's workshop**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Just as a carpenter chooses between a hammer, a drill, or a saw based on the job (building a chair vs. fixing a pipe), an engineer selects a specific robot configuration (SCARA, articulated, delta) based on the **application area** — painting a car door (continuous path) vs. picking small electronic components (high-speed pick-and-place).

A robot is **not** a general-purpose replacement for humans. It is a *domain-specific tool* optimized for a defined **Application Area**.

## 1.3 Physical & Operational Constants (KTU Standard Metrics)

| Metric | Standard Value (KTU Reference) |
|---|---|
| **Repeatability (Industrial)** | $\pm 0.02$ mm to $\pm 0.1$ mm |
| **Payload Range** | 0.5 kg – 1000 kg |
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | 3 – 7 (typical) |
| **Working Envelope Reach** | 0.3 m – 5 m |
| **Cycle Time (High-Speed Pick)** | $\leq 0.3$ seconds |

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Note:** For Module 1 questions, examiners expect students to clearly **map** a robot type to a *specific* application area — not a generic listing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Classification Matrix

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Application Areas

Robotic manipulator applications are classified hierarchically into **three primary domains**:

### A. Industrial Manufacturing Applications (≈ 70% of global deployments)

1. **Material Handling & Logistics**
   - Palletizing / Depalletizing
   - Pick-and-place operations
   - Machine tending (CNC loading/unloading)
   - Warehouse AGV-coupled robotic arms

2. **Welding Processes**
   - Spot welding (automotive body assembly)
   - Arc welding (MIG/TIG on heavy fabrications)
   - Laser welding (precision electronics)

3. **Painting & Coating**
   - Spray painting (continuous-path control)
   - Powder coating & electrostatic painting
   - Sealing & adhesive dispensing

4. **Assembly Operations**
   - Precision mechanical assembly (gears, bearings)
   - PCB component insertion (SMT)
   - Screwdriving & fastening

5. **Inspection & Quality Control**
   - Vision-integrated defect detection
   - Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM) probing
   - Non-destructive testing (NDT) with ultrasonic/endoscopic tools

### B. Non-Industrial / Service Applications

| Sub-domain | Typical Robot Type | Task |
|---|---|---|
| **Medical & Surgical** | 6-DOF precision arm | Minimally invasive surgery (Da Vinci system) |
| **Agriculture** | Mobile manipulator | Fruit picking, selective spraying |
| **Defense & Security** | Heavy-duty manipulator | Bomb disposal (EOD robots) |
| **Space Exploration** | Long-reach arm | Sample collection (Canadarm on ISS) |
| **Underwater (ROV/AUV)** | Pressure-rated arm | Pipeline repair, deep-sea mining |
| **Domestic / Hospitality** | Mobile manipulator | Cleaning, food serving |

### C. Emerging / Collaborative Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collaborative Robots (Cobots)** — A KTU 2024 priority area. Cobots operate in *shared workspaces* with humans using force-limited joints and ISO/TS 15066 safety standards. Applications include small-batch assembly in SME (Small-Medium Enterprise) factories, assistive caregiving, and lab automation.

## 2.2 Selection Criteria for an Application Area (Decision Logic)

When a robot is selected for an application, the following KTU-standard decision flow is applied:

```
STEP 1 → Identify Task Type
         (Handling | Processing | Assembly | Inspection)
            ↓
STEP 2 → Determine Payload & Reach Requirements
            ↓
STEP 3 → Evaluate Environment
         (Clean | Dirty | Hazardous | Sterile)
            ↓
STEP 4 → Calculate Required DOF
         (3-DOF = simple transfer; 6-DOF = full spatial dexterity)
            ↓
STEP 5 → Match Robot Configuration
         (Cartesian | Cylindrical | Spherical | SCARA | Articulated | Delta | Parallel)
            ↓
STEP 6 → Define End-Effector
         (Gripper | Welder | Sprayer | Vacuum tool | Custom)
            ↓
STEP 7 → Validate Cycle Time vs. Production Rate
```

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept / Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Robots Required** | $N_R = \dfrac{P \times (1 + S)}{C_t \times T_s \times U}$ | $P$ = production pieces/day, $S$ = scrap %, $C_t$ = cycle time, $T_s$ = shift hours, $U$ = utilization |
| **Robot Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Available Time}} \times 100\%$ | Ideal: 85–95% |
| **Cycle Time Constraint** | $C_t \leq \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand Rate}}$ | Seconds |
| **Workspace Volume (Spherical)** | $V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$ | m³ |
| **Workspace Volume (Cylindrical)** | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | m³ |
| **DOF Selection Rule** | DOF $\geq$ Task Constraints $(6 - \text{Constraints Imposed by Fixture})$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-pitfall:** Never use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables — always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid table syntax breakage during PDF rendering in KTU answer booklets.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Application area classification directly drives:

- **Capital Investment Decisions** (ROI calculation)
- **Cell Layout Design** (Module 5 topic)
- **Safety Standards Compliance** (ISO 10218, ISO/TS 15066)
- **End-effector Customization**
- **Sensors & Vision Integration**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Number of Robots Required for a Given Application Area

This is a **standard KTU 14-mark derivation** commonly tested when an application area (e.g., welding in an automotive plant) is specified.

### Given Problem (KTU-Style):
> A manufacturing cell produces **900 units per day** in **two 8-hour shifts**. The robot cycle time for each unit is **45 seconds**, scrap rate is **5%**, and robot utilization is targeted at **90%**. Determine the number of robots required.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify the governing equation (from KTU Formula Sheet):

$$
N_R = \frac{P \times (1 + S)}{C_t \times T_s \times U}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given parameters, ensuring **unit consistency** (cycle time in seconds → convert total shift time to seconds).

Total available production time per day:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 8 \times 3600 \; \text{seconds} = 57600 \; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the equation:

$$
N_R = \frac{900 \times (1 + 0.05)}{45 \times 57600 \times 0.90}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator:

$$
900 \times 1.05 = 945 \; \text{units (including scrap allowance)}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the denominator:

$$
45 \times 57600 \times 0.90 = 2332800 \; \text{units} \; (\text{robot-seconds equivalent})
$$

Wait — units inconsistency check. We must use the **time-availability form**:

$$
N_R = \frac{\text{Total Time Required}}{\text{Available Robot-Time per Day}}
$$

Let us re-derive cleanly:

$$
N_R = \frac{P \times (1 + S) \times C_t}{T_{\text{available}} \times U}
$$

Where $T_{\text{available}}$ is in **seconds**.

**Step 6:** Numerator (time required):

$$
\text{Time Required} = 900 \times 1.05 \times 45 = 42525 \; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 7:** Denominator (time available per robot):

$$
\text{Time per Robot} = 57600 \times 0.90 = 51840 \; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 8:** Final calculation:

$$
N_R = \frac{42525}{51840} = 0.820
$$

**Step 9:** Apply the **ceiling rule** (you cannot have a fractional robot):

$$
N_R = \lceil 0.820 \rceil = 1 \; \text{robot}
$$

### Verification:
With 1 robot running at 90% utilization, available time = 51840 s. Required time = 42525 s. Since 42525 < 51840, **one robot suffices** for this application.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pattern:** Examiners expect the student to (1) state the formula, (2) show unit conversion explicitly, (3) apply the ceiling rule, and (4) state a logical conclusion. Missing any step costs 2–3 marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Application Areas → Robot Type → DOF

| Application Area | Recommended Robot Configuration | DOF | End-Effector | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Arc Welding (heavy chassis) | **6-DOF Articulated** | 6 | MIG/TIG torch | Needs spatial dexterity to follow complex weld seams |
| PCB Pick-and-Place | **Delta / SCARA** | 3–4 | Vacuum gripper | High speed, vertical approach only |
| Palletizing (heavy boxes) | **4-DOF Articulated / Cartesian** | 4 | Claw gripper | High payload, vertical stacking |
| Machine Tending (CNC) | **6-DOF Articulated** | 6 | Parallel gripper | Door opening, part insertion require full dexterity |
| Painting (car body) | **6-DOF Articulated (hollow wrist)** | 6 | Spray gun | Continuous-path control, hollow wrist for hose routing |
| Surgical Assistance | **7-DOF Redundant Arm** | 7 | Specialized tool | Redundancy avoids singularities inside patient |
| Underwater Repair | **Pressure-rated 6-DOF Hydraulic Arm** | 6 | Gripper + Camera | Corrosion-resistant, high torque output |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing **DOF** with **axes**. A 6-DOF robot has *6 independently driven joints*, NOT necessarily 6 axes of the Cartesian frame. The DOF = the rank of the Jacobian matrix.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Map of Robotic Manipulator Application Areas

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robotic Manipulator Application Areas] --> B[Industrial Manufacturing]
    A --> C[Service and Non-Industrial]
    A --> D[Emerging and Collaborative]

    B --> B1[Material Handling]
    B --> B2[Welding]
    B --> B3[Painting and Coating]
    B --> B4[Assembly]
    B --> B5[Inspection and QC]

    B1 --> B1a[Palletizing]
    B1 --> B1b[Pick and Place]
    B1 --> B1c[Machine Tending]

    B2 --> B2a[Spot Welding]
    B2 --> B2b[Arc Welding]
    B2 --> B2c[Laser Welding]

    B3 --> B3a[Spray Painting]
    B3 --> B3b[Powder Coating]
    B3 --> B3c[Adhesive Dispensing]

    B4 --> B4a[Mechanical Assembly]
    B4 --> B4b[PCB Insertion]
    B4 --> B4c[Fastening]

    B5 --> B5a[Vision Inspection]
    B5 --> B5b[CMM Probing]
    B5 --> B5c[NDT Testing]

    C --> C1[Medical Surgery]
    C --> C2[Agriculture]
    C --> C3[Defense EOD]
    C --> C4[Space Exploration]
    C --> C5[Underwater ROV]
    C --> C6[Domestic Service]

    D --> D1[Cobots in SME Assembly]
    D --> D2[Assistive Robotics]
    D --> D3[Lab Automation]
    D --> D4[Human Robot HRI]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Selection Flow for an Application Area

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Step 1: Define Application Task] --> ANALYZE[Step 2: Analyze Payload Reach DOF]
    ANALYZE --> ENV[Step 3: Evaluate Environment Hazards]
    ENV --> CONFIG[Step 4: Select Robot Configuration]
    CONFIG --> EE[Step 5: Design End Effector]
    EE --> CONTROL[Step 6: Program and Integrate Sensors]
    CONTROL --> SAFETY[Step 7: Apply ISO 10218 Safety Standards]
    SAFETY --> DEPLOY[Step 8: Deploy in Work Cell]

    DEPLOY --> METRICS[Performance Metrics]
    METRICS --> ROI[ROI Cycle Time Uptime]
    METRICS --> QC[Quality Defect Rate]
    METRICS --> SAFE[Safety Incidents]
```

## 4.3 Robot Configuration vs Application Area Mapping (Visual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[Cartesian Gantry] --> A1[Pick Place CNC Tending]
    R2[Cylindrical] --> A2[Rotational Assembly Die Casting]
    R3[Spherical Polar] --> A3[Forging Stamping Old Plants]
    R4[SCARA] --> A4[PCB Assembly High Speed]
    R5[Articulated 6DOF] --> A5[Welding Painting General]
    R6[Delta Parallel] --> A6[Food Pharma Packaging]
    R7[Cobot 7DOF] --> A7[SME Assembly Human Collaboration]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `INPUT`, `B1a`) — no reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as node names. All labels use clean uppercase alphanumeric text with no markdown bold/italics inside quoted strings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List any six industrial application areas of robotic manipulators.**

**Model Answer:**
The six industrial application areas are:
1. Material handling and palletizing
2. Welding (spot, arc, laser)
3. Spray painting and coating
4. Mechanical assembly (gears, bearings, fasteners)
5. Inspection and quality control
6. Machine tending (CNC loading/unloading)

Each application demands specific robot configuration, payload, and reach specifications as defined in the KTU PCRAT303 Module 1 syllabus.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Distinguish between industrial robots and collaborative robots (Cobots) with two application examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Industrial Robot | Collaborative Robot (Cobot) |
|---|---|---|
| **Workspace** | Isolated by fencing/cages | Shared with humans |
| **Safety Standard** | ISO 10218 | ISO/TS 15066 |
| **Speed** | High | Force-limited, low speed |
| **Sensors** | Position encoders | Force/torque + proximity sensors |
| **Example Apps** | Spot welding, palletizing | Small-batch assembly, lab automation |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the application areas of robotic manipulators into industrial, non-industrial, and emerging categories. For each category, provide **at least three examples** with the corresponding robot type and degree of freedom (DOF).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing cell needs to produce **1200 units per day** in **two shifts of 8 hours each**. The robot cycle time is **30 seconds**, scrap rate is **4%**, and utilization is **85%**. Determine the number of robots required.

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks:

**Industrial Applications (3 examples):**
- **Spot Welding** → 6-DOF Articulated robot → automotive body assembly.
- **PCB Pick-and-Place** → Delta robot (3–4 DOF) → high-speed electronics assembly.
- **Spray Painting** → 6-DOF Hollow-wrist Articulated → automotive exterior coating.

**Non-Industrial Applications (3 examples):**
- **Surgical Assistance** → 7-DOF redundant arm → minimally invasive surgery (Da Vinci).
- **Bomb Disposal** → Heavy-duty hydraulic manipulator → defense EOD operations.
- **Space Manipulation** → Long-re Canadarm2 → ISS external maintenance.

**Emerging / Collaborative Applications (3 examples):**
- **Cobot Assembly** → 6-DOF force-limited arm → SME batch production.
- **Lab Automation** → SCARA / Cartesian → pipetting, sample handling.
- **Assistive Care** → Mobile manipulator → elderly care, patient lifting.

> [!TIP]
> Each example MUST include the robot type AND DOF for full 7-mark credit. Generic statements without DOF specification lose 2–3 marks.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1:** State the governing formula:

$$
N_R = \frac{P \times (1 + S) \times C_t}{T_{\text{available}} \times U}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
P = 1200, \quad S = 0.04, \quad C_t = 30 \; \text{s}, \quad U = 0.85
$$

$$
T_{\text{available}} = 2 \times 8 \times 3600 = 57600 \; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator (time required):

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1200 \times 1.04 \times 30 = 37440 \; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator (time available per robot):

$$
\text{Denominator} = 57600 \times 0.85 = 48960 \; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 5:** Final ratio:

$$
N_R = \frac{37440}{48960} = 0.7647
$$

**Step 6:** Apply the ceiling rule:

$$
\boxed{N_R = 1 \; \text{robot}}
$$

**Conclusion:** A single robot, operating at 85% utilization, is sufficient to meet the 1200 units/day production target for this application area.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **seven-step decision logic** used by engineers to select a robot for a specific application area. Use a real-world example (e.g., arc welding in a tractor manufacturing plant) to illustrate each step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a suitable diagram, describe the **functional architecture** for deploying a robotic manipulator in a hazardous environment (e.g., nuclear decommissioning). State the safety standards applicable.

---

### Model Solution for Question B (Key Points):

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks:

Apply the seven-step flow from Section 2.2:
1. **Task Identification** → Continuous arc welding along curved seams.
2. **Payload** → 8 kg (torch weight + cabling).
3. **Environment** → Hot sparks, fume extraction needed.
4. **DOF** → 6 DOF (for seam tracking on 3D curved surfaces).
5. **Configuration** → 6-DOF Articulated with hollow wrist.
6. **End-Effector** → MIG torch + seam-tracking laser sensor.
7. **Cycle Validation** → Weld time per joint ≤ takt time.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks:

A block diagram showing: **Robot → Radiation Shielded Enclosure → Tele-operation Console → Force/Torque Sensors → Vision (Radiation-hardened Cameras) → Safety Interlocks (ISO 10218, IEC 61508)**.

Applicable standards: **ISO 10218-1/2**, **IEC 61508 (SIL ratings)**, and **ANSI/RIA R15.06**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Application Area Questions:**
>
> 1. **Listing without justification** — Writing "articulated robots are used in welding" without stating WHY (DOF, dexterity, payload) costs 3 marks.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — In the "Number of Robots" derivation, failing to convert shift hours into seconds = 2 marks lost.
> 3. **Forgetting the ceiling rule** — Reporting $N_R = 0.82$ instead of $1$ robot = 1 mark lost.
> 4. **Confusing DOF with axes** — Writing "6-axis robot" instead of "6-DOF robot" — terminology matters.
> 5. **Ignoring safety standards** — Especially in collaborative/hazardous applications, omitting ISO 10218 or ISO/TS 15066 = 2 marks lost.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Application Area (Module 1)**

- **Application Area** = domain-specific deployment of robotic manipulators (industrial, service, emerging).
- **Four Classification Parameters:** Task Nature, Environment, Human Collaboration, Payload/Reach.
- **Seven Major Industrial Applications:** Material handling, welding, painting, assembly, inspection, machine tending, palletizing.
- **Non-Industrial Domains:** Medical, agriculture, defense (EOD), space, underwater, domestic.
- **Emerging Domain:** **Cobots** — collaborative robots governed by **ISO/TS 15066**.
- **Decision Logic** has 7 sequential steps from task identification to cycle validation.
- **Key Formula:** $N_R = \dfrac{P \times (1+S) \times C_t}{T_{\text{available}} \times U}$ with **ceiling rule**.
- **Configuration vs. Application Mapping:**
   - **SCARA** → PCB assembly.
   - **Delta** → High-speed packaging.
   - **6-DOF Articulated** → Welding, painting, general purpose.
   - **Cartesian** → Pick-place, CNC tending.
   - **Cobot (7-DOF)** → Human-collaborative assembly.
- **DOF Rule of Thumb:** Task constraints = 6, so DOF ≥ (6 − constraints imposed by fixture/jig).
- **Safety Standards to Memorize:** ISO 10218 (industrial), ISO/TS 15066 (collaborative), ANSI/RIA R15.06.
- **High-Yield Metric:** Repeatability range $\pm 0.02$ mm to $\pm 0.1$ mm for industrial robots.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Method of Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔧 Method of Control — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Method of Control" in Robotics?

The **Method of Control** refers to the systematic strategy and architecture used to command a robotic manipulator so that its end-effector (or joints) follow a desired trajectory, exert a desired force, or achieve a specified task in the presence of disturbances, model uncertainties, and environmental interactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCRAT303 – Module 1):**
> *The method of control deals with the underlying principle by which joint actuators are driven — whether by simple sequenced actuation (open-loop) or by feedback-regulated error correction (closed-loop). It forms the algorithmic heart of every robotic work cell.*

In engineering terms, control = the **law (algorithm)** that converts a *reference* (desired position/velocity/force) into an *actuator command* (torque/voltage/current) such that the *actual* robot state converges to the *desired* one.

---

## 1.2 The Two Grand Families of Control

| Family | Sub-types | Feedback? | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open-Loop (Non-Servo)** | Sequence control, timing control | ❌ No | Pick-and-place, low-cost toys, hard automation |
| **Closed-Loop (Servo)** | PTP, CP, PID, Computed Torque, Adaptive, Force/Impedance | ✅ Yes | Welding, painting, assembly, surgery |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Drivers"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Open-Loop = "The Blindfolded Archer":**
> Imagine an archer who *cannot* see the target. He draws the bow with a pre-decided force and angle (programmed in advance) and releases. Whether the arrow hits or misses, he will *not* correct mid-flight. This is **open-loop control** — accurate only if the model of the world is perfect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Closed-Loop = "The Self-Driving Car with a Camera":**
> A self-driving car constantly *measures* the gap to the car ahead, *compares* it to the desired gap, and *adjusts* throttle/brake. This is **closed-loop (feedback) control** — robust to wind, slope, and traffic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Servo vs Non-Servo = "Microwave vs Oven Thermostat":**
> A microwave runs for a *fixed time* (non-servo / open-loop). A conventional oven uses a *thermometer feedback* to maintain set temperature (servo / closed-loop). Robots behave the same way.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics (Bolded Standards)

- **Sampling period $T_s$** — typically **1 ms to 10 ms** in industrial robots.
- **Position accuracy** — typically **± 0.02 mm** for high-precision robots.
- **Bandwidth** — typically **10 Hz to 100 Hz** for joint servo loops.
- **Control update rate** — typically **100 Hz to 1 kHz**.

---

## 1.5 Why is "Method of Control" the *First* Topic in Module 1?

Because before we discuss kinematics, dynamics, trajectory planning, or work-cell layout, we must understand **HOW** the joints will actually be commanded. The choice of method (open vs closed, servo vs non-servo, point-to-point vs continuous path) cascades into:
1. **Hardware selection** (encoders, resolvers, tachogenerators).
2. **Drive selection** (hydraulic, pneumatic, servo-motor, stepper).
3. **Programming paradigm** (teach-pendant vs textual).
4. **Application suitability** (assembly vs painting).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently set the very first question of the ESE on the *comparison* between servo and non-servo, or PTP vs CP. Master the tables below.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Robotic Control Methods

A clear taxonomy is essential. The pyramid below is a synthesis of all the methods KTU expects you to know in Module 1.

```
                    ┌────────────────────────┐
                    │   INTELLIGENT CONTROL  │  (Fuzzy, Neural, AI)
                    ├────────────────────────┤
                    │   ADAPTIVE / ROBUST    │  (Self-tuning, H∞, SMC)
                    ├────────────────────────┤
                    │  NON-LINEAR / DYNAMIC  │  (Computed Torque, Feedback Lin.)
                    ├────────────────────────┤
                    │  CLASSICAL LINEAR      │  (PID, State Feedback, LQR)
                    ├────────────────────────┤
                    │ SERVO / CLOSED-LOOP    │  (PTP, CP)
                    ├────────────────────────┤
                    │ NON-SERVO / OPEN-LOOP  │  (Sequence, Pneumatic Logic)
                    └────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 2.2 Open-Loop (Non-Servo) Control — Step-by-Step Logic

**Operating Principle:**
1. A pre-defined sequence of ON/OFF actuator commands is stored (in a cam-timer, PLC ladder, or microcontroller).
2. Each actuator (e.g., a pneumatic cylinder) is fired for a *fixed duration* or until a *mechanical limit-switch* is reached.
3. **No positional feedback** is read during motion.
4. The system trusts the mechanism's repeatability.

**Where it shines:** High-speed pick-and-place between two fixed points; simple stop-go automation.

**Where it fails:** Variable loads, friction changes, temperature drift — all of which cause position error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Stepper motors* are an *electrical* form of open-loop control — every pulse rotates the shaft by a known angle **without** needing an encoder. KTU examiners love this subtle point.

---

## 2.3 Closed-Loop (Servo) Control — Step-by-Step Logic

**Operating Principle:**
1. **Sense** — Encoders/resolvers measure the *actual* joint position $q(t)$ and velocity $\dot{q}(t)$.
2. **Compare** — The controller subtracts actual from desired to compute the *error* $e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$.
3. **Compute** — A control law (e.g., PID, computed torque) maps the error to an actuator command $\tau(t)$.
4. **Actuate** — The motor driver converts $\tau(t)$ into voltage/current.
5. **Repeat** — At the next sampling instant $t+T_s$, the cycle restarts.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Sense → Compare → Compute → Actuate** loop. Memorize it — it appears verbatim in many 7-mark answers.

---

## 2.4 Point-to-Point (PTP) vs Continuous Path (CP) Control

| Feature | PTP Control | CP Control |
|---|---|---|
| Path between points | *Uncontrolled* (fastest, often joint-interpolated) | *Controlled* (smooth, e.g., linear or circular) |
| End-effector trajectory | Not specified | Specified mathematically |
| Typical use | Spot welding, pick-and-place, palletizing | Arc welding, painting, glue dispensing, cutting |
| Complexity | Low (open-loop possible) | High (requires continuous feedback) |
| Control law | Move joints to A, then to B | Interpolate via cubic splines & track $q_d(t)$ continuously |

**The KTU-Worthy Distinction:**
- PTP = *joint-space* concern (only end-states matter).
- CP = *Cartesian-space* concern (the path itself is the product).

---

## 2.5 PID Control — The Workhorse of Industrial Robots

The **Proportional-Integral-Derivative** controller is by far the most deployed control law in commercial manipulators.

**Time-domain equation:**

$$u(t) \;=\; K_p\, e(t) \;+\; K_i \!\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_d \,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- $K_p$ — proportional gain (reacts to *current* error; raises speed of response but causes overshoot).
- $K_i$ — integral gain (eliminates *steady-state* error; can cause oscillation).
- $K_d$ — derivative gain (dampens *rate of change*; improves stability).

**Laplace-domain transfer function of a PID controller:**

$$C(s) \;=\; K_p \;+\; \frac{K_i}{s} \;+\; K_d\,s \;=\; \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Closed-loop transfer function (unity feedback):**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{C(s)\,G(s)}{1 + C(s)\,G(s)}$$

where $G(s)$ is the plant (arm + actuator) transfer function.

---

## 2.6 Computed-Torque Control (Dynamic / Non-Linear)

For high-speed or high-payload manipulators, the **non-linear robot dynamics** must be cancelled. The computed-torque law does exactly that.

**Standard robot dynamic model (recap for Module 1):**

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} \;+\; G(q) \;=\; \tau$$

**Computed-torque control law:**

$$\tau \;=\; M(q)\!\left[\ddot{q}_d + K_d\,\dot{e} + K_p\,e\right] \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} \;+\; G(q)$$

with $e = q_d - q$.

**Why this works:** Substituting $\tau$ into the dynamic model gives a *decoupled, linear* error equation:

$$\ddot{e} \;+\; K_d\,\dot{e} \;+\; K_p\,e \;=\; 0$$

A standard second-order ODE whose gains $K_p, K_d$ are picked to guarantee stability.

---

## 2.7 Force / Impedance Control (Brief, Module-1-level)

When the robot interacts with the environment (assembly, polishing), pure position control is dangerous (it would either crush the part or lose contact). Two complementary methods:

1. **Impedance Control** — Regulate the *relationship* between force and position: $F - F_d = M_d(\ddot{x} - \ddot{x}_d) + B_d(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_d) + K_d(x - x_d)$.
2. **Hybrid Position/Force Control** — Partition task space into *position-controlled* directions and *force-controlled* directions (Raibert & Craig, 1981).

---

## 2.8 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **minimum** you must memorize for any 14-mark derivation. Use `\vert` (not `\vert`) inside cells.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Error definition | $e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$ | $q_d$ = desired, $q$ = actual, *rad* |
| 2 | PID (time domain) | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i\!\int e\,dt + K_d\,\dot{e}$ | $K_p$ [N·m/rad], $K_i$ [N·m/(rad·s)], $K_d$ [N·m·s/rad] |
| 3 | PID transfer function | $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ | Laplace domain |
| 4 | Closed-loop TF | $Y(s)/R(s) = C(s)G(s)/[1+C(s)G(s)]$ | Unity feedback |
| 5 | Robot dynamic model | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ | $M$ inertia, $C$ Coriolis, $G$ gravity, $\tau$ torque |
| 6 | Computed-torque law | $\tau = M(q)[\ddot{q}_d + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e] + C\dot{q} + G$ | Cancels non-linearities |
| 7 | Linearised error eq. | $\ddot{e} + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e = 0$ | Critically damped if $K_d^2 = 4K_p$ |
| 8 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = K_d / (2\sqrt{K_p})$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_p}$ | *rad/s* |
| 10 | Impedance law | $F - F_d = M_d\ddot{x} + B_d\dot{x} + K_d x$ | $M_d,B_d,K_d$ desired mass/damping/stiffness |
| 11 | Sampling rate | $T_s \le 10$ ms (industrial) | *s* |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Utility of these Methods

- **Open-loop (stepper)** → 3-D printers, small CNC, watch-making.
- **Closed-loop PID** → 90% of industrial articulated robots (e.g., FANUC, ABB).
- **Computed torque** → High-speed parallel robots (Delta), space manipulators (Canadarm).
- **Impedance** → Polishing, deburring, collaborative robots (cobots like UR5).
- **Force control** → Peg-in-hole assembly, robotic surgery (Da Vinci).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are worked out **line-by-line**. Every algebraic step is shown, as required by KTU 2024 Scheme valuations.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From the Robot Dynamic Model to a Linearised Error Equation using Computed-Torque

**Starting point** (rigid-body dynamics, Module-1 result):

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} \;+\; G(q) \;=\; \tau \quad (1)$$

**Define the tracking error** and its derivatives:

$$e \;=\; q_d - q \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q},\quad \ddot{e} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q} \quad (2)$$

**Propose a control law (computed torque):**

$$\tau \;=\; M(q)\!\left[\ddot{q}_d + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e\right] \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} \;+\; G(q) \quad (3)$$

**Substitute (3) into (1):**

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G \;=\; M(q)\!\left[\ddot{q}_d + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e\right] + C\dot{q} + G$$

**Cancel the common terms $C\dot{q}$ and $G$ from both sides** (these are exactly cancelled by the feed-forward terms):

$$M(q)\ddot{q} \;=\; M(q)\!\left[\ddot{q}_d + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e\right]$$

**Divide by the invertible inertia matrix $M(q)$:**

$$\ddot{q} \;=\; \ddot{q}_d + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e$$

**Rearrange using (2) — replace $\ddot{q}$ by $\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e}$:**

$$\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e} \;=\; \ddot{q}_d + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e$$

**Cancel $\ddot{q}_d$ from both sides and multiply by $-1$:**

$$\ddot{e} \;+\; K_d\dot{e} \;+\; K_p e \;=\; 0 \quad \blacksquare \quad (4)$$

**Interpretation:** Equation (4) is a *decoupled* second-order linear ODE. Picking $K_p, K_d > 0$ guarantees asymptotic stability; picking $K_d^2 = 4K_p$ gives *critical damping* (no overshoot).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a First-Order Plant with PID

**Plant** (approximate a single joint as a first-order lag):

$$G(s) \;=\; \frac{K}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

**PID controller:**

$$C(s) \;=\; K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \;=\; \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Forward path:**

$$C(s)\,G(s) \;=\; \frac{K(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau_p s + 1)}$$

**Closed-loop transfer function (unity feedback):**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$$

**Substitute:**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{K(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau_p s + 1) + K(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}$$

**Expand denominator:**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{K K_d s^2 + K K_p s + K K_i}{\tau_p s^2 + s + K K_d s^2 + K K_p s + K K_i}$$

**Group by power of $s$:**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{K K_d s^2 + K K_p s + K K_i}{(\tau_p + K K_d)s^2 + (1 + K K_p)s + K K_i}$$

**Steady-state error to a unit step input** (Final Value Theorem):

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0} s\,E(s) \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0} s\!\left[R(s) - Y(s)\right]$$

For $R(s) = 1/s$:

$$e_{ss} \;=\; 1 - \lim_{s \to 0}\frac{K K_d s^2 + K K_p s + K K_i}{(\tau_p + K K_d)s^2 + (1 + K K_p)s + K K_i} \;=\; 1 - \frac{K K_i}{K K_i} \;=\; 0$$

**Conclusion:** The integral term $K_i$ *guarantees zero steady-state error* to a step input — this is the principal reason industrial robots always include a small integral action.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Settling Time and Overshoot in Terms of $K_p, K_d$

Starting from the linearised error equation (4):

$$\ddot{e} + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

Compare with the standard second-order form:

$$\ddot{e} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{e} + \omega_n^2 e = 0$$

Matching coefficients:

$$\omega_n \;=\; \sqrt{K_p} \qquad 2\zeta\omega_n \;=\; K_d$$

Therefore:

$$\zeta \;=\; \frac{K_d}{2\sqrt{K_p}}$$

**Settling time (2% criterion):**

$$t_s \;\approx\; \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_n} \;=\; \frac{8}{K_d}$$

**Percentage overshoot:**

$$M_p \;=\; 100 \cdot e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Exam tip:* KTU's 14-mark problems often ask: *"Choose $K_p, K_d$ for $\zeta = 1$ and $t_s = 0.4$ s."* Apply $K_p = \omega_n^2 = (K_d/2)^2$ and $K_d = 8/t_s$ directly.

**Numerical example:** $t_s = 0.4$ s $\Rightarrow K_d = 20$. Critical damping $\Rightarrow \zeta = 1 \Rightarrow K_p = (K_d/2)^2 = 100$. Verify: $\omega_n = 10$ rad/s, $t_s \approx 4/(1 \times 10) = 0.4$ s ✓.

---

## 3.4 Stability Test — Routh-Hurwitz on the Closed-Loop Polynomial

Using the closed-loop characteristic polynomial from §3.2:

$$(\tau_p + K K_d)s^2 + (1 + K K_p)s + K K_i \;=\; 0$$

For a *second-order* polynomial, stability is guaranteed iff **all coefficients are positive**:

$$\tau_p + K K_d > 0,\quad 1 + K K_p > 0,\quad K K_i > 0$$

For a *third-order* version (e.g., with actuator dynamics $1/(\tau_a s + 1)$), the Routh array is built. The condition reduces to:

$$K_p K_d \;>\; K_i \cdot (\text{plant time-constant ratio}) \quad (\text{informal})$$

In KTU answers, the key stability *insight* is: **"Increase $K_p$ to speed up the response, but then increase $K_d$ proportionally to avoid overshoot and instability."**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop — Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph OL["OPEN-LOOP CONTROL"]
    direction LR
    R1[Reference Input] --> C1[Controller or Sequence]
    C1 --> A1[Actuator]
    A1 --> P1[Plant: Robotic Arm]
    P1 --> Y1[Output: Joint Position]
    X1[Disturbance] -.->|affects but no correction| P1
  end

  subgraph CL["CLOSED-LOOP SERVO CONTROL"]
    direction LR
    R2[Reference q_d] --> SUM2((Sum junction))
    SUM2 --> C2[Controller: PID or Computed Torque]
    C2 --> A2[Actuator + Driver]
    A2 --> P2[Plant: Robotic Arm]
    P2 --> Y2[Actual Position q]
    Y2 --> S2[Sensor: Encoder or Resolver]
    S2 --> F2[Feedback Path]
    F2 --> SUM2
    X2[Disturbance] -.->|rejected by feedback| P2
  end

  classDef refNode fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#b8323c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef ctrlNode fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef plantNode fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef sumNode fill:#fff1b8,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef senseNode fill:#e2cfea,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

  class R1,R2 refNode
  class C1,C2 ctrlNode
  class A1,A2,P1,P2 plantNode
  class SUM2 sumNode
  class S2 senseNode
```

**How to read the diagram in the exam:**
- The **left** block has no path from output back to input ⇒ *open-loop*.
- The **right** block has a feedback path `F2` returning to the summing junction ⇒ *closed-loop*.
- Disturbance $X$ acts on both plants, but only the closed-loop system *actively rejects* it.

---

## 4.2 PTP vs CP — Behavioural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Start([Start: Joint Command Issued]) --> Q{Path Specification?}
  Q -- Specified as endpoints only --> PTP[PTP CONTROL]
  Q -- Specified as full trajectory --> CP[CP CONTROL]

  PTP --> PTP1[Compute joint angles q_A, q_B]
  PTP1 --> PTP2[Joint-interpolated move A to B]
  PTP2 --> PTP3[End-effector path: UNCONTROLLED]
  PTP3 --> App1[Use: Spot welding, Palletizing]

  CP --> CP1[Define splines or analytic path]
  CP1 --> CP2[Sample q_d at T_s intervals]
  CP2 --> CP3[Closed-loop tracking of q_d]
  CP3 --> CP4[End-effector path: CONTROLLED]
  CP4 --> App2[Use: Arc welding, Painting, Cutting]

  classDef ptp fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#a85b00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef cp fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef start fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef q fill:#ffc6ff,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

  class PTP,PTP1,PTP2,PTP3,App1 ptp
  class CP,CP1,CP2,CP3,CP4,App2 cp
  class Start start
  class Q q
```

---

## 4.3 Computed-Torque Control — Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  qd[Desired q_d] --> SUM1((+))
  q[Actual q] --> SUM2((+))
  SUM2 --> neg1[Sign flip]
  neg1 --> SUM1
  SUM1 --> e[Error e]
  e --> Kp[Kp proportional]
  e --> Kd[Kd derivative]
  e --> Ki[Ki integral]
  Kp --> SUM3((+))
  Kd --> SUM3
  Ki --> SUM3
  SUM3 --> v[Control signal v]
  v --> NL[Non-linear Cancellation M q double dot plus C q dot plus G]
  NL --> tau[Torque tau]
  tau --> ARM[Robot Arm M of q]
  ARM --> qout[q and q dot]
  qout --> SENSE[Encoder]
  SENSE --> q
  qd --> ACC[Acceleration q double dot d]
  ACC --> NL
  qd --> VEL[Velocity q dot d]
  VEL --> NL

  classDef ref fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#b8323c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef err fill:#fff1b8,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef gain fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef plant fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef sense fill:#e2cfea,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

  class qd,ACC,VEL ref
  class e,SUM1,SUM2,SUM3,neg1 err
  class Kp,Kd,Ki gain
  class ARM,NL,tau,v plant
  class SENSE,q,qout sense
```

**Reading aid for the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (sum junctions, gains) is the *servo* part — exactly a PD on the tracking error.
- The **inner block "Non-linear Cancellation"** computes the *feed-forward* $M(q)\ddot{q}_d + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d + G(q)$ that linearises the plant.

---

## 4.4 Hierarchical Control Pyramid in a Robotic Work Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  L4[Level 4: Work-Cell Supervisor: Task Scheduling]
  L3[Level 3: Task Planner: Trajectory Generation]
  L2[Level 2: Servo Controller: PID or Computed Torque]
  L1[Level 1: Joint Motor Driver: Voltage and Current]
  L0[Level 0: Mechanical Arm: Links, Joints, Gears]

  L4 --> L3
  L3 --> L2
  L2 --> L1
  L1 --> L0

  classDef lvl0 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef lvl1 fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef lvl2 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef lvl3 fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#283593,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef lvl4 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

  class L0 lvl0
  class L1 lvl1
  class L2 lvl2
  class L3 lvl3
  class L4 lvl4
```

> [!TIP]
> KTU Module 1's "Method of Control" is essentially the **boundary between Levels 1 and 2** — the *servo controller* and *motor driver*. Master the terminology; the 14-mark question often asks you to "describe the control hierarchy of a robot work cell."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]  
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control methods in a robotic manipulator. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Open-Loop (Non-Servo) | Closed-Loop (Servo) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | No feedback of output | Continuous feedback via sensors |
| Accuracy | Depends on model accuracy | Self-correcting |
| Cost | Low | Higher (sensors + drivers) |
| Example | Pick-and-place pneumatic arm; 3-D printer (stepper) | FANUC articulated welding robot |

A *3-D printer* uses a stepper motor in open-loop; an *arc-welding robot* uses DC servo with encoder feedback. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for comparison, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]  
**Explain the difference between Point-to-Point (PTP) and Continuous Path (CP) control with suitable applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **PTP Control** moves the tool from point A to point B with *no control* over the intermediate path; speed is maximised. Application: *spot welding, palletizing, drilling*.
- **CP Control** tracks a *specified trajectory* (linear, circular, spline) between points using closed-loop servo. Application: *arc welding, painting, contour cutting*.
- PTP is implemented in joint space; CP requires trajectory generation in Cartesian space. *(1 mark each for the two definitions + 1 mark for application contrast.)*

---

## 📚 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module-Level Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **classical PID control method** as applied to a single robotic joint. Derive the closed-loop transfer function and obtain the linearised error differential equation. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **computed-torque control** method. Show that the resulting error dynamics are linear and decoupled. (7 marks)

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution to Q-A(a) — PID Control (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define the PID law (1 mark):**

$$u(t) \;=\; K_p\,e(t) \;+\; K_i\!\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_d\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Take the Laplace transform (1 mark):**

$$C(s) \;=\; K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \;=\; \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Let the joint be modelled as a first-order plant (1 mark):**

$$G(s) \;=\; \frac{K}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Form the closed-loop transfer function (1.5 marks):**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1+C(s)G(s)} \;=\; \frac{K(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{(\tau_p + K K_d)s^2 + (1+K K_p)s + K K_i}$$

**Step 5 — Discuss the role of each gain (1.5 marks):**
- $K_p$: speed of response; too high ⇒ overshoot, instability.
- $K_i$: eliminates steady-state error.
- $K_d$: dampens oscillation; acts as a *predictor* of future error.

**Step 6 — Ziegler-Nichols tuning hint (1 mark):** Mention that industrial robot joint gains are typically tuned using the Z-N closed-loop method (ultimate gain $K_u$, ultimate period $T_u$, then $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $K_i = 2K_p/T_u$, $K_d = K_p T_u/8$).

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution to Q-A(b) — Computed-Torque Control (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State the rigid-body dynamic model (1 mark):**

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

**Step 2 — Define the tracking error (0.5 marks):**

$$e = q_d - q$$

**Step 3 — Propose the control law (1.5 marks):**

$$\tau = M(q)\!\left[\ddot{q}_d + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e\right] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the dynamic model (1.5 marks):**

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = M(q)(\ddot{q}_d + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e) + C\dot{q} + G$$

Cancel $C\dot{q}$ and $G$:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} = M(q)(\ddot{q}_d + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify to obtain the linearised error equation (1.5 marks):**

Using $\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e}$:

$$\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e} = \ddot{q}_d + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e \;\Rightarrow\; \ddot{e} + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

This is a *decoupled* (one equation per joint) second-order linear ODE. With $K_p, K_d > 0$, the system is asymptotically stable. **Hence the computed-torque law linearises and decouples the manipulator dynamics.** (1 mark for the conclusion.)

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare in detail **servo and non-servo control** in robots. Bring out the differences with respect to feedback, hardware, accuracy, cost and applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain **hybrid position/force control** with a suitable block diagram. Why is it necessary in robotic assembly tasks? (7 marks)

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution to Q-B(a) — Servo vs Non-Servo (7 marks)

**Comparative table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Non-Servo (Open-Loop) | Servo (Closed-Loop) |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback** | None; limit switches only | Continuous (encoder/resolver) |
| **Drive** | Pneumatic / stepper / bang-bang hydraulic | Servo motor (DC, AC, or hydraulic servo) |
| **Position control** | End-points only | Every point along the trajectory |
| **Accuracy** | Low to moderate; depends on mechanics | High (typically ± 0.02 mm) |
| **Speed control** | Limited; bang-bang profile | Smooth velocity profiles possible |
| **Cost** | Low (no sensors, simple drivers) | High (sensors, drives, controller) |
| **Programming** | Sequence-based (limit switches, timers) | Coordinate-based (teach pendant, textual) |
| **Application** | Pick-and-place, simple transfers | Welding, painting, assembly, machining |
| **Examples** | Sealed pneumatic arms; 3-D printers | FANUC, ABB, KUKA industrial robots |

**Block diagram — servo system (1 mark):** Reference → error → controller (PID) → amplifier → motor → arm → encoder → back to summing junction.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Servo control is essential for trajectory tracking; non-servo is sufficient for end-point tasks.

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution to Q-B(b) — Hybrid Position/Force Control (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):**  
In assembly (e.g., peg-in-hole), a robot must *control force* along the insertion direction and *control position* in the plane perpendicular to it. Pure position control would either crush the part or fail to make contact.

**Step 2 — Task decomposition (1.5 marks):**  
The task space is partitioned into a *position-controlled* subspace (dimension $m$) and a *force-controlled* subspace (dimension $n-m$), with $m + n = 6$ for a 6-DOF robot. A *compliance selection matrix* $S$ is diagonal with 1s in position directions and 0s in force directions; the complementary matrix $\bar{S} = I - S$ selects force directions.

**Step 3 — Control law (1.5 marks):**  
Position loop: $x_d^{pos} \to$ Cartesian PID $\to q_d \to$ joint servo.  
Force loop: $F_d \to$ force error $F_d - F \to$ force PID $\to$ velocity command along the force-controlled directions.

**Step 4 — Block diagram (2 marks):**  
[Cartesian reference $x_d$] → [Position controller] → [Inverse Jacobian] → [Joint servo] → [Robot arm] → [End-effector] → [Force sensor] → [Force controller] → [Velocity reference] → joins back into the position loop via the *selection matrix*.

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):**  
The Raibert–Craig hybrid controller allows independent, simultaneous control of position and force along orthogonal directions, which is indispensable for compliant assembly, deburring, and polishing.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on "Method of Control" questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "servo" with "PID".** Servo = *closed-loop architecture*; PID = *one possible control law inside a servo loop*. Examiners deduct a full mark if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the units of $K_p, K_i, K_d$.** $K_p$ is in N·m/rad, not just a number. Always state the unit.
> 3. **In computed-torque derivations**, students *assume* $M(q)$ is a constant and cancel it "because it is in both terms." This is wrong — $M(q)$ is multiplied *after* it appears in the cancellation. Show $M^{-1}(q)M(q) = I$ explicitly.
> 4. **PTP vs CP** — students often say "CP is *more accurate*." It is not about accuracy, it is about *path specification*. Use the word "trajectory."
> 5. **Hybrid control** — students forget to define the *selection matrix* $S$. Without it, the examiner cannot award the 1.5 marks reserved for the control law.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Two grand families:** Open-loop (non-servo) vs Closed-loop (servo).
- ⭐ **PTP** = end-points matter; **CP** = the path itself matters.
- ⭐ **PID** = $K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$; integral kills steady-state error.
- ⭐ **Computed-torque** linearises the non-linear robot dynamics; the result is a decoupled second-order ODE $\ddot{e} + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e = 0$.
- ⭐ **Hybrid position/force control** uses a *selection matrix* to partition task space.
- ⭐ **Stepper motors** are the *electrical* form of open-loop control.
- ⭐ **Force / impedance control** is mandatory for any robot that *physically interacts* with the environment (assembly, polishing).
- ⭐ **Servo loop** is the *sampling-rate-critical* element — typical $T_s$ = 1 to 10 ms.
- ⭐ **Stability** of PID requires all characteristic-polynomial coefficients to be positive (Routh-Hurwitz).
- ⭐ **Hierarchy**: Supervisor → Planner → Servo Controller → Motor Driver → Mechanical Arm. KTU loves this diagram.
- ⭐ **Units reminder:** $K_p$ [N·m/rad], $K_i$ [N·m/(rad·s)], $K_d$ [N·m·s/rad], $\omega_n$ [rad/s], $t_s$ [s].
- ⭐ **Memorable keywords:** Sense → Compare → Compute → Actuate (the servo mantra).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Geometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Robotic Manipulator Geometry** is the branch of kinematics that describes the spatial arrangement, spatial relationships, and mathematical mapping between successive links of a serial or parallel manipulator. It encompasses the assignment of coordinate frames to each link, the representation of **position** (linear translation) and **orientation** (angular rotation) of every link relative to a reference (base) frame, and the use of **homogeneous transformation matrices** to compose these mappings into a unified kinematic model.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, the geometry module establishes the *language of robotics* — i.e., the mathematical machinery needed before any motion planning, dynamics, or control study can be performed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Geometry):**
> Students are expected to master (i) position and orientation description, (ii) coordinate transformation matrices, (iii) Euler/roll–pitch–yaw angles, (iv) Denavit–Hartenberg (D-H) parameter assignment, and (v) the link-coordinate transformation chain $\ ^{0}_{n}T$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **crane operator** in a port. To lift a container, you must know three things:

1. **Where** is the container? (Position)
2. **Which way is it facing?** (Orientation)
3. **What is the angle of your boom, the rotation of your cabin, and the length of your cable?** (Joint geometry)

If you cannot describe these, you cannot grab the container. A **robotic manipulator** is exactly such a crane — but with a brain. The *geometry* of the robot is the set of rules that tells the brain how joint angles translate into the position and orientation of the end-effector (the "hand" or tool).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Geometry ≠ Motion. Geometry answers "Where is the tool given the joint angles?" while motion (kinematics) answers "How did it get there, and how fast?"

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants in Robotics Geometry

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $x, y, z$ | Position components | **meters (m)** or **millimeters (mm)** |
| $\phi, \theta, \psi$ | Orientation angles (roll, pitch, yaw) | **radians (rad)** or **degrees (°)** |
| $\ ^{i}_{j}T$ | Homogeneous transform from frame $i$ to $j$ | **dimensionless $4 \times 4$ matrix** |
| $a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i$ | Denavit–Hartenberg link parameters | **m** and **rad** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **$4 \times 4$ homogeneous transformation matrix** is the central mathematical object of robot geometry. Every link relationship, no matter how complex, is encoded in one such matrix.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Position Description

A point $P$ in 3-D space relative to a frame $\{A\}$ is described by a **$3 \times 1$ position vector**:

$$
\ ^{A}P = \begin{bmatrix} p_x \\ p_y \\ p_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

This vector encodes the Cartesian coordinates of $P$ along the $X_A$, $Y_A$, $Z_A$ axes respectively.

### 2.2 Orientation Description

Orientation cannot be a single number — a rigid body in 3-D space has **3 rotational degrees of freedom**. Three common representations are:

#### (a) Rotation Matrix (Direction Cosine Approach)
A $3 \times 3$ orthonormal matrix $R$ whose columns are the unit vectors of frame $\{B\}$ expressed in $\{A\}$:

$$
\ ^{A}_{B}R = \begin{bmatrix} \ ^{A}X_B & \ ^{A}Y_B & \ ^{A}Z_B \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Properties:
- Orthonormal: $R^T R = I$ and $\det(R) = +1$
- Inverse equals transpose: $R^{-1} = R^T$

#### (b) Euler Angles (Z-Y-X / Roll–Pitch–Yaw)
The orientation is built as three sequential rotations about moving (body) axes:

$$
\ ^{A}_{B}R_{Z-Y-X}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma) = R_Z(\alpha) \, R_Y(\beta) \, R_X(\gamma)
$$

#### (c) Fixed-Angle Representation
Same final result, but rotations are about the *fixed* (world) reference axes — order of multiplication reverses.

### 2.3 Homogeneous Transformation Matrix

The position vector and rotation matrix are unified into a single **$4 \times 4$ homogeneous transformation matrix**:

$$
\ ^{A}_{B}T = \begin{bmatrix} \ ^{A}_{B}R_{3 \times 3} & \ ^{A}P_{B \, \text{org}} \\ 0_{1 \times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\ ^{A}P_{B \, \text{org}}$ is the origin of frame $\{B\}$ expressed in $\{A\}$.

### 2.4 Mapping (Coordinate Transformation)

To express a point $\ ^{B}P$ given in frame $\{B\}$ into the coordinates of frame $\{A\}$:

$$
\ ^{A}P = \ ^{A}_{B}T \ \ ^{B}P
$$

This is the **fundamental mapping equation of robot geometry**.

### 2.5 Composite Transformations

If a point is known in frame $\{C\}$ and $\{C\}$ is known in $\{B\}$ and $\{B\}$ is known in $\{A\}$:

$$
\ ^{A}_{C}T = \ ^{A}_{B}T \ \ ^{B}_{C}T
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order matters in matrix multiplication:** $T_1 T_2 \ne T_2 T_1$ in general. The **leftmost** transform is applied **last** to the point vector (post-multiplication rule for moving frames).

### 2.6 Operator Transforms (for active rotations of a vector)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_X(\theta) &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & c\theta & -s\theta \\ 0 & s\theta & c\theta \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
R_Y(\theta) &= \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & 0 & s\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -s\theta & 0 & c\theta \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
R_Z(\theta) &= \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & -s\theta & 0 \\ s\theta & c\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $c\theta = \cos\theta$ and $s\theta = \sin\theta$.

### 2.7 Translation Operator

$$
\text{Trans}(a,b,c) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & a \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & b \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & c \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Point position | $\ ^{A}P = [p_x \ p_y \ p_z]^T$ | $3 \times 1$ |
| 2 | Orientation matrix | $\ ^{A}_{B}R = [^{A}X_B \ ^{A}Y_B \ ^{A}Z_B]$ | Orthonormal, $\det = +1$ |
| 3 | Homogeneous transform | $\ ^{A}_{B}T = \begin{bmatrix} R & p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $4 \times 4$ |
| 4 | Coordinate mapping | $\ ^{A}P = \ ^{A}_{B}T \ ^{B}P$ | Core equation |
| 5 | Composite | $\ ^{A}_{C}T = \ ^{A}_{B}T \ ^{B}_{C}T$ | Order-sensitive |
| 6 | Inverse transform | $\ ^{A}_{B}T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Use $R^{-1} = R^T$ |
| 7 | RPY → R | $R = R_Z(\phi) R_Y(\theta) R_X(\psi)$ | Z-Y-X convention |
| 8 | Translation op. | $\text{Trans}(a,b,c)$ | Pure translation |
| 9 | Roll-Pitch-Yaw | $\phi$ (roll), $\theta$ (pitch), $\psi$ (yaw) | About moving axes |
| 10 | DH param equation | $\ ^{i-1}_{i}T = R_X(\alpha_{i-1}) \text{Trans}(a_{i-1},0,0) \text{Trans}(0,0,d_i) R_Z(\theta_i)$ | Used in Module 1/2 |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 theory papers, the examiners **love** testing the **inverse homogeneous transform** and the **composite transform** — both are 14-mark favorites.

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial welding robots** (e.g., KUKA, ABB) use homogeneous transforms to plan weld paths.
- **Surgical robots** (Da Vinci) require sub-millimeter position accuracy, all derived from geometry.
- **Autonomous vehicles** use continuous chained transforms: $\ ^{world}_{car}T \cdot \ ^{car}_{lidar}T \cdot \ ^{lidar}_{point}T = \ ^{world}_{point}T$.
- **Space robotics** (Canadarm on ISS) uses the same DH-framework to compute end-effector pose from joint angles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Rotation about an Arbitrary Axis Composed from RPY

**Problem:** Derive the roll–pitch–yaw (RPY) rotation matrix $\ ^{A}_{B}R_{Z-Y-X}(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ used in robot geometry.

**Step 1 — Define the three elementary rotations** (about moving axes, intrinsic):
- Roll $\phi$ about $X$-axis: $R_X(\phi)$
- Pitch $\theta$ about $Y$-axis: $R_Y(\theta)$
- Yaw $\psi$ about $Z$-axis: $R_Z(\psi)$

**Step 2 — Compose them in sequence (moving-frame convention):**
The body frame first rotates by $\phi$ about $X$, then by $\theta$ about the *new* $Y$, then by $\psi$ about the *new* $Z$. The composed matrix is:

$$
\ ^{A}_{B}R_{Z-Y-X} = R_Z(\psi) \, R_Y(\theta) \, R_X(\phi)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the elementary operator matrices:**

$$
R_X(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & c\phi & -s\phi \\ 0 & s\phi & c\phi \end{bmatrix}, \quad
R_Y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & 0 & s\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -s\theta & 0 & c\theta \end{bmatrix}, \quad
R_Z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi & -s\psi & 0 \\ s\psi & c\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R_Y(\theta) R_X(\phi)$ first:**

$$
R_Y(\theta) R_X(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & 0 & s\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -s\theta & 0 & c\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & c\phi & -s\phi \\ 0 & s\phi & c\phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying row-by-column:

$$
R_Y(\theta) R_X(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & s\theta \, s\phi & s\theta \, c\phi \\ 0 & c\phi & -s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta \, s\phi & c\theta \, c\phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Left-multiply by $R_Z(\psi)$:**

$$
\ ^{A}_{B}R = R_Z(\psi) \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & s\theta s\phi & s\theta c\phi \\ 0 & c\phi & -s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Final expanded form:**

$$
\ ^{A}_{B}R_{Z-Y-X}(\phi,\theta,\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta c\psi & s\phi s\theta c\psi - c\phi s\psi & c\phi s\theta c\psi + s\phi s\psi \\ c\theta s\psi & s\phi s\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi & c\phi s\theta s\psi - s\phi c\psi \\ -s\theta & s\phi c\theta & c\phi c\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **standard RPY rotation matrix** used throughout KTU robotics problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Notes:**
> - $c\alpha$ denotes $\cos\alpha$, $s\alpha$ denotes $\sin\alpha$.
> - The result is orthonormal; you may verify $R^T R = I$ as a sanity check.
> - For small angles ($\phi, \theta, \psi \to 0$), the matrix tends to identity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Inverse of a Homogeneous Transformation Matrix

**Problem:** Given $\ ^{A}_{B}T = \begin{bmatrix} R & p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, find $\ ^{A}_{B}T^{-1} = \ ^{B}_{A}T$.

**Step 1 — Write the unknown inverse in block form:**

$$
\ ^{B}_{A}T = \begin{bmatrix} Q & q \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Impose the definition of inverse:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} R & p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} Q & q \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} I & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Block multiply:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} RQ & Rq + p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} I & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate blocks:**

- Top-left: $RQ = I \Rightarrow Q = R^{-1} = R^T$ (since $R$ is orthonormal)
- Top-right: $Rq + p = 0 \Rightarrow q = -R^T p$

**Step 5 — Final inverse formula:**

$$
\ ^{B}_{A}T = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This elegant formula is one of the most frequently tested items in KTU Module 1.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Composite Transformation (Chained Multiplication)

**Problem:** A camera frame $\{C\}$ is mounted on a robot end-effector $\{E\}$, and the robot base is $\{B\}$. The base-to-end-effector transform is $\ ^{B}_{E}T$ and the end-effector-to-camera transform is $\ ^{E}_{C}T$. Find the position of an object observed at $\ ^{C}P$ in the base frame.

**Step 1 — Write the chain of coordinate transformations:**

A point in $\{C\}$ can be expressed in $\{E\}$ as $\ ^{E}P = \ ^{E}_{C}T \ ^{C}P$.

A point in $\{E\}$ can be expressed in $\{B\}$ as $\ ^{B}P = \ ^{B}_{E}T \ ^{E}P$.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\ ^{B}P = \ ^{B}_{E}T \ (\ ^{E}_{C}T \ ^{C}P) = (\ ^{B}_{E}T \ ^{E}_{C}T) \ ^{C}P
$$

**Step 3 — Define the composite transform:**

$$
\ ^{B}_{C}T = \ ^{B}_{E}T \ ^{E}_{C}T
$$

**Step 4 — Final mapping equation:**

$$
\ ^{B}P = \ ^{B}_{C}T \ ^{C}P
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** When multiplying $\ ^{A}_{B}T \ ^{B}_{C}T$, the **inner subscripts must match** ($B$ here). This is the "chained railroad" rule.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A frame $\{B\}$ is obtained by rotating $\{A\}$ about $Z_A$ by $30°$ and translating by $(2, 5, 3)$ units. A point $P$ has coordinates $\ ^{B}P = [1, 0, 2]^T$. Find $\ ^{A}P$.

**Step 1 — Compute $R_Z(30°)$:**

$$
c30 = \cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866, \quad s30 = \sin 30° = 0.5
$$

$$
R_Z(30°) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & -0.5 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Build the homogeneous transform $\ ^{A}_{B}T$:**

$$
\ ^{A}_{B}T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & -0.5 & 0 & 2 \\ 0.5 & 0.866 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Write $\ ^{B}P$ in homogeneous form:**

$$
\ ^{B}P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$
\ ^{A}P = \ ^{A}_{B}T \ ^{B}P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & -0.5 & 0 & 2 \\ 0.5 & 0.866 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute each row:
- Row 1: $0.866(1) + (-0.5)(0) + 0(2) + 2(1) = 2.866$
- Row 2: $0.5(1) + 0.866(0) + 0(2) + 5(1) = 5.5$
- Row 3: $0(1) + 0(0) + 1(2) + 3(1) = 5$
- Row 4: $1$

**Step 5 — Final answer:**

$$
\ ^{A}P = \begin{bmatrix} 2.866 \\ 5.5 \\ 5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification trick:** The homogeneous row (last row) is **always** $[0 \ 0 \ 0 \ 1]$ for transforms and the last entry of the point vector is **always** $1$. If you ever get a different value, you have made a multiplication mistake.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Robot Geometry Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Base Frame 0] --> B[Link 1 Frame 1]
    B --> C[Link 2 Frame 2]
    C --> D[Link 3 Frame 3]
    D --> E[Link n Frame n]
    E --> F[End Effector Tool Frame]
    F --> G[Object Workpiece Frame]

    H[Joint Angle Theta] --> B
    I[Joint Angle Theta] --> C
    J[Joint Angle Theta] --> D
    K[Joint Angle Theta] --> E

    L[Position Vector P] --> G
    M[Orientation RPY] --> G
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each link frame is related to the previous one by a homogeneous transform $\ ^{i-1}_{i}T$, which is a function of the joint angle $\theta_i$ (or $d_i$ for prismatic). The "chain" of frames is precisely what robot geometry builds.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Forward Kinematics Geometry Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Frame 0] -->|T01| B[Frame 1]
    B -->|T12| C[Frame 2]
    C -->|T23| D[Frame 3]
    D -->|Tn-1 n| E[Tool Frame n]

    A1[Joint 1 Theta1] -.->|modulates| B
    A2[Joint 2 Theta2] -.->|modulates| C
    A3[Joint 3 Theta3] -.->|modulates| D
    AN[Joint n Thetan] -.->|modulates| E

    E --> F[End Effector Pose x y z phi theta psi]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each $\ ^{i-1}_{i}T$ is a function of one joint variable. The product $\ ^{0}_{n}T = \ ^{0}_{1}T \ ^{1}_{2}T \ \dots \ ^{n-1}_{n}T$ gives the end-effector pose. This is the *forward kinematics* built entirely on geometry.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mapping of a Point Through Multiple Frames

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point P in Frame C] --> T1[Multiply by TBC]
    T1 --> P2[Point P in Frame B]
    P2 --> T2[Multiply by TAB]
    T2 --> P3[Point P in Frame A]

    TF[Transform Chain T AC = T AB T BC] --> P3
```

**Reading the diagram:** A point defined in any local frame can be "carried up" the chain to the base frame by a sequence of homogeneous multiplications. The "railroad" rule is visually evident: matched inner subscripts.

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Robot Geometry in Industrial Work Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[World Frame W] --> B[Robot Base Frame B]
    B --> C[End Effector Frame E]
    C --> D[Camera Frame C]
    D --> E[Workpiece Frame Wp]

    F[Operator Teach Pendant] --> B
    G[Vision System] --> D
    H[Conveyor Tracking] --> E
```

**Reading the diagram:** A real work cell has *many* frames co-existing. Robot geometry provides the *mathematical bridge* between them, enabling hand-eye calibration, conveyor tracking, and coordinated motion.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Note:** KTU 2024 Part A tests direct definitions and one-line applications. Marks are awarded for *correctness* and *keyword usage*.

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "homogeneous transformation matrix" as used in robot manipulator geometry. Write its general $4 \times 4$ form.**

**Model Answer:**
A homogeneous transformation matrix is a $4 \times 4$ matrix that compactly represents the position and orientation of one coordinate frame relative to another. It has the form:

$$
\ ^{A}_{B}T = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & p_x \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & p_y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & p_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The upper-left $3 \times 3$ block is the rotation matrix (orientation) and the upper-right $3 \times 1$ block is the position vector. (3 marks)

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**List the three elementary rotation operators about the X, Y, and Z axes used in robot geometry.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
R_X(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & c\theta & -s\theta \\ 0 & s\theta & c\theta \end{bmatrix}, \quad
R_Y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & 0 & s\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -s\theta & 0 & c\theta \end{bmatrix}, \quad
R_Z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & -s\theta & 0 \\ s\theta & c\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** For every 14-mark question, the paper setter provides an internal choice. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) A frame $\{B\}$ is obtained by rotating $\{A\}$ about the $X$-axis by $90°$ and then translating by $(4, 5, 6)$ units. Find the homogeneous transformation matrix $\ ^{A}_{B}T$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Rotation about $X$ by $90°$:

$$
R_X(90°) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos 90° & -\sin 90° \\ 0 & \sin 90° & \cos 90° \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 2 — Translation vector:

$$
p = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 5 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 3 — Form the homogeneous matrix:

$$
\ ^{A}_{B}T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 6 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Marks distribution:** Rotation matrix — 3 marks, translation vector — 1 mark, assembly into $4 \times 4$ — 3 marks.

---

**(b) A point $P$ has coordinates $\ ^{B}P = [2, 3, 4]^T$ in frame $\{B\}$ from the above problem. Find its coordinates in frame $\{A\}$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Express $P$ in homogeneous form:

$$
\ ^{B}P = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 2 — Multiply:

$$
\ ^{A}P = \ ^{A}_{B}T \ ^{B}P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 6 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 3 — Row-wise computation:
- Row 1: $1(2) + 0(3) + 0(4) + 4(1) = 6$
- Row 2: $0(2) + 0(3) + (-1)(4) + 5(1) = 1$
- Row 3: $0(2) + 1(3) + 0(4) + 6(1) = 9$
- Row 4: $1$

Step 4 — Final answer:

$$
\ ^{A}P = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 1 \\ 9 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Marks distribution:** Setting up the multiplication — 2 marks, computation — 4 marks, final result — 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Define roll, pitch, and yaw angles. Derive the roll–pitch–yaw rotation matrix $\ ^{A}_{B}R_{Z-Y-X}$ in expanded form. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Definitions:
- **Roll ($\phi$):** rotation about the $X$-axis.
- **Pitch ($\theta$):** rotation about the $Y$-axis.
- **Yaw ($\psi$):** rotation about the $Z$-axis.

In the Z-Y-X (moving frame) convention, the frame rotates by $\phi$ about $X$, then $\theta$ about the *new* $Y$, then $\psi$ about the *new* $Z$.

Step 2 — Composition:

$$
\ ^{A}_{B}R_{Z-Y-X} = R_Z(\psi) \, R_Y(\theta) \, R_X(\phi)
$$

Step 3 — Substitute elementary matrices (refer Section 3.1 derivation for full step-by-step):

$$
\ ^{A}_{B}R_{Z-Y-X} = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta c\psi & s\phi s\theta c\psi - c\phi s\psi & c\phi s\theta c\psi + s\phi s\psi \\ c\theta s\psi & s\phi s\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi & c\phi s\theta s\psi - s\phi c\psi \\ -s\theta & s\phi c\theta & c\phi c\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Marks distribution:** Definitions — 2 marks, composition rule — 2 marks, expanded result — 3 marks.

---

**(b) Given $\phi = 30°$, $\theta = 45°$, $\psi = 60°$, compute the rotation matrix and verify its orthonormality. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute trigonometric values:
- $\cos 30° = 0.866$, $\sin 30° = 0.5$
- $\cos 45° = \sin 45° = 0.7071$
- $\cos 60° = 0.5$, $\sin 60° = 0.866$

Step 2 — Plug into the matrix:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} (0.7071)(0.5) & (0.5)(0.7071)(0.5)-(0.866)(0.866) & (0.866)(0.7071)(0.5)+(0.5)(0.866) \\ (0.7071)(0.866) & (0.5)(0.7071)(0.866)+(0.866)(0.5) & (0.866)(0.7071)(0.866)-(0.5)(0.5) \\ -0.7071 & (0.5)(0.7071) & (0.866)(0.7071) \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 3 — Numerical computation:

$$
R \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.3536 & -0.5732 & 0.7392 \\ 0.6124 & 0.7392 & 0.2803 \\ -0.7071 & 0.3536 & 0.6124 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 4 — Orthonormality check (sample column-pair):
- $R^T R$ first column dot first column: $0.3536^2 + 0.6124^2 + (-0.7071)^2 \approx 0.125 + 0.375 + 0.5 = 1.000$ ✓
- First column dot second column: $0.3536(-0.5732) + 0.6124(0.7392) + (-0.7071)(0.3536) \approx -0.2027 + 0.4527 - 0.25 = 0.000$ ✓

Hence, $R^T R = I$, confirming orthonormality.

**Marks distribution:** Trig values — 1 mark, matrix computation — 3 marks, orthonormal verification — 3 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones (Module 1 – Geometry):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the homogeneous coordinate "1"** at the end of position vectors → loses 1 mark silently.
> 2. **Mixing up operator transform vs. mapping transform:** $R_X(\theta)$ rotates a *vector*; $\ ^{A}_{B}T$ transforms a *frame*. Examiners love this trap.
> 3. **Order of multiplication in RPY:** $R_Z R_Y R_X$ vs. $R_X R_Y R_Z$ — the *order* changes the result. Always state the convention (Z-Y-X or X-Y-Z).
> 4. **Inverse transform error:** Students often write $\ ^{A}_{B}T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^{-1} & p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — **wrong**! The correct form is $\begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. Loses 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Composite transform order:** $T_{AC} = T_{AB} T_{BC}$ — the middle subscripts must match. Examiners check this **first**.
> 6. **Radian vs. degree mismatch** in calculator steps: ensure consistency.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Robot Manipulator Geometry (Module 1)**

- [x] A **position vector** is a $3 \times 1$ (or $4 \times 1$ homogeneous) Cartesian coordinate.
- [x] An **orientation** is a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix — *orthonormal* ($\det = +1$, $R^T = R^{-1}$).
- [x] A **homogeneous transformation matrix** is a $4 \times 4$ matrix combining orientation and position.
- [x] The **mapping equation** is $\ ^{A}P = \ ^{A}_{B}T \ ^{B}P$.
- [x] The **composite transform** is $\ ^{A}_{C}T = \ ^{A}_{B}T \ ^{B}_{C}T$ (inner subscripts must match).
- [x] The **inverse transform** is $\ ^{A}_{B}T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- [x] The **RPY convention** is $R = R_Z(\psi) R_Y(\theta) R_X(\phi)$ for Z-Y-X moving-frame rotations.
- [x] The **translation operator** $\text{Trans}(a,b,c)$ is identity in the rotation block with $[a \ b \ c]^T$ in the position block.
- [x] **Order matters** in matrix multiplication — non-commutativity is fundamental.
- [x] For DH parameters (next module): $\ ^{i-1}_{i}T = R_X(\alpha_{i-1}) \text{Trans}(a_{i-1},0,0) \text{Trans}(0,0,d_i) R_Z(\theta_i)$ — memorize the structure.
- [x] **Always sanity-check:** homogeneous row $[0 \ 0 \ 0 \ 1]$, orthonormal columns, $\det(R) = +1$.

> [!NOTE]
