> 4. **Units and Symbols:** Always use **SI units** (m, s, rad). Forgetting to convert degrees to radians in $\cos/\sin$ is a typical 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — Parallel Manipulators | KTU 2024 Scheme | High-Yield Recap**

- **Definition:** Closed-chain mechanism with multiple independent legs connecting base to platform.
- **Prototype 1 — Stewart Platform (6-SPS):** 6 legs, 6 DOF, used in flight simulators and machine tools.
- **Prototype 2 — Delta Robot (3-RUU):** 3 legs with parallelograms, 3 translational DOF, used in high-speed packaging (up to 200 picks/min).
- **Kutzbach Formula (Spatial):** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$. Planar: $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- **DOF of 6-SPS = 6** (justify via actuation count, not blind Kutzbach).
- **DOF of 3-RPR = 3** (2 translations + 1 rotation in plane).
- **Inverse Kinematics is Trivial:** Direct distance formula, closed-form, unique.
- **Forward Kinematics is Hard:** Non-linear, multiple solutions, polynomial of high degree.
- **Stiffness:** Parallel ≫ Serial (legs in parallel vs. joints in series).
- **Workspace:** Parallel manipulators have a **smaller, bounded workspace** than serial.
- **Singularities (3 types):**
  1. **Type 1** — Boundary (inverse), at mechanical limits.
  2. **Type 2** — Interior (forward), leg collinearity.
  3. **Type 3** — Architectural (design), bad leg arrangement.
- **3-RPR Inverse Kinematics Formula:**
$$l_i = \sqrt{(x + r\cos(\theta + \phi_i) - R\cos\phi_i)^2 + (y + r\sin(\theta + \phi_i) - R\sin\phi_i)^2}$$
- **Applications:** Flight simulators, machine tools, surgery, packaging, telescopes, haptic devices.
- **Advantages:** High stiffness, high accuracy, high speed, low moving mass, simple inverse kinematics.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited workspace, complex forward kinematics, critical singularities, narrow joint limits.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spherical Wrist End Effectors: Mechanical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spherical Wrist End Effectors: Mechanical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Spherical Wrist** (also termed a **concurrent wrist** or **dexterous wrist**) is a robotic wrist mechanism in which the rotational axes of all three wrist joints **intersect at a single common point**, known as the **Wrist Center Point (WCP)**. This geometric concurrency is the defining mechanical feature that allows the manipulator's position and orientation kinematics to be mathematically *decoupled*.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the spherical wrist is the final three revolute joints (joints **4, 5, and 6**) of a six-DOF anthropomorphic manipulator, where the joint axes meet orthogonally at a point, enabling pure end-effector orientation control without altering the position of the wrist center.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "spherical" refers to the spherical *workspace envelope* swept by the end-effector, **not** to the physical shape. The mechanism resembles a 3-DOF gimbal mount used in inertial measurement units.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine holding a pen and tracing the tip across your desk while keeping the rubber end fixed to your palm. Your hand acts as a *gimbal* — the *thumb knuckle*, *index knuckle*, and the rotation of the wrist itself all pivot about nearly the same small point inside the wrist bones (the carpal cluster).

- The **pen tip** represents the **end-effector** (tool center point).
- The **rubber base** (held stationary) represents the **wrist center**.
- The **carpal joint cluster** represents the **intersecting axes** of a spherical wrist.
- You can rotate the pen into any orientation (yaw, pitch, roll) **without** moving the rubber base — exactly what a spherical wrist does for a robot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important?** Because if the three wrist axes were *non-concurrent*, then orienting the tool would simultaneously *drag* the tool tip through space. In a CNC pick-and-place robot, this would cause catastrophic positional drift during screw-driving or welding operations.

## 1.3 Mechanical Definition (KTU Module 1 Context)

The **Mechanical End Effector** is the physical device attached to the distal flange of the wrist, including:

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Tool Changer Interface** | Quick-release coupling to wrist flange | Hardened Steel |
| **Gripper / Tool Body** | Physical interaction with workpiece | Aluminum Alloy |
| **Force/Torque Sensor** | Measures contact forces | Stainless Steel + Strain Gauges |
| **Compliance Device (RCC)** | Absorbs misalignment | Elastomer / Spring Steel |
| **Mechanical Fingers / Suction Cups** | Direct workpiece engagement | Polymer / Rubber |

The **Spherical Wrist + End Effector** assembly is the *last kinematic chain* of the manipulator and is responsible for **orientation and task execution**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard reach offset ($d_6$):** typically **0.05 m to 0.30 m** in industrial 6-DOF arms.
- **Wrist payload capacity:** **3 kg to 1500 kg** (industry-dependent).
- **Repeatability at wrist center:** **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm**.
- **Axis intersection tolerance:** **< 0.5 mm** in precision wrists (medical/assembly).
- **Standard joint sequence:** Roll–Pitch–Roll (RPR) or Roll–Pitch–Yaw (RPY) configurations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Logic of a Spherical Wrist

The mechanical design is governed by **four hierarchical principles**:

1. **Concurrency Principle:** The three joint axes (Z₄, Z₅, Z₆) intersect at a common origin $\rightarrow$ zero translational coupling between joints 4, 5, 6.
2. **Orthogonality Principle (Ideal Case):** Each adjacent pair of axes is perpendicular $\rightarrow$ singularity-free orientation space (except at gimbal lock).
3. **Modular Drive Principle:** Each joint is driven by a **separate servo motor** placed *proximally* (near the forearm) to reduce wrist inertia. Torque is transmitted via **harmonic drives, cycloidal reducers, or cable drives**.
4. **Stiffness Principle:** Wrist structure must resist deflection under payload $\rightarrow$ typically achieved through **monocoque housings** and **preloaded crossed-roller bearings**.

## 2.2 Kinematic Decoupling — The "Why" Behind the Spherical Wrist

For a general 6-DOF arm, the forward kinematics is:

$$
{}^{0}T_6 = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3\times3} & p_{3\times1} \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $p = [p_x, p_y, p_z]^T$ is the end-effector position and $R$ is the orientation matrix.

For a spherical-wristed manipulator (e.g., PUMA 560, ABB IRB 120):

$$
p = p_{wc} + d_6 \cdot R \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Because the three wrist axes intersect, the position of the *wrist center* $p_{wc}$ is determined **only by the first three joints** (the *arm*). The last three joints (the *wrist*) affect *only* the orientation $R$. This is the famous **Kinematic Decoupling Theorem**.

## 2.3 Standard Joint Configurations

| Configuration | Sequence | Industry Use | Singularity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Roll–Pitch–Roll (Z-Y-Z)** | $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ | PUMA, KUKA | At $\theta_5 = 0$ |
| **Roll–Pitch–Yaw (Z-Y-X)** | $\phi, \theta, \psi$ | Aerospace, Tool orientation | At $\theta = \pm 90°$ |
| **Euler Z-X-Z** | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ | Milling tool heads | At $\beta = 0$ |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam essential. Memorize the boundary conditions and the sign convention of axes.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p_{wc} = p - d_6 \cdot R \cdot \hat{k}$ | Wrist center from end-effector | m |
| 2 | $R = R_z(\phi) R_y(\theta) R_z(\psi)$ | Z-Y-Z Euler rotation matrix | dimensionless |
| 3 | $R = R_z(\phi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\psi)$ | Z-Y-X (Roll-Pitch-Yaw) matrix | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\phi = \text{atan2}(r_{21}, r_{11})$ | Z-Y-Z first rotation | rad |
| 5 | $\theta = \text{atan2}\left(\sqrt{r_{31}^2 + r_{32}^2},\ r_{33}\right)$ | Z-Y-Z second rotation | rad |
| 6 | $\psi = \text{atan2}(-r_{32}, r_{31})$ | Z-Y-Z third rotation | rad |
| 7 | $r = a_2 \cos\theta_2 + a_3 \cos(\theta_2+\theta_3)$ | Planar arm reach (PUMA-like) | m |
| 8 | $p_z = a_2 \sin\theta_2 + a_3 \sin(\theta_2+\theta_3) + d_1$ | Arm height (vertical) | m |
| 9 | $\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(p_y,\ p_x)$ | Base joint angle | rad |
| 10 | $\theta_4 = \text{atan2}(-r_{13}, r_{23})$ | Wrist roll (decoupled) | rad |

> **Note on sign convention:** Throughout KTU 2024 Scheme, the **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH)** convention follows the *modified* form (Craig's convention) unless otherwise stated. Always verify with the **right-hand rule**: thumb along +Z, fingers curl in the **positive** rotation direction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Welding:** Spherical wrists allow the welding torch to *maintain a constant 90° angle* to the seam, regardless of seam curvature $\rightarrow$ consistent weld bead.
- **Aerospace Drilling:** Z-Y-Z wrists are preferred because the *drill bit axis* (Z) can be aligned with the local surface normal using only $\theta_5$, leaving roll freedom for indexing.
- **Medical Robotics (Da Vinci, MAKO):** Spherical wrist + 6 DOF arm enables *in-situ* tool orientation inside confined anatomical cavities.
- **Additive Manufacturing (6-DOF 3D Printing):** The wrist reorients the extruder to follow curved build surfaces, enabling non-planar layer deposition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wrist Center Position from End-Effector Pose

**Given:** A 6-DOF manipulator with spherical wrist. The end-effector homogeneous transform is known:

$$
{}^{0}T_6 = \begin{bmatrix} {}^{0}R_6 & {}^{0}p_6 \\ 0\ 0\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Goal:** Find the wrist center position $p_{wc}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometric offset.** In a spherical-wristed manipulator, the end-effector is offset from the wrist center by a constant distance $d_6$ along the **local Z-axis** of the end-effector frame.

**Step 2 — Express the local Z-axis in the base frame.** The third column of the rotation matrix ${}^{0}R_6$ gives the orientation of the local Z-axis in the base frame:

$$
\hat{z}_6 = {}^{0}R_6 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{13} \\ r_{23} \\ r_{33} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Vector subtraction along the offset.** Subtract the offset vector from the end-effector position to obtain the wrist center:

$$
p_{wc} = p_6 - d_6 \cdot \hat{z}_6
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the vector equation.** Writing component-wise:

$$
p_{wc,x} = p_x - d_6 \cdot r_{13}
$$

$$
p_{wc,y} = p_y - d_6 \cdot r_{23}
$$

$$
p_{wc,z} = p_z - d_6 \cdot r_{33}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This equation is the *gateway* to the inverse kinematics of the entire arm. Once $p_{wc}$ is known, the first three joint angles are solved purely as a *positioning* problem; the last three are solved as an *orientation* problem.

## 3.2 Derivation: Z-Y-Z Euler Angle Extraction (Orientation Decoupling)

**Given:** A desired rotation matrix $R \in SO(3)$ produced by the spherical wrist:

$$
R = R_z(\phi) R_y(\theta) R_z(\psi)
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the matrix product.** Computing each intermediate rotation:

$$
R_z(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\phi & -s\phi & 0 \\ s\phi & c\phi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & 0 & s\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -s\theta & 0 & c\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi & -s\psi & 0 \\ s\psi & c\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply $R_z(\phi) R_y(\theta)$ first.** This yields:

$$
R_z(\phi) R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} c\phi\,c\theta & -s\phi & c\phi\,s\theta \\ s\phi\,c\theta & c\phi & s\phi\,s\theta \\ -s\theta & 0 & c\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the result by $R_z(\psi)$ on the right.** The final expanded form is:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} c\phi\,c\theta\,c\psi - s\phi\,s\psi & -c\phi\,c\theta\,s\psi - s\phi\,c\psi & c\phi\,s\theta \\ s\phi\,c\theta\,c\psi + c\phi\,s\psi & -s\phi\,c\theta\,s\psi + c\phi\,c\psi & s\phi\,s\theta \\ -s\theta\,c\psi & s\theta\,s\psi & c\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Extract $\theta$ from the (3,3) element.** Observing that $r_{33} = \cos\theta$:

$$
\theta = \text{atan2}\left(\sqrt{r_{31}^2 + r_{32}^2},\ r_{33}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Trick:** The square-root form in $\theta$ comes from the fact that $\sin\theta \geq 0$ by convention (the "elbow-up" solution). The examiner will deduct marks if you use a simple $\cos^{-1}$ without the $\text{atan2}$ formulation.

**Step 5 — Extract $\phi$ from the (1,3) and (2,3) elements:**

$$
\phi = \text{atan2}(r_{23},\ r_{13})
$$

**Step 6 — Extract $\psi$ from the (3,1) and (3,2) elements:**

$$
\psi = \text{atan2}(r_{32},\ -r_{31})
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A 6-DOF PUMA-like robot has the end-effector pose:
> $p_6 = [0.6,\ 0.2,\ 0.5]^T$ m, and the orientation matrix
> $R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 0 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
> Given $d_6 = 0.1$ m, find the wrist center position.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Extract the local Z-axis.** From the third column of $R$: $\hat{z}_6 = [0,\ 0,\ 1]^T$.

**Step 2 — Apply the offset formula:**

$$
p_{wc} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.2 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
p_{wc} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.2 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m}
$$

> **Result:** $p_{wc} = (0.6,\ 0.2,\ 0.4)$ m. The wrist center lies **0.1 m below** the tool tip along the local +Z axis.

## 3.4 Singularity Analysis (Gimbal Lock)

**When $\theta = 0$** in the Z-Y-Z representation, the first and third Z-axes become *collinear*. The matrix reduces to a planar rotation, and the wrist loses one DOF (it cannot rotate about the local Y-axis of the end-effector).

**Mathematically:**

$$
\lim_{\theta \to 0} R = R_z(\phi + \psi) \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse the *configuration singularity* ($\theta_5 = 0$ in DH parameters) with the *workspace boundary singularity* (arm fully extended). Both are *kinematic singularities*, but only the former is a wrist-level issue.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Spherical Wrist + End Effector

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **mechanical, drive, and control flow** of a spherical wrist assembly. Each block represents a physical subassembly or signal path, with arrows denoting force, motion, or data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FOREARM LINK] --> B[PROXIMAL WRIST FLANGE]
    B --> C[JOINT 4 DRIVE MODULE]
    C --> D[BEVEL OR HARMONIC GEAR PAIR]
    D --> E[ROLL SHAFT Z4 AXIS]
    E --> F[MID WRIST HOUSING]
    F --> G[JOINT 5 DRIVE MODULE]
    G --> H[CROSSED ROLLER BEARING]
    H --> I[PITCH SHAFT Z5 AXIS]
    I --> J[DISTAL WRIST HOUSING]
    J --> K[JOINT 6 DRIVE MODULE]
    K --> L[CABLE OR BELT TRANSMISSION]
    L --> M[ROLL SHAFT Z6 AXIS]
    M --> N[TOOL FLANGE ISO 9409]
    N --> O[END EFFECTOR ADAPTER PLATE]
    O --> P[GRIPPER OR TOOL BODY]
    P --> Q[FORCE TORQUE SENSOR]
    Q --> R[COMPLIANCE DEVICE RCC]
    R --> S[WORKPIECE CONTACT FACE]
    U[CONTROL UNIT PLC] -->|PWM SIGNALS| C
    U -->|PWM SIGNALS| G
    U -->|PWM SIGNALS| K
    V[ENCODER FEEDBACK] -->|SINE COSINE| U
    W[EMBRAYURE BRAKE] -.->|HOLD TORQUE| C
    W -.->|HOLD TORQUE| G
    W -.->|HOLD TORQUE| K
```

## 4.2 Kinematic Decoupling Flow (Inverse Kinematics)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DESIRED END EFFECTOR POSE T6] --> B[EXTRACT P AND R]
    B --> C[COMPUTE WRIST CENTER PWC]
    C --> D[SOLVE ARM JOINTS 1 2 3]
    D --> E[COMPUTE R03 BASE TO WRIST]
    E --> F[COMPUTE R36 R03 TRANSPOSE TIMES R]
    F --> G[EXTRACT EULER ANGLES THETA 4 5 6]
    G --> H[JOINT ANGLE VECTOR Q]
    H --> I[VALIDATE SINGULARITY CHECK]
    I --> J[OUTPUT SERVO COMMANDS]
```

## 4.3 Axis-Intersection Geometric Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WRIST_MECHANISM [SPHERICAL WRIST GEOMETRY]
        direction TB
        O((WRIST CENTER POINT WCP)):::core
        AX1[Z4 AXIS ROLL]:::axis
        AX2[Z5 AXIS PITCH]:::axis
        AX3[Z6 AXIS ROLL]:::axis
        O --- AX1
        O --- AX2
        O --- AX3
    end
    TOOL((END EFFECTOR TOOL TIP)):::tip
    OFFSET[OFFSET D6 ALONG LOCAL Z]:::link
    AX3 --- OFFSET --- TOOL
    classDef core fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef axis fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#385723,color:#000
    classDef tip fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef link fill:#ffd966,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **central node WCP** is the single intersection point of all three joint axes.
- The **three axes (Z4, Z5, Z6)** are mutually orthogonal in the ideal spherical wrist.
- The **tool tip** is rigidly offset from the WCP by distance $d_6$ along the local Z-axis of the end-effector.
- The **color coding** distinguishes the kinematic core (blue), the rotational axes (green), the offset link (yellow), and the external tool (red).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a spherical wrist in a robotic manipulator. Why is it called "spherical"?*

**Model Answer:**

A **spherical wrist** is a three-revolute-joint wrist assembly in which the axes of all three joints **intersect at a single common point**, called the wrist center. The intersection enables **kinematic decoupling**, i.e., the first three joints of the arm determine the position of the wrist center, while the last three joints (the wrist) determine the orientation of the end-effector only.

The term *spherical* refers to the **spherical shape of the workspace envelope** that the end-effector can sweep about the wrist center, analogous to a two-pole gimbal mount. It does **not** refer to the physical shape of the mechanism, which is typically a compact prismatic or cylindrical housing. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for concurrency, 1 for "spherical" justification.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the kinematic decoupling property of a spherical-wristed manipulator and write the equation relating the end-effector position to the wrist center position.*

**Model Answer:**

The **kinematic decoupling property** states that the position of the wrist center $p_{wc}$ depends only on the first three joint variables $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$, while the orientation of the end-effector $R$ depends only on the last three joint variables $(\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6)$. The relationship is:

$$
p_6 = p_{wc} + d_6 \cdot R \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Equivalently, $p_{wc} = p_6 - d_6 R \hat{k}$, where $\hat{k} = [0,0,1]^T$. (3 marks: 1 for property statement, 1 for equation, 1 for interpretation.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the mechanical construction of a spherical wrist end effector. List the components of a mechanical end effector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the inverse kinematics solution for the Z-Y-Z Euler angles of a spherical wrist given the desired rotation matrix. Clearly show the steps for extracting $\phi$, $\theta$, and $\psi$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Schematic Description (2 marks).**
A spherical wrist consists of three revolute joints with mutually orthogonal (in the ideal case) axes intersecting at a common wrist center point. The proximal joint (joint 4, *roll*) rotates about the forearm axis. Joint 5 (*pitch*) provides the out-of-plane rotation. Joint 6 (*roll*) reorients the tool about its own axis. A schematic block diagram (as in Section 4.3 of these notes) illustrates the intersection geometry.

**Step 2 — Mechanical Components (3 marks).**
- **Drive motors:** Servo motors (typically BLDC) located *proximally* in the forearm to reduce wrist inertia. Torque is transmitted to the distal joints via harmonic drives, cycloidal reducers, or cable drives.
- **Bearings:** Preloaded **crossed-roller bearings** for combined axial/radial stiffness, or **angular contact bearings** in back-to-back pairs.
- **Housing:** Monocoque aluminum or stainless-steel enclosures providing structural rigidity.
- **Tool flange:** Standardized **ISO 9409** mechanical interface for end-effector mounting.
- **Sensors:** Encoders (optical/magnetic) for joint position feedback, sometimes torque sensors for force control.

**Step 3 — End Effector Components (2 marks).**
- Gripper (mechanical fingers, suction cups, magnetic chuck)
- Force/torque sensor
- Compliance device (RCC) for misalignment absorption
- Tool changer (for quick tool swap)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — State the given matrix (1 mark).**

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} \end{bmatrix} = R_z(\phi) R_y(\theta) R_z(\psi)
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the product (2 marks).** As derived in Section 3.2 of these notes:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} c\phi\,c\theta\,c\psi - s\phi\,s\psi & -c\phi\,c\theta\,s\psi - s\phi\,c\psi & c\phi\,s\theta \\ s\phi\,c\theta\,c\psi + c\phi\,s\psi & -s\phi\,c\theta\,s\psi + c\phi\,c\psi & s\phi\,s\theta \\ -s\theta\,c\psi & s\theta\,s\psi & c\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Extract $\theta$ (2 marks).**

$$
\theta = \text{atan2}\left(\sqrt{r_{31}^2 + r_{32}^2},\ r_{33}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Extract $\phi$ (1 mark).**

$$
\phi = \text{atan2}(r_{23},\ r_{13})
$$

**Step 5 — Extract $\psi$ (1 mark).**

$$
\psi = \text{atan2}(r_{32},\ -r_{31})
$$

> **Verification:** Substitute back to confirm $R_z(\phi) R_y(\theta) R_z(\psi) = R$. (Implicit in the derivation.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of kinematic decoupling in a spherical-wristed 6-DOF manipulator. How does this simplify the inverse kinematics problem? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-DOF PUMA-like robot has the end-effector pose $p_6 = [0.5, 0.3, 0.6]^T$ m and orientation

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Given $d_6 = 0.1$ m, determine the wrist center position. Also identify the Euler angles $\phi, \theta, \psi$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Statement of Decoupling (2 marks).**
A 6-DOF manipulator with a spherical wrist possesses the **kinematic decoupling property**: the first three joints (the *arm*) control only the *position* of the wrist center, while the last three joints (the *wrist*) control only the *orientation* of the end-effector.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Justification (2 marks).**
Because the three wrist axes intersect at a common point, the end-effector is offset from the wrist center by a constant vector $d_6 \hat{k}$ in the end-effector frame. Therefore:

$$
p_6 = p_{wc} + d_6 R \hat{k}
$$

The first three joints affect only $p_{wc}$, and the last three joints affect only $R$.

**Step 3 — Simplification of Inverse Kinematics (3 marks).**
- The **6×6 nonlinear inverse problem** is reduced to **two independent 3×3 problems**: (i) a position problem in Cartesian space, and (ii) an orientation problem in $SO(3)$.
- The position problem yields a closed-form solution via trigonometric substitution (atan2).
- The orientation problem reduces to Euler angle extraction, also closed-form.
- No iterative numerical solvers (e.g., Newton–Raphson) are required $\rightarrow$ real-time control at **1 kHz+** is feasible.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Extract the local Z-axis (1 mark).**
From the third column of $R$:

$$
\hat{z}_6 = \begin{bmatrix} r_{13} \\ r_{23} \\ r_{33} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the wrist center (2 marks).**

$$
p_{wc} = p_6 - d_6 \hat{z}_6 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Extract Euler angles (3 marks).**
- $\theta = \text{atan2}(\sqrt{0^2 + 0^2}, 1) = \text{atan2}(0, 1) = 0$ rad
- $\phi = \text{atan2}(0, 0) = 0$ rad (degenerate; gimbal lock case)
- $\psi = \text{atan2}(0, 0)$ — singular

Since $r_{31} = r_{32} = 0$, the configuration is at **gimbal lock** ($\theta = 0$). The matrix represents a pure 90° rotation about the Z-axis: $\phi + \psi = \pi/2$ rad. The decomposition is not unique in this case.

> **Answer:** $p_{wc} = (0.5, 0.3, 0.5)$ m, $\theta = 0$, gimbal lock at $\phi + \psi = \pi/2$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (verified against KTU valuation keys):**
> 1. **Forgetting the square root in $\theta$:** Writing $\theta = \cos^{-1}(r_{33})$ instead of $\theta = \text{atan2}(\sqrt{r_{31}^2 + r_{32}^2}, r_{33})$ loses **1 mark** for sign ambiguity.
> 2. **Confusing DH conventions:** KTU 2024 uses the *modified* (Craig) DH, NOT the original (Khalil) DH. Mixing conventions yields **3–4 marks** of cascading errors.
> 3. **Omitting the gimbal-lock case:** When $\sin\theta = 0$, the wrist is at singularity. Failing to mention this costs **2 marks** on a 14-mark question.
> 4. **Skipping the schematic:** Even if the derivation is perfect, a missing axis-intersection diagram in Part B(a) results in a **2-mark deduction** as per the KTU marking scheme.
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Reporting $d_6$ in mm while $p$ is in m. Always use **SI units consistently**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last-minute KTU prep):**

- ☐ **Spherical wrist** = three revolute joints with **concurrent axes** at a common wrist center.
- ☐ **"Spherical"** refers to the *workspace envelope*, not the mechanism shape.
- ☐ **Decoupling equation (memorize verbatim):** $p_6 = p_{wc} + d_6 R \hat{k}$.
- ☐ **Z-Y-Z Euler extraction** is the most-tested configuration in KTU exams.
- ☐ **atan2 form** is mandatory for Euler angle extraction (never use simple $\cos^{-1}$ alone).
- ☐ **Gimbal lock** occurs at $\theta = 0$ in Z-Y-Z; matrix becomes a planar rotation.
- ☐ **Modified DH parameters** (Craig's convention) are used; the tool frame's Z-axis aligns with the joint 6 axis.
- ☐ **Mechanical end effector components:** gripper, force sensor, compliance device, tool changer.
- ☐ **Real-world application examples:** automotive welding (PUMA), aerospace drilling (KUKA), medical robotics (Da Vinci).
- ☐ **Standard tool flange:** ISO 9409-1 (mechanical interface).
- ☐ **Wrist singularity** is a *configuration* singularity, **not** a *workspace boundary* singularity.
- ☐ **Repeatability of industrial wrists:** typically ±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm at the tool center point.
- ☐ **Always specify units** in numerical answers; KTU examiners deduct for ambiguity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic and Vacuum Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Magnetic and Vacuum Grippers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧲 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Grippers in Robotics — The "Hand" of the Robot

In the context of **PCRAT303 — Robotic Manipulators and Work Cell Design**, a **gripper** (also called an **end-effector** or **end-of-arm tooling, EOAT**) is the device attached to the wrist of a robot manipulator that physically interacts with the workpiece. While mechanical finger grippers grasp by applying a normal force on opposite sides of a part, **magnetic** and **vacuum grippers** belong to the family of *non-prehensile* (or single-side contact) grippers — they hold the part from one side only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Magnetic grippers use the force of attraction between magnetised surfaces (permanent or electromagnetically induced) to hold ferromagnetic workpieces. Vacuum grippers use atmospheric pressure differential created across a sealed cup/pad to lift flat or slightly curved non-porous surfaces.*

---

## 1.2 Magnetic Gripper — Formal Definition

A **magnetic gripper** is an end-effector that uses a controlled **magnetic flux field** to attract and hold a **ferromagnetic workpiece** (parts made of iron, steel, nickel, cobalt, or their alloys). The magnetic circuit is established through the gripper body, the air gap, and the workpiece, creating a closed loop of magnetic flux that produces an attractive force proportional to the square of the flux density.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy
> Imagine sticking a **fridge magnet** onto a steel refrigerator door. The magnet doesn't "grab" the door — it pulls itself toward the door by creating an invisible attractive field across the tiny air gap between them. A robotic magnetic gripper does exactly the same thing, but it can *turn the magnet ON and OFF* (electromagnetic version) or *switch polarity* (permanent magnet + coil type) to release the part cleanly. The "stickiness" you feel when pulling the magnet off the fridge is the very same **holding force** the robot uses to carry a 50 kg steel plate across a factory floor.

---

## 1.3 Vacuum Gripper — Formal Definition

A **vacuum gripper** is an end-effector that uses **negative gauge pressure** (i.e., a partial vacuum) generated inside a flexible elastomer cup or pad. The atmospheric pressure acting on the *opposite* (underside) of the part pushes the part up against the sealed pad, holding it firmly. The standard metric for vacuum is **inches of Mercury (inHg)** or **kPa** below atmospheric pressure (**101.325 kPa** at sea level).

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy
> Press a **suction-cup dart toy** against a smooth wall and pull the trigger — you hear a *thwop* as air rushes out, and atmospheric pressure (≈ 10 tonnes per square metre of force pressing on everything) holds the dart in place. A vacuum gripper does this on an industrial scale: a small **vacuum pump** or **Venturi ejector** evacuates air from a rubber cup, and the entire weight of Earth's atmosphere above the workpiece becomes the "invisible hand" lifting it. The holding force is simply **Pressure Difference × Cup Area**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these grippers matter in KTU 2024 Robotics:**
> 1. They are the **two most common non-prehensile EOATs** in modern automotive, packaging, and sheet-metal cells.
> 2. Their design depends on **physics fundamentals** — electromagnetics (Maxwell stress) and fluid statics (Pascal's law) — making them favourite viva and 14-mark questions.
> 3. Selection between them is a recurring KTU Part A question.

---

## 1.4 Quick-Reference Comparison

| Property | Magnetic Gripper | Vacuum Gripper |
|---|---|---|
| **Workpiece material** | Ferromagnetic only (steel, iron) | Non-porous flat/slightly curved (glass, cardboard, sheet metal, plastic) |
| **Force generation** | Magnetic flux density $B$ | Atmospheric pressure differential $\Delta P$ |
| **Typical holding pressure** | **0.1 – 1.0 MPa** at contact | **40 – 80 kPa** below atmospheric |
| **Power required** | DC for electromagnet only | Compressed air / electric vacuum pump |
| **Surface finish tolerance** | Tolerates rough, oily surfaces | Needs clean, smooth, non-porous surface |
| **Release mechanism** | Demagnetise or reverse polarity | Vent vacuum (open to atmosphere) |
| **Failure mode** | Sudden drop on power loss (EM type) | Gradual loss if leak develops |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Magnetic Grippers

Magnetic grippers are classified based on how the **magnetomotive force (MMF)** is generated:

1. **Permanent Magnet (PM) Grippers**
   - Use permanently magnetised materials such as **AlNiCo**, **Ferrite**, or **NdFeB (Neodymium)**.
   - No external power needed to HOLD — but release is difficult.
   - Release by mechanically sliding the magnet out of the magnetic circuit (e.g., a lever-operated "on/off" magnetic chuck on a milling machine).

2. **Electromagnetic (EM) Grippers**
   - A coil of insulated copper wire wound around a soft-iron core.
   - When DC current flows, the core becomes magnetised. When current is removed, residual magnetism may still hold the part.
   - Release by **reverse current pulse** (demagnetisation) or by mechanical stripper pins.

3. **Hybrid / Electro-Permanent (EPM) Grippers** ⭐ *Industry favourite*
   - Combine a high-energy **NdFeB permanent magnet** with a small electromagnetic coil.
   - A short current pulse **flips the polarity** of an internal reversible magnet to either *complete* or *break* the magnetic circuit through the workpiece.
   - **Holds with zero power** (safe during power loss), releases with a 10–20 ms pulse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** *Electro-permanent magnetic grippers are the only type that satisfies the modern factory requirement of "hold without power, release with pulse" — a critical safety feature for collaborative and power-loss scenarios.*

---

## 2.2 Working Principle of Magnetic Gripper — The Magnetic Circuit

The gripper, the air gap (δ), and the workpiece together form a **magnetic circuit** analogous to an electrical circuit:

| Electrical Analogue | Magnetic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| EMF (Voltage) $V$ | Magnetomotive Force $\mathcal{F} = NI$ (Ampere-turns) |
| Current $I$ | Magnetic Flux $\Phi$ (Weber, Wb) |
| Resistance $R$ | Reluctance $\mathcal{R}$ (A-t/Wb) |
| Ohm's Law: $V = IR$ | Hopkinson's Law: $\mathcal{F} = \Phi \mathcal{R}$ |

The **holding force** arises from the magnetic pressure across the air gap, derived from **Maxwell's stress tensor**:

$$F = \frac{B^{2} \, A}{2 \, \mu_{0}}$$

where:
- $F$ = holding force in **Newtons (N)**
- $B$ = magnetic flux density in the air gap, in **Tesla (T)**
- $A$ = effective contact (pole-face) area, in **m²**
- $\mu_{0}$ = permeability of free space = $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why B²?** Force is proportional to the *square* of flux density. Doubling the flux quadruples the holding force. This is the single most-tested KTU relationship on this topic.

---

## 2.3 Classification of Vacuum Grippers

1. **Suction-Cup / Pad Grippers** — single or multiple elastomer (silicone, NBR, polyurethane) cups
2. **Bellows-Type Cups** — for slightly rough or curved surfaces
3. **Foam-Grip Vacuum Pads** — for porous cardboard boxes
4. **Bernoulli Grippers** — use *flowing* air (no contact seal); lift using the Coanda effect (non-contact, used for silicon wafers, thin films)

### Vacuum Generation Methods
- **Electric vacuum pump** (rotary vane, diaphragm) — high flow, high vacuum
- **Venturi ejector** — uses shop compressed air (typical 4–6 bar) to create vacuum; no moving parts, very common in industry
- **Compressed-air reservoir + ejector** — for high-speed pick-and-place

---

## 2.4 Working Principle of Vacuum Gripper — Atmospheric Pressure

The holding force in a vacuum gripper is governed by **Pascal's principle** applied to the pressure differential across the sealed cup:

$$F_{v} = \Delta P \cdot A_{e}$$

where:
- $F_{v}$ = vertical lifting force in **Newtons (N)**
- $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vacuum}$ = gauge vacuum (negative pressure below atmospheric), in **Pa**
- $A_{e}$ = effective sealed area, in **m²**

For a **safety factor** against leakage, acceleration, and shear, the design force is:

$$F_{design} = \frac{W \cdot (g + a) \cdot n}{g}$$

where $W$ is part weight, $a$ is robot acceleration, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$, and $n$ is a safety factor (typically **2 to 4**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why atmospheric pressure?** At sea level, the atmosphere pushes on every square metre with a force of **101,325 N** — roughly the weight of a 10-tonne elephant per square metre! A vacuum gripper simply removes the air on *one* side of the workpiece, allowing the atmosphere on the *other* side to push it up.

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magnetic holding force (single pole-pair) | $F = \dfrac{B^{2}A}{2\mu_{0}}$ | N | Maxwell stress form |
| 2 | Hopkinson's law (magnetic circuit) | $\mathcal{F} = \Phi \mathcal{R}$ | A-t vs Wb, A-t/Wb | $\mathcal{F} = NI$ |
| 3 | Reluctance of air gap | $\mathcal{R}_{g} = \dfrac{\delta}{\mu_{0} A}$ | A-t/Wb | $\delta$ = gap length |
| 4 | Total flux | $\Phi = \dfrac{NI}{\sum \mathcal{R}}$ | Wb | Series reluctance sum |
| 5 | Vacuum holding force | $F_{v} = \Delta P \cdot A_{e}$ | N | $\Delta P$ in Pa |
| 6 | Atmospheric pressure (sea level) | $P_{atm} = 101.325$ | kPa | Standard reference |
| 7 | Theoretical max vacuum force | $F_{max} = P_{atm} \cdot A_{e}$ | N | If $P_{vacuum} \to 0$ |
| 8 | Design force with safety factor | $F_{design} = \dfrac{W(g+a)\,n}{g}$ | N | $n = 2 \text{ to } 4$ |
| 9 | Number of cups required | $N_{cups} = \dfrac{F_{design}}{F_{single\,cup}}$ | — | Round up |
| 10 | Power of vacuum pump | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta P}{\eta}$ | W | $Q$ = flow rate, $\eta$ = efficiency |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Magnetic Holding Force (Maxwell Stress)

### Statement
The attractive force between a magnetised pole-face and a ferromagnetic surface separated by a small air gap is given by the **Maxwell stress equation**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy stored in the air gap**
The magnetic energy stored in a volume $V$ of air gap is the energy density times the volume. Magnetic energy density is:

$$w = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \quad \text{[J/m}^{3}\text{]}$$

For a uniform air gap of cross-section $A$ and length $\delta$, volume $V = A\delta$:

$$W = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot A \cdot \delta$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance (virtual work principle)**
If the workpiece moves a tiny distance $d\delta$ toward the magnet, the stored energy decreases. By **conservation of energy**, the work done by the magnetic attraction force $F$ equals the change in stored energy:

$$F \cdot d\delta = -dW = -\frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot A \cdot d\delta$$

*(We assume the flux $B$ is maintained constant — the constant-flux case, which corresponds to permanent-magnet operation.)*

**Step 3 — Solve for F**

$$F = \frac{B^{2} \cdot A}{2\mu_{0}} \quad \text{(Newtons)}$$

> ✅ **Final Result:** $\boxed{F = \dfrac{B^{2}A}{2\mu_{0}}}$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark question)

**Problem:** A robotic magnetic gripper has two circular pole faces, each of diameter $60$ mm. The flux density in the air gap is **0.8 T**. Calculate the total holding force on a flat steel plate.

**Solution:**

$$A = 2 \times \pi r^{2} = 2 \times \pi (0.030)^{2} = 2 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} = 5.655 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \text{ H/m}$$

$$F = \frac{B^{2} A}{2\mu_{0}} = \frac{(0.8)^{2} \times 5.655 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F = \frac{0.64 \times 5.655 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5132 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{3.619 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5132 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F \approx 1440 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{147 \text{ kgf}}$$

> 🎯 **Engineering Insight:** A modest 60 mm × 2 pole-face gripper at 0.8 T can hold ≈ 147 kg — more than enough for a 50 kg steel panel. This is why magnetic grippers are the standard in steel-mill robotics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Vacuum Holding Force

### Statement
The lifting force of a vacuum gripper equals the pressure differential across the sealed pad multiplied by the effective sealing area.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define gauge vacuum**
The vacuum source reduces the absolute pressure inside the cup from $P_{atm}$ to $P_{v}$. The pressure difference (driving force) is:

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{v} \quad \text{[Pa]}$$

**Step 2 — Force from pressure (Pascal's principle)**
Pressure is force per unit area. The atmosphere pushes UP on the bottom of the part, while low pressure inside the cup pushes DOWN with a much smaller force. The net upward force is:

$$F_{v} = (P_{atm} - P_{v}) \cdot A_{e} = \Delta P \cdot A_{e}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical maximum**
If the pump achieves a *perfect* vacuum ($P_{v} \to 0$):

$$F_{max} = P_{atm} \cdot A_{e} = 101{,}325 \cdot A_{e} \quad \text{[N]}$$

**Step 4 — Practical limit**
In reality, shop compressors and pumps achieve only **60–80 % vacuum** (i.e., $\Delta P \approx 60$ to 80 kPa). So the design uses:

$$F_{design} = 0.7 \, P_{atm} \cdot A_{e} \cdot \eta_{seal}$$

where $\eta_{seal}$ (0 to 1) accounts for leaks, deformation, surface roughness.

> ✅ **Final Result:** $\boxed{F_{v} = \Delta P \cdot A_{e} \cdot \eta_{seal}}$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark question)

**Problem:** A vacuum gripper has a single circular pad of diameter 100 mm. The Venturi ejector produces a vacuum of **70 kPa** (gauge). The seal efficiency is 0.85. Calculate (a) the theoretical maximum lifting force, (b) the actual lifting force, and (c) the maximum mass that can be lifted vertically at 1g.

**Solution:**

$$A_{e} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.100)^{2}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**(a) Theoretical maximum** ($\Delta P = P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa):

$$F_{max} = 101{,}325 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 795.8 \text{ N}$$

**(b) Actual force** with 70 kPa gauge vacuum and $\eta = 0.85$:

$$F_{v} = 70{,}000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.85 = 467.3 \text{ N}$$

**(c) Maximum liftable mass** at $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$m = \frac{F_{v}}{g} = \frac{467.3}{9.81} \approx 47.6 \text{ kg}$$

> 🎯 **Engineering Insight:** A 10 cm-diameter vacuum pad can safely lift about 47 kg of a smooth glass sheet — a remarkable figure for a "non-prehensile" device that touches the part from one side only.

---

## 3.3 Selection Trade-off Matrix

| Selection Criterion | Magnetic Gripper | Vacuum Gripper |
|---|---|---|
| Heavy ferromagnetic parts (steel) | ✅ **Best** | ❌ Poor |
| Flat non-metallic sheets (glass, PCB) | ❌ Cannot hold | ✅ **Best** |
| Porous surfaces (cardboard, foam) | ⚠ Tolerates | ❌ Leaks |
| Hot parts (forging, casting) | ✅ Heat tolerant | ❌ Rubber degrades |
| Cleanroom / silicon wafer | ⚠ Particle from EM coil | ✅ Bernoulli gripper ideal |
| Quick release required | ⚠ Needs pulse / stripper | ✅ Instant vent |
| Power-loss safety | ❌ EM drops, EPM holds | ⚠ Gradual loss |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Magnetic Gripper System (Electro-Permanent Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Wrist / Tool Flange] --> B[Gripper Housing - Low-Carbon Steel]
    B --> C1[NdFeB Permanent Magnet Core]
    B --> C2[Reversible Permanent Magnet Disc]
    C1 --> D1[North Pole Face 60mm dia]
    C2 --> D2[South Pole Face 60mm dia]
    D1 --> E1[Air Gap delta = 0.5 to 2 mm]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F[Steel Workpiece]
    B --> G[Reversing Coil - 24V DC, 20ms pulse]
    G --> H[Pulse Control Unit - PLC / Robot I/O]
    H --> I1[HOLD: Pulse 1 polarity - magnetic circuit CLOSED through part]
    H --> I2[RELEASE: Pulse 2 opposite polarity - magnetic circuit SHORTED inside gripper]
    F --> J[Force Sensor - Optional for confirmation]
    J --> K[Robot Controller Feedback]
```

**Operating Logic (mapped to physical reality):**
- The **NdFeB magnet** provides constant flux. The **reversible disc** acts as a "magnetic valve".
- In **HOLD mode**, the disc is polarised so flux is forced to *exit through the pole faces, across the air gap, into the steel workpiece, and back* — completing the magnetic circuit through the part. Force is maximised.
- In **RELEASE mode**, a short reverse-polarity pulse flips the disc so flux circulates *inside* the gripper body, never reaching the workpiece — the part drops free.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Vacuum Gripper System (Venturi-Ejector Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Wrist Mounting Plate] --> B[Vacuum Manifold - Aluminium]
    B --> C1[Suction Cup 1 - Silicone 50mm]
    B --> C2[Suction Cup 2 - Silicone 50mm]
    B --> C3[Suction Cup 3 - Silicone 50mm]
    B --> C4[Suction Cup 4 - Silicone 50mm]
    C1 --> D1[Check Valve - prevents backflow]
    C2 --> D2[Check Valve]
    C3 --> D3[Check Valve]
    C4 --> D4[Check Valve]
    D1 --> E[Common Vacuum Line]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Vacuum Switch / Pressure Sensor - set to -60 kPa]
    F --> G[Venturi Ejector - 6 bar shop air input]
    G --> H[Compressed Air Supply 4 to 7 bar]
    E --> I[Solenoid Valve - 3/2 way NC]
    I --> J[Vent to Atmosphere for Release]
    F --> K[PLC / Robot I/O]
    K --> L1[PART PRESENT confirmation signal]
    K --> L2[VACUUM OK signal to robot motion interlock]
```

**Operating Logic (mapped to physical reality):**
- Compressed air (6 bar) drives the **Venturi ejector**, which evacuates air from the manifold and cups through a restriction (the *vena contracta*), creating negative pressure.
- The **vacuum switch** sends a binary signal to the robot: "vacuum above 60 kPa → part is gripped" or "vacuum dropped → part lost".
- For release, the **3/2 solenoid** opens a path to atmosphere, equalising pressure across the cup, and the part falls.

---

## 4.3 Selection Flow-Chart for KTU Design Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: New Gripper Selection] --> Q1{Workpiece Material?}
    Q1 -->|Ferromagnetic Steel Iron| Q2{Is the part HOT greater than 200 deg C?}
    Q1 -->|Non-magnetic Aluminum Plastic Glass| Q3{Flat non-porous surface?}
    Q1 -->|Cardboard Wood| Q4[Use Mechanical / Needle Gripper]
    Q2 -->|Yes| M1[Electromagnetic Gripper with water cooling]
    Q2 -->|No| M2[Electro-Permanent Magnetic Gripper]
    Q3 -->|Yes Flat| V1[Single or Multi-Cup Vacuum Gripper]
    Q3 -->|Yes Slight Curve| V2[Bellows-Type Vacuum Cup]
    Q3 -->|Yes Very Thin Film| V3[Bernoulli Non-Contact Gripper]
    Q3 -->|No Porous or Rough| V4[Use Foam-Grip Pad or Mechanical]
    M1 --> OUT1[Hold Force = B squared A over 2 mu0]
    M2 --> OUT1
    V1 --> OUT2[Hold Force = Delta P times A eta]
    V2 --> OUT2
    V3 --> OUT2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between permanent-magnet, electromagnetic, and electro-permanent magnetic grippers. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Holding Principle | Power for Holding | Power for Release | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Flux from hard magnetic material (AlNiCo, NdFeB) | None (always on) | Mechanical slider / push-off pin | Manual magnetic chucks on milling machines, scrap-lifting cranes |
| **Electromagnetic (EM)** | Flux from DC current in wound coil | Continuous DC current required | Reverse-current demagnetising pulse | Steel-mill billet handling, stamping-press feeding |
| **Electro-Permanent (EPM)** | Flux from NdFeB + reversible disc | **Zero power** (passive hold) | 20 ms reverse-polarity current pulse | Automotive body-panel handling, collaborative robots (cobots) |

**Key advantage of EPM:** Safe hold during power failure — critical in modern Industry 4.0 cells.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**With a neat sketch, explain the working of a Venturi-ejector-based vacuum gripper. State the formula for holding force.**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** A **Venturi ejector** consists of a converging nozzle, a throat, and a diverging section. Compressed air (4–7 bar) is forced through the nozzle. As the air accelerates through the narrow throat, its velocity rises and its static pressure drops below atmospheric (Bernoulli's principle). This low-pressure region is connected via tubing to the suction cup placed on the workpiece.

**Working:**
1. Compressed air enters the ejector.
2. Air rushes through the throat, creating a low-pressure zone.
3. Atmospheric pressure on the *underside* of the workpiece pushes the part up against the cup, sealing it.
4. Vacuum is sustained as long as compressed-air supply is ON.
5. To release, a solenoid valve vents the cup to atmosphere.

**Holding force formula:**

$$F_{v} = \Delta P \cdot A_{e} \cdot \eta_{seal}$$

where $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vacuum}$, $A_e$ is the effective cup area, and $\eta_{seal}$ is the sealing efficiency factor (0.7 to 0.95 for clean smooth surfaces).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

---

### **🔵 Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**A robotic work cell is required to lift a flat steel plate of mass 25 kg from a horizontal conveyor. The robot accelerates at 1.5 m/s² during transfer. Design a suitable magnetic gripper with two identical circular pole faces. Assume flux density $B = 0.75$ T, single air gap $\delta = 1$ mm, and a safety factor of 2.5.**

#### **Part (a) — Calculate the required pole-face area and diameter. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Design force (including acceleration and safety factor):**

$$F_{design} = \frac{W(g+a)\,n}{g} = \frac{(25)(9.81+1.5)(2.5)}{9.81}$$

$$F_{design} = \frac{25 \times 11.31 \times 2.5}{9.81} = \frac{706.9}{9.81} = 72.06 \times 9.81 = 707.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — From Maxwell stress, solve for total area $A$:**

$$F = \frac{B^{2} A}{2\mu_{0}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{2\mu_{0} F}{B^{2}}$$

$$A = \frac{2 \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 707.1}{(0.75)^{2}}$$

$$A = \frac{1.777 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5625} = 3.159 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Since there are TWO pole faces, area per face:**

$$A_{face} = \frac{A}{2} = 1.580 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} = 1580 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Diameter of each circular pole:**

$$A_{face} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{face}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1580}{\pi}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{2011} \approx 44.8 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{d \approx 45 \text{ mm}}$$

✅ **Answer:** Use **two pole faces of 45 mm diameter each** at $B = 0.75$ T.

#### **Part (b) — Discuss the selection of magnetic-gripper type (PM/EM/EPM) and calculate the holding force if the air gap doubles to 2 mm due to surface roughness. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Type selection:** Since the cell runs **continuously** with frequent transfers and safety during power loss is mandatory, an **electro-permanent (EPM)** gripper is selected. It holds with **zero holding power** (only a brief 20 ms release pulse), so during a power outage the steel plate is **not dropped** — a critical safety feature.

**Effect of doubled air gap (δ = 2 mm) on force:**

For a uniform air gap, the **reluctance** is $\mathcal{R}_g = \delta / (\mu_0 A)$. Doubling $\delta$ doubles $\mathcal{R}_g$. In a magnetic circuit where iron reluctance is negligible, the flux $\Phi \propto 1/\mathcal{R}_g \propto 1/\delta$. Hence:

$$\Phi_{new} = \frac{\Phi_{old}}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_{new} = \frac{B_{old}}{2} = \frac{0.75}{2} = 0.375 \text{ T}$$

Since $F \propto B^{2}$:

$$F_{new} = F_{old} \times \left(\frac{B_{new}}{B_{old}}\right)^{2} = 707.1 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} = 707.1 \times 0.25$$

$$F_{new} \approx 176.8 \text{ N}$$

✅ **Answer:** With the doubled air gap, holding force drops to **176.8 N** — which is still *above* the static weight ($25 \times 9.81 = 245$ N) only barely under dynamic conditions. **Conclusion:** Surface roughness must be controlled to ≤ 1 mm, otherwise a larger pole area or higher $B$ is required.

---

### **🟢 Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**A robotic pick-and-place system must lift a 15 kg smooth glass panel ($1.2 \text{ m} \times 0.8 \text{ m}$) from a crate. The robot accelerates at $1.0$ m/s² horizontally. Design a vacuum gripper using identical circular cups, each 80 mm in diameter. Available vacuum source: 65 kPa gauge. Seal efficiency 0.8. Safety factor 2.5.**

#### **Part (a) — Calculate the number of suction cups required. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Design force:**

$$F_{design} = \frac{W(g+a)\,n}{g} = \frac{15 \times (9.81+1.0) \times 2.5}{9.81} = \frac{15 \times 10.81 \times 2.5}{9.81}$$

$$F_{design} = \frac{405.4}{9.81} = 41.32 \times 9.81 = 405.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Effective area of one 80 mm cup:**

$$A_{cup} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^{2}}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Holding force of one cup:**

$$F_{cup} = \Delta P \cdot A_{cup} \cdot \eta_{seal} = 65{,}000 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.8$$

$$F_{cup} = 261.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Number of cups required:**

$$N = \frac{F_{design}}{F_{cup}} = \frac{405.4}{261.4} = 1.55 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{N = 2 \text{ cups (round up)}}$$

✅ **Answer:** **2 suction cups** are sufficient. With 2 cups, total holding force = $2 \times 261.4 = 522.8$ N ≥ 405.4 N ✓

**Practical layout:** Place the 2 cups symmetrically along the 1.2 m edge, separated by ≈ 800 mm, so the panel does not tilt.

#### **Part (b) — If the glass surface is oily, predict the consequences and suggest two design modifications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Consequence of oily surface:**
The seal between the elastomer cup and the glass is compromised. Oil fills the micro-gaps and provides a leakage path for air, rapidly destroying the vacuum. The seal efficiency $\eta_{seal}$ can drop from 0.8 to as low as 0.2 or 0.3, reducing the per-cup force to:

$$F_{cup}^{oily} = 65{,}000 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.25 = 81.7 \text{ N}$$

Then $2$ cups give only $163.4$ N, **far less** than the required $405.4$ N — the glass would slip.

**Two design modifications:**

1. **Add a third (and fourth) cup** to provide redundancy. With 4 cups: $4 \times 81.7 = 326.8$ N — still marginal. With 6 cups spaced across the panel: $6 \times 81.7 = 490$ N ≥ 405.4 N ✓.

2. **Use bellows-type cups with internal ribs and a higher durometer silicone compound** (e.g., 60 Shore A), which can plough through the oil film and seal on the micro-roughness of the glass. Also **install an in-line air-blow jet** that briefly blasts the surface with 4 bar air just before cup contact, removing the oil film.

3. **(Bonus)** Add a **vacuum reservoir tank** between the ejector and the cups — this buffers leakage and maintains holding force for several seconds even if the seal is imperfect.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most-commonly LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** in design force ($F_{design} = W(g+a)n/g$). Many students calculate only $W$ or $W(g+a)/g$ and lose **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Using $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ in wrong units** (e.g., missing the $10^{-7}$). A numerical slip of 7 orders of magnitude is a common error.
> 3. **Mixing $P_{atm}$ and gauge vacuum.** Always use the *gauge* (differential) value $\Delta P$, not the absolute $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, unless you are computing the *theoretical* maximum.
> 4. **Conflating permanent-magnet and electro-permanent types** in viva — the difference is the *internal reversible disc* in EPM. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Forgetting to ROUND UP** the number of vacuum cups. You cannot have 1.55 cups — always round to the next integer.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Magnetic gripper** uses **Maxwell stress**: $F = B^{2}A/(2\mu_{0})$ — force ∝ **B²** (square law).
- [x] **Vacuum gripper** uses **pressure differential**: $F_v = \Delta P \cdot A_e \cdot \eta_{seal}$ — force ∝ **area**.
- [x] **Three magnetic types**: PM, EM, EPM — EPM is safest (holds with zero power).
- [x] **Vacuum generation methods**: Venturi ejector (most common), electric pump, Bernoulli non-contact.
- [x] **Holding-force design equation** (always include dynamics + safety): $F_{design} = W(g+a)n/g$.
- [x] **Atmospheric pressure** at sea level: **101.325 kPa** (≈ 10 tonnes per m²).
- [x] **Typical industrial vacuum level**: 60–80 kPa gauge (40–80% vacuum).
- [x] **Number of cups** must always be rounded **UP** to the next integer.
- [x] **Magnetic grippers** = best for **ferromagnetic, hot, rough, oily** parts.
- [x] **Vacuum grippers** = best for **flat, smooth, non-porous, non-magnetic** sheets.
- [x] **Safety pitfall**: EM gripper **drops the part** on power loss; EPM and vacuum with reservoir do not.
- [x] **Hopkinson's law** ($\mathcal{F} = \Phi\mathcal{R}$) is the magnetic analogue of Ohm's law — common viva question.
- [x] **Air-gap effect**: Doubling δ → halves B → quarters the holding force (since F ∝ B²).

> 🏁 **End of Topic — Magnetic and Vacuum Grippers | PCRAT303 Module 1 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active and Passive Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Active and Passive Grippers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Gripper?

In the context of industrial robotics and the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *Robotic Manipulators and Work Cell Design*, a **gripper** (also called a **mechanical end-effector** or **end-of-arm tooling, EOAT**) is the device attached to the wrist or tool flange of a robotic manipulator whose primary function is to **grasp, hold, manipulate, and release** a workpiece during a manufacturing or assembly operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCRAT303, Module 1):**
> *"Grippers are the terminal elements of a robot manipulator that establish physical contact with the workpiece. They are broadly classified as **Active Grippers** (where an external energy source or actuator actively drives the gripping action) and **Passive Grippers** (where the gripping action is achieved intrinsically by the geometry, weight, or material properties of the gripper/workpiece system without dedicated actuation)."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hand" of the Robot

Imagine giving a task to a person: **"Pick up that glass of water and place it on the table."** Your brain (the controller) commands your hand (the end-effector) to perform the action. Now consider how your hand actually grips:

- **Active gripping** is what your fingers do when you actively close around an object — you actively squeeze, you control the force, you can adjust. This requires *energy expenditure from your muscles* (the actuator).
- **Passive gripping** is what happens when you slide a heavy book across a table simply by trapping it between your forearm and the table edge, or when you pour rice into a balloon and squeeze — the *shape itself* does the gripping. No active muscle control is needed at the gripping interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Parallel:** A **bench vice** used in a workshop is an *active gripper* (you turn the handle, applying active force via a screw mechanism). A **clothespin hanging on a wire** is a *passive gripper* (gravity and geometry create the holding force with no external actuator).

## 1.3 Formal Classification

### A. Active Grippers (Powered / Externally Actuated)

These require an **external energy source** — electrical, pneumatic, or hydraulic — to generate and sustain the gripping force. The robot controller actively commands the grip force and timing.

**Sub-classes of Active Grippers:**

| Sub-class | Driving Principle | Typical Industry Use |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Mechanical (Jaw) Gripper** | Linkage / cam mechanism actuated by pneumatic or servo motor | General pick-and-place |
| **Vacuum Gripper** | Pressure differential (atmospheric vs. suction) | Sheet metal, glass, PCB |
| **Magnetic Gripper** | Electromagnet or permanent magnet flux | Ferrous parts in CNC tending |
| **Pneumatic Finger Gripper** | Compressed air through piston | High-speed assembly lines |
| **Hydraulic Gripper** | Hydraulic fluid pressure | Heavy payload handling |
| **Servo-Electric Gripper** | Electric motor + gear train | Precision assembly, force feedback |

### B. Passive Grippers (Non-Powered / Compliant / Mechanical-Form-Based)

These use **no external actuator at the gripper interface**. The grip is achieved through:
1. **Form closure** (geometric interlocking — hooks, scoops, cavities)
2. **Force closure** (weight of the object creates the holding force, like a swing carrying a load)
3. **Material compliance** (soft materials that conform and create friction)
4. **Granular jamming** (vacuum-packed granular media solidifies around an object)

**Sub-classes of Passive Grippers:**

| Sub-class | Mechanism | Typical Industry Use |
|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Jamming Gripper** | Granular media stiffens under vacuum | Universal grasping of varied shapes |
| **Capillary / Surface Tension Gripper** | Liquid surface tension adhesion | Micro-scale part handling |
| **Mechanical Hook / Clevis** | Gravity-driven hooking | Hanging parts, conveyor transfer |
| **Cavity / Scoop Gripper** | Part nests into a molded pocket | Pre-oriented parts on a feeder |
| **Needle / Spine Gripper** | Array of thin needles penetrates foam-like material | Unstructured soft object grasping |

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Always Quoted in KTU Answers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any KTU question on gripper selection requires you to state these **standard design metrics**, usually in **bold** as **key performance indicators**:
> - **Payload Capacity (W):** Maximum mass the gripper can reliably hold, in **kg** or **N**.
> - **Gripping Force (F):** Total clamping force applied by the jaws, in **N**.
> - **Safety Factor (SF):** Typically **2 to 10** for industrial grippers; chosen based on inertia, acceleration, and shock loading.
> - **Stroke / Opening Width:** Maximum jaw opening, in **mm**.
> - **Cycle Time:** Time to open/close, in **seconds**.
> - **Repeatability:** Positional accuracy of grasp, in **mm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of an Active Mechanical Gripper

The most common active gripper is the **two-finger angular (scissor) jaw gripper** driven by a pneumatic piston. Its operating logic can be broken down into five distinct steps:

1. **Actuation Trigger:** The robot controller sends a pneumatic/electrical signal (e.g., 24 V DC solenoid valve energizes, allowing 6 bar compressed air into the cylinder).
2. **Energy Conversion:** Compressed air pushes the piston; the piston converts pneumatic pressure into linear force $F_p = P \cdot A_p$, where $A_p$ is the piston cross-sectional area.
3. **Linkage Transmission:** The linear piston motion is converted to angular jaw motion via a **four-bar linkage, cam-follower, or rack-and-pinion** mechanism, multiplying or reducing force.
4. **Jaw Closure & Contact:** The jaws move inward until contact is made with the workpiece. The contact creates a **normal force $N$** on each jaw.
5. **Friction-Based Holding:** The friction force $\mu N$ (where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction between jaw pad and workpiece) balances the workpiece weight and any inertial loads during motion.

> **Why this matters (KTU Cognitive Level — Understand):** A robot moving with acceleration $a$ imparts an inertial force $m \cdot a$ on the workpiece. The gripper must overcome **both** static weight $W = mg$ **and** the dynamic inertial load. This is why the *safety factor* is non-negotiable in gripper design.

## 2.2 Static Force Analysis on a Two-Jaw Gripper (The Core KTU Derivation)

Consider a workpiece of weight **W** held vertically between two symmetric jaws. Each jaw exerts a normal force $N$ on the workpiece, and friction coefficient between jaw and workpiece is $\mu$.

### Free Body Diagram Reasoning:

- The workpiece is held by friction on **two** contact surfaces (left jaw + right jaw).
- Each surface contributes $\mu N$ of upward friction force.
- For static equilibrium (no slipping, no acceleration):

$$\text{Total upward friction} = \text{Weight of workpiece} + \text{Inertial load}$$

$$2 \mu N = W + m a$$

Solving for the required **normal force per jaw**:

$$N = \frac{W + m a}{2 \mu}$$

Including a **safety factor** $SF$:

$$N_{\text{design}} = SF \cdot \frac{W + m a}{2 \mu}$$

> **Engineering Utility:** This equation is used by every mechanical design engineer when sizing a pneumatic cylinder or servo motor for a gripper. Underestimating $N$ causes the part to slip; overestimating causes the part to be crushed or the jaws to deform.

## 2.3 Vacuum Gripper Operating Principle

A vacuum gripper uses **atmospheric pressure differential** to lift flat or near-flat objects. The simplified lifting force equation is:

$$F_{\text{lift}} = (P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vacuum}}) \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot n$$

Where:
- $F_{\text{lift}}$ = total lifting force available, in **N**
- $P_{\text{atm}}$ = atmospheric pressure ≈ **101.325 kPa** (≈ 1.01325 × 10⁵ N/m²)
- $P_{\text{vacuum}}$ = absolute pressure inside suction cup, in **kPa**
- $A_{\text{eff}}$ = effective contact area of suction cup, in **m²**
- $n$ = number of suction cups

A simplified practical form (ignoring leakage and using gauge vacuum $-P_v$ in kPa):

$$F_{\text{lift}} \approx P_v \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot n \cdot 1000 \quad \text{(N, if } A_{\text{eff}} \text{ in m}^2\text{)}$$

## 2.4 Magnetic Gripper Holding Force

For an electromagnet gripping a flat ferrous plate:

$$F_{\text{hold}} = \frac{B^2 \cdot A}{2 \mu_0}$$

Where:
- $B$ = magnetic flux density at the interface, in **Tesla**
- $A$ = contact area, in **m²**
- $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space = **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**

In engineering practice, magnetic gripper lift is conservatively given as:

$$F_{\text{hold}} \approx k \cdot \frac{N I}{g} \quad \text{(manufacturer-empirical form)}$$

where $N I$ is the ampere-turns and $g$ is the air gap.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the "must-memorize" formula bank for any Module 1 KTU question on grippers. Memorize the variables and units, not the LaTeX.**

| # | Formula | Variable Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{W + ma}{2\mu}$ | $N$ = jaw normal force; $W = mg$; $\mu$ = friction coeff. | Sizing jaw gripper actuator |
| 2 | $N_{\text{design}} = SF \cdot \dfrac{W + ma}{2\mu}$ | $SF$ = safety factor (2–10 typical) | Design-grade jaw force |
| 3 | $F_p = P \cdot A_p$ | $F_p$ = piston thrust; $P$ = air pressure; $A_p$ = piston area | Pneumatic cylinder sizing |
| 4 | $F_{\text{lift}} = \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot n$ | $\Delta P$ = pressure diff.; $A$ = cup area; $n$ = cup count | Vacuum gripper payload |
| 5 | $F_{\text{hold}} = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | $B$ = flux density; $A$ = area; $\mu_0$ = 4π×10⁻⁷ | Magnetic gripper force |
| 6 | $T_{\text{motor}} = F_{\text{jaw}} \cdot r \cdot \eta^{-1}$ | $T$ = torque; $r$ = linkage radius; $\eta$ = mech. efficiency | Servo gripper motor sizing |
| 7 | $V_{\text{jam}} = V_0 (1 - \varepsilon)$ | $V$ = jammed volume; $\varepsilon$ = bulk strain | Granular jamming gripper |
| 8 | $F_{\text{cap}} = \gamma \cdot L \cdot \cos\theta$ | $\gamma$ = surface tension; $L$ = contact length | Capillary gripper force |

> **Engineering Utility Across Industries:** These formulas appear in the design of automotive assembly robots (welding, painting, body-panel transfer), pharmaceutical pick-and-place lines, semiconductor wafer handling (vacuum), and warehouse automation (Amazon-style tote grippers).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Two-Jaw Gripper Holding Force with Inertia

> **KTU Cognitive Level:** Apply / Analyze
> **Typical Marks Allocated:** 7 to 14 marks in Part B questions

### Problem Setup

A robotic gripper with two parallel jaws is required to lift a steel block of mass **m = 5 kg** vertically with an upward acceleration of **a = 2 m/s²**. The coefficient of friction between the rubber jaw pads and the steel is **μ = 0.6**. A safety factor of **SF = 3** is mandated by the workshop safety code.

**Determine:**
(a) The minimum normal force required per jaw.
(b) The required piston thrust if the mechanism transmission ratio is **M = 1.5** (jaw force = M × piston force) and air pressure is **P = 5 bar**.
(c) The required piston diameter.

### Step 1 — Identify the Given Quantities

We list all given values in SI units (this is a critical KTU valuation habit):

- Mass of workpiece: $m = 5 \text{ kg}$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Vertical acceleration: $a = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.6$
- Safety factor: $SF = 3$
- Mechanical advantage (transmission ratio): $M = 1.5$
- Air supply pressure: $P = 5 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$

### Step 2 — Compute the Weight of the Workpiece

Using the standard weight relation:

$$W = m \cdot g$$

Substituting values:

$$W = 5 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 49.05 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Inertial Load

When the robot accelerates upward at $a$, the workpiece experiences an additional inertial reaction downward equal to $ma$:

$$F_{\text{inertia}} = m \cdot a = 5 \text{ kg} \times 2 \text{ m/s}^2 = 10 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Equilibrium of the Workpiece

The total downward load the gripper friction must counteract is:

$$F_{\text{total}} = W + m \cdot a = 49.05 \text{ N} + 10 \text{ N} = 59.05 \text{ N}$$

Since friction acts at **two** symmetric jaw surfaces, each contributing $\mu N$, the static equilibrium gives:

$$2 \mu N = F_{\text{total}} = 59.05 \text{ N}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Bare Normal Force per Jaw

$$N = \frac{F_{\text{total}}}{2 \mu} = \frac{59.05}{2 \times 0.6} = \frac{59.05}{1.2}$$

$$N = 49.208 \text{ N}$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Safety Factor

Including the required safety factor $SF = 3$:

$$N_{\text{design}} = SF \times N = 3 \times 49.208$$

$$N_{\text{design}} = 147.625 \text{ N} \approx 147.63 \text{ N}$$

> **Part (a) Answer:** Each jaw must apply a normal force of at least **147.63 N** to safely hold and accelerate the workpiece.

### Step 7 — Compute the Required Piston Thrust (Part b)

Given the transmission ratio $M = 1.5$ (jaw force is 1.5 times piston force):

$$F_{\text{jaw}} = M \times F_{\text{piston}}$$

Rearranging for piston thrust:

$$F_{\text{piston}} = \frac{N_{\text{design}}}{M} = \frac{147.63}{1.5}$$

$$F_{\text{piston}} = 98.42 \text{ N}$$

> **Part (b) Answer:** The pneumatic cylinder must deliver at least **98.42 N** of thrust.

### Step 8 — Compute the Required Piston Diameter (Part c)

The piston thrust equation is:

$$F_{\text{piston}} = P \times A_p$$

Solving for the cross-sectional area:

$$A_p = \frac{F_{\text{piston}}}{P} = \frac{98.42 \text{ N}}{5 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2}$$

$$A_p = 1.9684 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 196.84 \text{ mm}^2$$

Since the piston area is $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$, solving for diameter $D$:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_p}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 196.84}{\pi}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{250.62} = 15.83 \text{ mm}$$

> **Part (c) Answer:** The minimum piston diameter required is **≈ 15.83 mm**. In practice, a standard **16 mm** or **20 mm** bore cylinder would be selected.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Vacuum Gripper Payload

### Problem Setup

A vacuum gripper uses **two suction cups**, each of effective diameter **d = 60 mm**, to lift a glass sheet. A vacuum pump achieves a gauge vacuum of **-70 kPa**. The glass mass is **m = 8 kg**.

**Determine:** Whether the gripper can safely lift the glass with a safety factor of **2.5**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Effective Area of Each Cup

$$A_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.060)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Available Lifting Force

Using the simplified vacuum lift equation:

$$F_{\text{lift}} = P_v \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot n$$

Substituting values:

$$F_{\text{lift}} = (70 \times 10^3) \times (2.827 \times 10^{-3}) \times 2$$

$$F_{\text{lift}} = 70{,}000 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times 2$$

$$F_{\text{lift}} = 395.78 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Required Lift Force

$$F_{\text{required}} = SF \cdot m \cdot g = 2.5 \times 8 \times 9.81 = 196.2 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4 — Compare

Since $F_{\text{lift}} = 395.78 \text{ N} > F_{\text{required}} = 196.2 \text{ N}$, the gripper can safely lift the glass with a margin of $\frac{395.78}{196.2} \approx 2.02\times$ above the minimum requirement.

> **Conclusion:** The vacuum gripper is **suitable**. The actual safety factor achieved is $\approx 5.05$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Gripper System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interaction architecture** of an active jaw gripper, showing how the controller, actuator, transmission, and workpiece interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Controller CPU] -->|Digital Command 24V DC| B[Solenoid Valve]
    B -->|Air ON OFF| C[Compressed Air Supply 6 bar]
    C -->|Pressurized Air| D[Pneumatic Cylinder Piston]
    D -->|Linear Thrust Fp| E[Four Bar Linkage Mechanism]
    E -->|Angular Motion| F[Left Jaw with Rubber Pad]
    E -->|Angular Motion Mirrored| G[Right Jaw with Rubber Pad]
    F -->|Normal Force N| H[Workpiece]
    G -->|Normal Force N| H[Workpiece]
    H -->|Friction mu N Holds Load| I[Payload Lifted Successfully]
    J[Position Sensor on Jaw] -->|Feedback| A
    K[Pressure Sensor in Cylinder] -->|Feedback| A
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Vacuum Gripper Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vacuum Pump] -->|Suction Line| B[Vacuum Reservoir Tank]
    B -->|Hose| C[Suction Cup 1]
    B -->|Hose| D[Suction Cup 2]
    C -->|Contact Seal| E[Workpiece Surface]
    D -->|Contact Seal| E
    E -->|Atmospheric Pressure Pushes Up| F[Lifting Force FL]
    G[Vacuum Gauge] -->|Pressure Feedback| A
    H[Robot Controller] -->|Pump ON OFF Signal| A
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparison Architecture: Active vs. Passive Gripper Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gripper Selection Required] --> B{Does Application Allow External Power and Actuation?}
    B -->|YES| C[Choose ACTIVE Gripper]
    B -->|NO Limited Power OR Unstructured Objects| D[Choose PASSIVE Gripper]
    C --> C1[Mechanical Jaw Gripper for Rigid Parts]
    C --> C2[Vacuum Gripper for Flat Smooth Surfaces]
    C --> C3[Magnetic Gripper for Ferrous Parts]
    D --> D1[Jamming Gripper for Universal Grasping]
    D --> D2[Capillary Gripper for Micro Parts]
    D --> D3[Hook or Cavity Gripper for Known Geometry]
    C1 --> E[Outputs Controlled Force Programmable Cycle Time]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> F[Outputs Adaptive Compliance Energy Free]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Passive Jamming Gripper Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Approaches Object] --> B[Jamming Gripper Lowered Over Object]
    B --> C[Membrane Encloses Object Soft Contact]
    C --> D[Vacuum Pump Activated Air Evacuated]
    D --> E[Granular Media Inside Membrane Contracts]
    E --> F[Granular Media Solidifies Conforms Tightly to Object Shape]
    F --> G[Grip is Achieved without Active Clamping Force]
    G --> H[Robot Lifts and Transports Object]
    H --> I[Vacuum Released at Destination]
    I --> J[Membrane Relaxes Releases Object]
```

## 4.5 Concept Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Active Gripper | Passive Gripper |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| External Power | Required (electrical/pneumatic/hydraulic) | Not required |
| Control of Grip Force | Programmable, precise | Determined by physics (gravity, vacuum, surface tension) |
| Adaptability to Object Shape | Limited (custom fingers per part) | High (jamming/conforming) |
| Cycle Time | Fast (milliseconds to seconds) | Slower (vacuum pump cycle) |
| Energy Consumption | Continuous during hold | Only during engagement/release |
| Typical Payload Range | 0.1 kg to >1000 kg | 0.001 kg to ~5 kg (research-grade to light) |
| Cost | Moderate to high (actuators, valves, sensors) | Low to moderate (membrane, granular media) |
| Failure Mode | Power loss → drop object (risk!) | Power loss → may maintain grip (safe) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions test **Remember** and **Understand** cognitive levels (Bloom's Levels 1 & 2). Keep answers to **3 to 4 crisp sentences** with one defining formula.

### Question A1: Define an Active Gripper with Two Examples

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

An **active gripper** is an end-effector that uses an external energy source — such as compressed air, hydraulic fluid, or an electric motor — to actively drive the gripping action and apply a controlled clamping force on the workpiece. The grip force, open/close timing, and jaw position are all programmable via the robot controller.

**Examples:**
1. **Pneumatic two-jaw gripper** (commonly used in pick-and-place of cylindrical and box-shaped parts in automotive assembly).
2. **Servo-electric parallel gripper** (used in precision electronics assembly where force feedback and stroke control are critical).

---

### Question A2: Differentiate Between Form Closure and Force Closure in Passive Gripping

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Form closure** is achieved purely by the **geometry** of the gripper and the object — the object's shape locks it in place without any force being applied, like a hook holding a ring or a cavity nesting a matching part.

**Force closure**, in contrast, requires an external force (typically gravity or a clamping load) to maintain the grip; the object is held because the applied force creates reaction forces that prevent escape. A block resting inside a U-shaped bracket is held by form closure if its width exceeds the bracket opening; the same block resting on a flat plate is held by force closure (friction force).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part B in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) gives an **Internal Choice**. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Working Principle of a Pneumatic Two-Jaw Angular Gripper with a Neat Sketch. List Four Key Design Specifications.

**Model Solution:**
