                     & = c_\alpha s_\beta c_\gamma + s_\alpha s_\gamma \\
\text{Row 2, Col 1: } & s_\alpha c_\beta + c_\alpha \cdot 0 + 0\cdot(-s_\beta) = s_\alpha c_\beta \\
\text{Row 2, Col 2: } & s_\alpha s_\beta s_\gamma + c_\alpha c_\gamma + 0 = s_\alpha s_\beta s_\gamma + c_\alpha c_\gamma \\
\text{Row 2, Col 3: } & s_\alpha s_\beta c_\gamma + c_\alpha(-s_\gamma) + 0 = s_\alpha s_\beta c_\gamma - c_\alpha s_\gamma \\
\text{Row 3, Col 1: } & 0 \cdot c_\beta + 0 \cdot 0 + 1\cdot(-s_\beta) = -s_\beta \\
\text{Row 3, Col 2: } & 0 \cdot s_\beta s_\gamma + 0 \cdot c_\gamma + 1 \cdot c_\beta s_\gamma = c_\beta s_\gamma \\
\text{Row 3, Col 3: } & 0 \cdot s_\beta c_\gamma + 0 \cdot (-s_\gamma) + 1 \cdot c_\beta c_\gamma = c_\beta c_\gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: The Final RPY Rotation Matrix

$$
R_{RPY}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma) = \begin{bmatrix} c_\alpha c_\beta & c_\alpha s_\beta s_\gamma - s_\alpha c_\gamma & c_\alpha s_\beta c_\gamma + s_\alpha s_\gamma \\ s_\alpha c_\beta & s_\alpha s_\beta s_\gamma + c_\alpha c_\gamma & s_\alpha s_\beta c_\gamma - c_\alpha s_\gamma \\ -s_\beta & c_\beta s_\gamma & c_\beta c_\gamma \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Step (always do this in KTU exams):** Confirm orthonormality by checking $R^T R = I$ and $\det(R) = +1$. You should find $\det(R_{RPY}) = c_\beta^2(c_\alpha^2 + s_\alpha^2)(c_\gamma^2 + s_\gamma^2) + s_\beta^2 = 1$ after simplification.

### 3.3 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A robot end-effector has RPY angles $\alpha = 30°$, $\beta = 45°$, $\gamma = 60°$. Compute the RPY rotation matrix.

**Step 1 — Convert and tabulate trigonometric values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha = 30° &\Rightarrow c_\alpha = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866,\quad s_\alpha = \tfrac{1}{2} = 0.500 \\
\beta = 45° &\Rightarrow c_\beta = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.707,\quad s_\beta = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.707 \\
\gamma = 60° &\Rightarrow c_\gamma = \tfrac{1}{2} = 0.500,\quad s_\gamma = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the composite matrix formula:**

Element $(1,1)$: $c_\alpha c_\beta = 0.866 \times 0.707 = 0.6124$

Element $(1,2)$: $c_\alpha s_\beta s_\gamma - s_\alpha c_\gamma = (0.866)(0.707)(0.866) - (0.500)(0.500) = 0.5303 - 0.2500 = 0.2803$

Element $(1,3)$: $c_\alpha s_\beta c_\gamma + s_\alpha s_\gamma = (0.866)(0.707)(0.500) + (0.500)(0.866) = 0.3062 + 0.4330 = 0.7392$

Element $(2,1)$: $s_\alpha c_\beta = 0.500 \times 0.707 = 0.3535$

Element $(2,2)$: $s_\alpha s_\beta s_\gamma + c_\alpha c_\gamma = (0.500)(0.707)(0.866) + (0.866)(0.500) = 0.3062 + 0.4330 = 0.7392$

Element $(2,3)$: $s_\alpha s_\beta c_\gamma - c_\alpha s_\gamma = (0.500)(0.707)(0.500) - (0.866)(0.866) = 0.1768 - 0.7500 = -0.5732$

Element $(3,1)$: $-s_\beta = -0.707$

Element $(3,2)$: $c_\beta s_\gamma = 0.707 \times 0.866 = 0.6124$

Element $(3,3)$: $c_\beta c_\gamma = 0.707 \times 0.500 = 0.3535$

**Final numerical RPY rotation matrix:**

$$
R_{RPY} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6124 & 0.2803 & 0.7392 \\ 0.3535 & 0.7392 & -0.5732 \\ -0.7071 & 0.6124 & 0.3535 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Validity Check:** Compute $\det(R) = 0.6124(0.7392 \cdot 0.3535 - (-0.5732) \cdot 0.6124) - 0.2803(\ldots) + 0.7392(\ldots) \approx 1.000$. The determinant equalling +1 confirms a valid rotation matrix.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram: RPY Rotation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Base Frame F0] --> B[Step 1: Apply Roll about X axis by gamma]
    B --> C[Step 2: Apply Pitch about Y axis by beta]
    C --> D[Step 3: Apply Yaw about Z axis by alpha]
    D --> E[Composite R RPY = Rz times Ry times Rx]
    E --> F[Step 4: Validate Orthonormality R transpose R equals Identity]
    F --> G[Step 5: Validate Determinant det R equals plus 1]
    G --> H[End: Final Orientation of End Effector Frame]
    H --> I{Is beta equals plus minus 90 degrees}
    I -- No --> J[Unique RPY Solution Available]
    I -- Yes --> K[Singularity Gimbal Lock: Infinite Solutions]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: RPY-Based Robot Controller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InputA[Desired Roll gamma] --> BlockA[Rx gamma Matrix Generator]
    InputB[Desired Pitch beta] --> BlockB[Ry beta Matrix Generator]
    InputC[Desired Yaw alpha] --> BlockC[Rz alpha Matrix Generator]
    BlockA --> Mult
    BlockB --> Mult
    BlockC --> Mult
    Mult[Matrix Multiplication Unit: R equals Rz times Ry times Rx] --> OutR[Output 3x3 Rotation Matrix]
    OutR --> Validator[Validator: Orthonormality and det check]
    Validator --> Encoder[Joint Encoder Command Generator]
    Encoder --> Robot[6 DOF Industrial Manipulator]
    Robot --> Sensor[Feedback: Actual Joint Angles]
    Sensor --> InverseKin[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    InverseKin --> Mult
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map: RPY Within the Family of Rotation Representations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Rotation Representations in 3D Robotics]
    Root --> A1[3 Parameter Minimal Representations]
    Root --> A2[4 Parameter Redundant Representations]
    Root --> A3[9 Parameter Full Rotation Matrix]
    A1 --> B1[Fixed Angles XYZ]
    A1 --> B2[Euler Angles ZYZ ZYX etc]
    A1 --> B3[Roll Pitch Yaw RPY]
    A2 --> C1[Unit Quaternions]
    A2 --> C2[Angle Axis]
    A2 --> C3[Exponential Coordinates so3]
    B3 --> D1[Intrinsic ZYX Convention KTU Standard]
    B3 --> D2[Singularity at beta equals 90 deg]
    B3 --> D3[Used in Aerospace and Industrial Robots]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams represent a **functional / information-flow** view, as a literal 3D wireframe of a tilted end-effector is not natively supported by Mermaid syntax. For the KTU 2024 ESE, drawing a labeled 3D reference frame showing the original $\{X, Y, Z\}$ axes and the rotated $\{X', Y', Z'\}$ axes after each RPY rotation is also expected — use dashed lines for original axes and solid lines for rotated axes, with curved arrows indicating rotation direction (right-hand rule).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Roll, Pitch, and Yaw angles. State the elementary rotation matrices for each.**

**Model Answer:**

Roll, Pitch, and Yaw (RPY) is a three-parameter minimal representation of orientation in 3D space. **Roll (γ)** is the rotation about the x-axis, **Pitch (β)** is the rotation about the y-axis, and **Yaw (α)** is the rotation about the z-axis. All rotations follow the right-hand rule.

$$
R_x(\gamma) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma \\ 0 & \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R_y(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & 0 & \sin\beta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\beta & 0 & \cos\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R_z(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha & 0 \\ \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*(3 marks: 1 for definitions, 2 for the three matrices)*

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the gimbal-lock singularity in the context of RPY angles? Under what condition does it occur?**

**Model Answer:**

Gimbal-lock singularity is a degeneracy of the RPY representation in which **two of the three rotation axes become aligned**, causing a loss of one degree of freedom. It occurs when the pitch angle $\beta = \pm 90°$, i.e., $\cos(\beta) = 0$.

At this configuration, the elements $r_{11}$, $r_{21}$, and $r_{31}$ of the rotation matrix become such that $\sqrt{r_{11}^2 + r_{21}^2} = 0$, making the extraction of $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ non-unique — infinitely many $(\alpha, \gamma)$ pairs produce the same physical orientation. This is mathematically analogous to reaching the **poles of a sphere** in spherical coordinates.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for condition, 1 for consequence on DOF)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question B1A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the composite rotation matrix $R_{RPY} = R_z(\alpha)\, R_y(\beta)\, R_x(\gamma)$ for the intrinsic Z-Y-X RPY convention. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Given the RPY angles $\alpha = 90°$, $\beta = 30°$, $\gamma = 45°$, compute the composite rotation matrix and verify its orthonormality. **\[7 Marks\]**

##### Model Solution for (a)

The composite RPY rotation matrix for the intrinsic Z-Y-X convention is:

$$
R_{RPY}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma) = R_z(\alpha) \cdot R_y(\beta) \cdot R_x(\gamma)
$$

**Step 1 — Define elementary matrices:** Using the standard forms from Section 3.1, write out $R_z(\alpha)$, $R_y(\beta)$, $R_x(\gamma)$ explicitly with the notations $c_\alpha = \cos\alpha$, $s_\alpha = \sin\alpha$, etc.

**Step 2 — First inner product $R_y(\beta) R_x(\gamma)$:** As derived in Section 3.2, Step 1:

$$
R_y(\beta) R_x(\gamma) = \begin{bmatrix} c_\beta & s_\beta s_\gamma & s_\beta c_\gamma \\ 0 & c_\gamma & -s_\gamma \\ -s_\beta & c_\beta s_\gamma & c_\beta c_\gamma \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Outer pre-multiplication by $R_z(\alpha)$:** As derived in Section 3.2, Step 2:

$$
R_{RPY} = \begin{bmatrix} c_\alpha c_\beta & c_\alpha s_\beta s_\gamma - s_\alpha c_\gamma & c_\alpha s_\beta c_\gamma + s_\alpha s_\gamma \\ s_\alpha c_\beta & s_\alpha s_\beta s_\gamma + c_\alpha c_\gamma & s_\alpha s_\beta c_\gamma - c_\alpha s_\gamma \\ -s_\beta & c_\beta s_\gamma & c_\beta c_\gamma \end{bmatrix}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for setting up the problem, 3 for the inner product derivation, 2 for the final composite matrix)*

##### Model Solution for (b)

**Trigonometric values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha = 90° &\Rightarrow c_\alpha = 0,\quad s_\alpha = 1 \\
\beta = 30° &\Rightarrow c_\beta = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660,\quad s_\beta = \tfrac{1}{2} = 0.5000 \\
\gamma = 45° &\Rightarrow c_\gamma = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071,\quad s_\gamma = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071
\end{aligned}
$$

**Element-wise computation:**

| Element | Formula | Substitution | Value |
|---------|---------|--------------|-------|
| $r_{11}$ | $c_\alpha c_\beta$ | $0 \times 0.8660$ | $0$ |
| $r_{12}$ | $c_\alpha s_\beta s_\gamma - s_\alpha c_\gamma$ | $0 - 1 \times 0.7071$ | $-0.7071$ |
| $r_{13}$ | $c_\alpha s_\beta c_\gamma + s_\alpha s_\gamma$ | $0 + 1 \times 0.7071$ | $0.7071$ |
| $r_{21}$ | $s_\alpha c_\beta$ | $1 \times 0.8660$ | $0.8660$ |
| $r_{22}$ | $s_\alpha s_\beta s_\gamma + c_\alpha c_\gamma$ | $1 \times 0.5000 \times 0.7071 + 0$ | $0.3536$ |
| $r_{23}$ | $s_\alpha s_\beta c_\gamma - c_\alpha s_\gamma$ | $1 \times 0.5000 \times 0.7071 - 0$ | $0.3536$ |
| $r_{31}$ | $-s_\beta$ | $-0.5000$ | $-0.5000$ |
| $r_{32}$ | $c_\beta s_\gamma$ | $0.8660 \times 0.7071$ | $0.6124$ |
| $r_{33}$ | $c_\beta c_\gamma$ | $0.8660 \times 0.7071$ | $0.6124$ |

$$
R_{RPY} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -0.7071 & 0.7071 \\ 0.8660 & 0.3536 & 0.3536 \\ -0.5000 & 0.6124 & 0.6124 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Orthonormality verification:** Compute $R^T R$ and check the first diagonal element:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(R^T R)_{11} &= r_{11}^2 + r_{21}^2 + r_{31}^2 = 0 + (0.8660)^2 + (-0.5000)^2 \\
             &= 0 + 0.7500 + 0.2500 = 1.0000 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly all rows have unit norm and are mutually orthogonal. $\det(R) \approx +1$. Hence $R$ is a valid rotation matrix.

*(7 marks: 2 for trig values, 3 for matrix computation, 2 for orthonormality check)*

---

#### **Question B1B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the RPY convention as used in robotics. State the difference between intrinsic and extrinsic rotation sequences. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Given the rotation matrix

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 0 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

extract the RPY angles using the standard intrinsic Z-Y-X convention. **\[7 Marks\]**

##### Model Solution for (a)

**RPY Convention:** The Roll-Pitch-Yaw convention describes the orientation of a body-fixed (tool) frame $\{B\}$ relative to a reference (base) frame $\{A\}$ via three successive rotations. Starting from frame $\{A\}$:

- **Roll (γ)**: Rotate frame by angle $\gamma$ about the X-axis of the current frame.
- **Pitch (β)**: Rotate the resulting frame by angle $\beta$ about its (now rotated) Y-axis.
- **Yaw (α)**: Rotate the resulting frame by angle $\alpha$ about its (twice-rotated) Z-axis.

**Sketch requirement (textual description for exam):**
- Draw two 3D coordinate frames: original $\{X_A, Y_A, Z_A\}$ and final $\{X_B, Y_B, Z_B\}$.
- Show three curved arrows labeled γ, β, α about the successive axes.
- Mark the right-hand rule direction (curls of fingers = rotation direction).
- Use dashed lines for the original axes and solid lines for the rotated axes.

**Intrinsic vs Extrinsic Rotations:**

| Feature | Intrinsic (Body-Fixed) | Extrinsic (Space-Fixed) |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Axis of rotation | Rotates with the body | Always the original world axis |
| Matrix composition | Pre-multiplied: $R = R_z(\alpha) R_y(\beta) R_x(\gamma)$ | Post-multiplied: $R = R_x(\gamma) R_y(\beta) R_z(\alpha)$ |
| Final orientation | Identical for the same angle set | Identical for the same angle set |
| KTU convention | **Adopted standard** | Sometimes used in aerospace |
| Alias | "Euler angles" (commonly) | "Fixed angles" (commonly) |

*(7 marks: 3 for the sketch + convention explanation, 2 for the difference table, 2 for the matrix form distinction)*

##### Model Solution for (b)

Given:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 0 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Extract Pitch (β) from element $r_{31}$:**

$$
r_{31} = -s_\beta = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sin(\beta) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \beta = 0° \text{ or } 180°
$$

To resolve, inspect $\sqrt{r_{11}^2 + r_{21}^2}$:

$$
\sqrt{r_{11}^2 + r_{21}^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.75} = \sqrt{1} = 1 \neq 0
$$

So we are not at singularity. Choose $\beta = 0°$ (since $r_{31} = 0$ and $c_\beta = 1$).

**Step 2 — Extract Yaw (α) from $r_{21}$ and $r_{11}$:**

$$
\alpha = \text{atan2}(r_{21}, r_{11}) = \text{atan2}(0.866, 0.5) = 60°
$$

**Step 3 — Extract Roll (γ) from $r_{32}$ and $r_{33}$:**

$$
\gamma = \text{atan2}(r_{32}, r_{33}) = \text{atan2}(0, 1) = 0°
$$

**Result:** $\alpha = 60°$, $\beta = 0°$, $\gamma = 0°$.

**Verification:** Reconstruct $R_{RPY}(60°, 0°, 0°)$ using the composite formula. With $c_\beta = 1$, $s_\beta = 0$, $c_\gamma = 1$, $s_\gamma = 0$:

$$
R_{RPY} = \begin{bmatrix} c_\alpha \cdot 1 & c_\alpha \cdot 0 \cdot 1 - s_\alpha \cdot 1 & c_\alpha \cdot 0 \cdot 1 + s_\alpha \cdot 0 \\ s_\alpha \cdot 1 & s_\alpha \cdot 0 \cdot 1 + c_\alpha \cdot 1 & s_\alpha \cdot 0 \cdot 1 - c_\alpha \cdot 0 \\ -0 & 1 \cdot 0 & 1 \cdot 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 0 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \checkmark
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for pitch extraction, 2 for yaw extraction, 1 for roll extraction, 2 for verification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Sign Error in $R_y(\beta)$:** Students often write $\sin(\beta)$ in position $(1,3)$ as **positive** but forget the negative sign in position $(3,1)$. A full mark is deducted if any one element is wrong.
> 2. **Order of Multiplication:** Confusing $R_z(\alpha) R_y(\beta) R_x(\gamma)$ (intrinsic) with $R_x(\gamma) R_y(\beta) R_z(\alpha)$ (extrinsic) is a 1-mark penalty even if you get individual matrices right.
> 3. **Skipping the $R^T R = I$ verification:** Examiners allocate **at least 1 mark** for this; forgetting it costs you the same.
> 4. **Using degrees vs radians inconsistently:** Once you use degrees, every subsequent computation (e.g., the $\text{atan2}$ calls) must also be in degrees. Mixing units gives nonsensical results.
> 5. **Not stating the convention:** Always write *"Using the intrinsic Z-Y-X RPY convention…"* at the start. 1 mark reserved for this declaration.
> 6. **atan2 vs atan:** Use $\text{atan2}(y, x)$ — never $\arctan(y/x)$ — to handle all four quadrants correctly. KTU examiners explicitly look for this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**
>
> - **RPY = 3 parameters** (α yaw, β pitch, γ roll) — minimal representation of orientation.
> - **KTU standard convention:** Intrinsic Z-Y-X, i.e., $R = R_z(\alpha) R_y(\beta) R_x(\gamma)$.
> - **Memory trick:** Roll = X, Pitch = Y, Yaw = Z. Intrinsic XYZ ⇒ matrix order ZYX.
> - **Memorize the three elementary matrices** (signs of $\sin$ terms are critical).
> - **Composite matrix** is a $3 \times 3$ orthonormal matrix with $\det = +1$.
> - **Extraction formulas:** $\beta = \text{atan2}(-r_{31}, \sqrt{r_{11}^2+r_{21}^2})$, $\alpha = \text{atan2}(r_{21}, r_{11})$, $\gamma = \text{atan2}(r_{32}, r_{33})$.
> - **Singularity:** $\beta = \pm 90°$ → gimbal lock → $\alpha, \gamma$ become coupled.
> - **Real-world uses:** Aircraft attitude, drone flight control, 5-axis CNC, surgical robots, ROS.
> - **Alternative representations to know:** Fixed XYZ, ZYZ Euler, quaternions, axis-angle, exponential coordinates — each has its own singularities and trade-offs.
> - **Always verify** $R^T R = I$ and $\det(R) = +1$ at the end of derivations.
> - **Declare your convention** explicitly in the first line of every answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler Angles and Quaternions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Euler Angles and Quaternions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Euler Angles — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Euler Angles** are a minimal set of **three angular parameters** $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ that uniquely describe the orientation (attitude) of a rigid body in 3-D space relative to a fixed reference frame. Each angle represents a **successive elementary rotation** performed about a specified axis of a rotating coordinate frame, and the composition of these three rotations yields the complete orientation of the body.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PCRAT303), Euler angles are introduced as the **first compact orientation representation** a manipulator student encounters, since a rotation matrix $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$ carries nine elements with six constraints, whereas three Euler angles provide a **minimal 3-parameter** description.

There are **two principal families**:
1. **Proper Euler Angles** (Symmetric Convention): The first and third rotations share the same axis. Example: **Z-X-Z**, **Z-Y-Z**, **X-Y-X**.
2. **Tait–Bryan Angles** (Asymmetric Convention): All three rotations are about *different* axes. Example: **Z-Y-X** (Yaw–Pitch–Roll), used universally in robotics, aerospace, and the Denavit–Hartenberg convention.

The standard **Z-Y-X (Yaw–Pitch–Roll)** convention — used throughout KTU robotics modules — is mathematically stated as:

$$\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}_z(\gamma) \cdot \mathbf{R}_y(\beta) \cdot \mathbf{R}_x(\alpha)$$

where $\alpha$ is the **roll** (about body $x$), $\beta$ is the **pitch** (about body $y$), and $\gamma$ is the **yaw** (about body $z$).

## 1.2 Quaternions — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Quaternion** $\mathbf{q}$ is a four-dimensional hyper-complex number, first formalised by **Sir William Rowan Hamilton in 1843**, which extends the 2-D complex plane into a non-commutative 4-D algebraic structure. It is written as $\mathbf{q} = w + x\mathbf{i} + y\mathbf{j} + z\mathbf{k}$, where $\{w, x, y, z\} \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\{\mathbf{i}, \mathbf{j}, \mathbf{k}\}$ satisfy the famous **Hamiltonian relations** $\mathbf{i}^2 = \mathbf{j}^2 = \mathbf{k}^2 = \mathbf{ijk} = -1$.

For robotics, we are interested in **unit quaternions** $\mathbf{q} = [w, x, y, z]^T$ constrained by:

$$\vert \mathbf{q} \vert = \sqrt{w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2} = 1$$

Such a unit quaternion parameterises orientation as a **single rotation by angle $\theta$ about a unit axis $\hat{\mathbf{u}} = [u_x, u_y, u_z]^T$** in 3-D space:

$$\mathbf{q} = \left[\cos\frac{\theta}{2},\ u_x \sin\frac{\theta}{2},\ u_y \sin\frac{\theta}{2},\ u_z \sin\frac{\theta}{2}\right]^T$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Airplane Cockpit View

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy for Euler Angles:** Imagine a pilot in an aircraft cockpit. The aircraft's orientation in the sky can be described by three knobs the pilot adjusts: **Yaw** (turning the nose left/right, like shaking your head 'no'), **Pitch** (nose up/down, like nodding 'yes'), and **Roll** (tilting the wings, like cocking your head sideways). These three knobs, applied in sequence, can point the aircraft in *any* orientation. That is exactly what Euler angles do for a robot gripper!
>
> **Intuitive Analogy for Quaternions:** Now, instead of three separate knobs, imagine one **single, slick joystick** that rotates the aircraft by some angle about an arbitrary axis pointing in *any* 3-D direction. The joystick encodes this in **four numbers** — one for how much to rotate ($w$) and three for the direction of the axis ($x, y, z$). The "double-angle" ($\theta/2$) trick ensures we don't double-rotate when the joystick is flipped 180°, which would be an equivalent rotation.

## 1.4 Why Do We Need Both?

- **Euler Angles** are *human-readable* but suffer from **Gimbal Lock** — a singularity at $\beta = \pm 90°$ where two axes align and one degree of freedom is lost.
- **Quaternions** are *computationally robust*, have **no singularities**, enable **smooth spherical interpolation (SLERP)**, and require only **4 multiplications** to compose rotations — making them the de-facto standard in real-time robot controllers, spacecraft attitude control, and game engines.
- A rotation matrix **$\mathbf{R}$** is the bridge: it can be derived from, or converted to, either representation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The official KTU 2024 Scheme descriptor for PCRAT303 Module 1 mandates coverage of *both* Euler angle conventions (Z-Y-X and Z-Y-Z) and unit quaternions, including their mutual conversion to/from the rotation matrix — a recurring **14-mark question pattern** in the End-Semester Examination (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Euler Angles

- **Step 1 — Primitive Rotations:** Start with the three canonical $3\times 3$ rotation matrices about the principal axes.
- **Step 2 — Order Matters:** Multiplication of rotation matrices is **non-commutative**. $\mathbf{R}_x(\alpha)\mathbf{R}_y(\beta) \ne \mathbf{R}_y(\beta)\mathbf{R}_x(\alpha)$ in general. KTU examiners **deduct marks** for ambiguity in order.
- **Step 3 — Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic:** *Extrinsic* rotations are about the *fixed world frame* axes; *Intrinsic* rotations are about the *moving body frame* axes. The Z-Y-X extrinsic convention is mathematically identical to the X-Y-Z intrinsic convention — students must be explicit.
- **Step 4 — The 12-Convention Zoo:** There are exactly **12 valid Euler angle sequences** (6 Tait–Bryan + 6 Proper). The two most important for KTU are:
  - **Z-Y-X** (Tait–Bryan) — used in aerospace and DH-parameters.
  - **Z-Y-Z** (Proper) — used in classical mechanics, spherical wrists.
- **Step 5 — Gimbal Lock:** Occurs when the middle angle of a Tait–Bryan sequence equals $\pm 90°$, causing the first and third rotations to align and collapse the representation to only 2 effective degrees of freedom.

## 2.2 Theoretical Breakdown of Quaternions

- **Step 1 — Hamilton's Insight:** Hamilton spent 13 years seeking a 3-D analogue of complex numbers; he finally discovered that **four** dimensions, with a non-commutative product, are needed. The cost is that $\mathbf{i}\mathbf{j} = -\mathbf{j}\mathbf{i} \ne \mathbf{i}\mathbf{j}$.
- **Step 2 — Scalar-Vector Form:** Write $\mathbf{q} = [s, \mathbf{v}]^T$ where $s = w$ is the *scalar* part and $\mathbf{v} = [x, y, z]^T$ is the *vector* part. Then the product of two quaternions is:
$$\mathbf{q}_1 \otimes \mathbf{q}_2 = \left[s_1 s_2 - \mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{v}_2,\ \ s_1 \mathbf{v}_2 + s_2 \mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_1 \times \mathbf{v}_2 \right]^T$$
- **Step 3 — Conjugate and Inverse:** The conjugate of $\mathbf{q} = [s, \mathbf{v}]$ is $\mathbf{q}^* = [s, -\mathbf{v}]$. For a unit quaternion, $\mathbf{q}^{-1} = \mathbf{q}^*$.
- **Step 4 — Rotating a Point:** A pure quaternion $\mathbf{p} = [0, \mathbf{p}_{xyz}]$ is rotated by:
$$\mathbf{p}' = \mathbf{q} \otimes \mathbf{p} \otimes \mathbf{q}^{-1}$$
This elegant sandwich product is the foundation of all modern robotic attitude algorithms.
- **Step 5 — Double Cover:** $\mathbf{q}$ and $-\mathbf{q}$ represent the *same* physical rotation. This "double cover" property of $SU(2) \to SO(3)$ is why the $\theta/2$ half-angle appears in the axis-angle-to-quaternion map.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Formula | Key Constraint / Unit |
|---|---------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Roll matrix $\mathbf{R}_x(\alpha)$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ 0 & \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}$ | $\alpha \in [0, 2\pi)$ rad |
| 2 | Pitch matrix $\mathbf{R}_y(\beta)$ | $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & 0 & \sin\beta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\beta & 0 & \cos\beta \end{bmatrix}$ | $\beta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ rad |
| 3 | Yaw matrix $\mathbf{R}_z(\gamma)$ | $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0 \\ \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\gamma \in [-\pi, \pi]$ rad |
| 4 | Z-Y-X composite $\mathbf{R}$ | $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}_z(\gamma)\mathbf{R}_y(\beta)\mathbf{R}_x(\alpha)$ | $9$ entries, $3$ DOF |
| 5 | Quaternion definition | $\mathbf{q} = w + x\mathbf{i} + y\mathbf{j} + z\mathbf{k}$ | $(w, x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}^4$ |
| 6 | Unit norm constraint | $\vert \mathbf{q} \vert^2 = w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$ | Norm is dimensionless |
| 7 | Axis-angle map | $\mathbf{q} = \left[\cos\frac{\theta}{2},\ \hat{\mathbf{u}}\sin\frac{\theta}{2}\right]^T$ | $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$ rad |
| 8 | Quaternion product | $\mathbf{q}_1 \otimes \mathbf{q}_2 = [s_1 s_2 - \mathbf{v}_1\!\cdot\!\mathbf{v}_2,\ s_1\mathbf{v}_2 + s_2\mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_1\!\times\!\mathbf{v}_2]$ | Non-commutative |
| 9 | Quaternion inverse | $\mathbf{q}^{-1} = \mathbf{q}^* / \vert \mathbf{q} \vert^2$ | Reduces to $\mathbf{q}^*$ if $\vert\mathbf{q}\vert = 1$ |
| 10 | Point rotation | $\mathbf{p}' = \mathbf{q} \otimes \mathbf{p} \otimes \mathbf{q}^{-1}$ | $\mathbf{p}$ must be pure quaternion |
| 11 | Rotation matrix from $\mathbf{q}$ | $\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 1-2(y^2+z^2) & 2(xy-wz) & 2(wy+xz) \\ 2(wz+xy) & 1-2(x^2+z^2) & 2(yz-wx) \\ 2(xz-wy) & 2(wx+yz) & 1-2(x^2+y^2) \end{bmatrix}$ | $4$ DOF for $3$ DOF rotation |
| 12 | Gimbal lock condition (Z-Y-X) | $\beta = \pm 90°$ | Loses 1 DOF |
| 13 | Double-cover identity | $\mathbf{q} \equiv -\mathbf{q}$ | Same physical rotation |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Spacecraft (e.g., ISS, SpaceX Dragon) use **quaternions** in their attitude determination and control systems (ADCS) because they avoid singularities during 360° maneuvers and enable fuel-optimal control.
- **Industrial Robotics:** FANUC, ABB, and KUKA controllers internally use rotation matrices and quaternions for TCP (tool-center-point) tracking; the operator sees only Euler angles.
- **Animation & Gaming:** Unreal Engine and Unity 3D use quaternions for skeletal animation to **avoid gimbal lock** when a character flips upside down.
- **VR/AR:** Head-mounted displays (Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro) track orientation via IMU + quaternion fusion to keep the virtual camera stable.
- **Aerospace Yaw-Pitch-Roll:** The Z-Y-X convention directly maps to the **flight-control vocabulary** used in pilot cockpits and autopilot code.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Composite Rotation Matrix for Z-Y-X Euler Angles

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the closed-form expression for $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}_z(\gamma)\mathbf{R}_y(\beta)\mathbf{R}_x(\alpha)$.

**Step 1.** Write the three primitive rotation matrices. Each represents a *right-handed* rotation about the corresponding axis by its respective angle.

$$\mathbf{R}_x(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ 0 & \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{R}_y(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & 0 & \sin\beta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\beta & 0 & \cos\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{R}_z(\gamma) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0 \\ \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2.** Compute the inner product $\mathbf{R}_y(\beta) \mathbf{R}_x(\alpha)$. We multiply row-by-column.

$$\mathbf{R}_y(\beta) \mathbf{R}_x(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & 0 & \sin\beta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\beta & 0 & \cos\beta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ 0 & \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$

Element (1,1): $\cos\beta \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 + \sin\beta \cdot 0 = \cos\beta$.
Element (1,2): $\cos\beta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot \cos\alpha + \sin\beta \cdot \sin\alpha = \sin\beta \sin\alpha$.
Element (1,3): $\cos\beta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-\sin\alpha) + \sin\beta \cdot \cos\alpha = \sin\beta \cos\alpha$.

Element (2,1): $0 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0 = 0$.
Element (2,2): $0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot \cos\alpha + 0 \cdot \sin\alpha = \cos\alpha$.
Element (2,3): $0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot (-\sin\alpha) + 0 \cdot \cos\alpha = -\sin\alpha$.

Element (3,1): $-\sin\beta \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 + \cos\beta \cdot 0 = -\sin\beta$.
Element (3,2): $-\sin\beta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot \cos\alpha + \cos\beta \cdot \sin\alpha = \cos\beta \sin\alpha$.
Element (3,3): $-\sin\beta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-\sin\alpha) + \cos\beta \cdot \cos\alpha = \cos\beta \cos\alpha$.

$$\mathbf{R}_y(\beta) \mathbf{R}_x(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & \sin\beta \sin\alpha & \sin\beta \cos\alpha \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ -\sin\beta & \cos\beta \sin\alpha & \cos\beta \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3.** Now compute the full product $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}_z(\gamma)[\mathbf{R}_y(\beta)\mathbf{R}_x(\alpha)]$.

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0 \\ \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & \sin\beta \sin\alpha & \sin\beta \cos\alpha \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ -\sin\beta & \cos\beta \sin\alpha & \cos\beta \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$

Carrying out the multiplication (row $i$ × column $j$ of the two matrices):

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\gamma \cos\beta & \cos\gamma \sin\beta \sin\alpha - \sin\gamma \cos\alpha & \cos\gamma \sin\beta \cos\alpha + \sin\gamma \sin\alpha \\
\sin\gamma \cos\beta & \sin\gamma \sin\beta \sin\alpha + \cos\gamma \cos\alpha & \sin\gamma \sin\beta \cos\alpha - \cos\gamma \sin\alpha \\
-\sin\beta & \cos\beta \sin\alpha & \cos\beta \cos\alpha
\end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU Z-Y-X rotation matrix**. Students should memorise the bottom row — it gives $\beta$ directly as $\arcsin(-R_{31})$ and is the key to inverse Euler extraction.

**Step 4.** Sanity check — verify orthonormality. A valid rotation matrix must satisfy $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{R}^T = \mathbf{I}$ and $\det(\mathbf{R}) = +1$. The detailed verification is left to the student as a tutorial exercise; the matrix above is the standard result and is universally tabulated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Extracting Euler Angles from a Rotation Matrix (Z-Y-X)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Given $\mathbf{R} = [r_{ij}]$, recover $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$.

**Step 1.** From the (3,1) element of $\mathbf{R}$:
$$r_{31} = -\sin\beta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \beta = \arcsin(-r_{31}) = -\arcsin(r_{31})$$

This is valid only if $\vert r_{31} \vert \le 1$, which is guaranteed by orthonormality.

**Step 2.** When $\cos\beta \ne 0$ (i.e., $\beta \ne \pm 90°$, *non-singular case*):
$$\alpha = \arctan_2(r_{32}, r_{33}) = \arctan_2(\cos\beta \sin\alpha, \cos\beta \cos\alpha)$$

$$\gamma = \arctan_2(r_{21}, r_{11}) = \arctan_2(\sin\gamma \cos\beta, \cos\gamma \cos\beta)$$

The 2-argument arctangent $\arctan_2(y, x)$ correctly handles all four quadrants.

**Step 3.** When $\cos\beta = 0$ (Gimbal lock at $\beta = \pm 90°$): $r_{31} = \mp 1$. The two elementary rotations collapse into one; we can only recover $\alpha \pm \gamma$, not the individual angles. Conventionally, set $\gamma = 0$ and report $\alpha = \arctan_2(-r_{23}, r_{22})$ — this is the KTU-accepted convention for resolving gimbal lock.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Rotation Matrix from a Unit Quaternion

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the $3 \times 3$ matrix $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{q})$ in terms of $w, x, y, z$.

**Step 1.** Express the rotated pure quaternion. Let $\mathbf{p} = [0, \mathbf{p}_x, \mathbf{p}_y, \mathbf{p}_z]$ and $\mathbf{q} = [w, x, y, z]$. Compute the intermediate $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{q} \otimes \mathbf{p}$ first.

Using the Hamilton product formula $\mathbf{q}_1 \otimes \mathbf{q}_2 = [s_1 s_2 - \mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{v}_2,\ s_1 \mathbf{v}_2 + s_2 \mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_1 \times \mathbf{v}_2]$, with $s_1 = w$, $\mathbf{v}_1 = [x, y, z]$, $s_2 = 0$, $\mathbf{v}_2 = [p_x, p_y, p_z]$:

$$\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{q} \otimes \mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} -(x p_x + y p_y + z p_z) \\ w p_x + y p_z - z p_y \\ w p_y + z p_x - x p_z \\ w p_z + x p_y - y p_x \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2.** Now compute $\mathbf{p}' = \mathbf{r} \otimes \mathbf{q}^{-1}$. For a unit quaternion, $\mathbf{q}^{-1} = \mathbf{q}^* = [w, -x, -y, -z]$. Using the same Hamilton product:

$$s_{\mathbf{r}} = -(x p_x + y p_y + z p_z), \quad \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{r}} = [w p_x + y p_z - z p_y,\ w p_y + z p_x - x p_z,\ w p_z + x p_y - y p_x]^T$$

$$s_{\mathbf{q}^*} = w, \quad \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{q}^*} = [-x, -y, -z]^T$$

**Step 3.** The scalar part of $\mathbf{p}'$ is:
$$s_{\mathbf{p}'} = s_{\mathbf{r}} s_{\mathbf{q}^*} - \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{r}} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{q}^*} = -w(x p_x + y p_y + z p_z) + (-1)(w p_x + y p_z - z p_y)(-x) + \cdots$$

The full expansion, after cancelling cross-terms using $w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$, yields scalar part $= 0$, confirming $\mathbf{p}'$ is again a pure quaternion. The vector part reduces to:

$$\mathbf{p}'_x = (w^2 + x^2 - y^2 - z^2) p_x + 2(xy - wz) p_y + 2(wy + xz) p_z$$

$$\mathbf{p}'_y = 2(xy + wz) p_x + (w^2 - x^2 + y^2 - z^2) p_y + 2(yz - wx) p_z$$

$$\mathbf{p}'_z = 2(xz - wy) p_x + 2(wx + yz) p_y + (w^2 - x^2 - y^2 + z^2) p_z$$

**Step 4.** Reading off the coefficients as a matrix:

$$\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
w^2 + x^2 - y^2 - z^2 & 2(xy - wz) & 2(wy + xz) \\
2(xy + wz) & w^2 - x^2 + y^2 - z^2 & 2(yz - wx) \\
2(xz - wy) & 2(wx + yz) & w^2 - x^2 - y^2 + z^2
\end{bmatrix}$$

Equivalently, using the $1 - 2(\cdot)$ form (often easier to memorise):

$$\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
1 - 2(y^2 + z^2) & 2(xy - wz) & 2(wy + xz) \\
2(xy + wz) & 1 - 2(x^2 + z^2) & 2(yz - wx) \\
2(xz - wy) & 2(wx + yz) & 1 - 2(x^2 + y^2)
\end{bmatrix}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Verification (Identity):** Set $\mathbf{q} = [1, 0, 0, 0]$ (no rotation). The matrix becomes $\mathbf{I}_3$. ✓
> **Quick Verification ($\pi$ rotation about $z$):** $\mathbf{q} = [0, 0, 0, 1]$, then $\mathbf{R} = \text{diag}(1-2, 1-2, 1) = \text{diag}(-1, -1, 1)$ — the expected 180° flip about $z$. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Shepperd's Method for Quaternion from Rotation Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Given a numerically stable rotation matrix $\mathbf{R} = [r_{ij}]$, extract the quaternion $\mathbf{q} = [w, x, y, z]$ robustly.

**Step 1.** Compute the four candidate "trace" quantities. Define:

$$T_0 = 1 + r_{11} + r_{22} + r_{33} \quad (\text{four times } w^2)$$

$$T_1 = 1 + r_{11} - r_{22} - r_{33} \quad (\text{four times } x^2)$$

$$T_2 = 1 - r_{11} + r_{22} - r_{33} \quad (\text{four times } y^2)$$

$$T_3 = 1 - r_{11} - r_{22} + r_{33} \quad (\text{four times } z^2)$$

**Step 2.** Identify the largest of $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3$ — this avoids division by near-zero. Suppose $T_0$ is largest. Then:

$$w = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{T_0}, \quad x = \frac{r_{32} - r_{23}}{4w}, \quad y = \frac{r_{13} - r_{31}}{4w}, \quad z = \frac{r_{21} - r_{12}}{4w}$$

Analogous formulas follow cyclically for the other three cases. This is **Shepperd's algorithm** and is the gold standard in robotics for $\mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{q}$ conversion.

**Step 3.** Renormalise to enforce $\vert \mathbf{q} \vert = 1$ (mitigates floating-point drift):

$$\mathbf{q} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{q}}{\sqrt{w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2}}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Similar]`
**Define Euler angles. Explain the term "Gimbal Lock" with reference to the Z-Y-X convention.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Euler angles are a set of three angular parameters $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ that describe the orientation of a rigid body in 3-D space by performing three successive elementary rotations about chosen axes of the body frame. In the Z-Y-X (Yaw–Pitch–Roll) convention used in KTU robotics, the composite rotation is $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}_z(\gamma)\mathbf{R}_y(\beta)\mathbf{R}_x(\alpha)$.
- **Gimbal Lock (1.5 marks):** Gimbal lock is a singularity that occurs when the middle rotation $\beta = \pm 90°$. At this value, the $x$- and $z$-axes of the body frame become aligned, and the two corresponding rotations ($\alpha$ and $\gamma$) become indistinguishable — the manipulator effectively loses one degree of freedom. Mathematically, $\cos\beta = 0$ in the inverse-extraction formulas, making $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ indeterminate.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024 Similar]`
**What is a unit quaternion? Why is it preferred over Euler angles for representing robot orientation in real-time applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A **unit quaternion** is a four-element tuple $\mathbf{q} = [w, x, y, z]^T \in \mathbb{R}^4$ constrained by $w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$. It encodes a 3-D rotation as a *single* rotation by angle $\theta$ about a unit axis $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$ via the half-angle identity $\mathbf{q} = [\cos(\theta/2), u_x \sin(\theta/2), u_y \sin(\theta/2), u_z \sin(\theta/2)]^T$.
- **Advantages over Euler angles (1.5 marks):**
  1. **No singularities** — gimbal lock is impossible regardless of orientation.
  2. **Efficient composition** — combining two rotations requires only 16 multiplications via the Hamilton product, avoiding the $3\times 3$ matrix multiplication.
  3. **Smooth interpolation (SLERP)** — enables continuous, shortest-path rotation animations.
  4. **Numerical stability** — single renormalisation step keeps drift bounded.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always specify the convention!** Stating "rotation matrix" without saying Z-Y-X or Z-Y-Z costs 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Sign errors in $\sin\beta$ terms** are the single most common reason for losing 2–3 marks in Euler derivations. The element $R_{31} = -\sin\beta$ is a favourite trap.
> 3. **Quaternion-to-matrix: order of $x, y, z$ matters.** Memorise the formula *as written in this module*; do not improvise.
> 4. **Forget the half-angle**: Students often write $\sin\theta$ instead of $\sin(\theta/2)$ in the axis-angle-to-quaternion map. This costs 2 marks outright.
> 5. **Forgot unit norm** when extracting a quaternion from a matrix — always renormalise at the end.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Mod 1 Module-End Choice Pattern]`

**(a) Derive the composite rotation matrix for the Z-Y-X (Yaw–Pitch–Roll) Euler angle convention, starting from the three primitive rotation matrices. Clearly state the convention used.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The Z-Y-X convention means the body is first rotated about its $z$-axis by yaw $\gamma$, then about its (new) $y$-axis by pitch $\beta$, and finally about its (new) $x$-axis by roll $\alpha$. The composite is:

$$\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}_z(\gamma) \cdot \mathbf{R}_y(\beta) \cdot \mathbf{R}_x(\alpha)$$

The three primitive rotation matrices are written as (1 mark each):

$$\mathbf{R}_x(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ 0 & \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{R}_y(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & 0 & \sin\beta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\beta & 0 & \cos\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{R}_z(\gamma) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0 \\ \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute $\mathbf{R}_y(\beta) \mathbf{R}_x(\alpha)$ (already shown in Section 3.1 above):

$$\mathbf{R}_y(\beta) \mathbf{R}_x(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & \sin\beta \sin\alpha & \sin\beta \cos\alpha \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ -\sin\beta & \cos\beta \sin\alpha & \cos\beta \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}_z(\gamma)[\mathbf{R}_y(\beta)\mathbf{R}_x(\alpha)]$. The result, as derived in Section 3.1, is the canonical Z-Y-X matrix:

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\gamma \cos\beta & \cos\gamma \sin\beta \sin\alpha - \sin\gamma \cos\alpha & \cos\gamma \sin\beta \cos\alpha + \sin\gamma \sin\alpha \\
\sin\gamma \cos\beta & \sin\gamma \sin\beta \sin\alpha + \cos\gamma \cos\alpha & \sin\gamma \sin\beta \cos\alpha - \cos\gamma \sin\alpha \\
-\sin\beta & \cos\beta \sin\alpha & \cos\beta \cos\alpha
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification remark (1 mark):** $\det(\mathbf{R}) = +1$ and $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{R}^T = \mathbf{I}$ — confirming it is a proper rotation matrix in $SO(3)$.

---

**(b) Starting from a unit quaternion $\mathbf{q} = [w, x, y, z]^T$, derive the $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{q})$ that rotates a 3-D point. State the unit-norm constraint used.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the unit-norm constraint: $w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$. State the pure quaternion representation of a 3-D point $\mathbf{p} = [0, p_x, p_y, p_z]^T$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the rotation operation: $\mathbf{p}' = \mathbf{q} \otimes \mathbf{p} \otimes \mathbf{q}^{-1}$. For a unit quaternion, $\mathbf{q}^{-1} = \mathbf{q}^* = [w, -x, -y, -z]^T$.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Use the Hamilton product twice. The scalar-vector form of the Hamilton product is:
$$\mathbf{q}_1 \otimes \mathbf{q}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} s_1 s_2 - \mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{v}_2 \\ s_1 \mathbf{v}_2 + s_2 \mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_1 \times \mathbf{v}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

First compute $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{q} \otimes \mathbf{p}$. Then compute $\mathbf{p}' = \mathbf{r} \otimes \mathbf{q}^{-1}$, keeping terms up to second order in the quaternion components. The intermediate and final steps are shown in Section 3.3 above.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Collect the coefficients of $p_x, p_y, p_z$ in the vector part of $\mathbf{p}'$. The result, as derived in Section 3.3, is the closed-form rotation matrix:

$$\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
1 - 2(y^2 + z^2) & 2(xy - wz) & 2(wy + xz) \\
2(xy + wz) & 1 - 2(x^2 + z^2) & 2(yz - wx) \\
2(xz - wy) & 2(wx + yz) & 1 - 2(x^2 + y^2)
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Quick verification (0 marks extra, but useful):** Substituting $\mathbf{q} = [1, 0, 0, 0]$ yields the $3 \times 3$ identity matrix, as expected for the zero-rotation case.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Mod 1 Module-End Choice Pattern - Alternative]`

**(a) Explain the two main families of Euler angle conventions (Proper Euler and Tait–Bryan). Discuss Gimbal Lock in detail with a labelled sketch description.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** **Proper Euler Angles.** The first and third rotations are about the *same* axis. Six valid sequences: X-Y-X, X-Z-X, Y-X-Y, Y-Z-Y, Z-X-Z, Z-Y-Z. Used in classical rigid-body dynamics, e.g., Z-Y-Z for analysing symmetric top spin and spherical-wrist manipulators. The middle angle (e.g., $\beta$ in Z-Y-Z) is the **precession** angle; the others are **nutation** and **spin**.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Tait–Bryan Angles.** The three rotations are about *three distinct* axes. Six valid sequences: X-Y-Z, X-Z-Y, Y-X-Z, Y-Z-X, Z-X-Y, Z-Y-X. The Z-Y-X sequence is the most common in robotics and is called **Yaw–Pitch–Roll**. Used in aircraft (heading-elevation-bank), nautical navigation, and robot end-effector specification.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** **Gimbal Lock Mechanism.**
- A gimbal is a mechanical mounting with three concentric rings allowing rotation about three orthogonal axes.
- **Failure mode:** When the middle ring rotates by $\pm 90°$, the inner and outer rings become *coplanar* — the inner and outer axes coincide.
- **Consequence:** Two of the three Euler angles now describe rotation about the *same* axis, so one degree of freedom is lost; the orientation is no longer uniquely described by the three angles.
- **Example in Z-Y-X:** When $\beta = 90°$, the third column of the rotation matrix becomes a linear combination of the first and second columns; $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ become indistinguishable.
- **Real-world example:** The Apollo 11 lunar mission famously relied on correct orientation handling; modern spacecraft use quaternions precisely to avoid this failure mode.

---

**(b) Given an arbitrary rotation matrix $\mathbf{R} = [r_{ij}]_{3\times 3}$, describe **Shepperd's algorithm** to extract the corresponding unit quaternion $[w, x, y, z]^T$. Why is this method preferred over the naive trace-based method?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define the four "trace-like" quantities:
$$T_0 = 1 + r_{11} + r_{22} + r_{33} = 4w^2$$
$$T_1 = 1 + r_{11} - r_{22} - r_{33} = 4x^2$$
$$T_2 = 1 - r_{11} + r_{22} - r_{33} = 4y^2$$
$$T_3 = 1 - r_{11} - r_{22} + r_{33} = 4z^2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Identify the **largest** of $\{T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3\}$ (numerically robust choice). Suppose $T_0$ is largest:
$$w = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{T_0}, \quad x = \frac{r_{32} - r_{23}}{4w}, \quad y = \frac{r_{13} - r_{31}}{4w}, \quad z = \frac{r_{21} - r_{12}}{4w}$$

The other three cases follow cyclically (rotate indices and components):
- If $T_1$ largest: $x = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{T_1}$, then $w = (r_{32}-r_{23})/(4x)$, $y = (r_{12}+r_{21})/(4x)$, $z = (r_{13}+r_{31})/(4x)$.
- If $T_2$ largest: $y = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{T_2}$, with analogous formulas.
- If $T_3$ largest: $z = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{T_3}$, with analogous formulas.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Renormalise: $\mathbf{q} \leftarrow \mathbf{q} / \sqrt{w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ to remove floating-point error.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** **Why preferred over the naive trace method?**
- The naive method uses $w = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{1 + r_{11} + r_{22} + r_{33}}$. When the rotation angle $\theta$ is small, the term inside the square root is close to 4 (i.e., $w \approx 1$), but the difference $1 + r_{11} + r_{22} + r_{33}$ suffers from **catastrophic cancellation** because the individual terms are all close to 1 but their sum is close to 4 only after *adding* large nearly-equal numbers — a numerical trap.
- Shepperd's algorithm picks the *largest* of the four candidates, ensuring the quantity inside the square root is always well-separated from zero. This guarantees a **numerically stable extraction** for all rotations, including those near the identity (where $w \to 1$) and those near 180° (where $w \to 0$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 1 — Euler Angles and Quaternions**

- ☐ **Euler angles are 3 parameters;** rotation matrices are 9 (with 6 constraints); quaternions are 4 (with 1 constraint). All three describe the same 3-DOF orientation.
- ☐ **Z-Y-X = Yaw (about $z$) → Pitch (about $y$) → Roll (about $x$).** The composite is $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}_z \mathbf{R}_y \mathbf{R}_x$. Multiplication order is read right-to-left as the order of application.
- ☐ **Bottom row of the Z-Y-X matrix is $[-\sin\beta, \cos\beta\sin\alpha, \cos\beta\cos\alpha]$** — this is the most testable fact and the basis of inverse extraction.
- ☐ **Gimbal Lock occurs at $\beta = \pm 90°$ in Z-Y-X** (Tait–Bryan). For Proper Euler Z-Y-Z, gimbal lock occurs at $\beta = 0$ or $\pi$. Singularities are *inevitable* in any 3-parameter representation (topology of $SO(3)$).
- ☐ **Quaternions are non-commutative:** $\mathbf{q}_1 \otimes \mathbf{q}_2 \ne \mathbf{q}_2 \otimes \mathbf{q}_1$ in general.
- ☐ **Hamilton product formula:** $\mathbf{q}_1 \otimes \mathbf{q}_2 = [s_1 s_2 - \mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{v}_2,\ s_1 \mathbf{v}_2 + s_2 \mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_1 \times \mathbf{v}_2]$.
- ☐ **Half-angle identity:** $\mathbf{q} = [\cos(\theta/2), \hat{\mathbf{u}}\sin(\theta/2)]^T$ — *never* $\sin\theta$. The half-angle is what makes the double-cover $\mathbf{q} \equiv -\mathbf{q}$ work.
- ☐ **Unit norm:** Always enforce $w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$ after any computation involving quaternions; floating-point drift is real.
- ☐ **Rotation matrix entries from $\mathbf{q}$:** the off-diagonal pattern is $R_{12} = 2(xy - wz)$, $R_{21} = 2(xy + wz)$ (the diagonal pairs sum/difference); the diagonal entries are $1 - 2$*(sum of squares of the other two vector components).
- ☐ **Shepperd's algorithm for $\mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{q}$** is the numerically safe way; *never* use the naive $w = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\text{trace}+1}$ alone in production code.
- ☐ **Point rotation:** $\mathbf{p}' = \mathbf{q} \otimes \mathbf{p} \otimes \mathbf{q}^{-1}$. The point $\mathbf{p}$ must be a *pure* quaternion with zero scalar part.
- ☐ **Double cover:** $\mathbf{q}$ and $-\mathbf{q}$ represent the same rotation. Sign-flip ambiguity — pick a convention (e.g., always $w \ge 0$) for consistency.
- ☐ **Real-world preferences:** Use **Euler angles for human display and DH-parameter tables; quaternions for control loops and SLERP; rotation matrices for kinematic chain composition.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of a Robot Manipulator

A **Robot Manipulator** is a mechanical device, typically consisting of a series of rigid bodies (links) connected by joints, that is used to perform tasks by manipulating objects in space under automated control. It is the fundamental mechanical arm of an industrial robot, designed to mimic the function of a human arm in moving, gripping, or assembling parts within a defined workspace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCRAT303 - Module 2):**
> A robot manipulator is a programmable, multi-functional mechanism designed to move materials, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks. It consists of a kinematic chain of rigid links and joints forming a mechanical structure that enables the positioning of an end-effector in 3D space.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

**Conceptual Analogy: Your Own Human Arm!**

Imagine your own arm as a robot manipulator:
- Your **upper arm, forearm, and hand** are the **links** (rigid bodies).
- Your **shoulder, elbow, and wrist** are the **joints** (revolute, allowing rotation).
- Your **fingers holding a pen** are the **end-effector** (tool at the tip).
- The **pen tip drawing on paper** is performing a task in a **workspace** (a plane in 3D space).

A robot manipulator essentially does the same thing — but with **precise, repeatable, programmable motion** instead of human muscle control.

---

### 1.3 Definitions of Key Terminologies in Manipulator Kinematics

| Term | Formal KTU Definition | Simple Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **Kinematics** | The study of motion of a mechanical system without considering the forces that cause the motion. | "How it moves, not why it moves." |
| **Forward Kinematics** | The process of computing the position and orientation of the end-effector given the joint variables. | Joint angles → Hand position |
| **Inverse Kinematics** | The process of computing the joint variables required to place the end-effector at a desired position and orientation. | Desired hand position → Joint angles |
| **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** | The number of independent parameters that define the configuration of a mechanical system in space. | The minimum coordinates needed to describe its pose. |
| **Link** | A rigid body in the manipulator that maintains a fixed geometric relationship between any two points on it. | A "bone" of the robot arm. |
| **Joint** | A connection between two adjacent links that allows relative motion between them. | A "hinge" or "slider". |
| **End-Effector** | The device or tool attached to the last link of the manipulator, used to perform the intended task. | The "hand" or "gripper" of the robot. |
| **Workspace** | The set of all positions and orientations that the end-effector of the manipulator can reach. | The total "drawing area" of the robot. |
| **Base** | The fixed reference link of the manipulator, attached to the ground or a stationary platform. | The "shoulder mount" — origin of the robot. |
| **Tool Frame** | The coordinate frame attached to the end-effector, defining the position and orientation of the tool. | The "tip of the pen". |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Definitions Matter in KTU Exams:**
> Module 2 of PCRAT303 begins with these foundational definitions because every subsequent derivation — from Denavit-Hartenberg parameters to forward/inverse kinematics — is built on these terminologies. Examiners frequently test these definitions for **2-3 marks** as short-answer questions.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Joints in a Manipulator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:**
> In robotic manipulators, only **two types of joints** are industrially standardized. Every manipulator joint is either a **Revolute Joint (R)** or a **Prismatic Joint (P)**.

| Joint Type | Symbol | Motion Allowed | DOF Provided | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Revolute Joint (R)** | R | Rotation about a single axis | 1 DOF (rotational) | Shoulder, elbow, wrist of human arm |
| **Prismatic Joint (P)** | P | Linear translation along a single axis | 1 DOF (translational) | Hydraulic piston, drawer slider |

**Combinations like RR, RP, PR, PP** describe a manipulator with two joints. A typical industrial 6-DOF manipulator is described as **RRRRRR** (all revolute, like the PUMA robot).

---

### 1.5 Definition of Position, Orientation, and Pose

- **Position**: The location of a point in 3D space, represented as a **3×1 position vector**:

$$
p = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}
$$

- **Orientation**: The angular alignment of an object in 3D space, represented as a **3×3 rotation matrix** $R$ satisfying $R^T R = I$ and $\det(R) = 1$.

- **Pose** (Position + Orientation): The complete spatial description of a rigid body in 3D space, represented as a **4×4 homogeneous transformation matrix**:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} R & p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The homogeneous transformation matrix is the **most important single concept** in Module 2. It is used to represent the pose of the end-effector relative to the base frame — and it is the foundation of forward kinematics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why "Definition" is the Bedrock of Module 2

Every derivation in manipulator kinematics — from DH parameters to Jacobian matrices — depends on a precise understanding of these definitions. The logic chain is:

> **Definition of Links & Joints** → **Definition of DOF** → **Definition of Coordinate Frames** → **Definition of Homogeneous Transformation** → **Forward Kinematics** → **Inverse Kinematics**

Each step requires the previous one to be rigorously defined.

---

### 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — Detailed Analysis

**Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
The Degree of Freedom of a robot manipulator is the **number of independent parameters (joint variables)** required to completely specify the configuration of the manipulator in space.

**Grübler's Formula** for planar and spatial mechanisms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \quad \text{(Planar mechanism)} \\
DOF &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 \quad \text{(Spatial mechanism)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $n$ = total number of links (including ground)
- $j_1$ = number of lower-pair joints (1 DOF each)
- $j_2$ = number of higher-pair joints (2 DOF each)
- $j_3$ = number of higher-pair joints (3 DOF each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Practical Rule:** For a general-purpose industrial manipulator, the required DOF is **6** (3 for position + 3 for orientation). Any redundant manipulator has DOF > 6.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Position Vector | $p = [x, y, z]^T$ | meters (m) | Locating end-effector |
| 2 | Rotation Matrix | $R \in \mathbb{R}^{3\times 3}, R^T R = I$ | dimensionless | Orientation representation |
| 3 | Homogeneous Transform | $T = \begin{bmatrix} R & p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | mixed | Pose representation |
| 4 | Roll-Pitch-Yaw (ZYX Euler) | $R = R_z(\gamma) R_y(\beta) R_x(\alpha)$ | radians (rad) | Orientation in workspace |
| 5 | DOF (Spatial) | $6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3$ | dimensionless | Manipulator design |
| 6 | DOF (Planar) | $3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | dimensionless | Planar mechanism design |
| 7 | Joint Variable (Revolute) | $\theta_i$ | radians (rad) | Angular joint motion |
| 8 | Joint Variable (Prismatic) | $d_i$ | meters (m) | Linear joint motion |
| 9 | Workspace Volume (Spherical) | $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi (r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3)$ | $m^3$ | Reachability analysis |
| 10 | Forward Kinematics | $T_n^0 = A_1 A_2 \cdots A_n$ | mixed | End-effector pose from joint angles |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In your exam answer sheets, **never use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables** to denote absolute value or determinant (e.g., $\vert R \vert$). Always use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table formatting.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** 6-DOF manipulators (like the ABB IRB series) use these definitions to define welding paths.
- **Surgical Robotics:** The Da Vinci surgical robot uses redundant DOF (7) for precise tool orientation in confined spaces.
- **Space Manipulators (Canadarm):** NASA's Canadarm2 uses definitions of joint types and DOF to manipulate payloads in zero gravity.
- **Warehouse Automation:** Amazon's Kiva robots use definitions of workspace and pose to navigate and pick items.

---

### 2.5 Definition of Kinematic Chain

A **Kinematic Chain** is an assembly of rigid links connected by joints, where each joint provides a specific type of relative motion (rotational or translational) between adjacent links.

- **Open Chain**: The links form a serial structure with one fixed end (base) and one free end (end-effector). Example: PUMA robot arm.
- **Closed Chain**: The links form a loop, with multiple links connected to the ground. Example: Stewart platform.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Industrial Relevance:** Most industrial manipulators (PUMA, ABB, KUKA) are **open kinematic chains**. Parallel manipulators (Delta robot) are **closed kinematic chains**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Homogeneous Transformation Matrix

**Starting Point:** We need a unified mathematical representation that combines both **position** and **orientation** of a rigid body in 3D space.

**Step 1: Represent Position**
A point in 3D space has 3 coordinates:

$$
p = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Represent Orientation**
The orientation of a rigid body relative to a reference frame is captured by a 3×3 rotation matrix:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matrix is built from the projections of the body's local coordinate axes onto the global axes. For example, the rotation about the **z-axis** by angle $\theta$ is:

$$
R_z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Combine Position and Orientation**
We can write the position of a point $p$ in frame $B$ relative to frame $A$ as a 3D vector. To unify this with orientation, we extend the 3-vector to a **4-dimensional homogeneous coordinate** by appending a 1:

$$
p_{hom} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Form the Homogeneous Transformation Matrix**
We combine $R$ and $p$ into a single 4×4 block matrix:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3\times 3} & p_{3\times 1} \\ 0_{1\times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & x \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Verify the Properties**

- **Orthogonality of Rotation:** $R^T R = I_3$
- **Determinant of Rotation:** $\det(R) = +1$
- **Bottom Row Invariant:** The last row is always $[0, 0, 0, 1]$
- **Composition Property:** $T_{C}^{A} = T_{B}^{A} \cdot T_{C}^{B}$

**Why this matters:** When we chain multiple transformations (e.g., from base to joint 1 to joint 2 ... to end-effector), we simply **multiply** the individual $T$ matrices. This is the cornerstone of forward kinematics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: DOF of a 6R Industrial Manipulator (PUMA-type)

**Given:** A PUMA-type manipulator has 6 revolute joints and 7 links (including base and end-effector).

**Step 1: Identify the parameters for Grübler's formula (spatial mechanism)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 7 \text{ (links: base, 5 arm links, end-effector)} \\
j_1 &= 6 \text{ (six revolute joints, each 1 DOF)} \\
j_2 &= 0 \\
j_3 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Grübler's Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 \\
DOF &= 6(7-1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) \\
DOF &= 6(6) - 30 \\
DOF &= 36 - 30 \\
DOF &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret the Result**
A 6-DOF manipulator can position its end-effector at any point (x, y, z) **and** orient it with any roll-pitch-yaw (φ, θ, ψ) within its workspace. This is the **minimum DOF for a general-purpose industrial manipulator**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** A manipulator with DOF < 6 cannot achieve arbitrary pose. For example, a 3-DOF SCARA robot can position in a plane but cannot orient the end-effector in 3D space.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Joint Types

| Property | Revolute (R) | Prismatic (P) |
|---|---|---|
| Joint Variable | $\theta$ (angle) | $d$ (linear distance) |
| DOF Provided | 1 rotational | 1 translational |
| Range of Motion | Bounded by mechanical limits (e.g., ±180°) | Bounded by stroke length |
| Power Transmission | Motor + gearbox typical | Lead screw, ball screw, or linear actuator |
| Typical Use | Shoulder, elbow, wrist joints | Arm extension, vertical lift |
| Industrial Example | PUMA 560, ABB IRB 120 | Cartesian gantry robot |
| Backlash Issue | Yes (gearbox dependent) | Minimal (direct drive) |
| Stiffness | Moderate | High |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Workspace Volume for a Spherical Manipulator

**Given:** A spherical manipulator has a fixed base, a telescoping link, and a rotational joint. Its reach varies from $r_{min}$ to $r_{max}$.

**Step 1: Recognize the geometry** — The reachable workspace is the spherical shell between two concentric spheres.

**Step 2: Apply the volume of a spherical shell**

$$
V_{workspace} = \frac{4}{3}\pi (r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3)
$$

**Step 3: Special case (no minimum reach, $r_{min} = 0$)**

$$
V_{workspace} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_{max}^3
$$

This is the **maximum theoretical workspace** for a spherical-coordinate manipulator.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Definitions in Module 2

```mermaid

    A[Robot Manipulator Definition] --> B[Kinematic Chain]
    A --> C[Joints and Links]
    A --> D[Degrees of Freedom]
    A --> E[End Effector and Workspace]

    B --> B2[Closed Chain]

    C --> C1[Revolute Joint R]

    E --> E1[Tool Frame Definition]

    B1 --> F[Position and Orientation]

    C2 --> F

    D2 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F

    F --> F1[Homogeneous Transformation Matrix T]

```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Kinematic Structure of a 6R Industrial Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Frame 0] --> B[Joint 1 Revolute Waist]
    B --> C[Link 1]
    C --> D[Joint 2 Revolute Shoulder]

    G --> H[Joint 4 Revolute Forearm Roll]

    I --> J[Joint 5 Revolute Wrist Pitch]

    L --> M[End Effector Tool Frame]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Forward vs Inverse Kinematics Flow

