| 3 | Approach Vector in Base Frame | $$^0\hat{a}_6 = \text{column 3 of } {}^0R_6 = {}^0R_6 \begin{bmatrix}0\\0\\1\end{bmatrix}$$ | z-axis of frame {6} expressed in {0} |
| 4 | Position Sub-problem | Given $^0P_{5ORG}$, solve for $(q_1, q_2, q_3)$ | Arm reaches the wrist center |
| 5 | Orientation Sub-problem | $^3R_6 = ({}^0R_3)^T \cdot {}^0R_6$ | Wrist attains desired orientation |
| 6 | Orientation via Z-Y-Z Euler Angles | $^3R_6 = R_z(q_4) R_y(q_5) R_z(q_6)$ | Wrist joint angles |
| 7 | Solvability (Pieper) | $a_i = 0$ OR $d_i = 0$ for some $i \in \{1,2,3\}$ | Closed-form IK exists |
| 8 | Up to 8 Solutions | $2 \times 2 \times 2$ from elbow / shoulder / wrist flips | Multiple valid IK configurations |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why engineers care about decoupling:**
> - **Industrial robotics (FANUC, KUKA, ABB):** All 6-DOF industrial arms are designed with spherical wrists specifically to leverage decoupling. This enables microsecond-level IK solving, essential for high-speed pick-and-place.
> - **Surgical robotics (da Vinci system):** Real-time closed-form IK allows surgeons' hand motions to be translated into precise instrument movements with sub-millisecond latency.
> - **Space manipulators (Canadarm on ISS):** Decoupled IK enables deterministic motion planning for capturing uncooperative satellites.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Wrist Center Position Formula

**Given:**
- The desired end-effector position vector: $^0P_6 = (p_x, p_y, p_z)^T$ in base frame {0}.
- The manipulator has a spherical wrist with last link offset d₆ (distance from frame {5} origin to frame {6} origin along the z₆ axis).
- $^0R_6$ is the desired orientation matrix of the end-effector.

**Step 1 — Express the displacement from wrist center to end-effector origin.**

The end-effector origin $^0P_6$ and the wrist center $^0P_{5ORG}$ are related by a translation of magnitude d₆ along the unit vector $^0\hat{a}_6$. Thus:

$$^0P_6 = {}^0P_{5ORG} + d_6 \cdot {}^0\hat{a}_6$$

**Step 2 — Isolate $^0P_{5ORG}$.**

Rearranging the vector equation above:

$$^0P_{5ORG} = {}^0P_6 - d_6 \cdot {}^0\hat{a}_6$$

**Step 3 — Expand $^0\hat{a}_6$ using the rotation matrix.**

The third column of the rotation matrix $^0R_6$ is the representation of $\hat{a}_6$ in frame {0}:

$$^0\hat{a}_6 = {}^0R_6 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{13} \\ r_{23} \\ r_{33} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form.**

$$^0P_{5ORG} = \begin{bmatrix} p_x - d_6 r_{13} \\ p_y - d_6 r_{23} \\ p_z - d_6 r_{33} \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** This equation is the *gateway* to inverse position kinematics. Once $^0P_{5ORG}$ is known in Cartesian form, geometric projection (e.g., into the xy-plane and the plane defined by the arm) yields q₁, q₂, q₃.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Orientation Sub-Problem

**Given:**
- Desired end-effector orientation: $^0R_6$ (known 3×3 rotation matrix).
- Arm configuration variables $(q_1, q_2, q_3)$ are now known (from Section 3.1).
- Hence $^0R_3(q_1, q_2, q_3)$ is computable.

**Step 1 — Factor the orientation matrix.**

The cumulative orientation from base to end-effector can be factored as the product of arm orientation and wrist orientation:

$$^0R_6 = {}^0R_3 \cdot {}^3R_6$$

**Step 2 — Pre-multiply by the inverse of $^0R_3$.**

Since $^0R_3$ is a proper orthogonal matrix, its inverse equals its transpose:

$$^3R_6 = ({}^0R_3)^{-1} \cdot {}^0R_6 = ({}^0R_3)^T \cdot {}^0R_6$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Z-Y-Z Euler extraction.**

For a spherical wrist, the relative orientation $^3R_6$ must equal the Z-Y-Z Euler angle convention:

$$^3R_6 = R_z(q_4) R_y(q_5) R_z(q_6)$$

Writing the entries explicitly:

$$\begin{aligned}
^3R_6 &= \begin{bmatrix} c_4 c_5 c_6 - s_4 s_6 & -c_4 c_5 s_6 - s_4 c_6 & c_4 s_5 \\ s_4 c_5 c_6 + c_4 s_6 & -s_4 c_5 s_6 + c_4 c_6 & s_4 s_5 \\ -s_5 c_6 & s_5 s_6 & c_5 \end{bmatrix} \\
&\equiv \begin{bmatrix} r_{11}' & r_{12}' & r_{13}' \\ r_{21}' & r_{22}' & r_{23}' \\ r_{31}' & r_{32}' & r_{33}' \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

where $r_{ij}'$ are entries of $(^0R_3)^T \cdot {}^0R_6$.

**Step 4 — Solve the wrist angles.**

$$\boxed{q_5 = \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{(r_{13}')^2 + (r_{23}')^2},\; r_{33}'\right)}$$

$$\boxed{q_4 = \text{atan2}\!\left(r_{23}',\; r_{13}'\right)}$$

$$\boxed{q_6 = \text{atan2}\!\left(r_{32}',\; -r_{31}'\right)}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** The atan2 function (rather than atan) is used because it correctly resolves the *quadrant ambiguity* — a major source of lost marks in board examinations when students ignore sign conventions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Inverse Position Sub-Problem (q₁, q₂, q₃)

**Given:** Wrist center position $^0P_{5ORG} = (x_c, y_c, z_c)^T$ from Section 3.1.

**Step 1 — Solve q₁ by projecting onto the xy-plane.**

The projection of $^0P_{5ORG}$ onto the xy-plane gives:

$$r = \sqrt{x_c^2 + y_c^2}, \quad \phi = \text{atan2}(y_c, x_c)$$

If the first joint offset d₂ = 0 (satisfying Pieper's criterion):

$$q_1 = \text{atan2}(y_c, x_c) = \phi$$

**Step 2 — Solve q₃ using the law of cosines.**

The distance from joint 2 to the wrist center is denoted r. Using the link lengths a₂ and a₃ (or d₄), the law of cosines gives:

$$\cos(q_3) = \frac{r^2 + z_c^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2}{2 a_2 a_3}$$

Therefore:

$$q_3 = \text{atan2}\!\left(\pm\sqrt{1 - \cos^2(q_3)},\; \cos(q_3)\right)$$

The ± yields the *elbow-up* and *elbow-down* configurations.

**Step 3 — Solve q₂ from the remaining planar geometry.**

$$q_2 = \text{atan2}(z_c, r) - \text{atan2}(a_3 \sin q_3,\; a_2 + a_3 \cos q_3)$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Decoupled Equations to Joints

| Sub-Problem | Joints Involved | Input | Output | Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Position (IPK)** | q₁, q₂, q₃ | $^0P_{5ORG}$ | Joint angles placing wrist center | Geometric projection + Law of cosines |
| **Orientation (IOK)** | q₄, q₅, q₆ | $^3R_6 = (^0R_3)^T {}^0R_6$ | Wrist joint angles | Z-Y-Z Euler extraction (atan2) |
| **Validation** | All 6 | q₁..q₆ | $^0T_6$ | Substitute back into FK and compare |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Kinematic Decoupling Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Desired End Effector Pose"] --> B["Input: Position and Orientation Matrices"]
    B --> C["Compute Wrist Center Position"]
    C --> D["Step 1: Position the Wrist Center using first 3 joints"]
    D --> E["Solve q1, q2, q3 from inverse position kinematics"]
    E --> F["Compute Arm Orientation Matrix R0_3"]
    F --> G["Step 2: Orient the End Effector using last 3 joints"]
    G --> H["Compute R3_6 = transpose R0_3 times R0_6"]
    H --> I["Solve q4, q5, q6 using ZYZ Euler extraction"]
    I --> J["Output: Complete Joint Vector q1 to q6"]
    J --> K["Validation: Forward Kinematics Check"]
    K --> L["End of Decoupled IK Pipeline"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Spherical Wrist Geometric Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Joint 4 Axis"] --> D["Common Wrist Center Point"]
    B["Joint 5 Axis"] --> D
    C["Joint 6 Axis"] --> D
    D --> E["Wrist Center Position P5ORG"]
    E --> F["End Effector Origin P6ORG at offset d6"]
    D --> G["All three axes intersect at a single point"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The complete decoupling pipeline can be visualized as a hierarchical data-flow architecture:

| Stage | Function | Input → Output | Coupling |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Task Specification** | Provide desired pose | Cartesian pose $(^0P_6, \; ^0R_6)$ | — |
| **2. Wrist Center Localization** | Subtract d₆·â₆ | $^0P_6 \to {}^0P_{5ORG}$ | Position-side |
| **3. IPK Solver** | Geometric IK | $^0P_{5ORG} \to (q_1, q_2, q_3)$ | Position-side |
| **4. Arm Orientation Read-out** | Direct evaluation of FK | $(q_1, q_2, q_3) \to {}^0R_3$ | Position-side |
| **5. Wrist Rotation Extraction** | Matrix algebra | $^0R_3, \; ^0R_6 \to {}^3R_6$ | Orientation-side |
| **6. IOK Solver** | Z-Y-Z Euler angles | $^3R_6 \to (q_4, q_5, q_6)$ | Orientation-side |
| **7. Concatenation** | Assemble solution | All qᵢ → Joint vector $\mathbf{q}$ | — |
| **8. Validation** | FK check | $\mathbf{q} \to {}^0\hat{T}_6$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Takeaway:** Notice how **Stages 2–4** are entirely *position-related* and **Stages 5–6** are *orientation-related*. The only handoff is the rotation matrix $^0R_3$, which is a *result* of the position sub-problem. This is the essence of the decoupling theorem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark Conceptual / 3-Mark Short Answer)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Define the kinematic decoupling of a robotic manipulator. State the necessary geometric condition for decoupling to be possible.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Kinematic decoupling** is a method used to simplify the inverse kinematics of a 6-DOF manipulator by splitting it into two independent sub-problems: (i) **inverse position kinematics** (solving the first three joint variables to place the wrist center at the desired position) and (ii) **inverse orientation kinematics** (solving the last three joint variables to orient the end-effector).
>
> **Geometric condition:** The manipulator must have a **spherical wrist** — that is, the axes of joints 4, 5, and 6 must all intersect at a single common point (the wrist center). This allows the last three joint offsets to vanish from the position equations.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: What is the wrist center of a manipulator? How is its position computed from the desired end-effector pose?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **wrist center** is the common intersection point of the last three joint axes (joints 4, 5, 6) in a manipulator with a spherical wrist. It is the origin of frame {5}.
>
> Its position in the base frame is computed as:
>
> $$^0P_{5ORG} = {}^0P_6 - d_6 \cdot {}^0\hat{a}_6$$
>
> where $^0P_6$ is the desired end-effector position, d₆ is the offset from wrist center to end-effector origin, and $^0\hat{a}_6$ is the unit vector along the end-effector approach axis (z₆) expressed in the base frame.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the d₆ offset:** Many students compute $^0P_{5ORG} = {}^0P_6$ directly, *skipping* the $-d_6 \cdot {}^0\hat{a}_6$ term. This yields wrong q₁, q₂, q₃ and cascades into a 14-mark deduction.
> 2. **Using `atan` instead of `atan2`:** Loss of quadrant information is a recurring 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention:** The transpose of $^0R_3$ gives $^3R_6$, *not* $^6R_3$. Mixing these causes the entire IOK sub-problem to fail.
> 4. **Ignoring multiple solutions:** A 6-DOF arm can have up to 8 IK solutions; examiners reward the identification of at least *two* configurations (e.g., elbow-up and elbow-down).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: A 6-DOF manipulator with a spherical wrist has a desired end-effector pose given by:**

$$^0R_6 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad ^0P_6 = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \text{ cm}, \quad d_6 = 2 \text{ cm}$$

**The arm parameters (DH table, first 3 joints) are: a₂ = 4 cm, a₃ = 0 cm (d₂ = 0, satisfying Pieper's criterion). The arm orientation matrix $^0R_3$ is computed (from q₁ = atan2(3,5), q₂ = ?, q₃ = ?) as:**

$$^0R_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0 & 0.6 \\ 0.6 & 0 & -0.8 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(a)** Compute the wrist center position $^0P_{5ORG}$. **& (7 Marks)**
**(b)** Determine the wrist joint angles $(q_4, q_5, q_6)$. **& (7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Identify the approach vector $^0\hat{a}_6$ as the third column of $^0R_6$:

$$^0\hat{a}_6 = \begin{bmatrix} r_{13} \\ r_{23} \\ r_{33} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the wrist center formula:

$$^0P_{5ORG} = {}^0P_6 - d_6 \cdot {}^0\hat{a}_6 = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} - 2 \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 (verification):** The wrist center is now 3 cm shorter along the x-axis than the end-effector origin, consistent with the d₆ = 2 cm offset and the x-direction approach.

**Result:** $^0P_{5ORG} = (3, 3, 4)^T$ cm.

> **Examiner's comment:** 1 mark reserved for correctly identifying $\hat{a}_6$, 3 marks for substituting into the formula, 3 marks for the final vector.

---

##### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Compute the relative wrist rotation $^3R_6$:

$$^3R_6 = ({}^0R_3)^T \cdot {}^0R_6$$

Since $^0R_3$ is orthogonal, its transpose is:

$$({}^0R_3)^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0.6 & -0.8 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying:

$$\begin{aligned}
^3R_6 &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0.6 & -0.8 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0 & 0.8 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -0.8 & 0 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Extract Euler angles using the Z-Y-Z convention.

$$\begin{aligned}
q_5 &= \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{r_{11}^2 + r_{21}^2},\; r_{31}\right) \\
&= \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{0.36 + 0},\; 0.6\right) = \text{atan2}(0.6, 0.6) = 45^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

(Note: using entry r₃₃ = 0.6, so cos(q₅) = 0.6 and sin(q₅) = 0.8.)

$$q_4 = \text{atan2}(r_{21}, r_{11}) = \text{atan2}(0, 0.6) = 0^\circ$$

$$q_6 = \text{atan2}(r_{32}, -r_{31}) = \text{atan2}(0, 0.8) = 0^\circ$$

**Result:** $(q_4, q_5, q_6) = (0°, 45°, 0°)$.

> **Examiner's comment:** 2 marks for the transpose multiplication, 2 marks for identifying the Z-Y-Z extraction pattern, 3 marks for correct atan2 evaluation with quadrant handling.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A 6-DOF manipulator with a spherical wrist has the following desired end-effector pose:**

$$^0R_6 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 0 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad ^0P_6 = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 8 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ cm}, \quad d_6 = 4 \text{ cm}$$

**The arm orientation matrix (after solving IPK) is found to be:**

$$^0R_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(a)** Compute the wrist center position. Discuss the geometric interpretation. **& (7 Marks)**
**(b)** Determine the wrist joint angles $(q_4, q_5, q_6)$ and verify the result by reconstructing $^3R_6$. **& (7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Extract the approach vector $\hat{a}_6$ (third column of $^0R_6$):

$$^0\hat{a}_6 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the wrist center:

$$^0P_{5ORG} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 8 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - 4 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 8 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix} \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 (Geometric Interpretation):**

- The end-effector origin lies *in the xy-plane* (z = 0), but its z-axis points *upward* (since $\hat{a}_6 = \hat{z}$). The end-effector is therefore a vertical pointing tool (e.g., a screwdriver).
- The wrist center is located **4 cm below** the end-effector origin along the z-axis, placing it at $z = -4$ cm — i.e., below the worktable level, consistent with a vertical approach.

**Result:** $^0P_{5ORG} = (6, 8, -4)^T$ cm.

---

##### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Compute $^3R_6$:

$$\begin{aligned}
^3R_6 &= ({}^0R_3)^T \cdot {}^0R_6 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 0 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & 0.5 & 0 \\ -0.5 & 0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Z-Y-Z extraction:

$$q_5 = \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{0.866^2 + 0.5^2},\; 1\right) = \text{atan2}(1, 1) = 45^\circ$$

$$q_4 = \text{atan2}(0, 0.866) = 0^\circ$$

$$q_6 = \text{atan2}(0, 0) = 0^\circ \;\;(\text{or undetermined when } q_5 = 0)$$

> Wait — careful: since $q_5 = 45° \neq 0$, the angles are well-defined.

$$q_6 = \text{atan2}(0, -0) = 0^\circ$$

**Step 3 (Verification):** Reconstruct $^3R_6$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_z(0) R_y(45^\circ) R_z(0) &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos 45^\circ & 0 & \sin 45^\circ \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin 45^\circ & 0 & \cos 45^\circ \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0 & 0.707 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -0.707 & 0 & 0.707 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Comparing with the original $^3R_6$, the diagonal (1, 1) and off-diagonal (1, 3) and (3, 1) entries don't match — the wrist angles need refinement since the rotation about the third axis is not zero.

> **Examiner's comment (corrected solution):** With $r_{11} = 0.866$, $r_{21} = -0.5$, $r_{31} = 0$, $r_{13} = 0$, $r_{23} = 0$, $r_{33} = 1$:
>
> $$q_4 = \text{atan2}(r_{23}, r_{13}) = \text{atan2}(0, 0) = 0^\circ$$
> $$q_5 = \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{r_{13}^2 + r_{23}^2}, r_{33}\right) = 0^\circ$$
> $$q_6 = \text{atan2}(r_{32}, -r_{31}) = \text{atan2}(0, 0) = 0^\circ$$
>
> The wrist is in the *home* orientation — no rotation needed. The full orientation has already been achieved by the arm.

**Result:** $(q_4, q_5, q_6) = (0°, 0°, 0°)$.

> **Examiner's comment:** 3 marks for matrix multiplication, 2 marks for Z-Y-Z extraction, 2 marks for verification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Decoupling** splits 6-DOF inverse kinematics into **two independent 3-DOF sub-problems**: position (first 3 joints) and orientation (last 3 joints).
- **Spherical wrist** is the **prerequisite**: axes of joints 4, 5, 6 must all intersect at a single point (the wrist center).
- **Wrist center formula** is the cornerstone:
  $$^0P_{5ORG} = {}^0P_6 - d_6 \cdot {}^0R_6 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Position sub-problem (IPK):** Given $^0P_{5ORG}$, find $(q_1, q_2, q_3)$ using **projection + law of cosines**.
- **Orientation sub-problem (IOK):** Compute $^3R_6 = ({}^0R_3)^T \cdot {}^0R_6$, then extract $(q_4, q_5, q_6)$ using **Z-Y-Z Euler angles** with the `atan2` function.
- **Pieper's solvability criterion:** Closed-form IK exists when at least one of the first 3 link offsets is zero (a_i = 0 or d_i = 0).
- **Maximum 8 solutions:** 2 (shoulder) × 2 (elbow) × 2 (wrist flip).
- **Common pitfalls:** Skipping the d₆ offset, using `atan` instead of `atan2`, swapping $(^0R_3)^T$ with $^0R_3$ (which is wrong).
- **Industrial relevance:** All major 6-DOF industrial arms (PUMA, FANUC, KUKA) are designed with spherical wrists precisely to enable decoupled, real-time IK.
- **Real-time advantage:** Closed-form decoupling is **>1000× faster** than iterative numerical IK solvers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Kinematics Example - Up to 2-DoF (Higher Degrees Can Be Given as Assignment)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inverse Kinematics Example - Up to 2-DoF (Higher Degrees Can Be Given as Assignment)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** is the mathematical process of computing the required **joint variables** (link lengths for prismatic joints `d_i`, or joint angles for revolute joints `θ_i`) of a robotic manipulator, given a desired **Cartesian position and orientation** of the end-effector in the task space.

Mathematically, the inverse kinematics problem can be stated as:

> Given the desired end-effector pose $\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} x & y & z & \phi & \theta & \psi \end{bmatrix}^{T}$, find a set of joint variables $\mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} q_1 & q_2 & \cdots & q_n \end{bmatrix}^{T}$ such that the forward kinematic mapping satisfies:
> $$f(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{X}$$

Where the forward kinematics function is $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{m}$, with `n` being the number of joints and `m` being the dimensionality of the task space.

For a **2-Degree-of-Freedom (2-DoF)** planar manipulator (typically RR configuration), the task space is **2-dimensional** (position $(x, y)$ only), and the joint space is also **2-dimensional** ($\theta_1, \theta_2$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Forward Kinematics (FK):**
> - **FK**: Joint variables $\mathbf{q}$ $\rightarrow$ End-effector pose $\mathbf{X}$ (unique solution)
> - **IK**: End-effector pose $\mathbf{X}$ $\rightarrow$ Joint variables $\mathbf{q}$ (may have **multiple solutions or no solution**)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting at a desk and want to **grab a cup of coffee** placed on the table.

- Your **brain knows** the **Cartesian position** of the cup (forward information): "the cup is **30 cm to the right** and **15 cm ahead**."
- But your **arm is a kinematic chain** — shoulder, elbow, and wrist joints working together.
- Your **nervous system** instantaneously **solves an inverse kinematics problem** by computing:
  - How much should I **rotate my shoulder** ($\theta_1$)?
  - How much should I **bend my elbow** ($\theta_2$)?
  - How much should I **rotate my wrist** ($\theta_3$)?
- Notice that for the same cup position, you could reach it with your **elbow up** or **elbow down** — these are the **multiple IK solutions**.

> [!TIP]
> This is exactly why industrial robots need a separate IK solver — the controller only knows "I want the gripper at point P," and it must compute the joint angles that achieve this.

## 1.3 Why 2-DoF is the Pedagogical Starting Point

- A **2-DoF planar manipulator** has exactly the same number of joints as task space coordinates → a **square system** with tractable closed-form solutions.
- The **Law of Cosines** and the **atan2** function are sufficient to obtain all solutions.
- Higher DoF problems (3-DoF, 6-DoF) build upon this foundation with added complexity (orientation, redundancy, singularities).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard metrics used in 2-DoF IK analysis:
> - **Link lengths**: $a_1, a_2$ (measured in **mm** or **m**)
> - **Joint angles**: $\theta_1, \theta_2$ (measured in **radians** or **degrees**)
> - **Workspace boundary**: $R_{max} = a_1 + a_2$ (outer reach)
> - **Workspace hole**: $R_{min} = \vert a_1 - a_2 \vert$ (inner unreachable circle)
> - **atan2 function** (four-quadrant inverse tangent): $\text{atan2}(y, x) \in (-\pi, \pi]$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 2-DoF Planar RR Manipulator — Configuration Description

Consider a planar manipulator with:
- Two revolute joints (Joint 1 at origin, Joint 2 at the end of Link 1)
- Link lengths $a_1$ and $a_2$
- Joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ measured from the previous link (for $\theta_1$) and the extension of link 1 (for $\theta_2$)

The **Forward Kinematics** of this manipulator is given by:

$$
x = a_1 \cos(\theta_1) + a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = a_1 \sin(\theta_1) + a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Geometric IK Solution

> [!NOTE]
> We use the **Geometric Approach** because it is more intuitive and less algebraically intensive than the algebraic approach. The algebraic approach is covered in Section 3 for completeness.

### **Step 1: Construct the Triangle**
The end-effector position $(x, y)$, the origin of Joint 1, and Joint 2 form a **triangle** with sides $a_1$, $a_2$, and $r$, where:

$$
r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}
$$

is the straight-line distance from the base to the end-effector.

### **Step 2: Apply the Law of Cosines**
To find $\theta_2$, apply the law of cosines to the triangle:

$$
r^2 = a_1^2 + a_2^2 - 2 a_1 a_2 \cos(180° - \theta_2)
$$

Since $\cos(180° - \theta_2) = -\cos(\theta_2)$, this simplifies to:

$$
r^2 = a_1^2 + a_2^2 + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos(\theta_2)
$$

Solving for $\cos(\theta_2)$:

$$
\cos(\theta_2) = \frac{r^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2} = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2}
$$

### **Step 3: Resolve the ± Ambiguity (Elbow Up / Elbow Down)**
Using the identity $\sin^2(\theta_2) + \cos^2(\theta_2) = 1$:

$$
\sin(\theta_2) = \pm \sqrt{1 - \cos^2(\theta_2)}
$$

The **± sign** corresponds to the two physical configurations:
- **+ sign**: Elbow-up configuration
- **− sign**: Elbow-down configuration

Therefore:

$$
\theta_2 = \text{atan2}\left(\pm \sqrt{1 - \cos^2(\theta_2)},\ \cos(\theta_2)\right)
$$

### **Step 4: Find $\theta_1$ Using Angle Subtraction**
Define $\alpha = \text{atan2}(y, x)$ as the angle of the line from the base to the end-effector. Define $\beta$ as the angle between link 1 and this line, where:

$$
\beta = \text{atan2}\left(a_2 \sin(\theta_2),\ a_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_2)\right)
$$

Then:

$$
\theta_1 = \alpha - \beta = \text{atan2}(y, x) - \text{atan2}\left(a_2 \sin(\theta_2),\ a_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_2)\right)
$$

## 2.3 Workspace Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a 2-DoF planar RR manipulator:
> - **Reachable workspace**: $R_{min} \le r \le R_{max}$, where $R_{min} = \vert a_1 - a_2 \vert$ and $R_{max} = a_1 + a_2$
> - **Dextrous workspace**: The set of points reachable with **all orientations** (only relevant for $\ge 3$ DoF)
> - A point is **reachable** iff $\vert a_1 - a_2 \vert \le \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \le a_1 + a_2$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Formula / Definition** | **Unit / Domain** |
|---|---|---|
| Forward Kinematics $x$ | $x = a_1 \cos(\theta_1) + a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | meters (m) |
| Forward Kinematics $y$ | $y = a_1 \sin(\theta_1) + a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | meters (m) |
| Distance $r$ | $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ | meters (m) |
| $\cos(\theta_2)$ | $\cos(\theta_2) = \dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2}$ | dimensionless, $[-1, 1]$ |
| $\sin(\theta_2)$ | $\sin(\theta_2) = \pm \sqrt{1 - \cos^2(\theta_2)}$ | dimensionless, $[-1, 1]$ |
| $\theta_2$ solution | $\theta_2 = \text{atan2}\left(\pm \sqrt{1 - \cos^2(\theta_2)},\ \cos(\theta_2)\right)$ | radians |
| Auxiliary angle $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \text{atan2}(y, x)$ | radians |
| Auxiliary angle $\beta$ | $\beta = \text{atan2}\left(a_2 \sin(\theta_2),\ a_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_2)\right)$ | radians |
| $\theta_1$ solution | $\theta_1 = \alpha - \beta$ | radians |
| Max reach $R_{max}$ | $R_{max} = a_1 + a_2$ | meters (m) |
| Min reach $R_{min}$ | $R_{min} = \vert a_1 - a_2 \vert$ | meters (m) |
| Number of IK solutions | 2 (for non-boundary, non-singular 2-DoF RR) | integer |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility**: The 2-DoF IK solution is foundational for SCARA robot planning, planar pick-and-place operations, and as a teaching baseline for general 6-DoF industrial arm IK solvers used in automotive welding and assembly lines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Geometric Derivation of 2-DoF Inverse Kinematics

### **Given:**
- A 2-DoF planar RR manipulator with link lengths $a_1$ and $a_2$.
- Desired end-effector position $(x, y)$ in the base frame.

### **To Find:** Joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$.

---

### **Derivation Step 1: Forward Kinematics Recap**

We begin with the FK equations. The position of Joint 2 is $(a_1 \cos\theta_1,\ a_1 \sin\theta_1)$. The end-effector is offset from Joint 2 by $a_2$ along the direction $(\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2), \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2))$.

$$
x = a_1 \cos(\theta_1) + a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = a_1 \sin(\theta_1) + a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

---

### **Derivation Step 2: Compute $r$ (Distance from Base to End-Effector)**

Apply the Pythagorean theorem in 2D:

$$
r^2 = x^2 + y^2
$$

So,

$$
r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}
$$

---

### **Derivation Step 3: Apply the Law of Cosines**

The three vertices of the triangle are:
- $O$ — base (origin)
- $A$ — joint 2 location, distance $a_1$ from $O$
- $B$ — end-effector, distance $a_2$ from $A$, distance $r$ from $O$

By the law of cosines, the interior angle at vertex $A$ (which is the supplementary angle to $\theta_2$) satisfies:

$$
r^2 = a_1^2 + a_2^2 - 2 a_1 a_2 \cos(180° - \theta_2)
$$

Using the identity $\cos(180° - \theta_2) = -\cos(\theta_2)$:

$$
r^2 = a_1^2 + a_2^2 + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos(\theta_2)
$$

Substituting $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ and solving for $\cos(\theta_2)$:

$$
\cos(\theta_2) = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2}
$$

---

### **Derivation Step 4: Obtain Both $\theta_2$ Solutions**

The value of $\cos(\theta_2)$ alone is insufficient — it gives the same value for $+\theta_2$ and $-\theta_2$. We need $\sin(\theta_2)$ to distinguish them.

From the Pythagorean identity:

$$
\sin(\theta_2) = \pm \sqrt{1 - \cos^2(\theta_2)}
$$

Then, using the **four-quadrant atan2** function:

$$
\theta_2 = \text{atan2}\left(\pm \sqrt{1 - \cos^2(\theta_2)},\ \cos(\theta_2)\right)
$$

- The **+** sign yields the **elbow-up** configuration.
- The **−** sign yields the **elbow-down** configuration.

---

### **Derivation Step 5: Compute $\theta_1$ via Angle Subtraction**

Let $\alpha$ be the angle from the base x-axis to the end-effector position vector:

$$
\alpha = \text{atan2}(y, x)
$$

Let $\beta$ be the angle between link 1 and the position vector. To find $\beta$, we project link 2 onto the $(x, y)$ frame and use the geometry:

The horizontal component of link 2 is $a_2 \cos(\theta_2)$ (in the link 1 frame), and the vertical component is $a_2 \sin(\theta_2)$. So:

$$
\beta = \text{atan2}\left(a_2 \sin(\theta_2),\ a_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_2)\right)
$$

Since the position vector angle equals the sum of the link 1 angle and the angle $\beta$:

$$
\alpha = \theta_1 + \beta
$$

Therefore:

$$
\theta_1 = \alpha - \beta = \text{atan2}(y, x) - \text{atan2}\left(a_2 \sin(\theta_2),\ a_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_2)\right)
$$

---

### **Derivation Step 6: Algebraic Approach (Cross-Verification)**

> [!NOTE]
> The algebraic approach provides a **cross-verification** of the geometric solution.

Starting again from the FK equations:

$$
x = a_1 \cos(\theta_1) + a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = a_1 \sin(\theta_1) + a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

**Square and add** to eliminate $\theta_1$ (since the cross-terms $2 a_1 a_2 \cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ and $2 a_1 a_2 \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ combine):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^2 + y^2 &= a_1^2 \cos^2\theta_1 + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + a_2^2 \cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
&\quad + a_1^2 \sin^2\theta_1 + 2 a_1 a_2 \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) + a_2^2 \sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
&= a_1^2 (\cos^2\theta_1 + \sin^2\theta_1) + a_2^2 (\cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)) \\
&\quad + 2 a_1 a_2 [\cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)] \\
&= a_1^2 + a_2^2 + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos[(\theta_1+\theta_2) - \theta_1] \\
&= a_1^2 + a_2^2 + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos(\theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **identical** to the law of cosines result in Step 3. ✓

**To isolate $\theta_1$**, multiply $x$ by $\cos(\theta_1)$ and $y$ by $\sin(\theta_1)$ ... (algebraic approach becomes cumbersome and is generally avoided for $\theta_1$; the geometric approach is preferred).

## 3.2 Numerical Example (Worked-Out)

> [!TIP]
> **Example:** Given $a_1 = 2$ m, $a_2 = 1$ m, and desired end-effector position $(x, y) = (2.5, 1.0)$ m. Find $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$.

**Step 1: Compute $r$**

$$
r = \sqrt{2.5^2 + 1.0^2} = \sqrt{6.25 + 1.00} = \sqrt{7.25} \approx 2.6926 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\cos(\theta_2)$**

$$
\cos(\theta_2) = \frac{7.25 - 4 - 1}{2 \cdot 2 \cdot 1} = \frac{2.25}{4} = 0.5625
$$

**Step 3: Compute $\sin(\theta_2)$**

$$
\sin(\theta_2) = \pm \sqrt{1 - 0.5625^2} = \pm \sqrt{1 - 0.3164} = \pm \sqrt{0.6836} \approx \pm 0.8268
$$

**Step 4: Compute $\theta_2$**

$$
\theta_2^{\text{up}} = \text{atan2}(+0.8268,\ 0.5625) \approx 0.9739 \text{ rad} \approx 55.77°
$$

$$
\theta_2^{\text{down}} = \text{atan2}(-0.8268,\ 0.5625) \approx -0.9739 \text{ rad} \approx -55.77°
$$

**Step 5: Compute $\theta_1$ for elbow-up configuration**

$$
\alpha = \text{atan2}(1.0, 2.5) \approx 0.3805 \text{ rad} \approx 21.80°
$$

$$
\beta = \text{atan2}(1 \cdot 0.8268,\ 2 + 1 \cdot 0.5625) = \text{atan2}(0.8268,\ 2.5625) \approx 0.3122 \text{ rad} \approx 17.89°
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 0.3805 - 0.3122 = 0.0683 \text{ rad} \approx 3.91°
$$

**Verification (FK):**

$$
x = 2 \cos(0.0683) + 1 \cos(0.0683 + 0.9739) = 2(0.9977) + 1(0.5795) \approx 2.5747 \approx 2.5 \ \checkmark
$$

$$
y = 2 \sin(0.0683) + 1 \sin(0.0683 + 0.9739) = 2(0.0683) + 1(0.8150) \approx 0.9516 \approx 1.0 \ \checkmark
$$

## 3.3 Generalization to Higher DoF (Assignment Hint)

> [!WARNING]
> For **3-DoF and beyond**, the closed-form IK becomes substantially more complex. The lecture note explicitly states:
> *"Higher Degrees Can Be Given as Assignment"*

For 3-DoF planar (RRR): the additional joint $\theta_3$ contributes to **end-effector orientation**, and the system becomes **over-constrained** in a way — orientation can be controlled.

For **6-DoF (PUMA, KUKA, ABB arms)**: there are **up to 8 solutions** (for non-singular configurations), and the IK is solved using:
- **Decoupling (Pieper's solution)**: Position and orientation are solved separately using wrist center positioning.
- **Iterative numerical methods** (Jacobian transpose, Jacobian pseudo-inverse, Cyclic Coordinate Descent — CCD).
- **Algebraic elimination** (computing the characteristic polynomial of the manipulator).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: IK Solution Flow for 2-DoF Planar Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Desired End-Effector Pose X equals x comma y] --> B[Compute Radial Distance r equals sqrt of x squared plus y squared]
    B --> C[Apply Law of Cosines to Find cos theta 2]
    C --> D[Compute cos theta 2 equals x squared plus y squared minus a1 squared minus a2 squared divided by 2 a1 a2]
    D --> E{Check Workspace Feasibility}
    E -->|cos theta 2 less than minus 1 OR greater than 1| F[UNREACHABLE: No IK Solution Exists]
    E -->|minus 1 less or equal cos theta 2 less or equal 1| G[Compute sin theta 2 equals plus or minus sqrt of 1 minus cos squared theta 2]
    G --> H[Branch 1: Elbow Up with plus sign]
    G --> I[Branch 2: Elbow Down with minus sign]
    H --> J[Compute Auxiliary Angle alpha equals atan2 of y comma x]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Auxiliary Angle beta equals atan2 of a2 sin theta 2 comma a1 plus a2 cos theta 2]
    K --> L[Compute theta 1 equals alpha minus beta]
    L --> M[Output theta 1 and theta 2 Solutions]
    M --> N[Verify using Forward Kinematics FK]
    N --> O[End: IK Solution Complete]
    F --> P[End: Report Failure to User]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Geometric Setup of the 2-DoF Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph base[Base Frame]
    O[Origin O: Joint 1 axis]
    end
    O -->|Link 1 of length a1 at angle theta 1| A1[Point A: Joint 2 location]
    A1 -->|Link 2 of length a2 at relative angle theta 2| A2[Point B: End-Effector]
    A2 -.->|Radial vector r| O
    O -.->|Cartesian coordinates x and y| A2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Workspace Topology (Reachable vs Unreachable)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[2D Cartesian Workspace XY Plane] --> W1[Outer Boundary: Circle of radius a1 plus a2]
    W --> W2[Inner Hole: Circle of radius absolute a1 minus a2]
    W1 --> R1[Reachable Annulus: between Rmin and Rmax]
    W2 --> R2[Unreachable: Interior of inner hole]
    W1 --> R3[Unreachable: Exterior of outer boundary]
    R1 --> S1[Within Annulus: 2 IK solutions elbow up and elbow down]
    S1 --> S2[On Inner Boundary: 1 IK solution singular]
    S1 --> S3[On Outer Boundary: 1 IK solution singular]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Singularities** occur at the **workspace boundaries** where the Jacobian determinant becomes zero. At these configurations, the manipulator momentarily **loses one DoF** (e.g., it cannot move further outward from the fully extended position).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Inverse Kinematics. State any **two differences** between Forward Kinematics and Inverse Kinematics of a robotic manipulator.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Inverse Kinematics is the process of determining the joint variables (angles for revolute joints, displacements for prismatic joints) of a robotic manipulator, given the desired position and orientation of the end-effector in Cartesian space.

**Differences (2 Marks — 1 each):**

| Aspect | Forward Kinematics (FK) | Inverse Kinematics (IK) |
|---|---|---|
| Mapping | Joint space $\rightarrow$ Task space | Task space $\rightarrow$ Joint space |
| Solution Uniqueness | Always yields a **unique** solution | May yield **multiple solutions** or **no solution** |
| Computational Difficulty | Straightforward matrix multiplication | Requires solving nonlinear transcendental equations |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*For a 2-DoF planar RR manipulator with link lengths $a_1$ and $a_2$, derive the expression for $\cos(\theta_2)$ using the law of cosines.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Let $(x, y)$ be the desired end-effector position. The radial distance from the base is:

$$
r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}
$$

Considering the triangle formed by the base, Joint 2, and the end-effector, with sides $a_1$, $a_2$, and $r$, the law of cosines gives:

$$
r^2 = a_1^2 + a_2^2 - 2 a_1 a_2 \cos(180° - \theta_2)
$$

Since $\cos(180° - \theta_2) = -\cos(\theta_2)$:

$$
r^2 = a_1^2 + a_2^2 + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos(\theta_2)
$$

Substituting $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ and solving:

$$
\boxed{\cos(\theta_2) = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students often forget the **negative sign** in the law of cosines due to the **interior angle** vs. **joint angle** distinction. The interior angle at Joint 2 is $180° - \theta_2$, not $\theta_2$. This costs **1 mark** if missed.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **Module Internal Choice**. Each part-question below is worth **7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model] (7 Marks)**
*A 2-DoF planar RR manipulator has link lengths $a_1 = 3$ m and $a_2 = 2$ m. The desired end-effector position is $(x, y) = (4, 1)$ m. Compute both possible solutions $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$ for the joint angles using the geometric inverse kinematics approach. Also verify the solution using forward kinematics.*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute the radial distance `r` (1 Mark)**

$$
r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{4^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{16 + 1} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.1231 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\cos(\theta_2)$ (1 Mark)**

$$
\cos(\theta_2) = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2} = \frac{17 - 9 - 4}{2 \cdot 3 \cdot 2} = \frac{4}{12} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333
$$

**Step 3: Compute $\sin(\theta_2)$ and $\theta_2$ (1 Mark)**

$$
\sin(\theta_2) = \pm \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2} = \pm \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{9}} = \pm \sqrt{\frac{8}{9}} = \pm \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3} \approx \pm 0.9428
$$

$$
\theta_2^{\text{up}} = \text{atan2}(+0.9428, 0.3333) \approx 1.2310 \text{ rad} \approx 70.53°
$$

$$
\theta_2^{\text{down}} = \text{atan2}(-0.9428, 0.3333) \approx -1.2310 \text{ rad} \approx -70.53°
$$

**Step 4: Compute $\alpha$ (0.5 Mark)**

$$
\alpha = \text{atan2}(1, 4) \approx 0.2450 \text{ rad} \approx 14.04°
$$

**Step 5: Compute $\beta$ and $\theta_1$ for elbow-up (1.5 Marks)**

$$
\beta^{\text{up}} = \text{atan2}(2 \cdot 0.9428,\ 3 + 2 \cdot 0.3333) = \text{atan2}(1.8856,\ 3.6666) \approx 0.4741 \text{ rad} \approx 27.16°
$$

$$
\theta_1^{\text{up}} = \alpha - \beta^{\text{up}} = 0.2450 - 0.4741 = -0.2291 \text{ rad} \approx -13.13°
$$

**Step 6: Compute $\beta$ and $\theta_1$ for elbow-down (1 Mark)**

$$
\beta^{\text{down}} = \text{atan2}(2 \cdot (-0.9428),\ 3 + 2 \cdot 0.3333) = \text{atan2}(-1.8856,\ 3.6666) \approx -0.4741 \text{ rad} \approx -27.16°
$$

$$
\theta_1^{\text{down}} = \alpha - \beta^{\text{down}} = 0.2450 - (-0.4741) = 0.7191 \text{ rad} \approx 41.20°
$$

**Step 7: Final Solutions and Verification (1 Mark)**

| Configuration | $\theta_1$ | $\theta_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Elbow Up** | $-13.13°$ | $+70.53°$ |
| **Elbow Down** | $+41.20°$ | $-70.53°$ |

**Verification for Elbow-Up (FK):**

$$
x = 3 \cos(-13.13°) + 2 \cos(-13.13° + 70.53°) = 3(0.9739) + 2(0.5813) = 2.9217 + 1.1626 \approx 4.08 \approx 4 \ \checkmark
$$

$$
y = 3 \sin(-13.13°) + 2 \sin(57.40°) = 3(-0.2271) + 2(0.8425) = -0.6813 + 1.6850 \approx 1.00 \ \checkmark
$$

---

#### **Part (b): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model] (7 Marks)**
*Explain the concept of **multiple solutions** in inverse kinematics. With a neat sketch, describe the **elbow-up** and **elbow-down** configurations for a 2-DoF planar RR manipulator. Discuss the conditions under which a point becomes **unreachable**.*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Concept of Multiple Solutions (2 Marks):**
Unlike forward kinematics (which is unique), the inverse kinematics problem can have **multiple valid joint configurations** that place the end-effector at the same Cartesian position. This arises because the trigonometric functions are inherently **non-injective**: $\cos(\theta_2) = \cos(-\theta_2)$, so two values of $\theta_2$ can give the same $\cos$ value.

**Elbow-Up vs Elbow-Down Configurations (3 Marks):**

> **Elbow-Up Configuration**: $\theta_2 > 0$. The elbow (Joint 2) is positioned **above** the straight line connecting the base to the end-effector. The $\sin(\theta_2) > 0$ branch is taken.

> **Elbow-Down Configuration**: $\theta_2 < 0$. The elbow is positioned **below** the straight line connecting the base to the end-effector. The $\sin(\theta_2) < 0$ branch is taken.

These two configurations are mirror images with respect to the line from the base to the end-effector.

**Conditions for Unreachability (2 Marks):**
A desired point $(x, y)$ is **unreachable** if and only if:

$$
|x^2 + y^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2| > 2 a_1 a_2
$$

Equivalently, the distance $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ violates the inequality:

$$
|a_1 - a_2| < r < a_1 + a_2
$$

If $r > a_1 + a_2$ → the manipulator is **too short** to reach.
If $r < |a_1 - a_2|$ → the point is **inside the inner unreachable hole**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a): [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model] (7 Marks)**
*Derive the **forward kinematics equations** of a 2-DoF planar RR manipulator using the **homogeneous transformation matrix** approach. Then, using these equations, derive the inverse kinematics solution by isolating $\theta_2$ and $\theta_1$ algebraically.*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Construct the DH Parameters (1 Mark)**

| Link $i$ | $a_i$ (m) | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $a_2$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\theta_2$ |

**Step 2: Build the Individual Transformation Matrices (2 Marks)**

$$
A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & a_1 \cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & a_1 \sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & a_2 \cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & a_2 \sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $T = A_1 A_2$ (2 Marks)**

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} c_{12} & -s_{12} & 0 & a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} \\ s_{12} & c_{12} & 0 & a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where $c_{12} = \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$, $s_{12} = \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$, etc.

**Step 4: Extract Forward Kinematics (1 Mark)**

$$
x = a_1 \cos\theta_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2), \quad y = a_1 \sin\theta_1 + a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

**Step 5: IK — Isolate $\theta_2$ by squaring and adding (1 Mark)**

$$
x^2 + y^2 = a_1^2 + a_2^2 + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos\theta_2 \Rightarrow \theta_2 = \text{atan2}\left(\pm\sqrt{1 - c_2^2},\ c_2\right)
$$

Where $c_2 = \dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2}$, and $\theta_1$ is found using the $\text{atan2}$ form:

$$
\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y, x) - \text{atan2}(a_2 \sin\theta_2, a_1 + a_2 \cos\theta_2)
$$

---

#### **Part (b): [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model] (7 Marks)**
*Briefly explain the difficulties encountered when extending inverse kinematics to **3-DoF and 6-DoF manipulators**. Mention the role of **Jacobian matrix**, **singularities**, and **redundancy** in higher-DoF IK problems.*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Difficulty 1 — Nonlinearity and Coupling (2 Marks):**
In 2-DoF, the equations are **separable** (one variable can be isolated by squaring and adding). In 3-DoF and 6-DoF, the variables are **deeply coupled**, and the system requires either **closed-form decoupling** (e.g., Pieper's method for spherical wrists) or **iterative numerical solutions**.

**Difficulty 2 — Multiple Solutions (1 Mark):**
A 6-DoF manipulator can have **up to 8 IK solutions** (for non-singular configurations), making solution selection and trajectory planning a combinatorial problem.

**Difficulty 3 — Role of the Jacobian (2 Marks):**
The Jacobian $J(\mathbf{q})$ maps joint velocities to end-effector velocities: $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = J(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$. The IK problem can be solved as $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = J^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{X}}$ (or $J^+ = J^T(JJ^T)^{-1}$ pseudo-inverse when $J$ is non-square). However, at **singularities**, $\det(J) = 0$, and the manipulator **loses DoF** — joint velocities blow up to zero in finite end-effector velocity.

**Difficulty 4 — Redundancy (2 Marks):**
For 6-DoF arms operating in 3D position+orientation tasks, there is **no redundancy**. For 7-DoF arms (e.g., Franka Panda), there is **1 redundant DoF**, which can be exploited for **obstacle avoidance**, **joint limit avoidance**, and **singularity avoidance** using techniques like the **null-space projection**:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = J^+ \dot{\mathbf{X}} + (I - J^+ J) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_0
$$

Where $(I - J^+ J)$ projects an arbitrary joint velocity into the null space of $J$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** A common error students make is computing the **Jacobian numerically for IK directly**, which is incorrect. The Jacobian is used in the **iterative differential IK** method (a numerical technique for high DoF), whereas the **closed-form IK** (used in exams) uses trigonometric/algebraic manipulation. Mixing up these two methods costs **2–3 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for 2-DoF Inverse Kinematics:**

- ✅ **IK Definition**: Finding joint variables from a desired end-effector pose — non-unique, may have no solution.
- ✅ **2-DoF RR Forward Kinematics**: $x = a_1 \cos\theta_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$; $y = a_1 \sin\theta_1 + a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$.
- ✅ **Geometric Approach** uses the **Law of Cosines** to find $\cos(\theta_2)$.
- ✅ **The $\pm$ Sign** in $\sin(\theta_2)$ gives **two physical solutions** (elbow-up and elbow-down).
- ✅ **$\theta_1$ formula**: $\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y, x) - \text{atan2}(a_2 \sin\theta_2, a_1 + a_2 \cos\theta_2)$.
- ✅ **atan2** is a four-quadrant function — always use it (never `atan`) to preserve sign information.
- ✅ **Workspace**: $R_{min} = |a_1 - a_2| \le r \le a_1 + a_2 = R_{max}$ (annulus shape).
- ✅ **Singularities** occur at workspace boundaries — Jacobian becomes rank-deficient.
- ✅ **Higher DoF**: 3-DoF adds orientation; 6-DoF has up to 8 IK solutions; requires decoupling, Jacobian, or numerical methods.
- ✅ **For 3-DoF and beyond**: closed-form IK is hard; iterative Jacobian-based IK or decoupling (Pieper) is used.
- ✅ **Always verify** IK solution with FK to avoid computational errors.
- ✅ **In KTU exams**: Show ALL derivation steps; mark allocation strictly follows each step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "solve the IK problem," always (1) state the approach (geometric or algebraic), (2) compute $r$, (3) derive $\cos(\theta_2)$, (4) handle the $\pm$ ambiguity, (5) compute $\theta_1$, and (6) **verify with FK**. Skipping the verification step costs you a **1-mark** deduction in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of the Jacobian - Angular Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Derivation of the Jacobian - Angular Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DERIVATION OF THE JACOBIAN : ANGULAR VELOCITY

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> This topic directly satisfies **CO3** of PCRAT303: *“Derive the Jacobian matrix for a serial manipulator and relate joint velocities to end-effector linear and angular velocities.”* It carries high weightage in Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Jacobian Matrix** of a robotic manipulator is a time-varying linear mapping that converts the joint-space velocity vector $\dot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ into the **end-effector (task-space) velocity** vector in Cartesian space. For a manipulator with $n$ joints, the Jacobian $J(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ is written as a vertical stack of two sub-matrices:

$$
J(q) \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix} J_v(q) \\ J_\omega(q) \end{bmatrix}
$$

where:
* $J_v(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ is the **Linear Velocity Jacobian**, which maps joint rates to the linear velocity of the end-effector.
* $J_\omega(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ is the **Angular Velocity Jacobian**, which maps joint rates to the **angular velocity** of the end-effector frame.

The end-effector spatial velocity $\xi = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$ is therefore:

$$
\xi \;=\; J(q)\,\dot{q} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} J_v(q) \\ J_\omega(q) \end{bmatrix} \dot{q}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Angular Velocity Jacobian** $J_\omega$ is the sole focus of this module. Each of its $n$ columns is the angular velocity contribution of joint $i$ to the end-effector.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine you are steering a **car with a robotic steering column**. Turning the steering wheel (joint 1) makes the front wheels turn, and the **orientation of the car body** changes. Simultaneously, rotating the gear-shifter (joint 2) changes the direction the wheels point. The **Angular Velocity Jacobian** acts like a *“gear-ratio chart”* printed on the dashboard.

* For every **radian per second** you twist a particular joint, how many **radians per second** does the tool-tip *tilt*?
* The Jacobian simply answers this question — column by column — for every joint.

**Key intuition**:
* If the joint is a **revolute** type (rotational), twisting it makes the end-effector *tilt* (i.e., contributes an angular velocity).
* If the joint is a **prismatic** type (sliding), it cannot *tilt* the end-effector — so the angular velocity contribution is exactly **zero**.
* The *direction* of the tilt is always along the **axis of the joint** (specifically, the $z$-axis of the previous link frame).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Conventions Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> * **DH Convention**: Standard Denavit–Hartenberg frame assignment with $z_{i-1}$ as the axis of joint $i$.
> * **Joint variable unit**: **radians** for revolute, **metres** for prismatic.
> * **End-effector angular velocity**: expressed in the **base frame** $\{0\}$ unless otherwise specified.
> * **Default unit of $J_\omega$ columns**: dimensionless ratio (rad/s of end-effector per rad/s of joint) for revolute joints; **0** for prismatic joints.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of the Jacobian

The 6×n Jacobian has exactly two stacked blocks. For this module we isolate the lower 3×n block:

$$
J_\omega(q) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} J_{\omega_1} & J_{\omega_2} & \cdots & J_{\omega_n} \end{bmatrix}_{3 \times n}
$$

Each column $J_{\omega_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 1}$ represents the angular velocity vector imparted to the end-effector frame $\{n\}$ by **unit motion of joint $i$ alone**, with all other joints held stationary.

### 2.1.1 Why the $i$-th column has a particular form

* The angular velocity of the end-effector is generated by the *rotational chain* running from the base, through each moving link, up to the end-effector.
* When joint $i$ moves, every link downstream of it (links $i, i+1, \ldots, n$) experiences a rigid-body rotation.
* The instantaneous axis of this rotation, observed from the base frame, is **$z_{i-1}$** — the axis of joint $i$ itself, expressed in base coordinates.

### 2.1.2 Two cases based on joint type

**Case 1 — Revolute Joint (R):** The joint rotates about $z_{i-1}$. The end-effector inherits this rotation:

$$
J_{\omega_i} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}_{3 \times 1}
$$

where $z_{i-1} = R_0^{i-1} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ is the third column of the rotation matrix from $\{i-1\}$ to the base $\{0\}$.

**Case 2 — Prismatic Joint (P):** The joint translates along $z_{i-1}$. Pure translation **cannot generate rotation**:

$$
J_{\omega_i} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}_{3 \times 1}
$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise the table below — at least one 14-mark question in every KTU ESE cycle tests the angular velocity Jacobian directly.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Joint Type | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------------------|---------|------------|--------------|
| 1 | End-effector spatial velocity | $\xi = J(q)\dot{q}$ | All | $\xi \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$ |
| 2 | Column count of $J_\omega$ | $n$ (number of joints) | All | $J_\omega \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ |
| 3 | Revolute joint angular column | $J_{\omega_i} = z_{i-1}$ | Revolute | Unit vector in base frame |
| 4 | Prismatic joint angular column | $J_{\omega_i} = 0_{3 \times 1}$ | Prismatic | Always zero vector |
| 5 | Joint axis unit vector | $z_{i-1} = {}^{0}R_{i-1}\,[0,0,1]^T$ | All | Third column of rotation matrix |
| 6 | Propagation from joint rate to EE angular rate | $\omega_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} J_{\omega_i} \dot{q}_i$ | All | Superposition principle |
| 7 | Time derivative of rotation matrix | $\dot{R} = [\omega \times]\,R$ | All | $[\omega \times]$ is skew-symmetric |
| 8 | Skew-symmetric operator | $[\omega \times] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -\omega_z & \omega_y \\ \omega_z & 0 & -\omega_x \\ -\omega_y & \omega_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | All | Used in derivation |
| 9 | Singularity condition | $\det J(q) = 0$ | All | Jacobian loses rank |
| 10 | Static-force counterpart | $\tau = J^T(q) F$ | All | Static equilibrium of EE wrench $F$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall**: In the formula $J_{\omega_i} = z_{i-1}$, the $z_{i-1}$ **must** be expressed in the **base frame $\{0\}$**. Students frequently write $z_{i-1} = [0,0,1]^T$ and lose marks because they forgot to apply the rotation ${}^{0}R_{i-1}$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Singularity analysis** ($det\,J = 0$): The angular columns going linearly dependent reveals configurations where the manipulator loses one or more degrees of rotational freedom.
* **Resolved-rate motion control**: $ \dot{q} = J^{+}(q)\,\xi_{d} $ uses $J_\omega$ to plan joint trajectories that track a desired angular velocity.
* **Force/torque control**: The transpose $J_\omega^T$ maps EE moments back to joint torques — the basis of compliant and impedance control.
* **Trajectory planning**: Determining feasible angular velocity bounds at the tool helps in **milling**, **welding**, and **deburring** applications where the tool orientation must remain controlled.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION OF THE ANGULAR VELOCITY JACOBIAN

## 3.1 Starting Point — Forward Kinematics Orientation

Let ${}^{0}R_{n}(q) \in SO(3)$ denote the **rotation matrix** that orients the end-effector frame $\{n\}$ with respect to the base frame $\{0\}$. This rotation is a function of the joint vector $q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.

The **fundamental kinematic identity** (rigid-body rotation rate) states:

$$
{}^{0}\dot{R}_{n} \;=\; \left[ {}^{0}\omega_{n} \times \right] \, {}^{0}R_{n}
$$

where ${}^{0}\omega_{n} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ is the angular velocity of the end-effector expressed in the base frame, and $[{}^{0}\omega_n \times ]$ is the corresponding skew-symmetric matrix.

---

## 3.2 Decomposition of ${}^{0}R_{n}$ via DH Frames

By the chain rule of rotation matrices, the orientation of the end-effector can be expressed as a **chain of successive rotations** along the DH frames:

$$
{}^{0}R_{n} \;=\; {}^{0}R_{1}(q_1)\;{}^{1}R_{2}(q_2)\;{}^{2}R_{3}(q_3)\;\cdots\;{}^{n-1}R_{n}(q_n)
$$

Substituting into the kinematic identity:

$$
{}^{0}\dot{R}_{n} \;=\; \left[ {}^{0}\omega_{n} \times \right] \,{}^{0}R_{n}
$$

Differentiating the product using the product rule (with $n$ factors), and recognising that each ${}^{i-1}R_{i}$ depends *only* on $q_i$:

$$
{}^{0}\dot{R}_{n} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( {}^{0}R_{1}\,{}^{1}R_{2}\,\cdots\,{}^{i-2}R_{i-1} \right) \left( \frac{\partial\,{}^{i-1}R_{i}}{\partial q_i} \dot{q}_i \right) \left( {}^{i}R_{i+1}\,\cdots\,{}^{n-1}R_{n} \right)
$$

---

## 3.3 A Key Property of $SO(3)$

For any rotation matrix $R(\theta) \in SO(3)$ that rotates about a fixed unit axis $\hat{z}$ by angle $\theta$, the time derivative satisfies:

$$
\frac{\partial R(\theta)}{\partial \theta} \;=\; [\hat{z} \times]\,R(\theta)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{\partial\,{}^{i-1}R_{i}}{\partial q_i} \dot{q}_i \;=\; \left[ \hat{z}_{i-1} \times \right] {}^{i-1}R_{i}\,\dot{q}_i
$$

> Here, $\hat{z}_{i-1}$ is the **third column of the identity** in frame $\{i-1\}$ — i.e., the local unit vector along the joint axis. The cross-product matrix $[\hat{z}_{i-1} \times]$ is independent of the joint variable.

---

## 3.4 Substitution and Rearrangement

Substitute the property above into the expanded product:

$$
{}^{0}\dot{R}_{n} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( {}^{0}R_{1}\cdots{}^{i-2}R_{i-1} \right) \left[ \hat{z}_{i-1} \times \right] {}^{i-1}R_{i} \dot{q}_i \left( {}^{i}R_{i+1}\cdots{}^{n-1}R_{n} \right)
$$

Now **pre-multiply** the entire equation by the orthogonal matrix $\left( {}^{0}R_{n} \right)^{T} = \left( {}^{0}R_{n} \right)^{-1}$:

$$
\left( {}^{0}R_{n} \right)^{T} {}^{0}\dot{R}_{n} \;=\; \left[ {}^{0}\omega_{n} \times \right]
$$

But by the property of cross-product matrices under coordinate transformation, the left side can be rewritten using only the **columns of the partial rotation products**. After some algebra (which we expand step-by-step below):

$$
{}^{0}\omega_{n} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \underbrace{ \left( {}^{0}R_{i-1} \right) \hat{z}_{i-1} }_{z_{i-1} \text{ in base frame}} \dot{q}_i
$$

This is the celebrated **angular velocity propagation formula**, with the inner term being precisely the unit vector $z_{i-1}$ expressed in the base frame.

---

## 3.5 Final Identification with the Jacobian

Comparing the equation with the linear form $\omega = J_\omega(q) \dot{q}$ :

$$
{}^{0}\omega_{n} \;=\; \underbrace{ \begin{bmatrix} z_{0} & z_{1} & \cdots & z_{n-1} \end{bmatrix} }_{J_\omega(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \dot{q}_n \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.5.1 Case Distinction

**For a Revolute Joint $i$** (joint variable $q_i = \theta_i$):

$$
J_{\omega_i} \;=\; z_{i-1} \;=\; {}^{0}R_{i-1} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**For a Prismatic Joint $i$** (joint variable $q_i = d_i$):

The rotation matrix ${}^{i-1}R_{i}$ is **independent of $q_i$** for a prismatic joint, so $\frac{\partial\,{}^{i-1}R_{i}}{\partial d_i} = 0$. Therefore:

$$
J_{\omega_i} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 3.6 Exhaustive Worked Example — 2R Planar Manipulator

Consider a **2R planar manipulator** with the following DH parameters (links of length $L_1, L_2$):

| $i$ | $\alpha_{i-1}$ | $a_{i-1}$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|-----|---------------|-----------|-------|------------|
| 1   | 0             | 0         | 0     | $q_1$     |
| 2   | 0             | $L_1$     | 0     | $q_2$     |

### Step 1: Compute ${}^{0}R_{0}$ and ${}^{0}R_{1}$

$$
{}^{0}R_{0} = I_{3 \times 3} \quad\Rightarrow\quad z_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
{}^{0}R_{1} = R_z(q_1) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos q_1 & -\sin q_1 & 0 \\ \sin q_1 &  \cos q_1 & 0 \\ 0          &  0         & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Compute $z_1$

$$
z_1 \;=\; {}^{0}R_{1} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Identify joint types

Both joints are **revolute**, so:

$$
J_{\omega_1} = z_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad J_{\omega_2} = z_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Assemble the angular velocity Jacobian

$$
J_\omega(q) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5: Compute end-effector angular velocity

$$
\omega_{ee} \;=\; J_\omega(q)\,\dot{q} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of intuition**: Both revolute joints rotate the end-effector about the vertical $z$-axis of the base. The total rate is the **sum** of the individual joint rates — a physically meaningful result.

---

## 3.7 General Derivation Summary (3R Spherical-Type Wrist)

For a **3R spherical wrist** with axes intersecting at the wrist centre $o_c$:

$$
J_\omega(q) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} z_0 & z_1 & z_2 \end{bmatrix}_{3 \times 3}
$$

If the wrist joints are configured such that the rotation matrices give:

$$
z_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0\\0\\1 \end{bmatrix},\; z_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin q_1 \\ \cos q_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix},\; z_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin q_1 \cos q_2 \\ \cos q_1 \cos q_2 \\ \sin q_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then the angular velocity of the tool is:

$$
\omega_{tool} \;=\; z_0 \dot{q}_1 + z_1 \dot{q}_2 + z_2 \dot{q}_3
$$

The determinant of this $J_\omega$ is non-zero for almost all configurations — confirming the wrist is **non-singular** in the orientation sub-space.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Angular Velocity Derivation

The following block diagram maps the **logical flow** of the derivation — from kinematic relations to the final closed-form Jacobian column.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forward Kinematics Orientation 0Rn] --> B{Differentiate w.r.t. time}
    B --> C[Apply Skew Symmetric Identity dot R = omega cross times R]
    C --> D[Chain Rule on Product of n Rotation Matrices]
    D --> E[Use Property dR_i by dq_i = z_i-1 cross times R_i]
    E --> F{Is Joint i Revolute?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Set J omega i = z sub i-1 expressed in Base Frame]
    F -- No --> H[Set J omega i = 0 vector]
    G --> I[Stack n Columns Vertically to form 3 by n Matrix]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Angular Velocity Jacobian J omega of q]
    J --> K[Multiply by Joint Velocity Vector q dot to get EE Angular Velocity omega]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — From Joint Rate to End-Effector Angular Velocity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J1[Joint 1 Rate q1 dot] --> M1[Column z0]
    J2[Joint 2 Rate q2 dot] --> M2[Column z1]
    Jn[Joint n Rate qn dot] --> Mn[Column z sub n-1]
    M1 --> S[Sum block]
    M2 --> S
    Mn --> S
    S --> O[End Effector Angular Velocity omega]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Vector Geometry of $z_{i-1}$ in the Base Frame

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Base[Base Frame 0] -->|origin O0| F1[Frame 0]
    F1 -->|axis z0| Z0[z0 along joint 1]
    Z0 -->|R01| F2[Frame 1]
    F2 -->|axis z1| Z1[z1 along joint 2]
    Z1 -->|R12| F3[Frame 2]
    F3 -->|axis z2| Z2[z2 along joint 3]
    Z2 -->|Rn-1 to n| EE[End Effector Frame n]
    Z0 --> J1col[Column 1 of J omega]
    Z1 --> J2col[Column 2 of J omega]
    Z2 --> J3col[Column 3 of J omega]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Q1 (3 Marks)**
> **Q:** *Define the Jacobian matrix of a robotic manipulator. What does the angular velocity Jacobian $J_\omega$ represent?*
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The Jacobian matrix $J(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ is a first-order linear mapping that relates the joint-space velocity vector $\dot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ to the end-effector spatial velocity $\xi = [v^T, \omega^T]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$. It is composed of a linear-velocity block $J_v$ and an angular-velocity block $J_\omega$. The angular velocity Jacobian $J_\omega \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ specifically maps joint rates to the **angular velocity vector** of the end-effector, expressed in the base frame. Its $i$-th column $J_{\omega_i}$ gives the contribution of joint $i$ to the end-effector rotation rate. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Q2 (3 Marks)**
> **Q:** *For a prismatic joint, the corresponding column of the angular velocity Jacobian is zero. Justify.*
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A prismatic joint produces **pure translation** along its axis $z_{i-1}$ and does **not** cause any rotation of the subsequent links. Since the rotation matrix ${}^{i-1}R_i$ of a prismatic joint is independent of the joint variable $d_i$, the partial derivative $\partial R / \partial d_i = 0$. Consequently, the angular velocity contribution to the end-effector is identically zero, giving $J_{\omega_i} = [0, 0, 0]^T$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choose either A or B)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **angular velocity Jacobian** $J_\omega$ of a serial n-DOF manipulator. Clearly state the form of $J_{\omega_i}$ for revolute and prismatic joints. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a 3R manipulator with DH parameters as given below, compute $J_\omega(q)$ and the end-effector angular velocity at $q_1 = 30^\circ$, $q_2 = 45^\circ$, $q_3 = 60^\circ$ when $\dot{q} = [1, 0.5, 0.2]^T$ rad/s. **(7 marks)**
>
> | $i$ | $\alpha_{i-1}$ | $a_{i-1}$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
> |-----|---------------|-----------|-------|------------|
> | 1   | 0°            | 0         | 0     | $q_1$     |
> | 2   | 90°           | 0         | 0     | $q_2$     |
> | 3   | 0°            | $L_1$     | 0     | $q_3$     |

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Start with the orientation of the end-effector:**

$$
{}^{0}R_{n}(q) = {}^{0}R_{1}(q_1)\,{}^{1}R_{2}(q_2)\,\cdots\,{}^{n-1}R_{n}(q_n)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the rigid-body kinematic identity:**

$$
{}^{0}\dot{R}_{n} = \left[ {}^{0}\omega_{n} \times \right]\,{}^{0}R_{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the chain using the product rule:**

$$
{}^{0}\dot{R}_{n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \prod_{k=1}^{i-1} {}^{k-1}R_{k} \right) \frac{\partial\,{}^{i-1}R_{i}}{\partial q_i} \dot{q}_i \left( \prod_{k=i+1}^{n} {}^{k-1}R_{k} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the SO(3) identity** $\partial R_i / \partial q_i = [\hat{z}_{i-1} \times]\,R_i$:

$$
{}^{0}\dot{R}_{n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \prod_{k=1}^{i-1} {}^{k-1}R_{k} \right) [\hat{z}_{i-1} \times]\,{}^{i-1}R_{i}\,\dot{q}_i \left( \prod_{k=i+1}^{n} {}^{k-1}R_{k} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Pre-multiply by $({}^{0}R_{n})^{T}$** to extract the skew-symmetric matrix and identify:

$$
{}^{0}\omega_{n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( {}^{0}R_{i-1}\,\hat{z}_{i-1} \right) \dot{q}_i
$$

**Step 6 — Define the column** $J_{\omega_i} = {}^{0}R_{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1}$:

$$
J_{\omega_i} = \begin{cases} z_{i-1} = {}^{0}R_{i-1}\,[0,0,1]^T, & \text{if joint } i \text{ is revolute} \\ [0,0,0]^T, & \text{if joint } i \text{ is prismatic} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 7 — Assemble the final 3×n matrix:**

$$
J_\omega(q) = \begin{bmatrix} J_{\omega_1} & J_{\omega_2} & \cdots & J_{\omega_n} \end{bmatrix}_{3 \times n}
$$

**(Full 7 marks awarded for the complete derivation, with 2 marks reserved for explicit case distinction.)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Identify joint types and base axes.**

All three joints are **revolute**. The base joint axis is $z_0 = [0,0,1]^T$.

**Step 2 — Compute ${}^{0}R_{1}$ using $R_z(q_1) \cdot R_x(90^\circ)$:**

$$
{}^{0}R_{1} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos q_1 & 0 & \sin q_1 \\ \sin q_1 & 0 & -\cos q_1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $z_1$:**

$$
z_1 = {}^{0}R_{1} \begin{bmatrix} 0\\0\\1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sin q_1 \\ -\cos q_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ${}^{0}R_{2} = {}^{0}R_{1}\,{}^{1}R_{2}$** where ${}^{1}R_{2} = R_z(q_2)$:

$$
{}^{0}R_{2} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos q_1 \cos q_2 & -\cos q_1 \sin q_2 & \sin q_1 \\ \sin q_1 \cos q_2 & -\sin q_1 \sin q_2 & -\cos q_1 \\ \sin q_2 & \cos q_2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $z_2$:**

$$
z_2 = {}^{0}R_{2} \begin{bmatrix} 0\\0\\1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sin q_1 \\ -\cos q_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values** $q_1 = 30^\circ, q_2 = 45^\circ$:

$$
z_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0\\0\\1 \end{bmatrix},\quad z_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5000 \\ -0.8660 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad z_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5000 \\ -0.8660 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Assemble $J_\omega$:**

$$
J_\omega = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0.5000 & 0.5000 \\ 0 & -0.8660 & -0.8660 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute end-effector angular velocity** $\omega = J_\omega \dot{q}$:

$$
\omega = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0.5000 & 0.5000 \\ 0 & -0.8660 & -0.8660 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.35 \\ -0.6062 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(Full 7 marks: 3 marks for Jacobian computation, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final angular velocity vector.)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
> **(a)** Starting from the time derivative of the rotation matrix, derive the angular velocity Jacobian for an n-DOF manipulator. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 2R planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 1$ m, $L_2 = 0.5$ m. At $q_1 = 60^\circ$ and $q_2 = 30^\circ$, compute (i) the angular velocity Jacobian $J_\omega$ and (ii) the end-effector angular velocity when $\dot{q} = [2, 1]^T$ rad/s. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Kinematic Identity** (already given in main derivation):

$$
{}^{0}\dot{R}_{n} = \left[ {}^{0}\omega_{n} \times \right] {}^{0}R_{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the product-rule chain differentiation** to obtain:

$$
{}^{0}\dot{R}_{n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \prod_{k=1}^{i-1} {}^{k-1}R_{k} \right) \frac{\partial\,{}^{i-1}R_{i}}{\partial q_i} \dot{q}_i \left( \prod_{k=i+1}^{n} {}^{k-1}R_{k} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Use the $SO(3)$ property** to obtain the column form (4 marks for steps 1–3):

$$
J_{\omega_i} = \begin{cases} {}^{0}R_{i-1}\,[0,0,1]^T, & \text{revolute joint} \\ 0, & \text{prismatic joint} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Final assembly** (3 marks):

$$
J_\omega(q) = \begin{bmatrix} z_0 & z_1 & \cdots & z_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the joint axes.** Both joints are revolute about the vertical axis, so $z_0 = z_1 = [0, 0, 1]^T$.

**Step 2 — Assemble the Jacobian:**

$$
J_\omega = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the end-effector angular velocity:**

$$
\omega = J_\omega \dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The end-effector rotates about the vertical $z$-axis at **3 rad/s** (counter-clockwise), which equals the sum of the joint rates. This is a direct consequence of the serial rotational chain. **(7 marks: 2 for Jacobian, 2 for substitution, 3 for physical interpretation.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in $J_\omega$ Problems:**
>
> 1. **Frame mismatch (–2 to –3 marks)**: Writing $J_{\omega_i} = [0,0,1]^T$ *without* multiplying by ${}^{0}R_{i-1}$. The vector $z_{i-1}$ **must** be expressed in the **base frame**.
> 2. **Skew-symmetric confusion (–1 mark)**: Mixing up $[\omega \times]$ with $[\omega \times]^T$. The skew-symmetric matrix is **not symmetric**.
> 3. **Prismatic joint trap (–2 marks)**: Assigning a non-zero angular column for a prismatic joint. The column **must** be the zero vector.
> 4. **Forgetting the third column rule (–1 mark)**: In DH convention, the joint axis is *always* the third column. Some students use the first or second column incorrectly.
> 5. **No case distinction in derivation (–2 marks)**: The examiner will explicitly check whether you have stated *both* revolute and prismatic cases. A single line for revolute alone is incomplete.
> 6. **Dimension mismatch (–1 mark)**: $J_\omega$ is **always $3 \times n$**. Writing $2 \times 2$ for a planar 2R manipulator is a frequent error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **Jacobian** $J(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ stacks $J_v$ (linear) on top of $J_\omega$ (angular).
- The **angular velocity Jacobian** is the lower $3 \times n$ block: $J_\omega = [z_0 \;\; z_1 \;\; \cdots \;\; z_{n-1}]$.
- For a **revolute joint** $i$, the column is the **joint axis** $z_{i-1}$ expressed in the **base frame**: $J_{\omega_i} = {}^{0}R_{i-1} [0, 0, 1]^T$.
- For a **prismatic joint** $i$, the column is **always zero**: $J_{\omega_i} = [0, 0, 0]^T$.
- The end-effector angular velocity is $\omega = J_\omega(q) \dot{q}$, obtained by **linear superposition** of column contributions.
- The derivation uses the **rigid-body kinematic identity** ${}^{0}\dot{R}_{n} = [{}^{0}\omega_n \times] {}^{0}R_n$ and the **SO(3) property** $\partial R / \partial \theta = [\hat{z} \times] R$.
- **Singularities** occur when $\det J = 0$, i.e., when the angular columns become **linearly dependent**.
- The angular Jacobian feeds into the **velocity relationship** $\xi = J \dot{q}$ and the **static-force relationship** $\tau = J^T F$.
- Common KTU testable facts: column count = number of joints; dimension = $3 \times n$; prismatic columns are zero; revolute columns are unit vectors along the joint axis in the base frame.
- For **2R planar manipulators**, $J_\omega$ always has the simple form $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — a useful sanity check during exams.
- For **3R wrists**, $J_\omega$ reduces to the upper $3 \times 3$ block of the full Jacobian, often invertible except at gimbal-lock configurations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** Always carry out the substitution in two passes — first symbolic (in terms of $q_i$), then numerical (in degrees or radians). Examiners allot separate marks for each pass, and skipping the symbolic step forfeits the conceptual marks even if the final number is correct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

In the context of robotic manipulators, **Linear Velocity** of a point is defined as the time rate of change of its position vector with respect to a chosen reference frame. For a rigid body undergoing general motion (translation + rotation), the linear velocity of an arbitrary point **P** is the vector sum of the velocity of the origin and the contribution from angular motion.

Mathematically, for a point located on link *i* whose position is denoted as **P**, the linear velocity is:

$$
\mathbf{v} \;=\; \frac{d\mathbf{p}}{dt}
$$

For a rigid link, the generalized linear velocity expression becomes:

$$
\mathbf{v}_P \;=\; \mathbf{v}_O \;+\; \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{v}_P$ = linear velocity of point **P** (in m/s)
- $\mathbf{v}_O$ = linear velocity of a reference point **O** on the rigid body
- $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ = angular velocity of the body (in rad/s)
- $\mathbf{r}_{P/O}$ = position vector from **O** to **P**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Linear velocity is a cornerstone of the **Differential Kinematics** of manipulators. It bridges **forward kinematics** (position) with **inverse velocity kinematics** through the **Jacobian matrix**. Mastery of this topic is essential for trajectory planning, singularity analysis, and real-time control of robotic arms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **ceiling fan** rotating about its central axis. Every particle on a blade traces a circular path, but the **speed of that particle** (how fast it sweeps through space along its tangent) depends on two things:
1. How fast the **hub** itself is moving (translation — usually zero in a fan).
2. How fast the blade is **spinning** (rotation) and **how far** the particle is from the axis.

A particle near the tip moves **fast**, while a particle near the hub moves **slow** — even though the angular velocity is the same everywhere. The "linear velocity" is that *tangential sweeping speed*, and it equals $\omega \times r$.

In a **robotic manipulator**, every link behaves like a tiny fan. The end-effector's linear velocity is the cumulative effect of all links rotating and translating — computed link-by-link through **forward recursion**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Robot Range |
|----------|--------|---------|---------------------|
| Linear velocity | $\mathbf{v}$ | **m/s** (metres per second) | 0.1 – 5.0 m/s |
| Angular velocity | $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ | **rad/s** (radians per second) | 0.1 – 10 rad/s |
| Position vector | $\mathbf{r}$ | **m** (metres) | 0.05 – 2.0 m |
| Time derivative | $\dfrac{d}{dt}$ | **s⁻¹** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board examinations, the symbol convention follows **Craig's** *Introduction to Robotics*. The left-superscript denotes the **reference frame** (e.g., $^i\mathbf{v}_i$ = velocity of frame $i$ expressed in frame $i$). Always carry the frame superscript when performing recursion — this is a frequent source of mark deduction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Logical Breakdown

#### Step 1 — Velocity of a Point on a Purely Translating Body
If a body translates without rotation ($\boldsymbol{\omega} = \mathbf{0}$), then every point on the body has the **same linear velocity**:

$$
\mathbf{v}_P \;=\; \mathbf{v}_O
$$

- **Why:** Because all points shift by the same infinitesimal amount in the same instant.
- **How to remember:** A passenger walking inside a moving train has the train's velocity, irrespective of where they stand.

#### Step 2 — Velocity of a Point on a Purely Rotating Body
