For a body rotating about a **fixed point O** with angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$, the position of any point **P** is $\mathbf{r}_{P/O}$. The point sweeps along a circular arc, giving a tangential velocity:

$$
\mathbf{v}_P \;=\; \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}
$$

- **Why:** In time $dt$, the point moves perpendicular to both the radius and the axis of rotation. The cross product captures this orthogonality.
- **Magnitude:** $\vert \mathbf{v}_P \vert = \vert \boldsymbol{\omega} \vert \cdot \vert \mathbf{r}_{P/O} \vert \cdot \sin\theta$

#### Step 3 — General Rigid-Body Motion (Translation + Rotation)
Combining the two cases:

$$
\mathbf{v}_P \;=\; \mathbf{v}_O \;+\; \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}
$$

This is the **fundamental rigid-body velocity equation** and forms the basis of the manipulator linear-velocity propagation.

#### Step 4 — Frame-Dependent Representation
If a point's position is given in frame $\{i\}$ as $^i\mathbf{P}$ and the frame is rotating, then the time derivative taken in the **stationary world frame $\{0\}$** is:

$$
^0\dot{\mathbf{P}} \;=\; ^0\dot{\mathbf{P}}_{rel} \;+\; \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \, ^0\mathbf{P}
$$

- $\,^0\dot{\mathbf{P}}_{rel}$ = rate of change as seen by a co-rotating observer (zero for a fixed point on a rigid body)
- For a **rigidly attached** point on link $i$: $^0\dot{\mathbf{P}} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \, ^0\mathbf{P}$

#### Step 5 — Forward Linear-Velocity Propagation (Link to Link)
For successive links $i$ and $i+1$:

$$
^0\mathbf{v}_{i+1} \;=\; ^0\mathbf{v}_i \;+\; ^0\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \, ^0\mathbf{r}_{i+1/i}
$$

Equivalently, expressed in the local frame of link $(i+1)$:

$$
^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1} \;=\; ^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \, \bigl( \,^i\mathbf{v}_i \;+\; ^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*} \bigr)
$$

where $^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}$ is the position of the origin of frame $\{i+1\}$ expressed in frame $\{i\}$.

- **Why this matters:** It lets us propagate velocity from the base (where $^0\mathbf{v}_0 = \mathbf{0}$ for a fixed base) outwards to the end-effector.

#### Step 6 — Backward Linear-Velocity Propagation
Starting from the end-effector and working **toward the base**:

$$
^i\mathbf{v}_i \;=\; ^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \cdot {^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1}} \;+\; ^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}
$$

- **Why:** Useful when the end-effector's velocity is known (e.g., specified task-space trajectory) and we want to find base or intermediate link velocities.

#### Step 7 — Linear Velocity and the Jacobian
The end-effector's linear velocity in the world frame is related to joint rates through the **Jacobian matrix**:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} ^0\mathbf{v}_{0n} \\ ^0\boldsymbol{\omega}_{0n} \end{bmatrix} \;=\; {^0\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})} \; \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

The **upper 3×n block** of $^0\mathbf{J}$ is the **linear-velocity Jacobian** $^0\mathbf{J}_v$, and the **lower 3×n block** is the **angular-velocity Jacobian** $^0\mathbf{J}_\omega$:

$$
^0\mathbf{v}_{0n} \;=\; ^0\mathbf{J}_v(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{v} = \dfrac{d\mathbf{p}}{dt}$ | Point velocity (definition) | m/s |
| 2 | $\mathbf{v}_P = \mathbf{v}_O + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}$ | Rigid-body point velocity | m/s |
| 3 | $^0\mathbf{v}_{i+1} = \,^0\mathbf{v}_i + \,^0\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{i+1/i}$ | Forward recursion (world frame) | m/s |
| 4 | $^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1} = \,^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \bigl( \,^i\mathbf{v}_i + \,^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*} \bigr)$ | Forward recursion (local frame) | m/s |
| 5 | $^i\mathbf{v}_i = \,^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1} + \,^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}$ | Backward recursion (local frame) | m/s |
| 6 | $^0\mathbf{v}_{0n} = \,^0\mathbf{J}_v(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | End-effector linear velocity from Jacobian | m/s |
| 7 | $\mathbf{v} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}$ | Pure rotation case (no translation) | m/s |
| 8 | $\vert \mathbf{v} \vert = \omega \, r \, \sin\theta$ | Magnitude of cross-product velocity | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Linear-velocity analysis drives **resolved-rate motion control**, where the desired Cartesian tool speed is converted into joint actuator commands via $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{v}$. It also enables **collision-free trajectory planning** in cluttered work cells (used in automotive welding and pick-and-place assembly lines).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Velocity of a Point on a Rotating Rigid Body

**Statement:** Show that for a point **P** on a rigid body rotating with angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ about a fixed point **O**, the linear velocity is $\mathbf{v}_P = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}$.

**Setup:**
Let $\mathbf{r}_{P/O}(t)$ be the position vector of **P** relative to **O** at time $t$. Since the body is rigid, the magnitude $\vert \mathbf{r}_{P/O} \vert$ is constant, but the direction changes as the body rotates.

**Step 1 — Express rotation through a small angle $d\theta$**
In a small time interval $dt$, the body rotates by an angle $d\theta$ about the axis defined by the unit vector $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$. The angular velocity is $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \omega \hat{\mathbf{u}}$, and $d\theta = \omega\,dt$.

**Step 2 — Geometric change in the position vector**
The tip of the position vector $\mathbf{r}$ traces a circular arc of radius $\vert \mathbf{r} \vert \sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\mathbf{r}$ and $\boldsymbol{\omega}$. The arc length swept is:

$$
ds \;=\; \vert \mathbf{r} \vert \sin\theta \cdot d\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Direction of the change**
The vector $d\mathbf{r}$ is perpendicular to the plane containing $\mathbf{r}$ and $\boldsymbol{\omega}$. A vector perpendicular to both, with the right magnitude, is precisely the cross product $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}\,dt$.

**Step 4 — Formalise the derivative**

$$
\mathbf{v}_P \;=\; \lim_{dt \to 0} \frac{d\mathbf{r}}{dt} \;=\; \lim_{dt \to 0} \frac{\boldsymbol{\omega}\,dt \times \mathbf{r}}{dt} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The cross product emerges naturally because rotation moves the vector perpendicular to the radius, and the cross product is the only binary vector operation that produces a vector perpendicular to both operands with magnitude equal to the product of their magnitudes and the sine of the angle between them. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Forward Linear-Velocity Propagation Between Successive Links

**Statement:** Derive the relation $^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1} = \,^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R}\bigl(\,^i\mathbf{v}_i + \,^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}\bigr)$.

**Step 1 — Velocity of the origin of frame $\{i+1\}$ in the world frame**

The origin of frame $\{i+1\}$, denoted $O_{i+1}$, is a point rigidly attached to link $i$. Its position in the world frame is $^0\mathbf{P}_{i+1}$, and its velocity is $^0\mathbf{v}_{i+1}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the rigid-body velocity equation to link $i$**
The origin of frame $\{i\}$, $O_i$, is also a point on the same rigid link $i$. Using the rigid-body equation:

$$
^0\mathbf{v}_{i+1} \;=\; ^0\mathbf{v}_i \;+\; ^0\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{i+1/i}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the local position vector**
The vector from $O_i$ to $O_{i+1}$ expressed in frame $\{i\}$ is $^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}$. To express it in the world frame:

$$
^0\mathbf{r}_{i+1/i} \;=\; ^0_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the velocity equation**

$$
^0\mathbf{v}_{i+1} \;=\; ^0\mathbf{v}_i \;+\; ^0\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \bigl(\,^0_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Push the rotation inside the cross product**
Using the identity $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}) = (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a}) \times (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{b})$:

$$
^0\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^0_i\mathbf{R}\cdot\,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*} \;=\; \bigl(\,^0\boldsymbol{\omega}_i\bigr) \times \bigl(\,^0_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}\bigr) \;=\; \bigl(\,^0_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i\bigr) \times \bigl(\,^0_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}\bigr)
$$

So:

$$
^0\mathbf{v}_{i+1} \;=\; ^0\mathbf{v}_i \;+\; \bigl(\,^0_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i\bigr) \times \bigl(\,^0_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}\bigr)
$$

**Step 6 — Re-express both velocities in frame $\{i\}$**
Multiply the entire equation on the **left** by $^{i+1}_0\mathbf{R}$ to convert from world frame to frame $(i+1)$:

$$
^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1} \;=\; ^{i+1}_0\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^0\mathbf{v}_i \;+\; \bigl(^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i\bigr) \times \bigl(^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}\bigr)
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the left-hand side** (since $^{i+1}_0\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^0_i\mathbf{R} = \,^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R}$):

$$
^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1} \;=\; \,^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\mathbf{v}_i \;+\; \bigl(^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i\bigr) \times \bigl(^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}\bigr)
$$

**Step 8 — Pull the rotation out of the cross product** (using the same identity, backwards):

$$
^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1} \;=\; \,^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \Bigl[\,^i\mathbf{v}_i \;+\; \,^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*} \Bigr]
$$

**Conversion logic:** We have successfully expressed the velocity of frame $(i+1)$'s origin in frame $(i+1)$, using only quantities local to frame $i$. This is the cleanest form for **computational implementation** in robot controllers, and it is the canonical form used in KTU problems. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Linear-Velocity Jacobian Column Derivation

**Statement:** Derive the *i*-th column of the linear-velocity Jacobian $^0\mathbf{J}_v$ for a revolute joint.

**Step 1 — Recursive velocity contribution**
The end-effector origin is $O_n$. From the forward propagation, the *i*-th joint's motion contributes to the end-effector velocity only through a chain of cross products along the kinematic chain.

**Step 2 — For a revolute joint axis $\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1}$**

When joint *i* is revolute, rotating at $\dot{q}_i$, the angular velocity contribution at the end-effector is $\dot{q}_i \, \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1}$. The resulting linear velocity contribution at the end-effector is:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{contrib,i} \;=\; \dot{q}_i \, \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1} \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{n/i-1}
$$

**Step 3 — For a prismatic joint axis $\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1}$**

For a prismatic joint, there is **no rotation**, only translation along $\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1}$. The contribution is:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{contrib,i} \;=\; \dot{q}_i \, \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the Jacobian column**
The *i*-th column of $^0\mathbf{J}_v$ is therefore:

$$
^0\mathbf{J}_{v,i} \;=\; 
\begin{cases}
\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1} \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{n/i-1} & \text{(revolute)} \\[4pt]
\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1} & \text{(prismatic)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is a **kinematic decoupling**: revolute joints contribute a tangential velocity (cross product), while prismatic joints contribute a direct axial translation. This decoupling is heavily tested in KTU 14-mark problems. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — 2R Planar Manipulator

**Problem:** A 2R planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 1$ m, $L_2 = 0.5$ m, joint angles $q_1 = 30°$, $q_2 = 45°$, and joint rates $\dot{q}_1 = 0.5$ rad/s, $\dot{q}_2 = 1.0$ rad/s. Compute the end-effector linear velocity $^0\mathbf{v}_{0,2}$ in the world frame.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — End-effector position in the world frame**

$$
^0\mathbf{P}_{0,2} \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\[4pt]
L_1 \sin q_1 + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) \\[4pt]
0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
q_1 + q_2 \;=\; 30° + 45° \;=\; 75°
$$

$$
^0\mathbf{P}_{0,2} \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \cos 30° + 0.5 \cos 75° \\[4pt]
1 \sin 30° + 0.5 \sin 75° \\[4pt]
0
\end{bmatrix} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
0.8660 + 0.1294 \\[4pt]
0.5000 + 0.4829 \\[4pt]
0
\end{bmatrix} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
0.9954 \\[4pt]
0.9829 \\[4pt]
0
\end{bmatrix} \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the linear-velocity Jacobian for a 2R planar arm**

$$
^0\mathbf{J}_v \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
-L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \\[4pt]
L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \\[4pt]
0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
^0\mathbf{J}_v \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.5 - 0.4829 & -0.4829 \\[4pt]
0.8660 + 0.1294 & 0.1294 \\[4pt]
0 & 0
\end{bmatrix} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.9829 & -0.4829 \\[4pt]
0.9954 & 0.1294 \\[4pt]
0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the joint-rate vector**

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
0.5 \\[4pt]
1.0
\end{bmatrix} \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
^0\mathbf{v}_{0,2} \;=\; \,^0\mathbf{J}_v \cdot \dot{\mathbf{q}} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.9829(0.5) + (-0.4829)(1.0) \\[4pt]
0.9954(0.5) + (0.1294)(1.0) \\[4pt]
0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
^0\mathbf{v}_{0,2} \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.4915 - 0.4829 \\[4pt]
0.4977 + 0.1294 \\[4pt]
0
\end{bmatrix} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.9744 \\[4pt]
0.6271 \\[4pt]
0
\end{bmatrix} \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude and interpretation**

$$
\vert \,^0\mathbf{v}_{0,2} \vert \;=\; \sqrt{(-0.9744)^2 + (0.6271)^2} 
\;=\; \sqrt{0.9495 + 0.3933} 
\;=\; \sqrt{1.3428} 
\;\approx\; 1.159 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The negative $x$-component indicates the end-effector is moving in the $-x$ direction (rotating backward), while the positive $y$-component shows upward motion. The Jacobian is the **linear operator** that maps joint rates to Cartesian end-effector velocity — the most important result in differential kinematics. $\blacksquare$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Linear-Velocity Propagation Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Base Frame Frame 0: v0 = 0, omega0 = 0]:::startNode
    B[Link 1 Frame 1: Compute omega1 and v1]:::procNode
    C[Link 2 Frame 2: Compute omega2 and v2]:::procNode
    D[Link n Frame n: Compute omegan and vn]:::procNode
    E[End Effector Frame n: Final Linear Velocity vn]:::endNode
    F[Apply Forward Recursion Formula]:::formulaNode
    G[Apply Cross Product omega x r]:::formulaNode
    H[Apply Rotation Matrix to Local Frame]:::formulaNode
    I[Jacobian Formulation: v = Jv times q dot]:::formulaNode
    J[Output: End Effector Linear Velocity in World Frame]:::outputNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    B --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> C
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> I
    I --> J

    classDef startNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Point O on Rigid Body: Reference Origin]:::originNode

    D[Linear Velocity Vector: vP = omega cross r subscript P slash O]:::velocityNode
    E[Tangential Direction: Perpendicular to r]:::directionNode
    F[Axis of Rotation: Along omega]:::axisNode

    A --> C

    F --> G
```

### 4.3 Diagram Interpretation Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:**
> - **Diagram 1** traces the *computational pipeline*: starting from a stationary base, velocities propagate outward link-by-link via forward recursion, finally producing the end-effector's linear velocity. The Jacobian formulation acts as a parallel, equivalent computational path.
> - **Diagram 2** decomposes the **cross-product velocity** into its three conceptual ingredients: the rotation axis, the radius vector, and the resulting tangential velocity with its magnitude relation.
> - In board examinations, drawing a similar block diagram before the calculation earns **methodology marks** even if the final numerical answer has minor errors.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define linear velocity of a point on a rigid body in motion. Derive the expression for the linear velocity of a point on a body undergoing pure rotation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Linear velocity of a point is the time rate of change of its position vector with respect to a chosen reference frame: $\mathbf{v} = d\mathbf{p}/dt$.

**Derivation (2 marks):** For a rigid body rotating with angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ about a fixed point **O**, the position vector of point **P** is $\mathbf{r}_{P/O}$. In a small time $dt$, the body rotates through angle $d\theta = \omega\,dt$. The tip of $\mathbf{r}_{P/O}$ traces an arc of length $\vert \mathbf{r}_{P/O} \vert \sin\theta \cdot d\theta$ in a direction perpendicular to both $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ and $\mathbf{r}_{P/O}$. This is exactly the cross product:

$$
\mathbf{v}_P \;=\; \lim_{dt \to 0} \frac{\boldsymbol{\omega}\,dt \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}}{dt} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Write the forward recursion formula for linear velocity in a robotic manipulator. What is the initial condition for a manipulator with a fixed base?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (2 marks):** The forward recursion formula for the linear velocity of the origin of frame $\{i+1\}$ expressed in frame $\{i+1\}$ is:

$$
^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1} \;=\; \,^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \Bigl[\,^i\mathbf{v}_i \;+\; \,^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*} \Bigr]
$$

**Initial condition (1 mark):** For a manipulator with a **fixed (stationary) base**, the initial linear velocity of frame $\{0\}$ is $\,^0\mathbf{v}_0 = \mathbf{0}$ (zero vector), and the initial angular velocity is $\,^0\boldsymbol{\omega}_0 = \mathbf{0}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** *Derive the forward linear-velocity propagation formula for a robotic manipulator. Clearly state all assumptions and explain the role of the rotation matrix in the derivation. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A 3R spatial manipulator has the following DH parameters: $a_1 = 0.5$ m, $a_2 = 0.4$ m, $d_3 = 0.2$ m, with all joint angles $q_1 = q_2 = q_3 = 0$ initially. If $\dot{q}_1 = 0.2$ rad/s, $\dot{q}_2 = 0.4$ rad/s, $\dot{q}_3 = 0.1$ m/s (prismatic), compute the linear velocity of the end-effector in the world frame. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Forward Linear-Velocity Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Setup and assumption (1 mark):** Consider two successive links $i$ and $i+1$ connected by a joint. Both $O_i$ and $O_{i+1}$ are points rigidly attached to link $i$. The velocity of $O_{i+1}$ in the world frame is $^0\mathbf{v}_{i+1}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the rigid-body velocity equation (2 marks):**

$$
^0\mathbf{v}_{i+1} \;=\; ^0\mathbf{v}_i \;+\; ^0\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{i+1/i}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the position vector in frame $\{i\}$ (1 mark):**
$^0\mathbf{r}_{i+1/i} = \,^0_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}$

**Step 4 — Convert to local frame $\{i+1\}$ (2 marks):** Multiply throughout by $^{i+1}_0\mathbf{R}$ and use the identity $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}) = (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a}) \times (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{b})$:

$$
^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1} \;=\; \,^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \Bigl[\,^i\mathbf{v}_i \;+\; \,^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*} \Bigr]
$$

**Step 5 — Role of rotation matrix (1 mark):** The $^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R}$ rotation matrix re-expresses the world-frame-derived velocity back into the local frame of the child link, ensuring **frame consistency** for the next recursive step.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — End-Effector Velocity Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify joint types (1 mark):** Joint 1 — revolute, Joint 2 — revolute, Joint 3 — prismatic.

**Step 2 — Construct the Jacobian (3 marks):** Using the cross-product formula for the first two columns and direct axis for the third:

$$
^0\mathbf{J}_v \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_0 \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{3/0} & \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_1 \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{3/1} & \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

At the initial configuration, $\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_0 = \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_1 = \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_2 = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}^T$ (vertical axes).

The position vectors are:
- $^0\mathbf{r}_{3/0} = \begin{bmatrix}0.9 & 0 & 0.2\end{bmatrix}^T$ m (sum of link lengths along x and z)
- $^0\mathbf{r}_{3/1} = \begin{bmatrix}0.4 & 0 & 0.2\end{bmatrix}^T$ m

**Step 3 — Compute cross products (2 marks):**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_0 \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{3/0} \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 0 \\ 1\end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix}0.9 \\ 0 \\ 0.2\end{bmatrix} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}0 \cdot 0.2 - 1 \cdot 0 \\ 1 \cdot 0.9 - 0 \cdot 0.2 \\ 0 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot 0.9\end{bmatrix} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 0.9 \\ 0\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_1 \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{3/1} \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 0 \\ 1\end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix}0.4 \\ 0 \\ 0.2\end{bmatrix} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 0.4 \\ 0\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_2 \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 0 \\ 1\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble and multiply (1 mark):**

$$
^0\mathbf{v}_{0,3} \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 0 \\
0.9 & 0.4 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}0.2 \\ 0.4 \\ 0.1\end{bmatrix} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 0.9(0.2) + 0.4(0.4) \\ 0.1\end{bmatrix} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 0.34 \\ 0.1\end{bmatrix} \text{m/s}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** *Explain the concept of the linear-velocity Jacobian. Show that for a revolute joint, the i-th column of $^0\mathbf{J}_v$ is $\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1} \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{n/i-1}$, while for a prismatic joint it is simply $\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1}$. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A SCARA robot has the following parameters: $L_1 = 0.4$ m, $L_2 = 0.3$ m, $d_4 = 0.1$ m (variable). At the instant when $q_1 = 60°$, $q_2 = -30°$, $q_3 = 0$ (revolute joints 1 and 2 are active; joint 3 is prismatic with $\dot{q}_3 = 0$), with $\dot{q}_1 = 1$ rad/s and $\dot{q}_2 = 2$ rad/s, determine the linear velocity of the tool tip in the world frame. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Jacobian Column Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Concept of linear-velocity Jacobian (2 marks):**
The linear-velocity Jacobian $^0\mathbf{J}_v(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ linearly maps joint rates to the end-effector's linear velocity in the world frame:

$$
^0\mathbf{v}_{0,n} \;=\; \,^0\mathbf{J}_v(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Each column $^0\mathbf{J}_{v,i}$ represents the contribution of joint $i$ to the end-effector linear velocity.

**Step 2 — Revolute joint derivation (3 marks):** When joint $i$ is revolute and rotates at $\dot{q}_i$ about axis $\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1}$, the angular velocity contribution at the end-effector is $\dot{q}_i \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1}$. The induced linear velocity at the tool tip is:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{contrib,i} \;=\; \bigl(\dot{q}_i \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1}\bigr) \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{n/i-1}
$$

Equating with $^0\mathbf{J}_{v,i} \dot{q}_i$:

$$
^0\mathbf{J}_{v,i} \;=\; \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1} \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{n/i-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Prismatic joint derivation (2 marks):** For a prismatic joint, motion is **pure translation** along $\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1}$, with **zero angular velocity** contribution. Thus:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{contrib,i} \;=\; \dot{q}_i \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad ^0\mathbf{J}_{v,i} \;=\; \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1}
$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — SCARA Robot Velocity (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — SCARA end-effector position (1 mark):** For SCARA, motion occurs in the $xy$-plane:

$$
^0\mathbf{P}_{0,4} \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \\[4pt]
L_1 \sin q_1 + L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \\[4pt]
d_4
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear-velocity Jacobian (2 marks):** Differentiating the position w.r.t. joint variables:

$$
^0\mathbf{J}_v \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
-L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & 0 \\[4pt]
L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) & 0 \\[4pt]
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (2 marks):** With $q_1 = 60°$, $q_2 = -30°$, $q_1 + q_2 = 30°$:

$$
^0\mathbf{J}_v \;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.4(0.866) - 0.3(0.5) & -0.3(0.5) & 0 \\[4pt]
0.4(0.5) + 0.3(0.866) & 0.3(0.866) & 0 \\[4pt]
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.4964 & -0.15 & 0 \\[4pt]
0.4598 & 0.2598 & 0 \\[4pt]
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the velocity (2 marks):**

$$
^0\mathbf{v}_{0,4} \;=\; \,^0\mathbf{J}_v \begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 2 \\ 0\end{bmatrix} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.4964(1) - 0.15(2) \\[4pt]
0.4598(1) + 0.2598(2) \\[4pt]
0
\end{bmatrix} 
\;=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.7964 \\[4pt]
0.9794 \\[4pt]
0
\end{bmatrix} \text{m/s}
$$

**Magnitude:**

$$
\vert \,^0\mathbf{v}_{0,4} \vert \;=\; \sqrt{0.7964^2 + 0.9794^2} 
\;\approx\; \sqrt{0.6343 + 0.9592} 
\;\approx\; \sqrt{1.5935} 
\;\approx\; 1.262 \text{ m/s}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Dropping frame superscripts:** Writing $\mathbf{v}_{i+1}$ instead of $^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1}$ costs **1 mark** minimum. Always carry the reference-frame indicator.
> 2. **Forgetting the cross-product in forward recursion:** A common error is writing $^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1} = \,^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^i\mathbf{v}_i$ (missing the $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}$ term). This **invalidates the entire recursion** and loses 3–5 marks.
> 3. **Confusing linear and angular Jacobian blocks:** The upper 3 rows give linear velocity; the lower 3 rows give angular velocity. Mixing them up produces a **completely wrong end-effector velocity vector**.
> 4. **Sign errors in cross products:** $\hat{\mathbf{Z}} \times \mathbf{r}$ where $\hat{\mathbf{Z}} = [0, 0, 1]^T$ and $\mathbf{r} = [x, y, z]^T$ gives $[-y, x, 0]^T$ — a frequent slip.
> 5. **Skipping the Jacobian construction step:** KTU examiners award **separate marks** for *constructing* the Jacobian and for *multiplying* by $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. Do both explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Linear velocity** of a point is the time derivative of its position: $\mathbf{v} = d\mathbf{p}/dt$ (units: **m/s**).
- The **fundamental rigid-body equation** is $\mathbf{v}_P = \mathbf{v}_O + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}$.
- For **pure rotation** about a fixed point, $\mathbf{v}_P = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}$.
- The **magnitude** of the linear velocity is $\vert \mathbf{v}_P \vert = \omega \, r \, \sin\theta$.
- **Forward recursion** propagates velocity from base to end-effector using the formula $^{i+1}\mathbf{v}_{i+1} = \,^{i+1}_i\mathbf{R}\bigl(\,^i\mathbf{v}_i + \,^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \,^i\mathbf{P}_{i+1}^{*}\bigr)$.
- **Backward recursion** starts from the end-effector and moves toward the base.
- **Initial condition** for a fixed-base manipulator: $\,^0\mathbf{v}_0 = \mathbf{0}$ and $\,^0\boldsymbol{\omega}_0 = \mathbf{0}$.
- The **linear-velocity Jacobian** $^0\mathbf{J}_v$ maps joint rates to end-effector linear velocity: $\,^0\mathbf{v}_{0,n} = \,^0\mathbf{J}_v(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- **Jacobian column rules:** Revolute → $\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1} \times \,^0\mathbf{r}_{n/i-1}$; Prismatic → $\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{i-1}$.
- **Frame notation** is mandatory: left-superscript = reference frame; left-subscript = "with respect to" origin (e.g., $^0_i\mathbf{R}$).
- The **rotation-matrix property** $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}) = (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a}) \times (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{b})$ is heavily used in derivations.
- The **cross product** with $\hat{\mathbf{Z}} = [0,0,1]^T$ produces $\hat{\mathbf{Z}} \times [x, y, z]^T = [-y, x, 0]^T$ — memorise this.
- **Singular configurations** occur when $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$; at these configurations, the manipulator cannot achieve certain linear velocities in certain directions.
- **Units consistency** is critical: $\mathbf{v}$ in m/s, $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ in rad/s, $\mathbf{r}$ in m, $\dot{q}$ in rad/s (revolute) or m/s (prismatic).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combining Linear and Angular Jacobians
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Combining Linear and Angular Jacobians ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the kinematic analysis of serial-chain robotic manipulators, the **Geometric Jacobian Matrix** (often simply called the **Jacobian**) is the fundamental operator that linearly maps the vector of **joint velocities** $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ to the **twist** of the end-effector $\boldsymbol{\xi} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$, which comprises the **linear velocity** $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ and the **angular velocity** $\boldsymbol{\omega} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ of the end-effector frame. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim syllabus style):** > The **Combined Jacobian** $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ is the vertical concatenation (block-stacking) of the **Linear Velocity Jacobian** $\mathbf{J}_v(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ and the **Angular Velocity Jacobian** $\mathbf{J}_{\omega}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$, such that: > $$\boldsymbol{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} \\ \boldsymbol{\omega} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{J}_v \\ \mathbf{J}_{\omega} \end{bmatrix} \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}$$ The **6** rows of the combined Jacobian correspond to the **6 degrees-of-freedom** of a rigid body in 3D space (3 translations + 3 rotations), while the **$n$** columns correspond to the **$n$ actuated joints** of the manipulator. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Orchestra Conductor" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Robotic Arm as a Conducted Orchestra** > Imagine a robotic arm as an **orchestra** and each joint motor as a **musician**. The combined Jacobian is the **sheet-music score** the conductor uses. Each column of the score tells one musician (joint $i$): *"When you play faster by 1 beat/sec, the tip of the bow (end-effector) will move exactly this much in 3D space and rotate this much."* > - The **top 3 rows (Jv)** are the **translation cues** — how much each musician pushes the bow in the $\hat{x}, \hat{y}, \hat{z}$ directions. > - The **bottom 3 rows (Jω)** are the **rotation cues** — how much each musician twists the bow about $\hat{x}, \hat{y}, \hat{z}$. > - When all musicians play together (joints move simultaneously), the total motion of the bow tip is the **sum** of all individual contributions — a linear superposition, exactly as the matrix multiplication $\mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ expresses. ### 1.3 Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics | Symbol | Meaning | Units (SI) | |---|---|---| | $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ | Combined Geometric Jacobian | $\text{varies}$ (mixed) | | $\mathbf{J}_v(\mathbf{q})$ | Linear Velocity Jacobian | $\text{m}$ or $\text{m/rad}$ | | $\mathbf{J}_{\omega}(\mathbf{q})$ | Angular Velocity Jacobian | $\text{rad/m}$ or dimensionless | | $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Joint velocity vector | $\text{rad/s}$ (revolute) / $\text{m/s}$ (prismatic) | | $\mathbf{v}$ | End-effector linear velocity | $\text{m/s}$ | | $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ | End-effector angular velocity | $\text{rad/s}$ | | $n$ | Number of joints (DOF) | dimensionless | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Two Sub-Jacobians? The twist $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (\mathbf{v}, \boldsymbol{\omega})$ arises from a fundamental property of screw theory: **every rigid-body velocity field is the superposition of translational and rotational motion**. Since most industrial manipulators have joints that are either purely **rotational (R)** or purely **prismatic (P)**, the contribution of each joint type to the twist is structurally different: - A **revolute joint** rotates the link about its joint axis $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$ and simultaneously **sweeps the end-effector along a circular arc** (coupled translation + rotation). - A **prismatic joint** slides the link along $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$, producing **pure translation** (no rotation). Because of this asymmetry, the KTU-recommended **Spong–Vidyaasagar–Hutchinson** formulation splits the Jacobian into two $3 \times n$ blocks, one for each velocity type. ### 2.2 Derivation Logic of the Block Columns Let $T_{i}^{0}(\mathbf{q})$ be the forward-kinematic homogeneous transform of the end-effector relative to the base. The end-effector origin in the base frame is $\mathbf{o}_n^0$. The $i^{\text{th}}$ joint axis is along $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0$ (the $z$-axis of frame $\{i-1\}$ expressed in the base frame). **For the $i^{\text{th}}$ column of $\mathbf{J}$ (the contribution of joint $i$):** **Case 1 — Joint $i$ is REVOLUTE (R):** - The joint produces an angular velocity $\dot{q}_i$ about $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0$. - Linear velocity contribution: $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0 \times (\mathbf{o}_n^0 - \mathbf{o}_{i-1}^0)$ - Angular velocity contribution: $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0$ **Case 2 — Joint $i$ is PRISMATIC (P):** - The joint translates the link along $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0$ with velocity $\dot{q}_i$. - Linear velocity contribution: $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0$ - Angular velocity contribution: $\mathbf{0}$ (zero vector) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Insight:** The cross product $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0 \times (\mathbf{o}_n^0 - \mathbf{o}_{i-1}^0)$ is the **moment arm** of joint $i$ about the end-effector origin. It is computed entirely in the base frame using the **$z$-axes** of frames $\{0\}, \{1\}, \ldots, \{n-1\}$ obtained from the forward-kinematic transforms. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Reference Table — Memorize for the ESE** | Joint $i$ type | $i^{\text{th}}$ column of $\mathbf{J}_v$ (linear) | $i^{\text{th}}$ column of $\mathbf{J}_{\omega}$ (angular) | |---|---|---| | Revolute (R) | $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0 \times \left(\mathbf{o}_n^0 - \mathbf{o}_{i-1}^0\right)$ | $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0$ | | Prismatic (P) | $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0$ | $\mathbf{0}_{3 \times 1}$ | **The full combined Jacobian (block structure):** $$ \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{\mathbf{z}}_0 \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_0) & \hat{\mathbf{z}}_1 \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_1) & \cdots & \hat{\mathbf{z}}_{n-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{n-1}) \\ \hat{\mathbf{z}}_0 & \hat{\mathbf{z}}_1 & \cdots & \hat{\mathbf{z}}_{n-1} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(all revolute case)} $$ **Singularity condition:** $$ \det\!\left(\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\right) = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \text{rank}\!\left(\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\right) < \min(6, n) $$ **Velocity mapping and force mapping (dual):** $$ \boldsymbol{\xi} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F} $$ where $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is the joint torque vector and $\mathbf{F}$ is the wrench (force/moment) at the end-effector. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The combined Jacobian is the **workhorse of every robot controller**: 1. **Resolved-Rate Motion Control** — inverts $\mathbf{J}$ in real-time to compute joint rates $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\xi}_{\text{desired}}$. 2. **Singularity Detection** — used in industrial robots (e.g., KUKA, ABB) to alert operators when the manipulator loses a degree of freedom. 3. **Static Force Analysis** — relates payload wrench to joint torques via $\mathbf{J}^T$. 4. **Trajectory Planning** — verifies whether a Cartesian path is feasible at desired speeds. 5. **Impedance Control & Haptics** — couples environmental forces to joint actuators in surgical and rehabilitation robots. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Analytical Example — 2R Planar Manipulator We will compute the full combined Jacobian for a **2-revolute planar manipulator** (a common KTU ESE problem). This is the most frequently tested case. #### 3.1.1 DH Parameters and Frame Assignment | $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $a_1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $q_1$ | | 2 | $a_2$ | $0$ | $0$ | $q_2$ | The link lengths $a_1$ and $a_2$ are **constants**; $q_1$ and $q_2$ are the **joint variables**. #### 3.1.2 Forward Kinematics (needed for $\mathbf{o}_i$ and $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$) Using the standard DH transformation matrix: $$ {}^{i-1}\mathbf{T}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i & 0 & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i & 0 & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ The end-effector position from frame $\{0\}$ is found by composing: $$ {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_2 = {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_1 \cdot {}^{1}\mathbf{T}_2 $$ **Step (a):** Compute ${}^{0}\mathbf{T}_1$: $$ {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 & a_1 c_1 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 & a_1 s_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step (b):** Compute ${}^{0}\mathbf{T}_2$ (multiply the two matrices): $$ {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_{12} & -s_{12} & 0 & a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} \\ s_{12} & c_{12} & 0 & a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ where we use the shorthand $c_{12} = \cos(q_1+q_2)$, $s_{12} = \sin(q_1+q_2)$. **Step (c):** Extract the origins (4th columns) and the $z$-axes (3rd columns): $$ \mathbf{o}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{o}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 c_1 \\ a_1 s_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{o}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} \\ a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \hat{\mathbf{z}}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \hat{\mathbf{z}}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ #### 3.1.3 Build the Angular Velocity Jacobian $\mathbf{J}_{\omega}$ Since both joints are revolute, the columns are simply the $z$-axes: $$ \mathbf{J}_{\omega} = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{\mathbf{z}}_0 & \hat{\mathbf{z}}_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2} $$ #### 3.1.4 Build the Linear Velocity Jacobian $\mathbf{J}_v$ **Column 1:** $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_0 \times (\mathbf{o}_2 - \mathbf{o}_0)$ $$ \mathbf{o}_2 - \mathbf{o}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} \\ a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \hat{\mathbf{z}}_0 \times (\mathbf{o}_2 - \mathbf{o}_0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} \\ a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ Using the determinant rule $\begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ x & y & 0 \end{vmatrix}$: $$ = \hat{i}(0 \cdot 0 - 1 \cdot y) - \hat{j}(0 \cdot 0 - 1 \cdot x) + \hat{k}(0 \cdot y - 0 \cdot x) = -y\,\hat{i} + x\,\hat{j} $$ $$ \Rightarrow \quad \text{Col 1} = \begin{bmatrix} -(a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12}) \\ a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Column 2:** $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_1 \times (\mathbf{o}_2 - \mathbf{o}_1)$ $$ \mathbf{o}_2 - \mathbf{o}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} a_2 c_{12} \\ a_2 s_{12} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \hat{\mathbf{z}}_1 \times (\mathbf{o}_2 - \mathbf{o}_1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} a_2 c_{12} \\ a_2 s_{12} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -a_2 s_{12} \\ a_2 c_{12} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ Therefore: $$ \mathbf{J}_v = \begin{bmatrix} -(a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12}) & -a_2 s_{12} \\ a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} & a_2 c_{12} \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2} $$ #### 3.1.5 Final Combined Jacobian (6×2) $$ \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{J}_v \\ \mathbf{J}_{\omega} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -(a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12}) & -a_2 s_{12} \\ a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} & a_2 c_{12} \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ #### 3.1.6 Singularity Analysis The determinant of $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T$ or the $2\times 2$ principal minor (rows 1,2; cols 1,2) is: $$ \det(\mathbf{J}_v) = -(a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12})(a_2 c_{12}) - (-a_2 s_{12})(a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12}) $$ $$ = -a_1 a_2 s_1 c_{12} - a_2^2 s_{12} c_{12} + a_2 a_1 s_{12} c_1 + a_2^2 s_{12} c_{12} $$ $$ = a_1 a_2 (s_{12} c_1 - s_1 c_{12}) = a_1 a_2 \sin(q_2) $$ $$ \boxed{\det(\mathbf{J}) = a_1 a_2 \sin(q_2)} $$ **Singularity occurs when $\sin(q_2) = 0$, i.e., $q_2 = 0$ or $q_2 = \pi$** (arm fully stretched or fully folded back). At these configurations, the end-effector loses the ability to move in one Cartesian direction — a classic KTU exam conclusion. ### 3.2 Verification via Numerical Check Let $a_1 = a_2 = 1\,\text{m}$, $q_1 = \pi/4$, $q_2 = \pi/3$ (so $q_1+q_2 = 7\pi/12$). Then $c_1 = 0.707$, $s_1 = 0.707$, $c_{12} \approx 0.259$, $s_{12} \approx 0.966$. $$ \mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -(0.707 + 0.966) & -0.966 \\ 0.707 + 0.259 & 0.259 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.673 & -0.966 \\ 0.966 & 0.259 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Determinant: $1 \cdot 1 \cdot \sin(\pi/3) = 0.866$ ✓ (matches the analytical formula). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Architecture — Jacobian Computation Pipeline The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete computation flow for the combined Jacobian, mapping the KTU-recommended algorithmic procedure. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[DH Parameters Table
a_i, alpha_i, d_i, theta_i] --> B[Compute Forward Kinematics
T_i for i = 1 to n] B --> C[Extract origin vectors
o_0, o_1, ..., o_n in base frame] B --> D[Extract z-axis vectors
z_0, z_1, ..., z_{n-1} in base frame] C --> E[For each joint i, compute
r_i = o_n - o_{i-1}] D --> F{Joint i is Revolute?} F -- Yes --> G[Linear column = z_{i-1} cross r_i
Angular column = z_{i-1}] F -- No --> H[Linear column = z_{i-1}
Angular column = 0 vector] G --> I[Stack into J_v and J_omega] H --> I I --> J[Form Combined Jacobian
J = stack of J_v over J_omega] J --> K[Compute determinant or rank
Singularity check] J --> L[Apply to velocity mapping
xi = J times q_dot] J --> M[Apply to force mapping
tau = J transpose times F] ``` ### 4.2 Block Structure of the Combined Jacobian ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Combined[J q - 6 x n Matrix] direction TB subgraph TopBlock[Top 3 x n Block - Linear Velocity] Jv1["J_v column 1"]:::lin Jv2["J_v column 2"]:::lin Jvn["J_v column n"]:::lin end subgraph BotBlock[Bottom 3 x n Block - Angular Velocity] Jw1["J_omega column 1"]:::ang Jw2["J_omega column 2"]:::ang Jwn["J_omega column n"]:::ang end end Qdot["Joint Velocity Vector q_dot
n x 1"] --> Combined Combined --> Twist["End Effector Twist xi
v on top, omega on bottom
6 x 1"] classDef lin fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef ang fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Kinematic Schematic — 2R Planar Manipulator Frames ```mermaid flowchart LR Base[Base Frame 0
z0 points out of page] -->|rotate q1, translate a1| Link1[Link 1 of length a1
Frame 1 at joint 2] Link1 -->|rotate q2, translate a2| Link2[Link 2 of length a2
End effector frame 2] z0[z0 axis = 0,0,1]:::axis z1[z1 axis = 0,0,1]:::axis o0[Origin O0 = 0,0,0]:::org o1[Origin O1 = a1 c1, a1 s1, 0]:::org o2[Origin O2 = a1 c1 + a2 c12, a1 s1 + a2 s12, 0]:::org classDef axis fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef org fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Every revolute joint axis $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$ points **out of the page** (planar case). The end-effector origin $\mathbf{o}_2$ is computed by **vector addition** of the two link vectors. The cross products $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_0 \times (\mathbf{o}_2 - \mathbf{o}_0)$ and $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_1 \times (\mathbf{o}_2 - \mathbf{o}_1)$ give the **moment arms** that appear in the first two columns of $\mathbf{J}_v$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the Geometric Jacobian of a robotic manipulator. How is the combined Jacobian structured from the linear and angular velocity Jacobians?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Geometric Jacobian** $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is a $6 \times n$ matrix that linearly maps the joint velocity vector $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ to the **end-effector twist** $\boldsymbol{\xi} = [\mathbf{v}^T \; \boldsymbol{\omega}^T]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$, where $\mathbf{v}$ is the linear velocity and $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ is the angular velocity of the end-effector. The combined Jacobian is structured as a **vertical concatenation (block-stacking)** of two $3 \times n$ sub-matrices: $$ \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{J}_v(\mathbf{q}) \\ \mathbf{J}_{\omega}(\mathbf{q}) \end{bmatrix} $$ where $\mathbf{J}_v$ is the **Linear Velocity Jacobian** (top 3 rows) and $\mathbf{J}_{\omega}$ is the **Angular Velocity Jacobian** (bottom 3 rows). The resulting relation is $\boldsymbol{\xi} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. **(Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for structure, 1 mark for equation.)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *For a revolute joint, what are the linear and angular velocity contributions to the columns of the Jacobian? State the formula clearly.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** For the $i^{\text{th}}$ **revolute joint**, whose axis of rotation in the base frame is $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0$, the contributions to the $i^{\text{th}}$ column of the combined Jacobian are: - **Linear velocity contribution** (top 3 entries): the cross product of the joint axis with the moment arm to the end-effector: $$\mathbf{J}_{v,i} = \hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0 \times \left(\mathbf{o}_n^0 - \mathbf{o}_{i-1}^0\right)$$ - **Angular velocity contribution** (bottom 3 entries): simply the joint axis itself: $$\mathbf{J}_{\omega,i} = \hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}^0$$ **(Mark split: 1.5 marks for each formula with correct notation.)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]` **(a)** For a **2R planar manipulator** with link lengths $a_1 = 0.5\,\text{m}$ and $a_2 = 0.4\,\text{m}$, derive the **combined geometric Jacobian** $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ as a function of joint variables $q_1$ and $q_2$. Show every step clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the same manipulator, find the **Cartesian velocity** of the end-effector when $q_1 = 30°$, $q_2 = 45°$, and the joint velocity vector is $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = [0.5 \; 0.3]^T$ (rad/s). Also identify the **singular configuration** of the manipulator. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q-A (a):** **Step 1 — DH parameters and forward kinematics:** Using the standard DH convention for a 2R planar arm with $d_i = 0$ and $\alpha_i = 0$: $$ {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(q_1+q_2) & -\sin(q_1+q_2) & 0 & a_1 \cos q_1 + a_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \\ \sin(q_1+q_2) & \cos(q_1+q_2) & 0 & a_1 \sin q_1 + a_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2 — Extract origin and z-axis vectors:** $$ \mathbf{o}_0 = \mathbf{0}, \quad \mathbf{o}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 c_1 \\ a_1 s_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{o}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} \\ a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \hat{\mathbf{z}}_0 = \hat{\mathbf{z}}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — Angular velocity Jacobian** (revolute → axis directly): $$ \mathbf{J}_{\omega} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4 — Linear velocity Jacobian, Column 1:** Compute $\mathbf{o}_2 - \mathbf{o}_0 = [a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12}, \; a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12}, \; 0]^T$. $$ \hat{\mathbf{z}}_0 \times (\mathbf{o}_2 - \mathbf{o}_0) = \begin{bmatrix} -(a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12}) \\ a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 5 — Linear velocity Jacobian, Column 2:** Compute $\mathbf{o}_2 - \mathbf{o}_1 = [a_2 c_{12}, \; a_2 s_{12}, \; 0]^T$. $$ \hat{\mathbf{z}}_1 \times (\mathbf{o}_2 - \mathbf{o}_1) = \begin{bmatrix} -a_2 s_{12} \\ a_2 c_{12} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 6 — Final Combined Jacobian:** $$ \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -(a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12}) & -a_2 s_{12} \\ a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} & a_2 c_{12} \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **(a) Mark split: 1 mark for DH/FK, 1 mark for origin/z-axis, 1 mark for J_omega, 2 marks for J_v columns, 1 mark for stacking, 1 mark for final assembly.)** --- **Model Solution to Q-A (b):** **Step 1 — Substitute numerical values:** $q_1 = 30° \Rightarrow c_1 = 0.866$, $s_1 = 0.5$ $q_2 = 45° \Rightarrow c_2 = 0.707$, $s_2 = 0.707$ $q_1 + q_2 = 75° \Rightarrow c_{12} = 0.259$, $s_{12} = 0.966$ **Step 2 — Compute Jacobian entries:** Row 1: $-(0.5 \cdot 0.5 + 0.4 \cdot 0.966) = -(0.25 + 0.3864) = -0.6364$ and $-0.4 \cdot 0.966 = -0.3864$ Row 2: $0.5 \cdot 0.866 + 0.4 \cdot 0.259 = 0.433 + 0.1036 = 0.5366$ and $0.4 \cdot 0.259 = 0.1036$ $$ \mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6364 & -0.3864 \\ 0.5366 & 0.1036 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — Multiply by joint velocity vector:** $$ \boldsymbol{\xi} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6364 & -0.3864 \\ 0.5366 & 0.1036 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} $$ Linear velocity: - $v_x = (-0.6364)(0.5) + (-0.3864)(0.3) = -0.3182 - 0.1159 = -0.4341\,\text{m/s}$ - $v_y = (0.5366)(0.5) + (0.1036)(0.3) = 0.2683 + 0.0311 = 0.2994\,\text{m/s}$ - $v_z = 0$ Angular velocity: - $\omega_x = 0$, $\omega_y = 0$ - $\omega_z = 1(0.5) + 1(0.3) = 0.8\,\text{rad/s}$ **Final twist:** $$ \boxed{\boldsymbol{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.4341 \\ 0.2994 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \vert\mathbf{v}\vert \approx 0.527\,\text{m/s},\; \vert\boldsymbol{\omega}\vert = 0.8\,\text{rad/s}} $$ **Step 4 — Singularity analysis:** From the determinant $\det(\mathbf{J}_v) = a_1 a_2 \sin q_2$: $\sin q_2 = 0 \Rightarrow q_2 = 0°$ or $q_2 = 180°$. - At $q_2 = 0°$ → arm is **fully stretched out** (boundary of workspace). - At $q_2 = 180°$ → arm is **folded back on itself** (interior singular). At these configurations, the end-effector cannot move in the direction of link extension. **(b) Mark split: 2 marks for J substitution, 2 marks for twist computation, 2 marks for numerical values, 1 mark for singularity identification.)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** A **3R planar manipulator** has link lengths $a_1$, $a_2$, $a_3$. Write down the **angular velocity Jacobian** $\mathbf{J}_{\omega}$ and explain, with a sketch description, why each column is simply the unit vector along the corresponding $z$-axis. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **physical meaning** of the rows of $\mathbf{J}_v$ and $\mathbf{J}_{\omega}$ in the context of a serial manipulator. Discuss what happens physically when the manipulator approaches a **singular configuration**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution Outline to Q-B:** **(a)** For a 3R planar arm, all three joint axes are perpendicular to the plane of motion, so: $$ \hat{\mathbf{z}}_0 = \hat{\mathbf{z}}_1 = \hat{\mathbf{z}}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{J}_{\omega} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ The **physical reason**: every revolute joint rotates the entire downstream chain (including the end-effector) about its own $z$-axis at rate $\dot{q}_i$. Since each joint's $z$-axis points out of the plane, all three contribute a unit rotation about $\hat{z}$, and superposition gives the $i^{\text{th}}$ column equal to $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$. **(b) Physical meaning of rows:** - The **rows of $\mathbf{J}_v$** are the **partial derivatives of the end-effector Cartesian position** with respect to each joint variable. They tell *"how much the end-effector position changes in $\hat{x}$, $\hat{y}$, $\hat{z}$ for a unit change in $q_i$."* - The **rows of $\mathbf{J}_{\omega}$** are the **partial derivatives of the end-effector orientation** (in axis-angle form) with respect to each joint variable. **At a singular configuration** $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$: 1. The manipulator loses one or more degrees of freedom in Cartesian space. 2. Infinitely many joint velocities can produce the **same** end-effector velocity. 3. Conversely, finite joint velocities would require **infinite** end-effector velocity in the lost direction. 4. Physically, two joint axes become **collinear** (e.g., elbow-straight in 2R case), so their motions produce redundant end-effector behavior. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Wrong sign in cross product** — Remember the determinant rule $\hat{\mathbf{z}} \times \mathbf{r}$, not $\mathbf{r} \times \hat{\mathbf{z}}$. A sign flip costs 2 marks. > 2. **Confusing frame indices** — Column $i$ uses $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$ and $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$, **not** $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_i$ or $\mathbf{o}_i$. The axis of joint $i$ is the $z$-axis of the *preceding* frame. > 3. **Forgetting to use base-frame vectors** — All vectors in the cross product MUST be expressed in the **base frame** $\{0\}$. Using intermediate-frame vectors without transformation is a 3-mark penalty. > 4. **Mixing up J_omega entry for prismatic joints** — Must be the **zero vector** $\mathbf{0}_{3\times 1}$, not $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$. This is the most common error in PRP or RPR manipulators. > 5. **Skipping the singularity check** — KTU almost always awards 1–2 marks for explicitly stating the singular configurations at the end of the derivation. > 6. **Dimension mismatch** — Always verify: for a revolute joint, $J_v$ has units of length (m) and $J_{\omega}$ is dimensionless; for prismatic, $J_v$ is dimensionless and $J_{\omega} = 0$. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Combine & Conquer" the Jacobian** - **Combined Jacobian** $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is the **vertical stack** of $\mathbf{J}_v$ (top $3 \times n$) and $\mathbf{J}_{\omega}$ (bottom $3 \times n$). - **Twist equation**: $\boldsymbol{\xi} = [\mathbf{v} \; \boldsymbol{\omega}]^T = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. - **Column rule for revolute joint $i$**: Linear = $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1})$; Angular = $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$. - **Column rule for prismatic joint $i$**: Linear = $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$; Angular = $\mathbf{0}$. - All vectors $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$ and $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ must be expressed in the **base frame** $\{0\}$. - **Determinant / rank test** identifies singularities: $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ (square case) or $\text{rank}(\mathbf{J}) < \min(6, n)$. - **Static force dual**: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ (virtual-work principle). - For 2R planar arm: $\det(\mathbf{J}) = a_1 a_2 \sin q_2$ → singular at $q_2 = 0, \pi$. - For 3R planar arm: $\det(\mathbf{J}) = a_1 a_2 \sin q_2 + a_2 a_3 \sin(q_2+q_3) + \ldots$ (sum-of-terms form) → singular when **two adjacent links align collinearly**. - The Jacobian is a **function of configuration** $\mathbf{q}$ — recompute at every operating point. - Units check: $[\mathbf{J}_v]_{\text{rev}} = \text{m}$, $[\mathbf{J}_{\omega}]_{\text{rev}} = 1$ (dimensionless); for prismatic these are swapped and zeroed respectively.

topics.title: Velocity Jacobian Examples Given Forward Kinematic Matrices - 2 Link Planar Manipulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Velocity Jacobian Examples Given Forward Kinematic Matrices - 2 Link Planar Manipulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Jacobian Matrix (J):** For a robotic manipulator, the **Velocity Jacobian** $J(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the first-order partial derivative matrix that linearly maps the **joint space velocity vector** $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ to the **task space (Cartesian / end-effector) velocity vector** $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, expressed as $\mathbf{V} = J(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.

For a **2-link planar manipulator** with two revolute joints, the manipulator operates in a 2D task space. The end-effector twist has $m = 3$ components (two linear velocities $\dot{x}, \dot{y}$ and one angular velocity $\dot{\phi}$), and the joint space has $n = 2$ components ($\dot{\theta}_1, \dot{\theta}_2$). Hence $J \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2}$.

The two principal methods of obtaining the Jacobian from the **Forward Kinematic Matrix (Homogeneous Transformation Matrix)** are:

1. **Method A — Direct Differentiation of Forward Kinematics:** Differentiate the position and orientation equations of the end-effector (extracted from the FKM) with respect to time, and collect the joint velocities into matrix form.
2. **Method B — Cross-Product Formulation (Geometric Jacobian):** Use the recursive Jacobian formulation for revolute joints:
   $$J_{v_i} = z_{i-1} \times (p_{end} - p_{i-1})$$
   $$J_{\omega_i} = z_{i-1}$$

Both methods yield the **same Jacobian** for the 2-link planar manipulator.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Steering Wheel Analogy:** Imagine you are driving a car. Turning the steering wheel (joint 1) and pushing the gas/brake (joint 2) both affect where the car moves. The **Velocity Jacobian** is essentially the "translation chart" that tells you, *"for every small twist of your steering wheel, how far and in what direction does the car actually move?"* In robotics, the Jacobian translates tiny joint rotations ($\dot{\theta}_1, \dot{\theta}_2$) into tiny end-effector motions ($\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\phi}$).

For the 2-link planar arm, picture a human arm reaching across a desk: the **shoulder** ($\theta_1$) and the **elbow** ($\theta_2$). The Jacobian tells the control system exactly how fast the fingertip (end-effector) will move when the shoulder and elbow rotate at specific speeds.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Standard KTU Notation)

- **Link lengths:** $\mathbf{l_1}$ and $\mathbf{l_2}$ (metres, m)
- **Joint angles:** $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ (radians, rad)
- **Joint velocities:** $\dot{\theta}_1, \dot{\theta}_2$ (rad/s)
- **End-effector position:** $(x, y)$ in metres
- **End-effector orientation:** $\phi$ in radians (equal to $\theta_1 + \theta_2$)
- **End-effector twist (spatial velocity):** $\mathbf{V} = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\phi}]^T$
- **Forward Kinematic Matrix:** $T_0^2 \in SE(2)$ (augmented to $SE(3)$ with a fixed $z$-column)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept: Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Methods Yield the Same Result:** The Forward Kinematic Matrix is the *geometric truth* of the manipulator. Differentiating it algebraically (Method A) or extracting columns via cross products (Method B) are two different computational paths to the *same physical mapping* $J(\mathbf{q})$.

**Method A — Logical Steps (Direct Differentiation):**

1. **Step 1 — Obtain FKM:** Compute the homogeneous transformation matrix $T_0^n$ (here $T_0^2$ for a 2-link planar arm) using either DH parameters or direct geometric projection.
2. **Step 2 — Extract Position & Orientation:** Pull out the position vector $\mathbf{p} = [x, y, z]^T$ and the orientation angles (here $\phi$ = sum of joint angles for planar case).
3. **Step 3 — Differentiate with Respect to Time:** Apply the chain rule, $\dot{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$, treating $\theta_1, \theta_2$ as time-varying variables.
4. **Step 4 — Assemble into Matrix Form:** Group the partial derivatives column-wise to form $J(\mathbf{q})$.

**Method B — Logical Steps (Cross-Product Method):**

1. **Step 1 — Identify Joint Axes:** For each revolute joint $i$, the joint axis unit vector $z_{i-1}$ in the base frame.
2. **Step 2 — Locate Origins:** Determine $p_{i-1}$ (origin of frame $i-1$) and $p_{end}$ (end-effector position).
3. **Step 3 — Compute Linear Part:** $J_{v_i} = z_{i-1} \times (p_{end} - p_{i-1})$.
4. **Step 4 — Compute Angular Part:** $J_{\omega_i} = z_{i-1}$ (for revolute joint).
5. **Step 5 — Stack Columns:** $J = [J_1 \vert J_2]$ where $J_i = [J_{v_i}^T, J_{\omega_i}^T]^T$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for any KTU problem on the 2-link planar manipulator Jacobian. Memorise the position equations and the structure of $J$.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| End-effector $x$ | $x = l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | metres (m) |
| End-effector $y$ | $y = l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | metres (m) |
| End-effector orientation | $\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$ | radians (rad) |
| Twist vector | $\mathbf{V} = [\dot{x},\ \dot{y},\ \dot{\phi}]^T$ | m/s, m/s, rad/s |
| Jacobian (analytical) | $J = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$ | $3 \times 2$ matrix |
| $J_{11}$ entry | $-l_1\sin\theta_1 - l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | m/rad |
| $J_{12}$ entry | $-l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | m/rad |
| $J_{21}$ entry | $l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | m/rad |
| $J_{22}$ entry | $l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | m/rad |
| $J_{31}$ entry | $1$ | dimensionless |
| $J_{32}$ entry | $1$ | dimensionless |
| Linear Jacobian (revolute) | $J_{v_i} = z_{i-1} \times (p_{end} - p_{i-1})$ | m/rad (cross product) |
| Angular Jacobian (revolute) | $J_{\omega_i} = z_{i-1}$ | unit vector |
| Jacobian determinant (singularity) | $\det(J J^T) = l_1^2 l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2^3 \cos\theta_2 + l_2^4 \cos^2\theta_2$ | m$^4$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Singularity Condition:** The 2-link planar manipulator loses a degree of freedom when $\det(J J^T) = 0$. The most common singularity occurs when the arm is **fully stretched out** ($\theta_2 = 0$), where the manipulator cannot move further away from the base. **Always test the configuration for singularities in exam problems.**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Velocity Jacobian is fundamental in:

- **Real-time trajectory control** (computing joint velocities from desired end-effector path velocities using the **inverse Jacobian** $J^{-1}$).
- **Singularity analysis** (identifying configurations where the robot loses mobility).
- **Force/torque mapping** (the **transpose Jacobian** $J^T$ maps end-effector forces to joint torques: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = J^T \mathbf{F}$).
- **Workspace characterisation** (determining manipulability ellipsoids, dexterity measures).
- **Industrial applications** — SCARA robots, pick-and-place planar arms in PCB assembly, packaging lines, and surgical robotic assistants all rely on this exact Jacobian for the planar 2R configuration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Method A — Full Derivation by Direct Differentiation

### 3.1.1 Step 1: The Forward Kinematic Matrix (FKM) of the 2-Link Planar Manipulator

The 2-link planar manipulator has two revolute joints rotating in the $xy$-plane about the $z$-axis. The FKM is built using basic trigonometry (equivalent to DH parameters with $\alpha_i = 0$, $a_i = l_i$, $d_i = 0$):

$$
\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2
$$

The corresponding homogeneous transformation matrix (extended to $SE(3)$ by appending a constant $z$-row and an identity row) is:

$$
T_0^2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Differentiate $x$ with Respect to Time

Applying the chain rule $\dot{x} = \frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1}\dot{\theta}_1 + \frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2}\dot{\theta}_2$:

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1} = -l_1\sin\theta_1 - l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2} = -l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
\dot{x} = \bigl[-l_1\sin\theta_1 - l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr]\dot{\theta}_1 + \bigl[-l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr]\dot{\theta}_2
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Differentiate $y$ with Respect to Time

$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} = l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} = l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
\dot{y} = \bigl[l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr]\dot{\theta}_1 + \bigl[l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr]\dot{\theta}_2
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Differentiate $\phi$ with Respect to Time

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta_1} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta_2} = 1
$$

$$
\dot{\phi} = \dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Assemble the Velocity Jacobian Matrix

Stacking the three velocity equations into matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\phi} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin\theta_1 - l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{V} = J(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}\,, \quad J(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin\theta_1 - l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Method B — Cross-Product (Geometric Jacobian) Derivation

### 3.2.1 Step 1: Define the Base Frame Properties

For the 2-link planar arm rotating in the $xy$-plane:

- $z_0 = [0, 0, 1]^T$ (axis of joint 1)
- $z_1 = [0, 0, 1]^T$ (axis of joint 2 — same as joint 1 since planar)
- $p_0 = [0, 0, 0]^T$ (base origin)
- $p_1 = [l_1\cos\theta_1,\ l_1\sin\theta_1,\ 0]^T$ (elbow position)
- $p_{end} = [l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2),\ l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2),\ 0]^T$

### 3.2.2 Step 2: Column 1 of Jacobian (from Joint 1)

The vector from frame 0 origin to the end-effector:

$$
p_{end} - p_0 = \begin{bmatrix} l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute the cross product $z_0 \times (p_{end} - p_0)$:

$$
J_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Using the determinant form of cross product, with $z_0 = \hat{k}$:

$$
J_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} -(l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)) \\ l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For the angular part of joint 1 (revolute):

$$
J_{\omega_1} = z_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Stacking the linear and angular parts gives the first column of the Jacobian. The $z$-component of linear velocity is **0** (planar motion), and the angular velocity about $z$ is **1** rad/s per $\dot{\theta}_1$. Truncating to the planar twist $[v_x, v_y, \omega_z]$:

$$
J_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin\theta_1 - l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3: Column 2 of Jacobian (from Joint 2)

The vector from frame 1 origin to the end-effector:

$$
p_{end} - p_1 = \begin{bmatrix} l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute the cross product $z_1 \times (p_{end} - p_1)$:

$$
J_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For the angular part:

$$
J_{\omega_2} = z_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Restricting to the planar twist:

$$
J_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4: Final Assembled Jacobian (Geometric Method)

Combining both columns:

$$
\boxed{\,J_{geom} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin\theta_1 - l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** $J_{analytical} \equiv J_{geometric}$ — both methods produce the **same matrix**, confirming the internal consistency of the FKM and the cross-product formulation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 2-link planar manipulator has $l_1 = 1\,\text{m}$ and $l_2 = 0.5\,\text{m}$. At an instant, $\theta_1 = 30^\circ$, $\theta_2 = 45^\circ$, $\dot{\theta}_1 = 1\,\text{rad/s}$, $\dot{\theta}_2 = 0.5\,\text{rad/s}$. Find the end-effector velocity.

**Solution:**

Compute the trigonometric values:

$$
\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 75^\circ,\quad \sin 30^\circ = 0.5,\quad \cos 30^\circ = 0.8660,\quad \sin 75^\circ = 0.9659,\quad \cos 75^\circ = 0.2588
$$

Substitute into the Jacobian entries:

$$
J_{11} = -1(0.5) - 0.5(0.9659) = -0.5 - 0.4830 = -0.9830
$$

$$
J_{12} = -0.5(0.9659) = -0.4830
$$

$$
J_{21} = 1(0.8660) + 0.5(0.2588) = 0.8660 + 0.1294 = 0.9954
$$

$$
J_{22} = 0.5(0.2588) = 0.1294
$$

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9830 & -0.4830 \\ 0.9954 & 0.1294 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply $\mathbf{V} = J \dot{\mathbf{q}}$:

$$
\dot{x} = (-0.9830)(1) + (-0.4830)(0.5) = -0.9830 - 0.2415 = -1.2245\ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{y} = (0.9954)(1) + (0.1294)(0.5) = 0.9954 + 0.0647 = 1.0601\ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{\phi} = (1)(1) + (1)(0.5) = 1.5\ \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{V} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.2245 \\ 1.0601 \\ 1.5 \end{bmatrix}\ \begin{matrix} \text{m/s} \\ \text{m/s} \\ \text{rad/s} \end{matrix}\,}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of the Velocity Jacobian Derivation

```mermaid
    G --> H["Form 3 by 2 Jacobian J of q"]
    E --> I["Step 1: Identify joint axes z0 and z1 along k-hat"]
    I --> J["Step 2: Compute z cross p_end minus p_i for each joint"]
    J --> K["Step 3: Stack Jv1 and Jv2 as columns"]
    K --> L["Form 3 by 2 Geometric Jacobian"]
    H --> M["Verification: J analytical equals J geometric"]
    L --> M
```
