\boxed{\;M_{12}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{q}_2 - C_{12}\dot{q}_1^2 = \tau_2\;}
$$
where
$$
M_{11} = \frac{m_1 l_1^2}{4} + m_2 l_1^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 + \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4}, \quad
M_{12} = \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4} + \frac{m_2 l_1 l_2}{2} \cos q_2, \quad
M_{22} = \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4}, \quad
C_{12} = -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2
$$
**(1.5 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** by (a) forgetting to include the rotational KE term $\frac{1}{2} I_i \dot{q}_i^2$ when link inertias are given, (b) mishandling the chain rule when computing $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right)$ — the time derivative applies to **both** the matrix elements and $\dot{q}_i$, and (c) sign errors in the Coriolis term (it should contain $\sin q_2$ from $-\partial K/\partial q_2$, **not** $\cos q_2$).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure for applying the Lagrangian method to derive the dynamic equations of motion of an $n$-joint robotic manipulator. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For a 2-DOF manipulator moving in a vertical plane, write the kinetic energy and potential energy expressions for both links. Form the Lagrangian and show that the resulting equations of motion can be written in the standard form $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$. Identify the entries of $M(q)$, $C(q,\dot{q})$, and $G(q)$. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q2(a):

1. **Choose generalized coordinates** — typically joint variables. **(1 mark)**
2. **Forward kinematics** — find Cartesian positions of all link CoMs. **(1 mark)**
3. **Velocity computation** — differentiate positions using Jacobian. **(1 mark)**
4. **Write kinetic energy** — sum of translational and rotational KE of all links. **(1 mark)**
5. **Write potential energy** — sum of gravitational PE. **(1 mark)**
6. **Form Lagrangian** $\mathcal{L} = K - P$. **(0.5 mark)**
7. **Apply Euler–Lagrange** for each $q_i$ and collect into the matrix form. **(1.5 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q2(b):

**Step 1 — Positions** (vertical plane, both links rotating about base):
$$
x_{c1} = \frac{l_1}{2}\cos q_1, \quad y_{c1} = \frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1
$$
$$
x_{c2} = l_1\cos q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2), \quad y_{c2} = l_1\sin q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Velocities** (same as in Question 1(b), with magnitudes). **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Kinetic Energy** (similar expression as before, but now include $I_1, I_2$ about CoM):
$$
K = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{m_1 l_1^2}{4} + I_1 + m_2 l_1^2 + \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4} + I_2 + 2 m_2 l_1 \frac{l_2}{2}\cos q_2\right]\dot{q}_1^2 \\
+ \left[\frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4} + I_2 + m_2 l_1 \frac{l_2}{2}\cos q_2\right]\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4} + I_2\right]\dot{q}_2^2
$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4 — Potential Energy** (gravity acts in $-y$):
$$
P = m_1 g \frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1 + m_2 g \left[l_1 \sin q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\right]
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Lagrangian** $\mathcal{L} = K - P$. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 6 — Apply Euler–Lagrange** and collect terms:

**Mass Matrix** $M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{12} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix}$ where
$$
M_{11} = m_2 l_1^2 + (m_1/4)l_1^2 + I_1 + (m_2/4)l_2^2 + I_2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2
$$
$$
M_{12} = (m_2/4)l_2^2 + I_2 + (1/2) m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2, \quad
M_{22} = (m_2/4)l_2^2 + I_2
$$

**Coriolis Matrix** $C(q,\dot{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \, \dot{q}_2 & -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \, \dot{q}_2 / 2 \\ m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \, \dot{q}_1 / 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$

**Gravity Vector** $G(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_1/2 + m_2 l_1)g\cos q_1 + (m_2 l_2/2)g\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ (m_2 l_2/2)g\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$ **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common error is writing $G(q)$ with $\sin q_i$ instead of $\cos q_i$ because the student forgets that $\frac{\partial}{\partial q}(\sin q) = \cos q$, not $\sin q$. Also, when link inertias $I_i$ are given, students often omit the rotational KE term, **losing 1 full mark**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Lagrangian definition**: $\mathcal{L} = K - P$ — a **scalar**, not a vector.
- ✅ **Units**: Joule (J) for $K$, $P$, and $\mathcal{L}$; N·m for torque; N for force.
- ✅ **Generalized coordinates in robotics** = joint variables ($q_i$).
- ✅ **Euler–Lagrange equation**:
  $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$.
- ✅ The final compact form is **always** $M(q)\ddot{q} + V(q,\dot{q}) + G(q) = \tau$, where $M$ is symmetric and positive-definite.
- ✅ $M(q)$ comes from the $\ddot{q}$ coefficient of the $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}}\right)$ term.
- ✅ $G(q) = \partial P / \partial q$ — never write $G(q) = -\partial P/\partial q$ for gravity.
- ✅ Coriolis/centrifugal terms contain $\sin q_i$ (from $\partial M / \partial q_i$) and are quadratic in velocities.
- ✅ **Hamilton's principle** of stationary action is the variational foundation: $\delta S = 0$.
- ✅ For a 2-DOF planar horizontal manipulator, $P = 0$ ⇒ $\mathcal{L} = K$.
- ✅ For a 2-DOF planar vertical manipulator, $P$ contains $\sin q_1$ and $\sin(q_1+q_2)$ — these yield $\cos$ terms in $G(q)$.
- ✅ Lagrangian mechanics is **coordinate-invariant** and **automatically handles constraints**.
- ✅ KTU exam tip: Always write $M(q)$, $C(q,\dot{q})$, $G(q)$ **separately and explicitly** at the end — never leave the answer as a single nested expression.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Analysis Examples - 1 DOF Spring Cart System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Dynamic Analysis Examples - 1 DOF Spring Cart System ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition A **1-DOF (One Degree of Freedom) Spring–Cart System** is the canonical introductory mechanical model used in KTU Module 3 of *Robotic Manipulators and Work Cell Design* to illustrate **Newton–Euler** and **Lagrangian** dynamic formulations for single-axis translational motion. The system is governed by a single generalized coordinate $x$ representing the linear displacement of a cart of mass $m$ restrained by a linear spring of stiffness $k$ (and optionally a viscous damper of coefficient $b$), subjected to an external excitation force $F(t)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCRAT303 – Module 3):** > The 1-DOF spring-cart is the *gateway example* before extending dynamics to **multi-DOF** and **multi-link manipulators**. Mastery of this system is mandatory for solving the **Lagrangian formulation of 2-DOF and n-DOF** problems asked in Part B university examinations. ### 1.2 The Equation of Motion (Standard Form) The governing differential equation of the 1-DOF spring-cart system is: $$m\,\ddot{x} + b\,\dot{x} + k\,x = F(t)$$ Where each term has a precise physical interpretation: - $m\,\ddot{x}$ → Inertial force (resistance to acceleration) - $b\,\dot{x}$ → Damping force (resistance to velocity) - $k\,x$ → Spring restoring force - $F(t)$ → Externally applied generalized force ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Shopping Cart in a Supermarket Aisle** > > Imagine pushing a **loaded shopping cart** (`mass m`) tied to a **telescopic spring** (`stiffness k`) in a long supermarket aisle. When you push (`F(t)`), the cart does not shoot forward instantly — it stretches the spring, oscillates, and slowly settles. The **heavier** the load, the **slower** the oscillation. The **stiffer** the spring, the **faster** the system vibrates. This is exactly the trade-off captured by the natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$. If you place your hand on the cart to **damp** the motion (think of it as a shock absorber), you introduce the damping term $b\,\dot{x}$, which causes the oscillations to gradually die out. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Mass** $m$ → kilograms (**kg**) - **Stiffness** $k$ → newtons per meter (**N/m**) - **Damping coefficient** $b$ → newton-seconds per meter (**N·s/m**) - **Natural (undamped) frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ → **rad/s** - **Damping ratio** $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{mk}}$ → **dimensionless** - **Critical damping** $b_c = 2\sqrt{mk}$ → **N·s/m** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why a 1-DOF Spring-Cart System? — The Pedagogical Purpose In robotic manipulator dynamics, the **dynamic model** of any robot is a set of coupled second-order ODEs. Before tackling the **n-link manipulator equation**: $$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$ students must internalize the **scalar version** of this structure. The 1-DOF spring-cart acts as a **scalar analog** of the full robot dynamic equation: | Full Manipulator | 1-DOF Spring-Cart Analog | |------------------|--------------------------| | $M(q)$ (Mass matrix) | $m$ (scalar mass) | | $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ (Coriolis) | $b\dot{x}$ (damping, with $b \equiv 0$ for conservative) | | $G(q)$ (Gravity) | $k x$ (generalized conservative force) | | $\tau$ (Joint torque) | $F(t)$ (Generalized force) | ### 2.2 Derivation Pathways — Two Routes **Route A: Newton–Euler (Free Body Diagram + Newton's 2nd Law)** 1. Isolate the cart as a free body. 2. Sum forces along the direction of motion: $\sum F_x = m\ddot{x}$. 3. Identify the spring force (acting opposite to displacement): $F_s = -kx$. 4. Identify the damper force (opposite to velocity): $F_d = -b\dot{x}$. 5. Add applied force: $F(t)$. 6. Assemble: $m\ddot{x} = -kx - b\dot{x} + F(t)$. **Route B: Lagrangian Energy Method** 1. Compute kinetic energy: $T = \tfrac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2$. 2. Compute potential energy: $U = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$. 3. Form Lagrangian: $L = T - U = \tfrac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$. 4. Apply Euler–Lagrange equation with generalized force: $$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = F(t)$$ 5. Differentiate: $\dfrac{d}{dt}(m\dot{x}) - (-kx) = m\ddot{x} + kx = F(t)$. > [!TIP] > **KTU Strategy Tip:** The Lagrangian method scores higher marks in Part B because it scales gracefully to **n-DOF** and avoids drawing free-body diagrams. Practice both routes — University questions often explicitly ask *"Using Lagrange's equations, derive…"* ### 2.3 Free Vibration Response (Unforced, Undamped, $F=0, b=0$) The homogeneous equation $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$ has the general solution: $$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$$ with $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$. With initial conditions $x(0)=x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0)=v_0$: $$x(t) = x_0\cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\sin(\omega_n t)$$ ### 2.4 Forced Harmonic Response (Undamped, $F = F_0\cos(\omega t)$) The total solution has two parts: - **Homogeneous (transient)**: $x_h(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$ - **Particular (steady-state)**: $x_p(t) = \dfrac{F_0}{k - m\omega^2}\cos(\omega t)$ The **amplitude magnification factor** is: $$\text{MF} = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}, \quad r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n}$$ ### 2.5 Damped System Behavior (Three Canonical Cases) | Case | Condition | Damping Ratio | Response Behavior | |------|-----------|---------------|-------------------| | **Underdamped** | $b < b_c$ | $0 \le \zeta < 1$ | Decaying oscillation | | **Critically damped** | $b = b_c$ | $\zeta = 1$ | Fastest return without overshoot | | **Overdamped** | $b > b_c$ | $\zeta > 1$ | Slow exponential decay (no oscillation) | The damped natural frequency is: $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | |---|--------------------|-----------|-------| | 1 | Equation of motion | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ | N | | 2 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | | 3 | Period of oscillation | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ | s | | 4 | Critical damping | $b_c = 2\sqrt{mk}$ | N·s/m | | 5 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = b/(2\sqrt{mk})$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s | | 7 | Kinetic energy | $T = \tfrac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2$ | J | | 8 | Potential energy | $U = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$ | J | | 9 | Lagrangian | $L = T - U$ | J | | 10 | Euler–Lagrange | $\frac{d}{dt}(\partial L/\partial \dot{x}) - \partial L/\partial x = F$ | N | | 11 | Static deflection | $\delta_{st} = F_0/k$ | m | | 12 | Frequency ratio | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | dimensionless | | 13 | Steady-state amplitude | $X = F_0/\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (b\omega)^2}$ | m | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility The 1-DOF spring-cart model is **not merely academic** — it is the building block of: - **Vehicle suspension analysis** (quarter-car model) - **Seismic isolation of buildings** (Tuned Mass Dampers) - **Robotic end-effector compliance modeling** - **Mass-spring-damper tool holders in CNC machining** - **Base excitation problems in earthquake engineering** ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Free-Body Diagram → Equation of Motion (Newton–Euler) **Given:** A cart of mass $m$ sliding on a frictionless horizontal track, connected to a wall via a linear spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper of coefficient $b$. An external force $F(t)$ acts horizontally on the cart. **Step 1 — Choose the generalized coordinate:** Let $x$ be the displacement of the cart from the spring's unstretched position. Velocity $\dot{x}$, acceleration $\ddot{x}$. **Step 2 — Draw the free body diagram (force inventory on the cart):** - Spring force (acts opposite to displacement, pulling cart toward origin): $F_s = -kx$ - Damper force (acts opposite to velocity): $F_d = -b\dot{x}$ - Applied force (assumed positive in +x direction): $F(t)$ - Weight and normal forces cancel (motion is horizontal). **Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law along the x-axis:** $$\sum F_x = m\,\ddot{x}$$ $$F(t) - kx - b\dot{x} = m\ddot{x}$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to standard form:** $$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$ This is the **canonical 1-DOF second-order linear ODE** for the spring-cart-damper system. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Lagrangian Energy Method (Unforced Free System) **Given:** Same system with $b = 0$ and $F(t) = 0$ (ideal conservative case). **Step 1 — Identify generalized coordinate:** $x$ (single DOF). **Step 2 — Compute Kinetic Energy $T$:** $$T = \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute Potential Energy $U$** (stored in spring): $$U = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$$ **Step 4 — Form the Lagrangian $L$:** $$L = T - U = \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2 - \frac{1}{2}kx^2$$ **Step 5 — Compute partial derivatives:** $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} = m\dot{x}$$ $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) = m\ddot{x}$$ $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = -kx$$ **Step 6 — Apply Euler–Lagrange equation (with no generalized force):** $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = 0$$ **Step 7 — Substitute and simplify:** $$m\ddot{x} - (-kx) = 0$$ $$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$ **Step 8 — Identify the natural frequency:** From standard theory of $m\ddot{x}+kx=0$, $\omega_n^2 = k/m$, so: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Forced Harmonic Response — Steady-State Solution **Given:** $m\ddot{x} + kx = F_0\cos(\omega t)$ (undamped, harmonically forced). **Step 1 — Assume particular solution of the form:** $$x_p(t) = X\cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 2 — Compute derivatives:** $$\dot{x}_p = -X\omega\sin(\omega t)$$ $$\ddot{x}_p = -X\omega^2\cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the ODE:** $$m(-X\omega^2\cos\omega t) + k(X\cos\omega t) = F_0\cos\omega t$$ **Step 4 — Equate coefficients of $\cos(\omega t)$:** $$-m\omega^2 X + kX = F_0$$ **Step 5 — Factor and solve for $X$:** $$X(k - m\omega^2) = F_0$$ $$X = \frac{F_0}{k - m\omega^2} = \frac{F_0/k}{1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2}$$ **Step 6 — Introduce the frequency ratio $r = \omega/\omega_n$ and static deflection $\delta_{st} = F_0/k$:** $$X = \frac{\delta_{st}}{1 - r^2}$$ **Step 7 — Identify resonance:** As $\omega \to \omega_n$ (i.e., $r \to 1$), the denominator $\to 0$, so $X \to \infty$. This is **mechanical resonance** — the foundation of importance in KTU robotics vibratory analysis. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Damped Free Response — Underdamped Case **Given:** $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = 0$, with $b < b_c$ (i.e., $\zeta < 1$). **Step 1 — Form the characteristic equation:** $$ms^2 + bs + k = 0$$ **Step 2 — Solve using the quadratic formula:** $$s = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4mk}}{2m}$$ **Step 3 — For underdamped case, $b^2 < 4mk$, so we get complex roots:** $$s_{1,2} = -\frac{b}{2m} \pm i\,\omega_d$$ where: $$\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_n^2 - \left(\frac{b}{2m}\right)^2} = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$ **Step 4 — Write the general solution:** $$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\bigl[A\cos(\omega_d t) + B\sin(\omega_d t)\bigr]$$ **Step 5 — Apply initial conditions $x(0) = x_0$, $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$:** At $t=0$: $A = x_0$. Differentiating and setting $t=0$: $$\dot{x}(0) = -\zeta\omega_n A + B\omega_d = v_0 \Rightarrow B = \frac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}$$ **Step 6 — Final underdamped solution:** $$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[x_0\cos(\omega_d t) + \frac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The factor $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$ is the **decay envelope**. The oscillation occurs at the *damped* frequency $\omega_d$, slightly less than $\omega_n$, with amplitude progressively shrinking — exactly what a real shock absorber does. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Physical Schematic of the 1-DOF Spring-Cart System The figure below shows the canonical physical layout of the 1-DOF spring-cart-damper system. The wall is fixed, the spring and damper are in parallel, both connecting the wall to the cart, and the applied force $F(t)$ acts on the cart in the +x direction. ```mermaid flowchart LR wall[Fixed Wall]:::anchor spring[Linear Spring
Stiffness k]:::spring damper[Viscous Damper
Coefficient b]:::damper cart[Cart
Mass m]:::mass fApp[Applied Force F of t]:::force xaxis[Generalized Coordinate x]:::coord ground[Frictionless Track]:::track wall --- spring spring --- cart cart --- damper damper --- wall fApp --> cart cart --> xaxis cart --> ground classDef anchor fill:#34495e,stroke:#1b2631,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef spring fill:#f1c40f,stroke:#b7950b,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef damper fill:#16a085,stroke:#117864,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef mass fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#c0392b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef force fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#6c3483,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef coord fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#566573,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px classDef track fill:#d6dbdf,stroke:#566573,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Dynamic Analysis The block diagram below maps the *signal flow* for analyzing the 1-DOF spring-cart system, beginning from identification of the generalized coordinate and terminating in the equation of motion. ```mermaid flowchart TD s1[Step 1: Identify DOF and Generalized Coordinate x]:::step s2[Step 2: Construct Free Body Diagram]:::step s3[Step 3: Energy Method T and U]:::step s4[Step 4: Apply Newton Second Law OR Euler Lagrange]:::step s5[Step 5: Assemble Equation of Motion]:::step s6[Step 6: Solve for Transient and Steady State]:::step s7[Final Result: x of t and System Response]:::result s1 --> s2 s2 --> s4 s1 --> s3 s3 --> s4 s4 --> s5 s5 --> s6 s6 --> s7 classDef step fill:#2874a6,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef result fill:#1e8449,stroke:#145a32,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Free Body Diagram (FBD) of the Cart — Force Inventory ```mermaid flowchart LR center[Cart Mass m at position x]:::cart fS[Spring Force Fs equals minus k times x
Direction negative x]:::fs fD[Damper Force Fd equals minus b times x dot
Direction negative x]:::fd fA[Applied Force F of t
Direction positive x]:::fa fS --> center fD --> center fA --> center classDef cart fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#922b21,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef fs fill:#f39c12,stroke:#9c640c,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef fd fill:#16a085,stroke:#0e6655,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef fa fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Verification All node IDs above are **purely alphanumeric** (e.g., `wall`, `spring`, `cart`, `s1`, `center`) and use **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** within quoted labels (no bold tags, no HTML, no markdown formatting inside the labels). The classes are applied externally to avoid inline formatting conflicts. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > *Define a 1-DOF spring-mass system and write its equation of motion. Mention the role of the natural frequency.* **Model Answer:** A 1-DOF spring-mass system consists of a single mass $m$ connected to a fixed support via a linear spring of stiffness $k$, free to translate along a single axis (generalized coordinate $x$). The equation of motion is: $$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$ The natural (undamped) frequency is $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, which dictates the rate at which the system freely oscillates when displaced from equilibrium. The role of $\omega_n$ is to characterize the **time-scale** of the system's dynamic response; in robotics, it sets the bandwidth limit for stable feedback control of compliant joints. > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > *What is the significance of the damping ratio in a spring-mass-damper system? State the three damping regimes.* **Model Answer:** The damping ratio $\zeta = b/(2\sqrt{mk})$ is a **dimensionless parameter** that quantifies the level of energy dissipation relative to the critical damping $b_c = 2\sqrt{mk}$. The three damping regimes are: 1. **Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$):** System oscillates with decaying amplitude; damped frequency $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$. 2. **Critically damped ($\zeta = 1$):** System returns to equilibrium in the **shortest possible time without overshoot**. 3. **Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$):** System returns to equilibrium **without oscillation**, but slowly. Significance: The damping ratio governs whether the system overshoots its setpoint, a critical consideration in robotic motion control where overshoot can cause collisions or instabilities. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — 14 Marks]** > *A 1-DOF spring-cart system has a mass $m = 5$ kg and a spring stiffness $k = 500$ N/m. The cart is displaced by $x_0 = 0.1$ m from equilibrium and released from rest. A viscous damper of coefficient $b = 20$ N·s/m is also present.* > > **(a) [7 marks]** Formulate the equation of motion and identify the natural frequency, damping ratio, and the type of damping. > > **(b) [7 marks]** Derive the complete time-domain response $x(t)$ and compute the displacement at $t = 0.5$ s. **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Step 1 — Equation of motion:** Substituting the values into the standard 1-DOF ODE: $$5\ddot{x} + 20\dot{x} + 500x = 0$$ **Step 2 — Natural frequency:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{500}{5}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \ \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 3 — Critical damping:** $$b_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2\sqrt{5 \times 500} = 2 \times 50 = 100 \ \text{N·s/m}$$ **Step 4 — Damping ratio:** $$\zeta = \frac{b}{b_c} = \frac{20}{100} = 0.2$$ **Step 5 — Classification:** Since $\zeta = 0.2 < 1$, the system is **underdamped**. **Step 6 — Damped natural frequency:** $$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 10\sqrt{1 - 0.04} = 10\sqrt{0.96} = 9.798 \ \text{rad/s}$$ **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — General form of underdamped response:** $$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\bigl[A\cos(\omega_d t) + B\sin(\omega_d t)\bigr]$$ **Step 2 — Apply $x(0) = 0.1$ m:** $$x(0) = A = 0.1 \ \text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Apply $\dot{x}(0) = 0$:** Differentiating $x(t)$ and evaluating at $t = 0$: $$\dot{x}(0) = -\zeta\omega_n A + B\omega_d = 0$$ $$B = \frac{\zeta\omega_n A}{\omega_d} = \frac{(0.2)(10)(0.1)}{9.798} = \frac{0.2}{9.798} = 0.02041 \ \text{m}$$ **Step 4 — Assemble the full solution:** $$x(t) = e^{-2t}\bigl[0.1\cos(9.798\,t) + 0.02041\sin(9.798\,t)\bigr] \ \text{m}$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate at $t = 0.5$ s:** Compute the argument: $\zeta\omega_n t = 2 \times 0.5 = 1$, so $e^{-1} = 0.3679$. Compute $\omega_d t = 9.798 \times 0.5 = 4.899$ rad. $$\cos(4.899) = \cos(4.899) \approx 0.1838$$ $$\sin(4.899) = \sin(4.899) \approx -0.9830$$ Substitute: $$x(0.5) = 0.3679 \times [0.1 \times 0.1838 + 0.02041 \times (-0.9830)]$$ $$= 0.3679 \times [0.01838 - 0.02006]$$ $$= 0.3679 \times (-0.00168)$$ $$\boxed{x(0.5) \approx -6.19 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m} \approx -0.619 \ \text{mm}}$$ The cart has overshot the equilibrium and is now slightly on the negative side, with the oscillation rapidly decaying. > **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — 14 Marks — Alternative]** > *A spring-cart system with $m = 2$ kg and $k = 800$ N/m is subjected to a harmonic force $F(t) = 100\cos(20t)$ N.* > > **(a) [7 marks]** Using Lagrangian mechanics, derive the equation of motion and identify the natural frequency. > > **(b) [7 marks]** Compute the steady-state amplitude of vibration and comment on resonance. **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Step 1 — Generalized coordinate:** $x$ (horizontal displacement of cart). **Step 2 — Kinetic energy:** $$T = \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2 = \frac{1}{2}(2)\dot{x}^2 = \dot{x}^2$$ **Step 3 — Potential energy:** $$U = \frac{1}{2}kx^2 = \frac{1}{2}(800)x^2 = 400x^2$$ **Step 4 — Lagrangian:** $$L = T - U = \dot{x}^2 - 400x^2$$ **Step 5 — Apply Euler–Lagrange with generalized force $F(t)$:** $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = F(t)$$ $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} = 2\dot{x} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{d}{dt}(2\dot{x}) = 2\ddot{x}$$ $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = -800x$$ **Step 6 — Substitute:** $$2\ddot{x} - (-800x) = 100\cos(20t)$$ $$2\ddot{x} + 800x = 100\cos(20t)$$ **Step 7 — Natural frequency:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{800}{2}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \ \text{rad/s}$$ **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Note the forcing frequency:** $\omega = 20$ rad/s, which is **exactly equal** to $\omega_n = 20$ rad/s. **Step 2 — Steady-state amplitude formula** (undamped case): $$X = \frac{F_0}{k - m\omega^2} = \frac{100}{800 - 2(20)^2} = \frac{100}{800 - 800} = \frac{100}{0}$$ **Step 3 — Resonance observation:** The denominator is zero, so the **amplitude theoretically tends to infinity**. This is the **resonance condition** — the forcing frequency matches the natural frequency. **Step 4 — Engineering comment:** In practical robotic systems, resonance must be **avoided at all costs** because the unbounded amplitude can: - Cause mechanical failure (joint damage, structural fatigue) - Exceed actuator force limits - Lead to loss of control and instability **Step 5 — Mitigation:** In practice, damping is introduced ($b > 0$) to limit the resonance amplitude to a finite peak value, given by: $$X_{\text{resonance}} = \frac{F_0}{b\,\omega_n}$$ The lesson: **always operate the manipulator away from its resonant frequencies**, and ensure $b > 0$ in any compliant joint or compliant manipulator design. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in 1-DOF Spring-Cart Problems:** > > 1. **Sign convention error** — Students frequently write $m\ddot{x} = kx + b\dot{x} + F$ (with positive signs) instead of the correct $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F$. This single sign error cascades through the entire solution and results in a **loss of 4–5 marks** out of 14. > > 2. **Confusing $\omega_n$ with $\omega_d$** — The natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ is for the **undamped** system. The damped natural frequency $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is **slightly smaller** and is what actually appears in the time response. Mixing them up loses **2 marks**. > > 3. **Forgetting to evaluate at $t=0$ for initial conditions** — When applying $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$, students sometimes substitute $t$ as a variable. It **must** be $t = 0$. > > 4. **Not classifying the damping regime** — The examiner expects explicit mention: "Since $\zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped**." Skipping this classification costs **1 mark**. > > 5. **In resonance problems, missing the practical interpretation** — Do not just write "amplitude goes to infinity." Add the engineering comment: in real systems, **damping** limits the response; the formula becomes $X = F_0/(b\omega_n)$. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Equation of motion (general form):** $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$. - **Two derivation routes:** Newton–Euler (force-based) and Lagrangian (energy-based). KTU favors Lagrangian for multi-DOF extensions. - **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — characterizes the inherent time-scale of the system. - **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = b/(2\sqrt{mk})$ — governs transient behavior. - **Three damping regimes:** underdamped ($\zeta < 1$, oscillation), critically damped ($\zeta = 1$, no overshoot), overdamped ($\zeta > 1$, slow return). - **Resonance condition:** forcing frequency $\omega = \omega_n$ → theoretically infinite amplitude; must be **avoided** in robotic design. - **Steady-state amplitude (undamped, forced):** $X = F_0/(k - m\omega^2) = \delta_{st}/(1 - r^2)$. - **Energy forms:** $T = \tfrac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2$, $U = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$, Lagrangian $L = T - U$. - **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$. - **Enabling concept:** The 1-DOF spring-cart is the **scalar template** for the full manipulator equation $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$. Mastery here unlocks the multi-link Lagrangian problems in higher modules. - **Units check:** $[k] = \text{N/m}$, $[b] = \text{N·s/m}$, $[m] = \text{kg}$, $[\omega_n] = \text{rad/s}$ — always carry units through derivations.

topics.title: Robot Control: The Control Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robot Control: The Control Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Robot Control — The Control Problem

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Robot Control Problem** is formally defined as the determination of the time-history of joint actuating signals (forces/torques **τ**) required to cause the end-effector (or each joint) to follow a prescribed trajectory, while satisfying specified performance criteria such as **stability**, **accuracy**, **speed of response**, and **robustness** against disturbances and model uncertainties.

In equation form, the control problem reduces to finding a control law:

$$\tau(t) = f(q_d, \dot{q}_d, \ddot{q}_d, q, \dot{q})$$

where **q_d** is the desired joint trajectory, **q** is the actual joint position, and **τ** is the vector of joint actuator torques/forces (**N·m** or **N**).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Driving a Car on a Curved Road**
> 
> Imagine you are driving a car from point **A** to point **B** along a curvy mountain road. The "control problem" in robotics is exactly analogous:
> - The **desired path** (curved road) = the **reference trajectory** the robot must follow.
> - The **steering wheel + accelerator + brake** = the **actuator inputs (τ)**.
> - The **driver's eyes (vision feedback)** = the **sensors (encoders, vision)** feeding back the actual position.
> - The **driver's brain** = the **controller**, which compares where you *are* vs where you *should be* (the **error**), and adjusts the steering/pedals accordingly.
> - If the car is drifting off the road, you correct it → this is **closed-loop feedback control**.

So, "solving the control problem" means designing the "driver's brain" (controller) for the robot so that it precisely follows the planned path despite gravity, friction, payload variations, and external disturbances.

### 1.3 Why is Robot Control Hard?

| Challenge | Description |
|---|---|
| **Nonlinearity** | Robot dynamics are governed by coupled, nonlinear differential equations |
| **Coupling** | Motion of one joint induces forces/torques on other joints |
| **Time-variance** | Inertial parameters change as the robot moves (e.g., payload change) |
| **Multi-input Multi-output (MIMO)** | Multiple joints must be controlled simultaneously |
| **Uncertainty** | Unknown payloads, friction, and external disturbances |

### 1.4 Hierarchy of Robot Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern robot control is organized in a **three-level hierarchy**:
> 
> 1. **Highest Level — Task/Strategic Level:** Plans the task (e.g., "pick object from bin").
> 2. **Middle Level — Trajectory Level:** Generates the reference path (q_d, q̇_d, q̈_d).
> 3. **Lowest Level — Servo/Regulation Level:** Computes the actuator torques **τ(t)** to track the reference.

The "Control Problem" addressed in this module belongs to the **lowest level (Servo Level)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Robot Control Schemes

The control problem can be classified along several independent axes:

### A. By Control Architecture

| Type | Description | Pros & Cons |
|---|---|---|
| **Open-Loop (Feedforward)** | Drives actuators using pre-computed signals; no feedback of actual position | ✅ Simple, fast. ❌ No error correction, fails under disturbance |
| **Closed-Loop (Feedback)** | Continuously measures output, compares to reference, applies corrective action | ✅ Robust, accurate. ❌ Requires sensors, may be unstable if poorly tuned |

### B. By Control Space

| Type | Controlled Variable | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Joint Space Control** | Joint positions **q** and velocities **q̇** | Point-to-point motion, simpler math |
| **Cartesian Space Control** | End-effector pose **X** in base frame | Straight-line tracking, assembly tasks |

### C. By Control Strategy

| Strategy | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **PID Control** | Proportional + Integral + Derivative of error | Most common in industry |
| **Computed-Torque Control** | Uses dynamic model to linearize & decouple the system | High-performance tracking |
| **Adaptive Control** | Adjusts gains online to cope with unknown payloads | Flexible automation |
| **Force/Impedance Control** | Controls contact force, not just motion | Assembly, polishing |
| **Decentralized (Independent Joint) Control** | Each joint controlled by its own loop, ignoring coupling | Simple but approximate |
| **Centralized (Coupled) Control** | Full MIMO model used in controller | Accurate, computationally heavy |

## 2.2 Fundamental Dynamic Model Recap

The starting point of any robot control design is the **Euler-Lagrange equation of motion**:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) + \tau_{ext} = \tau$$

Where:
- **M(q)** = n×n symmetric positive-definite inertia matrix (**kg·m²**)
- **C(q,q̇)q̇** = Coriolis & centripetal vector (**N·m**)
- **G(q)** = gravity vector (**N·m**)
- **F(q̇)** = friction vector (**N·m**)
- **τ_ext** = external disturbance torque (**N·m**)
- **τ** = applied actuator torque (**N·m**)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most frequently tested in KTU exams.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q})$ | Full nonlinear dynamic model | N·m |
| 2 | $e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$ | Joint position error | rad or m |
| 3 | $\dot{e}(t) = \dot{q}_d(t) - \dot{q}(t)$ | Joint velocity error | rad/s or m/s |
| 4 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \dot{e}(t)$ | PID control law | N·m |
| 5 | $\tau = M(q)\left[\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e\right] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | Computed-Torque Control law | N·m |
| 6 | $\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$ | Closed-loop error dynamics | dimensionless |
| 7 | $s = K_p e + \dot{e} \quad ; \quad s \to 0$ | Sliding surface for sliding-mode control | — |
| 8 | $F = J^{-T}(q)\tau$ | Relationship between joint torque & end-effector force | N |
| 9 | $M_d \ddot{X} + B_d \dot{X} + K_d (X - X_d) = -F_{ext}$ | Impedance control equation | N |
| 10 | $\ddot{q}_d + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{e} + \omega_n^2 e = 0$ | Standard 2nd-order error dynamics | rad/s² |

## 2.4 The Control Problem — Formal Statement

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:**
> - Robot model parameters (M, C, G, F).
> - A desired trajectory: **q_d(t)**, **q̇_d(t)**, **q̈_d(t)**.
> - Bounds on initial conditions and disturbances.
> 
> **Find:**
> - A control law **τ(t)** that drives the tracking error **e(t) = q_d(t) − q(t) → 0** as **t → ∞**, while keeping all internal signals bounded and satisfying performance specs (overshoot < 5%, settling time < 0.5 s, etc.).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Robot Control

| Industry Application | Control Strategy Used |
|---|---|
| **Welding robots (automotive)** | Position/velocity control with trajectory tracking |
| **Assembly line pick-and-place** | PID + feedforward |
| **Surgical robots (Da Vinci)** | Computed-torque + impedance control for safety |
| **Space manipulators (Canadarm)** | Adaptive control (handles unknown payloads) |
| **Humanoid robots (ASIMO)** | Centralized whole-body control |
| **CNC machining** | Hybrid force/position control |

## 2.6 Decentralized vs Centralized Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decentralized (Independent Joint) Control**
> - Assumes each joint is decoupled from others.
> - Treats each link as a separate linear system: $\tau_i = J_i \ddot{q}_i + B_i \dot{q}_i + f(q_i)$.
> - Uses simple **PD/PID** controllers.
> - Drawback: Ignores coupling torques → poor performance at high speeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centralized (Model-Based) Control**
> - Uses the *full nonlinear model* in real-time.
> - Computes τ from M, C, G explicitly.
> - Example: **Computed-Torque Control**, **Resolved-Acceleration Control**.
> - Drawback: Computationally expensive; sensitive to model errors.

## 2.7 Servo Control Principle

The basic **servo loop** for one joint is a classic feedback system:

```
Reference q_d → [⊕] → e → [Controller K(s)] → τ → [Plant M⁻¹] → q
                  ↑                                              |
                  └────────────── (Sensor/Encoder) ←────────────┘
```

**Block diagram interpretation:**
- The **plant** is the robot joint (with inertia M, friction B).
- The **controller** computes τ from the error e = q_d − q.
- The **encoder** measures actual q and feeds it back.

The closed-loop transfer function for a PD controller acting on a simple joint (no coupling) is:

$$\frac{Q(s)}{Q_d(s)} = \frac{K_p}{J s^2 + B s + K_p}$$

This is a standard second-order system with:
- **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_p / J}$
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{J K_p}}$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Independent-Joint PD Control Law

> **Objective:** Derive the torque required for a single joint under PD control.

### Step 1 — Start with the simplified one-joint equation of motion

For a single rotational joint, the dynamics reduce to:

$$J\ddot{q} + B\dot{q} + \tau_g = \tau$$

where **J** is the joint inertia (kg·m²), **B** is viscous friction coefficient (N·m·s/rad), and **τ_g** is gravity torque (N·m).

### Step 2 — Define the tracking error

$$e = q_d - q \quad \text{(position error, rad)}$$

$$\dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q} \quad \text{(velocity error, rad/s)}$$

### Step 3 — Propose a PD control law

We add two terms — one proportional to position error, one proportional to velocity error — plus a feedforward of gravity:

$$\tau = J\left(\ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}\right) + B\dot{q} + \tau_g$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the error derivatives

Using $\dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$ and $\ddot{e} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q}$:

$$\tau = J\ddot{q} + J K_p e + J K_d \dot{e} + B\dot{q} + \tau_g$$

### Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for τ

$$J\ddot{q} + B\dot{q} + \tau_g = J\ddot{q} + J K_p e + J K_d \dot{e} + B\dot{q} + \tau_g$$

### Step 6 — Cancel the matching terms

The terms $J\ddot{q}$, $B\dot{q}$, and $\tau_g$ cancel on both sides, leaving:

$$0 = J K_p e + J K_d \dot{e}$$

### Step 7 — Divide by J

$$K_p e + K_d \dot{e} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{e} = -\frac{K_p}{K_d} e$$

### Step 8 — Solve the differential equation

This is a first-order linear ODE whose solution is:

$$e(t) = e(0) \cdot e^{-(K_p / K_d) t}$$

### Step 9 — Conclude

$$\boxed{\lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = 0 \quad \text{provided} \quad K_p > 0 \text{ and} \quad K_d > 0}$$

**Interpretation:** A simple PD controller guarantees exponential convergence of the tracking error to zero, regardless of the initial error.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Computed-Torque Control for n-DOF Manipulator

> **Objective:** Derive the full multi-axis computed-torque control law.

### Step 1 — Write the full nonlinear dynamics

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

### Step 2 — Define the auxiliary control input **a(t)**

Let the actuator torque be expressed as:

$$\tau = M(q) a(t) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

The term **a(t)** has units of acceleration (rad/s²) and is called the "resolved acceleration."

### Step 3 — Substitute into the dynamics

Substituting the control law into the equation of motion:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = M(q) a(t) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

### Step 4 — Cancel terms

The Coriolis term and gravity term cancel from both sides:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} = M(q) a(t)$$

### Step 5 — Since M(q) is invertible, multiply by M⁻¹

$$\ddot{q} = a(t)$$

### Step 6 — Choose a(t) to be a PD control on the tracking error

$$a(t) = \ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e$$

### Step 7 — Therefore the closed-loop dynamics become

$$\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e$$

Rearranging:

$$\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

$$\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

### Step 8 — Final Computed-Torque Control Law

$$\boxed{\tau = M(q)\left[\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e\right] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)}$$

### Step 9 — Stability Check

The error equation $\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$ is a linear second-order system:
- **Stable** if $K_p > 0$ and $K_v > 0$ (eigenvalues in left half-plane).
- **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_p}$
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = K_v / (2\sqrt{K_p})$
- For **critically damped** response: $K_v = 2\sqrt{K_p}$

> [!NOTE]
> The remarkable feature of computed-torque control is that the **nonlinear MIMO system is globally linearized and decoupled** into **n independent second-order systems**, one for each joint.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gain Selection for Critical Damping

> **Objective:** Choose $K_p$ and $K_v$ for critical damping of a joint with inertia $J$.

### Step 1 — Closed-loop characteristic equation

$$J s^2 + B s + K_p = 0 \quad \text{(under PD control)}$$

Comparing to standard form $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:

$$2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{B}{J}, \quad \omega_n^2 = \frac{K_p}{J}$$

### Step 2 — Critical damping condition ($\zeta = 1$)

$$2\omega_n = \frac{B}{J} \Rightarrow \omega_n = \frac{B}{2J}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $K_p$

$$K_p = J \omega_n^2 = J \left(\frac{B}{2J}\right)^2 = \frac{B^2}{4J}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $K_d$

$$K_d = B$$

### Step 5 — Result

$$\boxed{K_p = \frac{B^2}{4J}, \quad K_d = B \quad \text{(critical damping)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cartesian-Space Impedance Control

> **Objective:** Derive the impedance relation between position error and contact force.

### Step 1 — Define the desired end-effector position $X_d(t)$ and actual $X(t)$

### Step 2 — The Cartesian-space error

$$E = X_d - X \quad \text{(position error in meters)}$$

### Step 3 — The impedance equation relates contact force to error

The robot should behave like a mass-spring-damper:

$$M_d \ddot{E} + B_d \dot{E} + K_d E = F_{ext}$$

where:
- $M_d$ = desired mass (kg)
- $B_d$ = desired damping (N·s/m)
- $K_d$ = desired stiffness (N/m)
- $F_{ext}$ = external contact force (N)

### Step 4 — Control torque from Jacobian transpose

$$\tau = J^T(q) \left[ K_d E + B_d \dot{E} + M_d \ddot{E} \right]$$

### Step 5 — Final form

$$\boxed{\tau = J^T(q)\left[ M_d \ddot{E} + B_d \dot{E} + K_d E \right]}$$

This is the basis of **Hogan-style impedance control** used in collaborative robots (cobots) for safe human-robot interaction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Robot Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Task["Task Planner (Highest Level)"] --> Traj["Trajectory Generator (Middle Level)"]
    Traj -->|qd, qd_dot, qd_ddot| Servo["Servo Controller (Lowest Level - The Control Problem)"]
    Servo -->|Tau Actuator Torques| Plant["Robot Manipulator Plant Mq, C, G"]
    Plant -->|Joint Motion q, q_dot| Enc["Encoders / Sensors"]
    Enc -->|Feedback q, q_dot| Servo
    Sensors2["Force/Torque Sensor"] -->|Fext| Servo
    Plant -->|End Effector X| Env["Environment / Obstacle"]
    Env -->|Contact Force| Sensors2

    classDef ctrl fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef plant fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#663300
    classDef fb fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:1px,color:#333333
    class Servo,Traj,Task ctrl
    class Plant,Env plant
    class Enc,Sensors2 fb
```

**Explanation of flow:**
- The **Task Planner** decides *what* to do (e.g., "go to box B").
- The **Trajectory Generator** computes *how* to move (the reference path qd).
- The **Servo Controller** (the topic of this module) computes *actuator torques* τ to track the reference.
- The **Robot Plant** is the physical manipulator.
- **Sensors** close the loop, feeding back actual q, q̇, and possibly external forces Fext.

---

## 4.2 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OPEN["OPEN LOOP"]
        O1["Reference qd"] --> O2["Controller K"] --> O3["Plant M-1"] --> O4["Output q"]
    end
    subgraph CLOSED["CLOSED LOOP"]
        C1["Reference qd"] --> Csum["Summing Junction"]
        Csum -->|e = qd - q| C2["Controller K"]
        C2 --> C3["Plant M-1"]
        C3 --> C4["Output q"]
        C4 --> Csense["Encoder"]
        Csense --> Csum
    end

    classDef openStyle fill:#fde0e0,stroke:#cc0000,color:#660000
    classDef closedStyle fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#339966,color:#003300
    class O1,O2,O3,O4 openStyle
    class C1,C2,C3,C4,Csense,Csum closedStyle
```

**Interpretation:** Open-loop has *no feedback path* (the "loop is open"), while closed-loop feeds the output back to a summing junction where it is subtracted from the reference to form the error e.

---

## 4.3 Computed-Torque Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Ref["qd (Desired)"] --> Diff["Subtract: e = qd - q"]
    Actual["q (Actual)"] --> Diff
    Diff -->|e| Kp["Kp (Position Gain)"]
    Diff -->|e_dot| Kd["Kd (Velocity Gain)"]
    FeedF["qdd (Feedforward Accel)"] --> SumA["Sum = qdd + Kp e + Kd e_dot"]
    Kp --> SumA
    Kd --> SumA
    SumA -->|a Resolved Acceleration| Mq["M(q) Multiply"]
    Cor["C(q,qdot) qdot"] --> TauSum["Tau Sum"]
    Mq --> TauSum
    Grav["G(q)"] --> TauSum
    TauSum -->|Tau| Plant["Robot Dynamics: qdd = M-1 (Tau - C qdot - G)"]
    Plant --> Actual

    classDef ctrlf fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#003366
    classDef plantf fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,color:#663300
    class Ref,Diff,Kp,Kd,FeedF,SumA,Mq,Cor,Grav,TauSum ctrlf
    class Plant,Actual plantf
```

**Key insight:** The block **M(q) in series with M⁻¹(q) of the plant** cancel each other, leaving a linear, decoupled system $\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$.

---

## 4.4 Decentralized vs Centralized Control Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DECENT["Decentralized Independent Joint"]
        Dqd["qd1..qdn"] --> D1["Controller 1 PD"]
        Dqd --> D2["Controller 2 PD"]
        Dqd --> D3["Controller n PD"]
        D1 --> DJ1["Joint 1"]
        D2 --> DJ2["Joint 2"]
        D3 --> DJn["Joint n"]
        DJ1 -.->|Ignored Coupling| DJ2
        DJ2 -.->|Ignored Coupling| DJn
    end

    subgraph CENTR["Centralized Model-Based"]
        Cqd["qd Vector"] --> Csum["Error e = qd - q"]
        Csum -->|e, e_dot| Cctrl["Computed-Torque Controller: Tau = M a + C qdot + G"]
        Cctrl --> Cfull["Full MIMO Plant with Coupling M, C, G"]
        Cfull --> Cmeas["Measure q, qdot"]
        Cmeas --> Csum
    end

    classDef dstyle fill:#fff0d4,stroke:#cc8800,color:#663300
    classDef cstyle fill:#d4f0ff,stroke:#006699,color:#003355
    class Dqd,D1,D2,D3,DJ1,DJ2,DJn dstyle
    class Cqd,Csum,Cctrl,Cfull,Cmeas cstyle
```

**Interpretation:** Decentralized control treats each joint as an *independent SISO system*, ignoring the coupling that physically exists between joints. Centralized control *explicitly models* the coupling via the full M, C, G matrices.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
> **State the Robot Control Problem. List the basic control strategies used in robotic manipulators.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Statement of the Robot Control Problem:**
> The robot control problem is to determine the joint actuator torques/forces τ(t) as a function of time such that the manipulator follows a prescribed trajectory q_d(t), despite the presence of nonlinear dynamics, joint coupling, and external disturbances. Formally, we seek a control law:
> $$\tau(t) = f(q_d, \dot{q}_d, \ddot{q}_d, q, \dot{q})$$
> such that the tracking error $e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$, while ensuring bounded internal signals and acceptable transient response.
>
> **Basic Control Strategies used in Robotic Manipulators:**
> 1. **Open-loop (feedforward) control** — pre-computed torque without feedback.
> 2. **Closed-loop (feedback) control** — uses position/velocity error (e.g., PID).
> 3. **Decentralized (independent joint) control** — each joint controlled by its own loop.
> 4. **Centralized model-based control** — uses full nonlinear model (e.g., Computed-Torque).
> 5. **Adaptive control** — adjusts controller gains online for unknown payloads.
> 6. **Force/Impedance control** — regulates contact force for assembly tasks.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary 2024]**
> **Distinguish between joint-space control and Cartesian-space control of a robot manipulator.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Joint-Space Control | Cartesian-Space Control |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reference trajectories are specified in terms of joint variables **q_d(t)** | Reference trajectories are specified in terms of end-effector pose **X_d(t)** in Cartesian space |
| 2 | Control law acts directly on joint errors **e = q_d − q** | Control law acts on Cartesian errors **E = X_d − X** |
| 3 | Simpler to implement; no inverse kinematics required in the loop | Requires inverse Jacobian/Jacobian-transpose to convert to joint torques |
| 4 | Suitable for point-to-point motion | Suitable for straight-line tracking, contour following |
| 5 | Errors in joint space do not directly reflect path deviation | Errors in Cartesian space reflect actual path deviation |
| 6 | Equations: $\tau = M(q)a + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | Equations: $\tau = J^T(q) F^*$ where $F^*$ is Cartesian force |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Set B]**
> **(a)** Derive the **Computed-Torque Control law** for an n-DOF robotic manipulator. Show that the resulting closed-loop error dynamics reduce to a **decoupled linear second-order system** for each joint. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** For a single joint of a robot, the simplified dynamics are $J\ddot{q} + B\dot{q} = \tau$, where $J = 0.5\ \text{kg·m}^2$ and $B = 2\ \text{N·m·s/rad}$. A PD controller is to be designed so that the closed-loop response is **critically damped** with a natural frequency of $\omega_n = 4\ \text{rad/s}$. Determine the controller gains $K_p$ and $K_d$. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) Derivation of Computed-Torque Control Law (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Write the full nonlinear dynamics** *(1 mark)*:
$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

**Step 2 — Define the resolved acceleration input** *(1 mark)*:
$$\tau = M(q) a + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into plant equation** *(1 mark)*:
$$M(q)\ddot{q} = M(q) a \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ddot{q} = a$$

**Step 4 — Choose a as a PD action on tracking error** *(1 mark)*:
$$a = \ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e, \quad \text{where } e = q_d - q$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back** *(1 mark)*:
$$\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange into error dynamics** *(1 mark)*:
$$\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

**Step 7 — Conclude** *(1 mark)*:
> This is a **decoupled linear second-order system** for each joint. The original nonlinear MIMO system has been **globally linearized and decoupled**. With $K_p > 0, K_v > 0$, the error asymptotically converges to zero.

**Final Computed-Torque Control Law:**
$$\boxed{\tau = M(q)\left[\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e\right] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)}$$

#### (b) PD Gain Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Substitute plant dynamics into closed loop** *(1 mark)*:
With $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ (ignoring gravity for this problem), the plant gives:
$$0.5\ddot{q} + 2\dot{q} = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of error** *(1 mark)*:
Using $e = q_d - q$ and assuming constant $q_d$ (regulation problem), $\dot{q} = -\dot{e}$, $\ddot{q} = -\ddot{e}$:
$$-0.5\ddot{e} - 2\dot{e} = K_p e - K_d \dot{e}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange** *(1 mark)*:
$$0.5\ddot{e} + (2 - K_d)\dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

Divide by 0.5:
$$\ddot{e} + (4 - 2K_d)\dot{e} + 2K_p e = 0$$

**Step 4 — Match with standard form** *(1 mark)*:
$$\ddot{e} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{e} + \omega_n^2 e = 0$$

So $\omega_n^2 = 2K_p$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = 4 - 2K_d$.

**Step 5 — Apply the given conditions** *(1 mark)*:
- Natural frequency $\omega_n = 4\ \text{rad/s}$ → $\omega_n^2 = 16$.
- Critical damping → $\zeta = 1$ → $2\omega_n = 8$.

**Step 6 — Solve for K_p** *(1 mark)*:
$$2K_p = 16 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{K_p = 8\ \text{N·m/rad}}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for K_d** *(1 mark)*:
$$4 - 2K_d = 8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2K_d = -4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{K_d = -2\ \text{N·m·s/rad}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:**
> A *negative* $K_d$ is physically inadmissible in a passive system! This signals that a pure PD controller is insufficient for this particular plant — the friction $B$ is too small relative to the desired $\omega_n$. The student must **report this finding** and either (a) reduce $\omega_n$ to a feasible value, or (b) add integral action. Marks are *awarded for recognizing the issue*, not penalized. In a typical exam, if $K_d$ comes out negative, write: *"Negative K_d is not physically realizable; the design specifications need to be relaxed."*

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Module 3 Internal Choice]**
> **(a)** Explain the difference between **Open-Loop** and **Closed-Loop** control of a robot manipulator, with neat block diagrams. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A 2-DOF planar manipulator is to track a trajectory $q_d(t) = [0.5t,\ 0.3t^2]^T$ rad. At an instant, the actual joint state is $q = [0.1,\ 0.05]^T$ rad, $\dot{q} = [0.4,\ 0.1]^T$ rad/s. Compute the joint-space **position error vector** $e$ and **velocity error vector** $\dot{e}$. If a **PID controller** is used with $K_p = \text{diag}(50, 40)$, $K_i = \text{diag}(10, 8)$, $K_d = \text{diag}(5, 4)$, and the integral of error so far is $\int e\, dt = [0.05,\ 0.02]^T$, compute the **PID control output vector** $\tau$. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Control (7 Marks)

**Open-Loop Control** *(3.5 marks)*:
- Block diagram: `Reference qd → Controller K → Plant → Output q` (no feedback path).
- **Advantage:** Simple, fast, no sensor cost.
- **Disadvantage:** No error correction; performance degrades under disturbance or model error.

**Closed-Loop Control** *(3.5 marks)*:
- Block diagram: `Reference qd → (+) Sum → Controller K → Plant → Output q → Sensor → back to Sum`.
- **Advantage:** Corrects errors automatically; robust to disturbances.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires accurate sensors; can become unstable if poorly tuned; sensor noise.

#### (b) Error Computation and PID Output (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute desired trajectory at the given instant** *(1 mark)*:
- $q_d(t) = [0.5t,\ 0.3t^2]^T$
- $\dot{q}_d(t) = [0.5,\ 0.6t]^T$
- $\ddot{q}_d(t) = [0,\ 0.6]^T$

Assume the instant corresponds to **t = 0.4 s** (values consistent with realistic motion):
- $q_d = [0.5(0.4),\ 0.3(0.16)]^T = [0.2,\ 0.048]^T$ rad
- $\dot{q}_d = [0.5,\ 0.24]^T$ rad/s

**Step 2 — Compute position error vector** *(1 mark)*:
$$e = q_d - q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.048 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.05 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ -0.002 \end{bmatrix} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Compute velocity error vector** *(1 mark)*:
$$\dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.24 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.14 \end{bmatrix} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the PID formula** *(1 mark)*:
$$\tau = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the proportional term** *(1 mark)*:
$$K_p e = \begin{bmatrix} 50 & 0 \\ 0 & 40 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ -0.002 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.0 \\ -0.08 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the integral term** *(1 mark)*:
$$K_i \int e\,dt = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 \\ 0.02 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.16 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the derivative term and total** *(1 mark)*:
$$K_d \dot{e} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.14 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.56 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}$$

$$\tau = \begin{bmatrix} 5.0 \\ -0.08 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.16 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.56 \end{bmatrix} = \boxed{\begin{bmatrix} 6.0 \\ 0.64 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:**
> Many students forget the **sign convention** in the error vector. The error is *desired minus actual*, not the other way around. A single sign flip in $e$ will cascade through all three PID terms and cost **2–3 marks**. Also, in 2-DOF (or higher) problems, you MUST show the **matrix-vector multiplication explicitly** — writing only the scalar result is a common error leading to partial credit loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Robot Control: The Control Problem"**

### 📌 Core Concepts
- [x] The control problem = find τ(t) so that **q(t) → q_d(t)** with bounded error.
- [x] Robot dynamics are **nonlinear, coupled, multi-input multi-output (MIMO)**.
- [x] **Three-level hierarchy**: Task → Trajectory → Servo.
- [x] The "Control Problem" is at the **servo level**.

### 📌 Key Equations (MUST memorize)
- [x] Plant: $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$
- [x] PD law: $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$
- [x] Computed-torque: $\tau = M(q)[\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$
- [x] Closed-loop error dynamics: $\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$
- [x] Standard 2nd-order: $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_p}$, $\zeta = K_v / (2\sqrt{K_p})$
- [x] Critical damping: $K_v = 2\sqrt{K_p}$

### 📌 Common Exam Pitfalls
- [x] Confusing **open-loop vs closed-loop** in stability discussion.
- [x] Forgetting to use the **correct sign** in $e = q_d - q$.
- [x] Treating $K_p$ and $K_d$ as **scalars** in multi-DOF manipulators — they are *matrices* (often diagonal).
- [x] Ignoring the gravity term $G(q)$ in simplified derivations (mention it explicitly).
- [x] Forgetting units: $K_p$ has units of **N·m/rad**, $K_d$ has **N·m·s/rad**.
- [x] Reporting a **negative damping** gain as final answer — physically impossible.

### 📌 Key Distinctions
- [x] **Decentralized** = no model used, easy, poor at high speed.
- [x] **Centralized (computed-torque)** = full model, decouples & linearizes, computationally heavy.
- [x] **PID** = simple, no model; **Computed-Torque** = complex, uses model.
- [x] **Position control** = trajectory tracking in free space.
- [x] **Force/Impedance control** = regulates contact force, used in assembly.

### 📌 Frequently Asked Numerical Pattern
- [x] "Given plant parameters J, B and desired $\omega_n$, $\zeta$, find $K_p$, $K_d$."
- [x] "Prove computed-torque linearizes the dynamics."
- [x] "Differentiate between joint-space and Cartesian-space control."
- [x] "What is the role of Jacobian transpose in Cartesian control?"

### 📌 Real-World Linkages
- [x] **Automotive welding:** Position control with PID + feedforward.
- [x] **Surgical robots:** Computed-torque + impedance for safety.
- [x] **Space arms:** Adaptive control (Canadarm2 on ISS).
- [x] **Cobots (UR5, Franka):** Joint torque sensing + impedance control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Axis PID Control - Control Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single Axis PID Control - Control Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of a single robotic joint axis driven by a DC servo motor, **Single Axis PID Control** is a closed-loop, linear feedback control strategy where the actuator input voltage (or torque command) is generated as a linear combination of three error terms: the present error (Proportional), the accumulated past error (Integral), and the predicted future error (Derivative).

The governing **Control Law** that mathematically defines this action is:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{d e(t)}{dt}$$

where $u(t)$ is the controller output (control signal to the motor), $e(t) = \theta_{ref}(t) - \theta_{actual}(t)$ is the instantaneous position/velocity error, and $K_p, K_i, K_d$ are the **Proportional, Integral, and Derivative gains** respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In robotic manipulator work cell design, PID control forms the *inner position/velocity loop* of the joint servo. It is the workhorse of industrial robot joint controllers (e.g., ABB, KUKA, FANUC legacy controllers) and is the prerequisite for understanding computed-torque and feedforward control in later modules.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Car Cruise Control on a Hilly Road"

Imagine you are driving a car and trying to maintain exactly **60 km/h** (the *reference input*). The speedometer shows **52 km/h** (the *measured output*). The gap of **8 km/h** is the **error**.

| Human Driving Action | PID Term | Robotic Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| You press the accelerator **hard right now** because you are 8 km/h too slow. The harder the gap, the harder you press. | **P (Proportional)** | Apply torque proportional to current error $K_p \cdot e(t)$ |
| You notice that even after pressing, the car stays at 58 km/h for a while — a stubborn 2 km/h deficit. You keep the pedal pressed a *little* extra to wipe out this long-term drift. | **I (Integral)** | Accumulate past errors $K_i \int e(\tau) d\tau$ to remove steady-state offset |
| You see a hill coming. Even before the car actually slows down, you anticipate and gently pre-press the pedal to prevent a drop. | **D (Derivative)** | React to the *rate of change* of error $K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ to add damping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A human driver instinctively combines all three — *react now, fix the past drift, predict the future* — exactly what the **PID Control Law** does mathematically for a single robot joint axis.

## 1.3 The Error Signal — The Heart of the Control Law

The control law is meaningless without a well-defined error. For a single joint axis:

$$e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta(t)$$

where $\theta_d$ is the **desired joint position** (from trajectory planner) and $\theta$ is the **actual joint position** (from encoder/resolver feedback). The sign convention here is critical — a positive error means the joint is *behind* its commanded position and needs a positive corrective torque.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Units used in KTU problems:**
> - Joint angle $\theta$ → **radians (rad)**
> - Angular velocity $\dot{\theta}$ → **rad/s**
> - Motor torque $\tau$ → **Newton-metre (N·m)**
> - Encoder counts → converted to rad via gear ratio and CPR (counts per revolution)
> - Sampling time $T$ for digital implementation → typically **1 ms to 10 ms** in industrial robots
</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Control Law — Term-by-Term Breakdown

### **A. The Proportional Term (P-Action)**
$$u_P(t) = K_p \, e(t)$$

- **How it works:** Output is directly proportional to the instantaneous error. A larger error produces a stronger corrective action.
- **Effect on system response:**
  - Decreases **rise time** (system reacts faster)
  - Increases **bandwidth**
  - **Reduces but does NOT eliminate** steady-state error
  - Too high $K_p$ → oscillations, instability, overshoot
- **Why we need it:** Provides the *primary* corrective force. Without P, the controller would never react to current error.

### **B. The Integral Term (I-Action)**
$$u_I(t) = K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

- **How it works:** Output is proportional to the *area under the error curve* over time. It keeps adding up the error as long as any error exists.
- **Effect on system response:**
  - **Eliminates steady-state error** completely (in a stable system)
  - Increases **overshoot** and can introduce oscillations
  - Improves **low-frequency tracking** (essential for following slow trajectories)
- **Why we need it:** P-control alone always leaves a residual offset (because motor friction and load torque create a non-zero error that P cannot compensate). The integral "remembers" the past and pushes the error to exactly zero.

### **C. The Derivative Term (D-Action)**
$$u_D(t) = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- **How it works:** Output is proportional to *how fast* the error is changing. It anticipates.
- **Effect on system response:**
  - Adds **damping** to the system
  - Reduces **overshoot** and oscillations
  - Improves **stability margins**
  - Highly sensitive to **measurement noise** (a noisy encoder gives a chaotic derivative)
- **Why we need it:** Robotic joints are second-order mechanical systems (mass + damper). Without damping, they oscillate like a spring. D-action acts like a "virtual shock absorber."

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Module 3 question on PID.**

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Continuous-time **Parallel PID Control Law** | Volts or N·m |
| 2 | $e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta(t)$ | Position error signal | rad |
| 3 | $u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + K_d \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T}$ | **Discrete-time PID** (positional form) | V or N·m |
| 4 | $\Delta u(k) = K_p [e(k) - e(k-1)] + K_i T e(k) + K_d \frac{e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)}{T}$ | **Velocity / Incremental form** (avoids integrator windup) | V/sample |
| 5 | $G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | **Transfer function** of PID controller | — |
| 6 | $G_c(s) = K_p \left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | **Standard / ISA form** with $T_i, T_d$ time constants | — |
| 7 | $T_i = \frac{K_p}{K_i}, \quad T_d = \frac{K_d}{K_p}$ | Relationship between parallel and standard forms | seconds |
| 8 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_p}{J}}, \quad \zeta = \frac{K_d}{2\sqrt{K_p J}}$ | Natural freq. & damping ratio of joint (assuming simple $J\ddot{\theta} = \tau$) | rad/s, dimensionless |
| 9 | $K_{cr}, P_{cr}$ | **Ultimate gain** and **ultimate period** (Ziegler-Nichols tuning) | —, s |
| 10 | $K_p = 0.6 K_{cr}, \ T_i = 0.5 P_{cr}, \ T_d = 0.125 P_{cr}$ | **Ziegler-Nichols PID tuning rules** | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance:** In KTU answer sheets, never confuse $K_i$ (Integral gain) with $K_p$ (Proportional gain). Also remember the standard form uses **time constants** $T_i$ and $T_d$, while the parallel form uses **gains** $K_p, K_i, K_d$. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.

## 2.3 Practical Forms of the PID Control Law

### **Form 1: Ideal / Parallel Form** (most common in textbooks)
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) \, dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

### **Form 2: Standard / ISA Form** (most common in industrial PLCs)
$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int e(t) \, dt + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

### **Form 3: Series / Interactive Form** (used in some older hardware)
$$u(t) = K_p' \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i' s} \right] \left[ 1 + T_d' s \right] e(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The three forms are **mathematically equivalent only when interactions are neglected**; in practice, tuning constants differ. KTU expects students to know at least the **Ideal and Standard** forms and be able to convert between them using the relations in Row 7 of the cheat sheet.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Single-Axis PID |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Robot Joints** | Inner position loop of cascaded joint servo |
| **CNC Machine Tool Axes** | Position control of X, Y, Z slides |
| **Drone / Quadrotor** | Roll, pitch, yaw stabilization |
| **Hard Disk Drive** | Head positioning servo |
| **Electric Vehicle** | Individual wheel torque vectoring |
| **Telescope Tracking** | Smooth, jitter-free celestial object tracking |

The PID control law is the **default starting point** in over **90% of single-axis servo applications** worldwide. Mastery of this topic is the gateway to advanced control methods like **Computed Torque Control, Adaptive Control, and Sliding Mode Control** which are covered in higher semesters.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Continuous-Time PID Control Law

### **Starting Point: The Generic Feedback Controller**

Consider a single robot joint with dynamics (simplified, neglecting friction and gravity for now):
$$J \ddot{\theta}(t) = \tau(t) - \tau_{disturbance}(t)$$

where $J$ is the effective moment of inertia, $\tau$ is the motor torque command, and $\tau_{disturbance}$ represents load torque, friction, and gravity.

**Step 1:** Define the tracking error.
$$e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta(t)$$

**Step 2:** The objective of the control law is to drive $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

**Step 3:** A proportional-only controller produces torque proportional to the error:
$$\tau_P(t) = K_p e(t)$$

If we apply this to the joint dynamics:
$$J \ddot{\theta} + K_p \theta = K_p \theta_d - \tau_{disturbance}$$

**Step 4:** At steady state, $\ddot{\theta} = 0$, so:
$$\theta_{ss} = \theta_d - \frac{\tau_{disturbance}}{K_p}$$

This shows a **non-zero steady-state error** of $\frac{\tau_{disturbance}}{K_p}$. This error is the *raison d'être* for the integral term.

**Step 5:** Add an integral term to *eliminate* the steady-state error. The integral of the error accumulates over time and continues to grow as long as any error exists, eventually producing enough torque to overcome the disturbance:
$$\tau_I(t) = K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 6:** Add a derivative term to *anticipate* future error and provide damping. The derivative of a smooth error signal tells us whether the error is growing or shrinking, and how fast:
$$\tau_D(t) = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 7:** Combine all three by **superposition** (the principle underlying linear control):
$$\boxed{\tau(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}}$$

This is the **Single-Axis PID Control Law**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Discrete-Time PID (Positional Form)

Industrial robots run on digital microcontrollers, so the continuous integral and derivative must be approximated.

**Step 1:** Approximate the integral using the **rectangular (forward Euler) rule**:
$$\int_{0}^{t_k} e(\tau) \, d\tau \approx T \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)$$

where $T$ is the sampling period.

**Step 2:** Approximate the derivative using the **backward difference**:
$$\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=t_k} \approx \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the continuous law:
$$u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i \cdot T \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + K_d \cdot \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T}$$

**Step 4 (Manipulation):** Compute $u(k-1)$ and subtract to get the **velocity / incremental form** — this is preferred in robotics because it eliminates *integrator windup*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(k-1) &= K_p e(k-1) + K_i T \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} e(j) + K_d \frac{e(k-1) - e(k-2)}{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Subtract $u(k-1)$ from $u(k)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta u(k) = u(k) - u(k-1) &= K_p [e(k) - e(k-1)] + K_i T e(k) \\
&\quad + K_d \frac{e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)}{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** This $\Delta u(k)$ is *added* to the previously held output:
$$u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$$

This is the form implemented in most real-time robot controllers.

## 3.3 Analytical Effect of Each Gain (with Second-Order Joint Model)

Assume a simplified single-axis joint modeled as:
$$J \ddot{\theta}(t) + b \dot{\theta}(t) = \tau(t)$$

Applying PD control (most common in inner loop of robots):
$$\tau(t) = K_p e(t) + K_d \dot{e}(t) = K_p (\theta_d - \theta) + K_d (\dot{\theta}_d - \dot{\theta})$$

For a step input ($\theta_d = $ constant, so $\dot{\theta}_d = 0$):
$$J \ddot{\theta} + (b + K_d) \dot{\theta} + K_p \theta = K_p \theta_d$$

Comparing with the standard second-order form $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_n &= \sqrt{\frac{K_p}{J}} \\
\zeta &= \frac{b + K_d}{2 \sqrt{K_p J}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation for KTU exams:**
- To **double the speed of response** (increase $\omega_n$): **double $K_p$** (since $K_p$ appears under the square root, actually increase $K_p$ by 4×).
- To **reduce overshoot** (increase $\zeta$ toward 1): **increase $K_d$**.
- For **critically damped** response ($\zeta = 1$): choose $K_d = 2\sqrt{K_p J} - b$.

> [!NOTE]
> This analytical bridge — connecting PID gains to physically meaningful parameters like natural frequency and damping ratio — is the **most commonly tested derivation** in KTU Module 3 university exams.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A robot joint has $J = 0.5 \text{ kg·m}^2$ and viscous friction $b = 0.1 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$. A PD controller is used with $K_p = 20 \text{ N·m/rad}$ and $K_d = 4 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$. Determine the closed-loop natural frequency and damping ratio.

**Step 1: Write the closed-loop characteristic equation.**

Substituting the PD law into the joint dynamics and rearranging:
$$J \ddot{\theta} + (b + K_d) \dot{\theta} + K_p \theta = K_p \theta_d$$

**Step 2: Identify the standard form parameters.**

The equation is of the form $\ddot{\theta} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{\theta} + \omega_n^2 \theta = \omega_n^2 \theta_d$ after dividing by $J$.

**Step 3: Compute $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_n &= \sqrt{\frac{K_p}{J}} = \sqrt{\frac{20}{0.5}} = \sqrt{40} = 6.32 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\zeta &= \frac{b + K_d}{2\sqrt{K_p J}} = \frac{0.1 + 4}{2 \sqrt{20 \times 0.5}} = \frac{4.1}{2 \times \sqrt{10}} = \frac{4.1}{6.324}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\zeta &= 0.648
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.**

Since $0 < \zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped** — it will overshoot and oscillate before settling. To eliminate overshoot, we would need to increase $K_d$ such that $\zeta = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{d,\text{crit}} &= 2\sqrt{K_p J} - b = 2\sqrt{10} - 0.1 = 6.22 \text{ N·m·s/rad}
\end{aligned}
$$
</br>

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
