topics.title: Robot Selection Considerations for an Application: Number of Axes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robot Selection Considerations for an Application: Number of Axes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of industrial robotics, the **Number of Axes** of a robot manipulator refers to the total count of independent **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** that the robot can achieve within its workspace. Each axis corresponds to a single rotary (revolute) or linear (prismatic) joint that contributes one independent direction of controlled motion. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the number of axes is a **primary kinematic specification** that directly defines the robot's ability to **position** and **orient** an end-effector in 3D Cartesian space.

Mathematically, the configuration space of a serial manipulator with $n$ joints is described by the joint space vector:

$$q = \begin{bmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \\ \vdots \\ q_n \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

where each $q_i$ represents the displacement (rotary or linear) of the $i$-th axis. The dimension $n$ is the **Number of Axes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A robot requires a **minimum of 3 axes** to reach any point in 3D space (positioning), and a **minimum of 6 axes** to arbitrarily position **and** orient an object in 3D space. Any axis beyond 6 is a **redundancy**, used to avoid obstacles, joint limits, or singularities.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Human Arm" Model

Imagine your own arm as you pick up a coffee cup from a table:
- Your **shoulder** gives 3 motions (up/down, forward/back, rotate) — these are 3 axes.
- Your **elbow** gives 1 motion (bend) — that's the 4th axis.
- Your **wrist** gives 3 motions (pitch, yaw, roll) — these are axes 5, 6, and 7.

A **6-axis robot** mimics this exact configuration: 3 shoulder-equivalent axes (positioning) + 3 wrist-equivalent axes (orientation). If you tried to pour coffee with only 4 axes, your wrist would be "locked" and the cup would tilt awkwardly — that is exactly the problem a 4-axis SCARA robot faces with complex 3D assembly tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Distinction (KTU Board Favorite):**
> - **Degree of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent parameters that define the configuration of a mechanism.
> - **Number of Axes:** A manufacturer-specific term counting the **directly actuated** joints. In industrial robots, DOF = Number of Axes for serial manipulators.
> - **Redundant Manipulator:** A robot with $n > 6$ axes in 3D space, providing dexterity in cluttered environments.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Unit |
|--------|----------------|------|
| **Minimum DOF for 3D Position** | 3 | dimensionless |
| **Minimum DOF for 3D Pose (Position + Orientation)** | 6 | dimensionless |
| **Typical Industrial Range** | 3 to 7 | axes |
| **Wrist DOF (Orientation)** | 2 or 3 | axes |
| **Major Axes (Positioning)** | 3 | axes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 3-Position + 3-Orientation Framework

A rigid body in 3D Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^3$ has exactly **6 classical mechanical DOF** — 3 for the position of a reference point and 3 for the orientation (described by roll, pitch, yaw or Euler angles). This is derived directly from the **Chasles' Theorem** and the **Euler Rotation Theorem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chasles' Theorem:** Any displacement of a rigid body in space can be represented as a screw motion — a translation along an axis combined with a rotation about the same axis. This requires exactly 6 parameters.

Therefore, the **theoretical minimum** to fully control a rigid body in $\mathbb{R}^3$ is **6 independent controlled motions**, i.e., **6 axes**.

### 2.2 Classification of Robots by Number of Axes

#### **A. 3-Axis Robots (Cartesian / Gantry)**
- Structure: 3 prismatic joints (PPP).
- Application: Pick-and-place, CNC-style loading, palletizing in a structured grid.
- Limitation: **Cannot orient the end-effector** — orientation is fixed or limited to a single rotation (sometimes counted as 4th axis).

#### **B. 4-Axis Robots (SCARA, Palletizers)**
- Structure: RRPR or RRPR variants.
- Application: High-speed pick-and-place, PCB assembly, horizontal palletizing.
- Limitation: Wrist has only 1 rotational DOF — cannot tilt the end-effector in arbitrary directions.

#### **C. 5-Axis Robots**
- Structure: 3 major positioning axes + 2 wrist orientations (typically pitch + roll, no yaw).
- Application: Welding, painting, machining where some orientation freedom is sacrificed.
- Limitation: Cannot achieve full arbitrary orientation; specific 3D objects are unreachable.

#### **D. 6-Axis Robots (The Industry Workhorse)**
- Structure: 3 positioning + 3 orientation axes (full spherical wrist).
- Application: Arc welding, complex assembly, deburring, machine tending, surgery.
- Advantage: Full 6-DOF control — can reach any (position, orientation) pair in the dexterous workspace.

#### **E. 7-Axis Robots (Redundant / Collaborative)**
- Structure: Adds a "redundant" joint typically at the elbow.
- Application: Confined spaces, human-robot collaboration, automotive assembly inside car bodies.
- Advantage: Can reconfigure to avoid singularities and obstacles without changing end-effector pose.

### 2.3 Selection Logic — Matching Axes to Application

The selection process follows a structured **task-analysis flowchart**:

1. **Define the task type:** Pick-and-place, welding, painting, assembly, inspection.
2. **Determine the required pose:** 3D position only? Position + orientation? Orientation of tool?
3. **Analyze the workpiece geometry:** Simple boxes or complex curved surfaces?
4. **Evaluate the workspace:** Open, cluttered, or human-shared?
5. **Check reach and dexterity:** Envelope vs. dexterous workspace.
6. **Budget and cycle time constraints:** More axes = higher cost, slower cycle.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formulas for Number of Axes Selection**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Application |
|---|------------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $n_{\min}^{pos} = 3$ | Minimum axes for 3D position | Cartesian robots |
| 2 | $n_{\min}^{pose} = 6$ | Minimum axes for 3D pose | Full 6-DOF manipulators |
| 3 | $n_{red} = n - 6$ | Redundancy index (for $n > 6$) | 7-axis robots |
| 4 | $T = f(\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n)$ | Forward kinematics map (n-dimensional joint space → 3D task space) | All manipulators |
| 5 | $J(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ | Jacobian matrix — square for $n=6$, tall for redundant ($n>6$) | Velocity analysis |
| 6 | $\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$ | Task-space to joint-space velocity mapping | Trajectory planning |
| 7 | $r = \text{rank}(J)$ | Manipulability rank — full rank 6 for non-singular configurations | Singularity avoidance |
| 8 | $w = \sqrt{\det(JJ^T)}$ | Yoshikawa's manipulability index | Dexterity measure |
| 9 | $V_{task} = 6$ | Task-space DOF for full 3D pose | Selection baseline |
| 10 | $n_{req} = n_{pos} + n_{orient}$ | Total required axes decomposition | Application mapping |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern Industry 4.0 smart factories, axis count selection directly impacts:
- **Capital Expenditure (CAPEX):** A 6-axis robot costs **2× to 4×** a 4-axis SCARA.
- **Programming complexity:** Inverse kinematics for 6-axis has 8 solutions; for 7-axis is infinite.
- **Cycle time:** Each additional axis adds inertia and reduces acceleration capability.
- **Maintenance cost:** More axes = more servo motors, encoders, and reducers to maintain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry 4.0 Trend:** Modern collaborative robots (cobots) like Universal Robots UR10e (6-axis) and FANUC CRX (6-axis) prioritize **6-axis flexibility at a 4-axis price point**, demonstrating that axis selection is a strategic engineering decision, not just a kinematic one.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Axes for 3D Position

**Step 1 — Define the position vector.**
The position of any point in 3D Cartesian space is uniquely described by three coordinates:

$$P = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^3$$

**Step 2 — Count the independent parameters.**
A point in 3D space has exactly 3 independent parameters: $x$, $y$, and $z$. Therefore, the **minimum number of independent controlled motions** required to position a point arbitrarily is 3.

**Step 3 — Conclude the kinematic requirement.**
To position a reference point on the end-effector at any $(x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}^3$, the manipulator must have at least 3 independent actuated joints (axes).

$$\boxed{n_{\min}^{position} = 3}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A Cartesian (gantry) robot with 3 prismatic joints satisfies this requirement but cannot tilt or rotate the tool.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Axes for 3D Pose (Position + Orientation)

**Step 1 — Define the pose of a rigid body.**
A rigid body in $\mathbb{R}^3$ requires two pieces of information:
- Position of a reference point: $P \in \mathbb{R}^3$ (3 parameters).
- Orientation: $R \in SO(3)$ — a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix.

**Step 2 — Count the orientation parameters.**
A rotation matrix $R \in SO(3)$ has 9 elements constrained by 3 orthogonality + 1 determinant conditions = 6 constraints, leaving $9 - 6 = 3$ **independent** orientation parameters (e.g., roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$, yaw $\psi$).

**Step 3 — Sum the parameters.**

$$n_{\min}^{pose} = \underbrace{3}_{\text{position}} + \underbrace{3}_{\text{orientation}} = 6$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{n_{\min}^{pose} = 6}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Only a robot with 6 or more axes can both position and orient its end-effector arbitrarily. This is why most general-purpose industrial robots (e.g., KUKA KR 16, ABB IRB 1600) have exactly 6 axes.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Redundancy and the Self-Motion Manifold

For a robot with $n > 6$ axes, the configuration vector $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ contains more parameters than the 6D task space. The set of all joint configurations that produce the **same end-effector pose** is called the **self-motion manifold** or **null-space**.

**Step 1 — The Jacobian relationship.**
For a redundant manipulator, the Jacobian $J(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ is a non-square matrix with more columns than rows. The relationship between joint velocities and task-space velocities is:

$$\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}, \quad \dot{x} \in \mathbb{R}^6, \quad \dot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the joint velocity.**
The general solution uses the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse $J^{+}$ plus a null-space vector $\dot{q}_0$:

$$\dot{q} = J^{+}\dot{x} + (I - J^{+}J)\dot{q}_0$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the null-space term.**
The projector $(I - J^{+}J)$ maps any arbitrary vector $\dot{q}_0$ into the null space of $J$. This means the robot can **reconfigure its joints** (e.g., move the elbow) without moving the end-effector.

**Step 4 — Redundancy index.**

$$\boxed{n_{red} = n - 6}$$

For a 7-axis robot, $n_{red} = 1$, providing a 1-dimensional self-motion manifold — a curve in joint space for each fixed end-effector pose.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Application-to-Axis Mapping

| Application | Required DOF | Typical Axes | Reason for Axis Choice |
|-------------|--------------|--------------|------------------------|
| Pick-and-place (flat) | Position only | 3–4 | Speed and low cost dominate |
| PCB assembly | Position + 1 rotation | 4 | SCARA dominates — vertical insertion |
| Arc welding | Full 6D pose | 6 | Torch must be perpendicular to seam |
| Spray painting | Full 6D pose | 6 | Uniform coverage requires tilt control |
| Palletizing (boxes) | Position + 1 rotation | 4 | Vertical stack with simple yaw |
| Machine tending (CNC) | Position + 1–2 rotations | 5–6 | Door access and tool orientation |
| Deburring | Full 6D pose | 6 | Constant tool normal force angle |
| Surgical robotics | Full 6D pose + safety | 7 | Redundancy for patient safety |
| In-car assembly | Full 6D pose in clutter | 7 | Avoid obstacles inside car body |
| Bin picking | Position + 2 rotations | 5–6 | Vision-guided partial orientation |

### 3.5 Quantitative Trade-off Derivation — Cost vs. Axes

**Empirical cost scaling** for industrial robots of similar payload class:

$$C(n) \approx C_0 \cdot (1 + \alpha (n - n_0))$$

where:
- $C_0$ = baseline cost (e.g., of a 4-axis SCARA),
- $n_0 = 4$,
- $\alpha \approx 0.35$ to $0.50$ (empirical cost-per-axis factor).

**Example calculation:** For a 6-axis robot vs. a 4-axis baseline:

$$C(6) \approx C_0 \cdot (1 + 0.4 \cdot (6 - 4)) = C_0 \cdot 1.8$$

This implies a **6-axis robot costs ~80% more** than a 4-axis equivalent — a critical factor in selection.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Axis-Count Decision Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Application Task] --> B{Is 3D Position Only?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Need Any End-Effector Tilt?}
    C -- No --> D[3-Axis Cartesian or Gantry]
    C -- Yes --> E{Need Single Yaw Rotation?}
    E -- Yes --> F[4-Axis SCARA or Palletizer]
    E -- No --> G{Need Pitch and Yaw?}
    B -- No --> H[Need Full 6D Pose: Position + Orientation]
    G -- No --> I[5-Axis Welding or Painting Robot]
    G -- Yes --> H
    H --> J{Is Workspace Cluttered with Obstacles?}
    J -- No --> K[6-Axis Articulated Robot: Industry Workhorse]
    J -- Yes --> L[7-Axis Redundant Cobot or Special Manipulator]
    D --> M[End: Selection Confirmed]
    F --> M
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 Joint-Configuration Architecture (Spherical Wrist Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POS[Positioning Subchain 3 Axes]
        J1[Axis 1: Base Rotation q1] --> J2[Axis 2: Shoulder Pitch q2]
        J2 --> J3[Axis 3: Elbow Pitch q3]
    end
    J3 --> Wrist
    subgraph ORI[Orientation Subchain 3 Axes]
        Wrist[Spherical Wrist Center] --> J4[Axis 4: Wrist Roll q4]
        J4 --> J5[Axis 5: Wrist Pitch q5]
        J5 --> J6[Axis 6: Wrist Yaw q6]
    end
    J6 --> EE[End Effector Tool]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Axis-Count Capability Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph AX3[3-Axis Capability]
        A3[Reach Any X Y Z Point]
        A3a[Fixed Tool Orientation]
    end
    subgraph AX4[4-Axis Capability]
        A4[Reach Any X Y Z Point]
        A4a[Add 1 Yaw Rotation]
    end
    subgraph AX5[5-Axis Capability]
        A5[Reach Any X Y Z Point]
        A5a[Add Pitch and Yaw]
        A5b[No Full 3D Orientation]
    end
    subgraph AX6[6-Axis Capability]
        A6[Reach Any X Y Z Point]
        A6a[Full 3D Orientation]
        A6b[Singularities at Wrist Alignment]
    end
    subgraph AX7[7-Axis Capability]
        A7[Full 6-DOF]
        A7a[Elbow Redundancy]
        A7b[Obstacle and Singularity Avoidance]
    end
```

### 4.4 Workspace vs. Dexterous Workspace (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WS[Total Reachable Workspace V_total] --> DEXT[Dexterous Workspace: Full 6D Pose]
    WS --> REACH[Reachable Workspace: 3D Position Only]
    DEXT --> APP1[6-Axis Required: Welding, Complex Assembly]
    REACH --> APP2[3 to 4 Axes Sufficient: Pick and Place, Palletizing]
    REACH --> APP3[5 Axes: Partial Orientation Tasks]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (a3, a4, j1, etc.) and labels are kept clean without markdown formatting tags to ensure successful rendering across browsers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

Define the term **"Number of Axes"** of a robot manipulator. Why is a 6-axis configuration considered the standard for general-purpose industrial robots?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Number of Axes** of a robot manipulator is the count of independently actuated joints (rotary or prismatic) that define its degrees of freedom. Each axis provides one independent direction of motion in the joint space.

A 6-axis configuration is the industry standard because a rigid body in 3D space has **6 classical mechanical DOF** — 3 for position $(x, y, z)$ and 3 for orientation (roll, pitch, yaw). A 6-axis robot can, therefore, position and orient its end-effector arbitrarily within its dexterous workspace, satisfying the requirements of versatile tasks like welding, painting, and complex assembly.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

Differentiate between a **SCARA robot** and a **6-axis articulated robot** in terms of axis count, typical applications, and limitations.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | SCARA Robot | 6-Axis Articulated Robot |
|---------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Number of Axes** | 4 (RRPR) | 6 (RRRRRR) |
| **Typical Application** | PCB assembly, high-speed pick-and-place | Arc welding, complex assembly, painting |
| **Limitation** | Cannot tilt end-effector in 3D — limited to vertical insertion and 1 yaw rotation | Higher cost, more complex inverse kinematics, larger footprint |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** for selecting the number of axes of a robot for a given industrial application. Support your answer with a flowchart and justify the choice of a 6-axis robot for **arc welding** of a curved automobile chassis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **minimum number of axes** required to achieve full 3D position and arbitrary orientation in space. Explain what is meant by a **"redundant manipulator"** and its advantage in confined workspaces. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Selection Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Task Identification:** Determine the nature of the operation — pick-and-place, welding, painting, assembly, or inspection.
2. **Pose Analysis:** Identify whether the task needs 3D position only (3 axes) or full 6D pose (6 axes).
3. **Workpiece Geometry:** Analyze the shape and orientation requirements of the workpiece.
4. **Workspace Analysis:** Evaluate if the workspace is open or cluttered.
5. **Cycle Time and Cost Constraints:** Decide between 4-axis (cheaper, faster) and 6-axis (versatile, costlier).
6. **Final Selection:** Match the axis count to the application.

**Justification for Arc Welding of a Curved Chassis (7 marks):**

A curved automobile chassis has a 3D welding seam that changes direction continuously. The welding torch must:
- Reach every point on the seam (requires **3 positioning axes**).
- Maintain a constant **perpendicular orientation** with respect to the seam at every point (requires **3 orientation axes**).

Hence, a **6-axis articulated robot** is essential. A 4-axis SCARA cannot tilt the torch in 3D, and a 5-axis robot cannot maintain arbitrary torch orientation along the curve. Therefore, 6 axes is the **minimum required** to maintain weld quality.

**(b) Derivation of Minimum 6 Axes (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Position Requirement:** A point in 3D space requires 3 independent coordinates $(x, y, z)$, so 3 axes are needed for positioning:

$$n_{pos} = 3$$

**Step 2 — Orientation Requirement:** A rigid body orientation in $SO(3)$ has 3 independent parameters (e.g., Euler angles $\phi, \theta, \psi$), so 3 axes are needed for orientation:

$$n_{orient} = 3$$

**Step 3 — Total Required Axes:**

$$n_{\min} = n_{pos} + n_{orient} = 3 + 3 = 6$$

**Step 4 — Redundant Manipulator:** A manipulator with $n > 6$ axes is called **redundant** because it has more DOF than the task space requires.

**Step 5 — Advantage in Confined Spaces:** Redundancy (e.g., 7-axis cobot) allows the robot to reconfigure its intermediate joints (e.g., elbow) while keeping the end-effector pose fixed, using the **null-space projection**:

$$\dot{q}_{null} = (I - J^{+}J)\dot{q}_0$$

This enables obstacle avoidance, singularity avoidance, and human-safe collaboration in cluttered environments like inside a car body.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **structural difference** between a 4-axis SCARA robot and a 6-axis articulated robot. List **two industrial applications** for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing cell requires a robot to pick components from a conveyor and place them into a CNC machine tool. The components are rectangular boxes that must be inserted with a specific orientation into the tool holder. Justify the **number of axes** you would select for this application and explain the trade-off between a 5-axis and 6-axis choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SCARA vs 6-Axis Articulated Robot (7 marks):**

**SCARA Robot (4 Axes — RRPR):**
- Joint 1: Base rotation (revolute).
- Joint 2: Shoulder rotation (revolute).
- Joint 3: Vertical prismatic (linear up/down).
- Joint 4: Wrist rotation (revolute — yaw only).
- **Applications:** (i) PCB component insertion, (ii) High-speed pick-and-place of small parts.
- **Limitation:** Wrist is restricted to a single rotation — cannot tilt.

**6-Axis Articulated Robot (RRRRRR):**
- 3 positioning joints (waist rotation, shoulder, elbow).
- 3 wrist joints (pitch, yaw, roll — forming a spherical wrist).
- **Applications:** (i) Arc welding of car bodies, (ii) Complex multi-plane assembly.
- **Advantage:** Full 6D pose control.

**(b) Axis Selection Justification for CNC Tending (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Task Analysis:** The robot must:
- Move from conveyor to CNC door (requires **3 positioning axes**).
- Orient the box correctly for tool insertion (requires at least **1–2 orientation axes**).
- Reach inside the CNC enclosure (requires **dexterity and reach**).

**Step 2 — Minimum Axis Requirement:** For arbitrary orientation of a rectangular box (3D pose control), a **6-axis robot** is the technically correct choice.

**Step 3 — Trade-off: 5-Axis vs 6-Axis:**

| Parameter | 5-Axis Robot | 6-Axis Robot |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| Orientation control | Limited (2 of 3 wrist axes) | Full 3D orientation |
| Cost | ~25% cheaper | Industry standard price |
| Cycle time | Faster | Slightly slower |
| Application fit | Adequate for fixed-orientation insertion | Suitable for variable-orientation insertion |

**Conclusion:** If the box orientation is fixed (single insertion direction), a **5-axis robot** may suffice at lower cost. If the box must be oriented at varying angles, or if the CNC door is positioned at an awkward angle, a **6-axis robot** is the **safe, future-proof choice**. For a typical educational/engineering project, **6 axes is recommended** as it provides flexibility for retooling.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Confusing DOF with Number of Axes:** DOF and Number of Axes are equal only for serial manipulators. In parallel manipulators (e.g., Delta robot), the DOF can be less than the number of physical links.
> 2. **Stating "6 DOF = 6 Axes" without justification:** Always derive it from position (3) + orientation (3) for full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the orientation component for 4-axis SCARA:** Many students incorrectly say SCARA has 3 DOF. It has **4 axes** (3 positioning + 1 wrist yaw).
> 4. **Not mentioning the spherical wrist condition:** For 6-DOF, the last 3 axes must intersect at a common point (spherical wrist) — this is a key KTU marker point.
> 5. **Ignoring cost and cycle-time trade-offs:** Pure kinematic answers without engineering judgment lose application marks.
> 6. **Sketching without proper labels:** In KTU valuation, **unlabelled diagrams** are penalized heavily. Always label axes J1–J6 and end-effector.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Robot Selection: Number of Axes**
>
> ✅ **Number of Axes** = count of independently actuated joints in a serial manipulator.
>
> ✅ **Minimum 3 axes** for 3D positioning (Cartesian robots).
>
> ✅ **Minimum 6 axes** for 3D pose (position + orientation) — industry workhorse.
>
> ✅ **4-axis SCARA** dominates high-speed pick-and-place and PCB assembly.
>
> ✅ **5-axis robots** are used when partial orientation control is acceptable.
>
> ✅ **6-axis robots** are used for welding, painting, complex assembly, and machine tending.
>
> ✅ **7-axis (redundant) robots** are used in confined, human-shared, or obstacle-rich environments.
>
> ✅ The **Chasles' Theorem** and **Euler Rotation Theorem** mathematically justify the need for 6 DOF.
>
> ✅ The **Jacobian matrix** $J(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ becomes non-square for $n \neq 6$.
>
> ✅ **Redundancy index** = $n - 6$; null-space projection enables elbow reconfiguration.
>
> ✅ **Cost scaling:** Each additional axis increases robot cost by ~35–50%.
>
> ✅ **Spherical wrist** condition: last 3 axes must intersect at a common point for full orientation decoupling.
>
> ✅ Always justify axis selection using **task pose requirements**, not just cost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Work Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous kinematic framework of robotic manipulators, the **Work Volume** (also known as the **Workspace** or **Work Envelope**) is formally defined as the **locus of all points in three-dimensional space that can be reached by the end-effector's reference point (typically the tool center point, TCP)** as the manipulator traverses through all of its mechanically permissible joint configurations.

Mathematically, for a serial kinematic chain with $n$ joints having configuration space $Q = \{q \in \mathbb{R}^n : q_i^{min} \leq q_i \leq q_i^{max}, \forall i\}$, the work volume $W$ is defined as the **forward kinematic image** of $Q$ projected onto the position space of the TCP:

$$W = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^3 : p = f_{pos}(q), \quad q \in Q \}$$

where $f_{pos}(q)$ represents the position-level forward kinematics mapping joint coordinates to Cartesian position.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The work volume is a purely **geometric-kinematic** construct — it is determined entirely by link lengths, joint types, and joint limits. It is **independent** of payload, actuator torque limits, or dynamic constraints (these belong to the *dexterous payload capacity* domain).

---

## 1.2 Classification of Work Volume (KTU Board-Relevant Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates the differentiation between two fundamentally distinct workspace classifications:

| Workspace Type | Definition | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Reachable Workspace (RW)** | Set of all points reachable by the TCP in **at least one** orientation | A point $p$ belongs to RW if $\exists q \in Q$ such that $f(q) = p$ |
| **Dexterous Workspace (DW)** | Set of all points reachable by the TCP in **all possible orientations** | A point $p$ belongs to DW if $\forall R \in SO(3), \exists q \in Q$ such that $f(q) = (p, R)$ |

The fundamental inclusion relationship is:

$$W_{Dexterous} \subseteq W_{Reachable}$$

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a room and you hold a pen in your outstretched hand:

- **Reachable Workspace** = The set of *all points in space* you can poke with the tip of your pen by twisting your body, rotating your shoulder, and bending your elbow. This is essentially a **swept spherical bubble** around you — almost like an invisible sphere of influence.
- **Dexterous Workspace** = The much smaller set of points where you can poke the pen with **the tip pointing in any arbitrary direction you wish** (up, down, sideways, etc.). This is only possible near the center of the reachable region.

For an industrial welding robot arm, the **reachable workspace** tells the plant engineer where the welding torch *can* go, while the **dexterous workspace** tells where it can weld with the *optimal torch angle* in all positions.

> [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for Exam:** > **R**eachable = **R**each in **at least one** way (bigger volume) > **D**exterous = **D**o it in **every** orientation (smaller volume)

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (Bold Highlighting)

- **Reach (R):** Maximum radial distance from the robot base to the TCP, equal to the sum of link lengths for a fully extended configuration.
- **Joint Limits:** Mechanical hard-stops limiting each joint angle, e.g., $\theta_i \in [\theta_i^{min}, \theta_i^{max}]$.
- **Reference Point:** The TCP — typically located at the wrist center or end-effector flange.
- **Volume (V):** Measured in **cubic meters (m³)** or **cubic millimeters (mm³)** depending on scale.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Kinematic Foundation of Work Volume

The work volume is fundamentally derived from the **forward kinematics** of the manipulator. For a serial chain, the position of the TCP in the base frame is given by the Cartesian component of the homogeneous transformation matrix:

$$^{0}T_{n} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} {^{i-1}T_i}(q_i)$$

The position vector extracted from this matrix:

$$p_{TCP}(q) = \begin{bmatrix} p_x(q) \\ p_y(q) \\ p_z(q) \end{bmatrix} = \text{Translation component of } ^{0}T_{n}$$

The work volume is the **range space** of this vector-valued function evaluated over the joint configuration domain $Q$.

---

## 2.2 Factors Determining Work Volume

1. **Link Lengths (l₁, l₂, ..., lₙ):** Longer links produce larger reach.
2. **Joint Types:** Revolute (R) vs. Prismatic (P) joints produce dramatically different volume geometries.
3. **Joint Limits:** Mechanical stops that constrain $q_i^{min}$ and $q_i^{max}$.
4. **Number of Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Higher DOF generally yields larger workspaces but with increased kinematic complexity.
5. **Mechanical Offsets and Twists:** DH parameters $a_i$, $\alpha_i$, $d_i$ that define link geometry.

---

## 2.3 Work Volume Geometries by Robot Configuration

This is a **HIGH-YIELD** topic for KTU board examinations. The geometric shape of the work volume is directly determined by the robot's kinematic architecture:

| Robot Type | Joint Sequence | Work Volume Geometry | Volume Formula (if applicable) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (PPP) | Linear, Linear, Linear | Rectangular Box / Cuboid | $V = 2d_x \cdot 2d_y \cdot 2d_z$ |
| Cylindrical (RPP) | Revolute, Prismatic, Prismatic | Hollow Cylinder | $V = \pi h \left(r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2\right)$ |
| Spherical/Polar (RRP) | Revolute, Revolute, Prismatic | Spherical Sector | $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi r^3 (1 - \cos\phi_{max})$ |
| Articulated (RRR) | Revolute, Revolute, Revolute | Quasi-Spherical Lobes | Numerical integration |
| SCARA (RRP) | Revolute, Revolute, Prismatic | Cylindrical Column | $V = \pi h \left(r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2\right)$ |

In the volume formulas above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value when applied to scalars, but is rendered here without pipes for table compatibility.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** Students often confuse the work volume *shape* with the work volume *magnitude*. A Cartesian robot has a perfectly rectangular workspace but typically the smallest reach for a given link budget, whereas an articulated robot has a complex quasi-spherical workspace but maximum reach.

---

## 2.4 The Reachable vs. Dexterous Relationship for Common Robots

- **3-DOF Spherical Wrist Manipulator:** The dexterous workspace is a single point (the wrist center), since the wrist position is fixed for a given orientation — but the reachable workspace is a 3D region.
- **3-DOF Cartesian Manipulator:** The reachable and dexterous workspaces are **identical** (every reachable point can be attained with the same unique orientation in the standard alignment).
- **Fully Articulated 6-DOF Arm:** The dexterous workspace is a complex 3D region strictly contained within the reachable workspace.

---

## 2.5 Boundary Equations of the Workspace

The **boundary of the work volume** is a 2D surface in 3D space. It is formed when at least one joint of the manipulator reaches one of its limits. For each joint limit combination, a constraint surface is generated.

**General Principle:** A point $p$ lies on the workspace boundary $\partial W$ if and only if:

$$\exists \, q \in Q \text{ such that } f(q) = p \quad \text{AND} \quad \exists \, i \text{ such that } q_i = q_i^{min} \text{ or } q_i = q_i^{max}$$

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

$$W = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid p = f_{pos}(q),\ q \in Q \}$$

$$Q = \{ q \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid q_i^{min} \leq q_i \leq q_i^{max},\ \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\} \}$$

$$R_{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ell_i \quad \text{(sum of effective link lengths)}$$

$$W_{Dexterous} \subseteq W_{Reachable}$$

**2-Link Planar Manipulator Workspace:**

$$r_{min} = \vert \ell_1 - \ell_2 \vert \qquad r_{max} = \ell_1 + \ell_2$$

**Workspace Area (2-link planar, full joint range):**

$$A_{WS} = \pi \left[ (\ell_1 + \ell_2)^2 - (\ell_1 - \ell_2)^2 \right] = 4\pi \ell_1 \ell_2$$

**Cartesian Robot Volume:**

$$V = (2d_x)(2d_y)(2d_z) = 8 d_x d_y d_z$$

**Cylindrical Robot Volume (RPP):**

$$V = \pi h \left[ r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2 \right]$$

where $h$ is the prismatic stroke in $z$, $r_{max}$ is the maximum radial reach, and $r_{min}$ is the minimum radial reach (from the base column).

**Spherical Robot Volume (RRP):**

$$V = \frac{2}{3} \pi r_{max}^3 \left( 1 - \cos\phi_{max} \right)$$

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Workcell Layout Design:** The work volume dictates the optimal placement of the robot, fixtures, conveyors, and safety fencing in a manufacturing cell.
2. **Reachability Verification:** Before programming a task, the engineer must verify that every target point on the workpiece trajectory lies inside the work volume.
3. **Robot Selection:** A robot is selected such that its work volume *encloses* the workpiece's operational envelope with a safety margin (typically **10–20%**).
4. **Collision Avoidance:** Singularities and self-collisions can occur at the boundary of the work volume — understanding this region is critical for safe path planning.
5. **Offline Programming (OLP):** Software like RoboDK or DELMIA uses work volume data to validate cycle time and reachability before physical commissioning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: 2-Link Planar Manipulator Workspace

Consider a planar 2R manipulator with link lengths $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$, operating in the $xy$-plane with revolute joints at $O$ (shoulder) and $A$ (elbow). Both joints are unconstrained (i.e., $\theta_1 \in [0, 2\pi]$, $\theta_2 \in [0, 2\pi]$).

### Step 1: Forward Kinematics (Position)

The position of the end-effector $P$ in the base frame is:

$$p_x = \ell_1 \cos\theta_1 + \ell_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$p_y = \ell_1 \sin\theta_1 + \ell_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

These equations follow from the DH transformation chain for two consecutive revolute joints about parallel $z$-axes.

### Step 2: Distance from Base (Radial Reach)

The squared distance from the origin to the TCP is:

$$r^2 = p_x^2 + p_y^2$$

Substituting:

$$r^2 = \left[\ell_1 \cos\theta_1 + \ell_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]^2 + \left[\ell_1 \sin\theta_1 + \ell_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]^2$$

Expanding both squares (Pythagorean identity applied per cross-term):

$$r^2 = \ell_1^2 \cos^2\theta_1 + 2\ell_1 \ell_2 \cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \ell_2^2 \cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$
$$+ \ell_1^2 \sin^2\theta_1 + 2\ell_1 \ell_2 \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \ell_2^2 \sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

Grouping terms:

$$r^2 = \ell_1^2 (\cos^2\theta_1 + \sin^2\theta_1) + \ell_2^2 (\cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)) + 2\ell_1 \ell_2 [\cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)]$$

Applying the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2 + \sin^2 = 1$ and the cosine subtraction identity $\cos(A - B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$:

$$r^2 = \ell_1^2 + \ell_2^2 + 2\ell_1 \ell_2 \cos\theta_2$$

Therefore, the radial distance is:

$$r = \sqrt{\ell_1^2 + \ell_2^2 + 2\ell_1 \ell_2 \cos\theta_2}$$

### Step 3: Boundary Analysis

The radial reach $r$ depends **only on $\theta_2$** (not on $\theta_1$). The extrema occur when $\cos\theta_2 = \pm 1$:

- **Maximum reach** when $\theta_2 = 0$ (arm fully extended):

$$r_{max} = \sqrt{\ell_1^2 + \ell_2^2 + 2\ell_1 \ell_2} = \sqrt{(\ell_1 + \ell_2)^2} = \ell_1 + \ell_2$$

- **Minimum reach** when $\theta_2 = \pm\pi$ (arm folded back):

$$r_{min} = \sqrt{\ell_1^2 + \ell_2^2 - 2\ell_1 \ell_2} = \sqrt{(\ell_1 - \ell_2)^2} = \vert \ell_1 - \ell_2 \vert$$

### Step 4: Workspace Region

Since $\theta_1$ can sweep through the full $2\pi$ range, for every value of $r$ in $[\vert \ell_1 - \ell_2 \vert,\ \ell_1 + \ell_2]$, the TCP traces a full circle of radius $r$. Therefore, the workspace is an **annulus** (ring) with outer radius $\ell_1 + \ell_2$ and inner radius $\vert \ell_1 - \ell_2 \vert$.

### Step 5: Workspace Area

The area of this annulus:

$$A_{WS} = \pi r_{max}^2 - \pi r_{min}^2 = \pi \left[(\ell_1 + \ell_2)^2 - (\ell_1 - \ell_2)^2 \right]$$

Expanding the squares:

$$A_{WS} = \pi \left[ \ell_1^2 + 2\ell_1\ell_2 + \ell_2^2 - (\ell_1^2 - 2\ell_1\ell_2 + \ell_2^2) \right]$$

$$A_{WS} = \pi \left[ 4\ell_1 \ell_2 \right]$$

$$\boxed{A_{WS} = 4\pi \ell_1 \ell_2}$$

This is a beautiful result — the workspace area of a 2-link planar manipulator depends only on the **product** of the two link lengths.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Cylindrical Robot (RPP) Volume

A cylindrical robot has:
- Joint 1: Revolute about $z$ (angle $\theta_1 \in [\theta_1^{min}, \theta_1^{max}]$)
- Joint 2: Prismatic along radial direction $r$ (stroke $r \in [r_{min}, r_{max}]$)
- Joint 3: Prismatic along $z$ (stroke $z \in [z_{min}, z_{max}]$)

### Step 1: Cartesian Mapping

$$p_x = r \cos\theta_1, \quad p_y = r \sin\theta_1, \quad p_z = z$$

### Step 2: Volume Element

For an infinitesimal element in cylindrical coordinates, the volume element is:

$$dV = r \, dr \, d\theta_1 \, dz$$

### Step 3: Volume Integration

$$V = \int_{z_{min}}^{z_{max}} \int_{\theta_1^{min}}^{\theta_1^{max}} \int_{r_{min}}^{r_{max}} r \, dr \, d\theta_1 \, dz$$

Separating the integrals:

$$V = \left(\int_{z_{min}}^{z_{max}} dz\right) \left(\int_{\theta_1^{min}}^{\theta_1^{max}} d\theta_1\right) \left(\int_{r_{min}}^{r_{max}} r \, dr\right)$$

Evaluating each:

$$V = (z_{max} - z_{min}) \cdot (\theta_1^{max} - \theta_1^{min}) \cdot \left[\frac{r^2}{2}\right]_{r_{min}}^{r_{max}}$$

$$V = h \cdot \Delta\theta \cdot \frac{r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2}{2}$$

where $h = z_{max} - z_{min}$ and $\Delta\theta = \theta_1^{max} - \theta_1^{min}$. For a **full 360°** base rotation ($\Delta\theta = 2\pi$):

$$V = 2\pi h \cdot \frac{r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{V = \pi h \left(r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2\right)}$$

This confirms the KTU Formula Sheet entry for cylindrical robots.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Reach vs. Joint Type

| Configuration | DOF | Max Reach Equation | Workspace Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2R planar | 2 | $\ell_1 + \ell_2$ | Annulus |
| 3R planar | 3 | $\ell_1 + \ell_2 + \ell_3$ | Annulus (same as 2R) |
| RPP (Cylindrical) | 3 | $r_{max}$ (radial) | Hollow cylinder |
| RRP (Spherical) | 3 | $\ell_1 + \ell_2$ (or $r_{max}$) | Spherical sector |
| PPP (Cartesian) | 3 | $\sqrt{d_x^2 + d_y^2 + d_z^2}$ | Cuboid |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Joint Space to Work Volume

The following Mermaid block illustrates the conceptual flow from joint parameters through forward kinematics to the resulting work volume geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Link Parameters] --> B[DH Parameters: a, alpha, d, theta]
    C[Joint Limits: q-min, q-max] --> D[Configuration Space Q]
    B --> E[Forward Kinematics f-q]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Cartesian Position p-x, p-y, p-z]
    F --> G{Sweep over all Q}
    G --> H[Reachable Workspace W-R]
    G --> I[Dexterous Workspace W-D]
    H --> J[Boundary Surface: partial-W]
    I --> K[Subset Region: W-D subset of W-R]
    J --> L[Workcell Layout Validation]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Manufacturing Task Feasibility]
```

## 4.2 Work Volume Geometries by Robot Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Cartesian PPP] --> P2[Rectangular Box Volume]
    P3[Cylindrical RPP] --> P4[Hollow Cylinder Volume]
    P5[Spherical RRP] --> P6[Spherical Sector Volume]
    P7[Articulated RRR] --> P8[Quasi Spherical Lobes]
    P9[SCARA RRP] --> P10[Cylindrical Column]
    P11[2R Planar] --> P12[Annulus Ring]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Work Volume Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Define DH Table] --> S2[Step 2: Specify Joint Limits]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Derive Forward Position Kinematics]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Identify Constraint Equations]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Solve Boundary Equations]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Classify Reachable vs Dexterous]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Numerical Sampling for Volume]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Plot or Tabulate Workspace]
```

> [!NOTE] > **Visualization Note:** In KTU board examinations, students are expected to hand-sketch the work volume shape for any given robot configuration. Master the five canonical shapes: cuboid, hollow cylinder, spherical sector, articulated lobes, and annulus.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define work volume. Differentiate between reachable workspace and dexterous workspace.**

**Model Answer:**

The **work volume** (or workspace) of a robotic manipulator is the locus of all points in 3D space that can be reached by the end-effector's reference point (TCP) as the manipulator moves through all permissible joint configurations within its mechanical joint limits.

**Reachable Workspace (RW):** It is the set of all points that the TCP can reach in **at least one** orientation. It is a larger region defined by:

$$W_R = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid \exists\, q \in Q \text{ such that } f(q) = p \}$$

**Dexterous Workspace (DW):** It is the set of all points that the TCP can reach in **all possible orientations**. It is a smaller (or equal) region defined by:

$$W_D = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid \forall R \in SO(3),\ \exists\, q \in Q \text{ such that } f(q) = (p, R) \}$$

The fundamental relationship is $W_D \subseteq W_R$, meaning every dexterously reachable point is also a reachable point, but not vice-versa. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three factors that affect the work volume of a robot manipulator.**

**Model Answer:**

The work volume of a robot manipulator is influenced by:

1. **Link lengths** of the manipulator arms — longer links produce a larger maximum reach.
2. **Joint types** (revolute vs. prismatic) and the **kinematic configuration** (Cartesian, cylindrical, spherical, articulated, SCARA).
3. **Mechanical joint limits** (hard stops) which constrain the range of each joint angle.
4. **Number of degrees of freedom (DOF)** — higher DOF generally increases the reachable volume.
5. **Mechanical offsets and twists** specified in the DH parameters. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the work volume of a **cylindrical coordinate robot (RPP)** and derive its volume formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-link planar manipulator with link lengths $\ell_1 = 0.5$ m and $\ell_2 = 0.3$ m, determine the inner radius, outer radius, and area of the work volume. Assume unrestricted joint rotation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Sketch Description:** A cylindrical robot consists of a revolute joint at the base (rotating about the vertical $z$-axis), a prismatic joint providing radial extension, and a vertical prismatic joint providing height adjustment. The resulting workspace is a **hollow cylinder** with a possible sectorial cut depending on base rotation limits.

**Derivation:**

The position of the TCP in cylindrical coordinates is $(r, \theta_1, z)$. The joint ranges are:
- $\theta_1 \in [\theta_1^{min}, \theta_1^{max}]$
- $r \in [r_{min}, r_{max}]$
- $z \in [z_{min}, z_{max}]$

The volume element in cylindrical coordinates is $dV = r \, dr \, d\theta_1 \, dz$.

Total volume:

$$V = \int_{z_{min}}^{z_{max}} \int_{\theta_1^{min}}^{\theta_1^{max}} \int_{r_{min}}^{r_{max}} r \, dr \, d\theta_1 \, dz$$

Separating and integrating:

$$V = (z_{max} - z_{min}) \cdot (\theta_1^{max} - \theta_1^{min}) \cdot \frac{r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2}{2}$$

For full base rotation $\Delta\theta = 2\pi$:

$$\boxed{V = \pi h (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)}$$

where $h = z_{max} - z_{min}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (b):

Given:
- $\ell_1 = 0.5$ m, $\ell_2 = 0.3$ m
- Unrestricted joint rotation ($\theta_1 \in [0, 2\pi]$, $\theta_2 \in [-\pi, \pi]$)

**Inner radius** (arm fully folded):

$$r_{min} = \vert \ell_1 - \ell_2 \vert = \vert 0.5 - 0.3 \vert = 0.2 \text{ m}$$

**Outer radius** (arm fully extended):

$$r_{max} = \ell_1 + \ell_2 = 0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8 \text{ m}$$

**Workspace area** (annulus):

$$A_{WS} = \pi (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)$$

$$A_{WS} = \pi (0.8^2 - 0.2^2) = \pi (0.64 - 0.04) = 0.60\pi$$

$$\boxed{A_{WS} \approx 1.885 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Alternative verification using the derived formula $A_{WS} = 4\pi \ell_1 \ell_2$:**

$$A_{WS} = 4\pi (0.5)(0.3) = 0.60\pi \approx 1.885 \text{ m}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **work envelope** for an articulated (RRR) robot. Why is its shape complex compared to a Cartesian robot? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the work volume shapes of Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, and Articulated robot configurations with neat diagrams and indicate one typical application for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Work Envelope of an Articulated Robot:**

An articulated robot (also called a **jointed-arm** or **anthropomorphic** robot) consists of three revolute joints in series: the **waist** (base rotation about vertical axis), the **shoulder** (rotation about horizontal axis), and the **elbow** (another horizontal rotation). Each joint is subject to mechanical limits.

The work envelope is the **3D locus of all points** reachable by the wrist center (or TCP) as these three revolute joints sweep through their allowable angular ranges. Due to the serial revolute-revolute-revolute architecture:

- The **outer boundary** is approximately spherical (quasi-spherical), with the maximum reach equal to the sum of link lengths: $R_{max} = \ell_1 + \ell_2 + \ell_3$.
- The **inner boundary** contains a region of unreachability close to the shoulder (a "donut hole" or lobe structure).
- The actual shape is **lobed and asymmetric**, depending on joint limits.

**Why Complex Compared to Cartesian:**

1. **Nonlinear forward kinematics:** The position of the TCP involves nested trigonometric functions ($\sin\theta$, $\cos\theta$, $\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$), producing nonlinear boundary surfaces.
2. **Joint coupling:** The reach depends on multiple joint variables simultaneously (unlike the Cartesian robot, where each axis independently maps to one Cartesian axis).
3. **Self-intersecting reachable regions:** Some points can be reached with the elbow "up" or "down", creating multiple inverse kinematic solutions.
4. **Singular configurations:** At certain joint alignments (e.g., fully extended arm), the robot loses degrees of freedom, creating cusp-like boundary features.

In contrast, the Cartesian robot has **decoupled linear mappings** ($x = d_x$, $y = d_y$, $z = d_z$), producing a perfect rectangular box. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| Robot Type | Joint Sequence | Work Volume Shape | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian | P-P-P | Rectangular cuboid | CNC pick-and-place, gantry loading |
| Cylindrical | R-P-P | Hollow cylinder | Assembly of small parts, machine tending |
| Spherical | R-R-P | Spherical sector | Welding, die-casting extraction |
| Articulated | R-R-R (+ wrist) | Quasi-spherical lobes | Automotive welding, painting, palletizing |

**Diagrams (textual representation):**

- **Cartesian:** A perfect rectangular prism.
- **Cylindrical:** A hollow tube/can shape.
- **Spherical:** A spherical wedge or sector (like an orange slice extended).
- **Articulated:** A lobed sphere with a "kidney-shaped" cross-section.

**Applications Summary:**

- **Cartesian:** Used in CNC loading/unloading where the workpiece presents a rectangular reach envelope.
- **Cylindrical:** Used in rotary indexing tables and concentric assembly tasks.
- **Spherical:** Used in foundries and die-casting where reach in many orientations is needed.
- **Articulated:** Used in automotive body welding where the robot must reach into complex cavities with many orientations. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "work volume" with "payload capacity"** — they are completely different concepts. Work volume is geometric; payload is dynamic.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention joint limits** — without joint limits, the workspace is unbounded (theoretically infinite for prismatic joints).
> 3. **Stating $W_R = W_D$** — this is only true for specific 3-DOF configurations like the Cartesian robot. For most manipulators, $W_D$ is strictly smaller.
> 4. **Missing units in numerical answers** — always state m, m², or m³ explicitly.
> 5. **Not sketching** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for a labeled sketch even in numerical problems.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Work Volume = Workspace = Work Envelope** — these are synonymous terms in KTU 2024 syllabus.
- Work volume is determined by **forward kinematics + joint limits**, NOT by dynamics or payload.
- **Reachable Workspace (RW):** At least one orientation; **Dexterous Workspace (DW):** All orientations.
- The inclusion $W_D \subseteq W_R$ always holds; equality holds for special cases (e.g., 3-DOF Cartesian).
- For 2-link planar: $r_{max} = \ell_1 + \ell_2$, $r_{min} = \vert\ell_1 - \ell_2\vert$, $A_{WS} = 4\pi \ell_1 \ell_2$.
- For Cartesian: $V = 8 d_x d_y d_z$ (rectangular cuboid).
- For Cylindrical: $V = \pi h (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)$ (hollow cylinder).
- For Spherical: $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi r^3 (1 - \cos\phi_{max})$ (sector).
- Articulated robots have **nonlinear, lobed** workspaces due to coupled revolute joints.
- Work volume is **mandatory input** for workcell layout design, robot selection, and reachability verification.
- Always include a **neat sketch** of the work volume shape in KTU board answers — examiners reward visual clarity.
- The **dexterous workspace** of a 3-DOF spherical-wrist manipulator degenerates to a single point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity and Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAT303 - ROBOTIC MANIPULATORS AND WORK CELL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Capacity and Speed # 🏭 Module 4 — Capacity and Speed in Robotic Work Cells ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Capacity** of an industrial robot manipulator is defined as the **maximum payload (kg)** that the manipulator can repeatedly handle at **rated speed, rated reach, and rated repeatability** without violating its structural, thermal, or performance specifications. **Speed** refers to the **end-effector (TCP) translational and rotational velocities (m/s and rad/s)** achievable along programmed trajectories, and consequently the **cycle time (s)** of the work cell. In the context of **work cell design**, capacity is a system-level metric defined as the **maximum sustained throughput (parts/hour or parts/shift)** that the cell can produce under steady-state operation. The two are tightly coupled — the payload (per-cycle mass) limits the maximum speed a joint can deliver, which in turn sets the minimum cycle time, which fixes the capacity of the cell. | Term | Definition | KTU 2024 Reference | |---|---|---| | **Rated Payload (W)** | Maximum mass the robot can manipulate at full speed across its envelope | ASME B16.25 / ISO 9283 | | **Maximum Payload (Wmax)** | Absolute structural limit; usable only at reduced speed | Manufacturer datasheet | | **TCP Speed (VTCP)** | Linear velocity of the tool center point | Vector magnitude (m/s) | | **Cycle Time (Tc)** | Time for one complete pick-and-place (or assembly) cycle | seconds (s) | | **Throughput / Capacity (Cp)** | Parts produced per unit time | parts/hour (pph) | | **Availability (A)** | Uptime fraction over scheduled time | dimensionless (0–1) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Pankhawala (Strongman) and the Delivery Boy Imagine a **strongman (robot arm)** who must carry a **watermelon (payload)** from a **pickup counter (home position)** to a **delivery table (drop position)** inside a kitchen. - If the watermelon is light, the strongman can run fast → **low cycle time → high capacity (many deliveries per hour)**. - If the watermelon is heavy, the strongman must walk carefully (slower) to avoid dropping it or injuring his wrist → **high cycle time → low capacity**. - The **wrist joint** is the weakest link. A watermelon held at arm's length (large moment arm) is much harder than one held close to the body, even at the same weight. This is the **payload moment effect**. - The **kitchen layout** is the work cell. The shorter the path between pickup and drop, the faster each delivery → **shorter cycle time**. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Rule of Thumb (KTU Board favourite):** *Capacity is the inverse of cycle time. Speed is the lever that controls cycle time. Payload is the brake on that lever.* ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme This topic is **direct weightage-bearing** in the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE) for PCRAT303. It bridges: - **Module 1** (Robot anatomy and joint speeds) - **Module 3** (Drive systems and transmission) - **Module 4** (Work cell design — layout, throughput) Typical KTU 2024 scheme marks distribution: | Question Type | Marks | Frequency | |---|---|---| | Definition of payload / cycle time (Part A) | 3 | Every exam | | Cycle time / capacity calculation (Part B) | 7–14 | Every exam | | Trade-off numerical (Part B) | 7 | Often | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Layer View of "Capacity and Speed" Robotic work cell performance must be analysed at **two distinct layers**: ### Layer 1 — Manipulator-Level (Robot's own limits) 1. **Static payload limit** — structural and wrist torque constraints 2. **Dynamic payload limit** — drive torque and acceleration limits 3. **Joint speed limits** — motor RPM and gear ratio 4. **TCP speed limit** — synthesised from joint speeds via Jacobian 5. **Acceleration/deceleration limits** — drive current and brake energy ### Layer 2 — Work Cell-Level (System throughput) 1. **Motion time (tm)** — time for TCP to traverse path 2. **Grip / release time (tg)** — end-effector open/close, settling 3. **Wait/queue time (tw)** — synchronisation with conveyor, fixture 4. **Total cycle time (Tc)** — sum of all above 5. **Capacity (Cp)** — parts per unit time > [!NOTE] > **Manipulator capacity ≠ Work cell capacity.** A very fast robot inside a poorly designed cell (long paths, slow conveyor) still gives a low work cell capacity. ## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown ### 2.2.1 Payload Capacity and Wrist Torque When a robot picks up a payload W (Newtons) at a distance d (m) from the wrist axis, it produces a **payload moment**: $$M_{payload} = W \cdot d$$ This moment must be counteracted by the **wrist actuator torque Twrist**. The **rated payload** is set such that, at the **rated reach** (manufacturer-specified distance), the wrist torque stays within the continuous-rated torque of the wrist motor (typically 60% of peak torque for thermal safety). For a **multi-jointed arm**, the manipulator must also support: - **Self-weight moment** of the arm links - **Inertial moment** during acceleration: $M_{inertia} = I \cdot \alpha$ - **Gravity moment** which depends on arm configuration (worst case = horizontal reach) The **effective payload moment equation** at the wrist: $$T_{wrist,req} = (W \cdot d_{payload}) + (m_{arm} \cdot d_{cg}) + I_{eq} \cdot \alpha$$ For KTU problems, the simplified form is most common: $$W_{max} = \frac{T_{wrist,rating} - T_{arm,static}}{d_{payload,rated}}$$ ### 2.2.2 Speed: From Joints to TCP Each joint i has a **maximum joint speed** $\dot{q}_{i,max}$ (rad/s or mm/s for prismatic). The **TCP velocity vector** is given by the **robot Jacobian** J(q): $$V_{TCP} = J(q) \cdot \dot{q}$$ In a **non-singular configuration**, the inverse gives joint speeds for a desired TCP speed: $$\dot{q} = J(q)^{-1} \cdot V_{TCP}$$ The **maximum TCP speed** is constrained by whichever joint is "saturated" first (typically the wrist or elbow): $$V_{TCP,max} = \min_{i} \left( \frac{\dot{q}_{i,max}}{[J(q)^{-1}]_{i,row}} \right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Singularities** (where det(J) = 0) cause TCP speed to require infinite joint speeds — the robot cannot execute the commanded motion and must slow down or re-plan the path. ### 2.2.3 Acceleration Limits and the Trapezoidal Velocity Profile For straight-line moves, the standard industrial profile is the **trapezoidal velocity profile**: - **Acceleration phase**: $V(t) = a \cdot t$, duration $t_a = V_{max}/a$ - **Cruise phase**: constant $V_{max}$, duration $t_c = (D - 2 \cdot a \cdot t_a^2/2)/V_{max}$ - **Deceleration phase**: mirror of acceleration For a **single straight move of length D** at peak velocity V with acceleration a: $$T_{move} = \frac{D}{V} + \frac{V}{a}$$ This is the most-tested KTU formula for cycle time problems. ### 2.2.4 Work Cell Cycle Time and Capacity The **total cycle time** of a typical pick-and-place cell: $$T_c = t_{load} + t_{move1} + t_{grip} + t_{move2} + t_{release} + t_{index} + t_{return}$$ Where: - tload = waiting for part arrival on conveyor - tmove1 = TCP travel to pickup - tgrip = gripper close + settle - tmove2 = TCP travel to drop - trelease = gripper open - tindex = fixture indexing or conveyor advance - treturn = return to standby The **theoretical capacity** (parts per hour): $$C_p = \frac{3600}{T_c} \quad \text{[parts/hour]}$$ The **effective capacity** accounts for **availability (A)** and **quality yield (Y)**: $$C_{p,eff} = C_p \cdot A \cdot Y \quad \text{[good parts/hour]}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorise this table — it covers ~90% of Part B numerical questions on Capacity and Speed.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_{wrist} = W \cdot d + I \cdot \alpha$ | Wrist torque requirement | N·m | | 2 | $W_{max} = \dfrac{T_{rating} - T_{static}}{d_{rated}}$ | Maximum rated payload | kg or N | | 3 | $V_{TCP} = J(q) \cdot \dot{q}$ | TCP velocity via Jacobian | m/s | | 4 | $T_{move} = \dfrac{D}{V} + \dfrac{V}{a}$ | Straight-line motion time (trapezoidal) | s | | 5 | $t_a = V/a$ | Acceleration ramp time | s | | 6 | $D_{accel} = \dfrac{V^2}{2a}$ | Distance covered during accel | m | | 7 | $T_c = \sum t_i$ | Total work cell cycle time | s | | 8 | $C_p = 3600 / T_c$ | Capacity (parts per hour) | pph | | 9 | $C_{p,eff} = C_p \cdot A \cdot Y$ | Effective capacity | pph | | 10 | $M_{inertia} = m \cdot r^2 \cdot \alpha$ | Inertial moment on joint | N·m | | 11 | $F_{required} = m(g + a)$ | Drive force for vertical lift | N | | 12 | $T = I \cdot \alpha + T_{friction} + T_{load}$ | Motor torque balance | N·m | > [!NOTE] > All **vertical pipe** delimiters in the above table have been replaced with `\vert`-style or `·` (dot) separators to keep the markdown table intact. **No raw `|` is used inside cell content.** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive welding cells**: Capacity 60–90 parts/hour per robot; cycle times 40–60 s. Speed is limited by spot-weld gun mass (high payload → low acceleration). - **Pharmaceutical packaging**: Capacity 200–600 pph; limited by gripper settling (vision + compliance) rather than raw speed. - **Palletising cells**: Speed limited by vertical lift acceleration against gravity; payload 50–500 kg dominates the design. - **PCB assembly**: Pick-and-place machines hit 30,000+ components/hour — speed is king, payload is negligible (gram-scale). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No step is skipped. KTU examiners reward each correct transition.** ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum TCP Speed from Joint Limits **Problem statement:** A 2-DOF planar robot has link lengths L₁ = 0.5 m, L₂ = 0.4 m. Joint 1 max speed is 90°/s, Joint 2 max speed is 180°/s. The robot is in the configuration (θ₁ = 30°, θ₂ = -45°). Find the maximum TCP speed along the +X direction. **Step 1 — Write the forward kinematics (TCP position in base frame):** $$x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$ $$y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to time to get velocity:** $$\dot{x} = -L_1 \sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \cdot (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)$$ $$\dot{y} = +L_1 \cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \cdot (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)$$ **Step 3 — Express in Jacobian matrix form:** $$ \begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ +L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & +L_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ where $s_1 = \sin\theta_1$, $c_1 = \cos\theta_1$, $s_{12} = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, $c_{12} = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$. **Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:** $$s_1 = \sin 30° = 0.5, \quad c_1 = \cos 30° = 0.866$$ $$s_{12} = \sin(30° - 45°) = \sin(-15°) = -0.2588$$ $$c_{12} = \cos(-15°) = 0.9659$$ **Step 5 — Compute Jacobian entries:** $$J_{11} = -0.5(0.5) - 0.4(-0.2588) = -0.25 + 0.1035 = -0.1465$$ $$J_{12} = -0.4(-0.2588) = +0.1035$$ $$J_{21} = 0.5(0.866) + 0.4(0.9659) = 0.4330 + 0.3864 = 0.8194$$ $$J_{22} = 0.4(0.9659) = 0.3864$$ **Step 6 — To move purely along +X, set $\dot{y} = 0$:** $$\dot{y} = 0.8194 \, \dot{\theta}_1 + 0.3864 \, \dot{\theta}_2 = 0 \implies \dot{\theta}_2 = -2.121 \, \dot{\theta}_1$$ **Step 7 — Substitute into $\dot{x}$ equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= -0.1465 \, \dot{\theta}_1 + 0.1035 \, \dot{\theta}_2 \\ \dot{x} &= -0.1465 \, \dot{\theta}_1 + 0.1035 (-2.121 \, \dot{\theta}_1) \\ \dot{x} &= (-0.1465 - 0.2195) \, \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{x} &= -0.3660 \, \dot{\theta}_1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8 — Apply joint speed limits and find the binding constraint:** The ratio $\dot{\theta}_2 / \dot{\theta}_1 = -2.121$, so Joint 2 reaches its limit **2.121 times faster** than Joint 1 for the same increment in $\dot{\theta}_1$. - If Joint 1 limits: $\dot{\theta}_1 = 90°/s = 1.571$ rad/s, then $|\dot{\theta}_2| = 3.332$ rad/s ≈ 191°/s — **violates Joint 2 limit** (180°/s). - So Joint 2 limits: $|\dot{\theta}_2| = 180°/s = 3.142$ rad/s, then $\dot{\theta}_1 = 1.481$ rad/s ≈ 84.9°/s — within Joint 1 limit. ✓ **Step 9 — Final TCP speed:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{x}_{max} &= -0.3660 \times 1.481 = -0.542 \text{ m/s} \\ |\dot{x}_{max}| &= 0.542 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** This is why **path planning near singularities** is critical — joint speeds can exceed their physical limits, and the controller must either clamp the TCP speed or re-plan the path. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cycle Time with Trapezoidal Velocity Profile **Problem statement:** A robot moves a 2 kg part from pickup to drop, a straight distance D = 1.5 m. Peak TCP speed V = 1.2 m/s. Acceleration/deceleration a = 2.0 m/s². The gripper open/close time is 0.4 s, conveyor index time 0.6 s, return move (1.5 m, same V, a) 0.2 s wait at pickup, 0.3 s wait at drop. Calculate cycle time and capacity. **Step 1 — Outbound move time (D = 1.5 m, V = 1.2 m/s, a = 2.0 m/s²):** Distance needed to accelerate to V: $$D_{accel} = \frac{V^2}{2a} = \frac{1.44}{4.0} = 0.36 \text{ m}$$ Total accel + decel distance = 2 × 0.36 = 0.72 m. Since 0.72 < 1.5, the profile is **trapezoidal** (has a cruise phase). ✓ Cruise distance: $$D_{cruise} = 1.5 - 0.72 = 0.78 \text{ m}$$ Total outbound time: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{move1} &= \frac{D}{V} + \frac{V}{a} = \frac{1.5}{1.2} + \frac{1.2}{2.0} \\ T_{move1} &= 1.25 + 0.6 = 1.85 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Return move time (D = 1.5 m, V = 1.2 m/s, a = 2.0 m/s²):** Identical to outbound: $$T_{return} = 1.85 \text{ s}$$ **Step 3 — Sum all components of the cycle:** | Component | Time (s) | |---|---| | Wait at pickup (tw1) | 0.2 | | Gripper close (tgrip) | 0.4 | | Outbound move (Tmove1) | 1.85 | | Wait at drop (tw2) | 0.3 | | Gripper open (trelease) | 0.4 (assume same as close) | | Return move (Treturn) | 1.85 | | Conveyor index (tindex) | 0.6 | **Step 4 — Total cycle time:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_c &= 0.2 + 0.4 + 1.85 + 0.3 + 0.4 + 1.85 + 0.6 \\ T_c &= 5.6 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Theoretical capacity:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_p &= \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{5.6} \\ C_p &= 642.86 \text{ pph} \approx 643 \text{ pph} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Effective capacity (Availability A = 0.92, Yield Y = 0.98):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{p,eff} &= 643 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 \\ C_{p,eff} &= 579.5 \text{ good parts/hour} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Payload Limit from Wrist Torque **Problem statement:** A robot has wrist actuator rated torque Trating = 80 N·m. The arm's static (self-weight) moment at the wrist is Tstatic = 20 N·m. The gripper is mounted at a distance d = 0.25 m from the wrist axis. Determine (a) the maximum rated payload mass, and (b) the maximum payload if the part is gripped at d' = 0.4 m (e.g., using an extended gripper). **Step 1 — Available torque for payload:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{payload,avail} &= T_{rating} - T_{static} \\ T_{payload,avail} &= 80 - 20 = 60 \text{ N·m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — (a) Maximum payload at d = 0.25 m:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{max} &= \frac{T_{payload,avail}}{d} = \frac{60}{0.25} = 240 \text{ N} \\ m_{max} &= \frac{W_{max}}{g} = \frac{240}{9.81} = 24.47 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — (b) Maximum payload at d' = 0.4 m:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{max}' &= \frac{T_{payload,avail}}{d'} = \frac{60}{0.4} = 150 \text{ N} \\ m_{max}' &= \frac{150}{9.81} = 15.29 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to subtract the **static arm moment** from the rating. A wrong answer of 80/0.25 = 32 kg (or 80/0.4 = 20 kg) will be marked down 2–3 marks. Always check: *Is the arm's own weight included?* --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Speed × Payload Trade-off **Combined Problem:** A robot's joint motor has peak torque Tpeak = 100 N·m. During acceleration of payload at the wrist, the inertial moment is $M_I = (m_{payload} + m_{gripper}) \cdot r^2 \cdot \alpha$ where r = 0.2 m, α = 5 rad/s². The gripper mass is 3 kg. Gravitational moment = m · g · d (d = 0.25 m). What is the maximum payload for (a) α = 5 rad/s², (b) α = 10 rad/s²? Assume static arm moment = 15 N·m and torque safety factor = 0.6 (only 60% of peak is usable continuously). **Step 1 — Usable continuous torque:** $$T_{cont} = 0.6 \times 100 = 60 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 2 — Available torque for payload + inertia:** $$T_{avail} = 60 - T_{static} = 60 - 15 = 45 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3 — Sum of payload moment and inertial moment must equal Tavail:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{avail} &= (m_p + 3) \cdot g \cdot d + (m_p + 3) \cdot r^2 \cdot \alpha \\ T_{avail} &= (m_p + 3) \left[ g \cdot d + r^2 \cdot \alpha \right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for mp:** $$ \begin{aligned} m_p + 3 &= \frac{T_{avail}}{g \cdot d + r^2 \cdot \alpha} \\ m_p &= \frac{T_{avail}}{g \cdot d + r^2 \cdot \alpha} - 3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — (a) At α = 5 rad/s²:** $$ \begin{aligned} g \cdot d &= 9.81 \times 0.25 = 2.4525 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2 \\ r^2 \cdot \alpha &= 0.04 \times 5 = 0.20 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2 \\ \text{Sum} &= 2.6525 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2 \\ m_p &= \frac{45}{2.6525} - 3 = 16.97 - 3 = 13.97 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — (b) At α = 10 rad/s² (higher speed/acceleration):** $$ \begin{aligned} r^2 \cdot \alpha &= 0.04 \times 10 = 0.40 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2 \\ \text{Sum} &= 2.8525 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2 \\ m_p &= \frac{45}{2.8525} - 3 = 15.78 - 3 = 12.78 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key observation:** Increasing acceleration by 2× (5 → 10 rad/s²) reduces payload by ~8.5%. This is the **speed–payload trade-off** that defines the robot's "performance envelope curve" — exactly the kind of analysis KTU 2024 ESE questions test. --- ## 3.5 The Capacity–Speed–Payload Triangle (Master Summary) The three metrics form an **interdependent triangle**: - **Increase speed** → cycle time drops → capacity rises, BUT inertial loads rise → payload must drop - **Increase payload** → inertia and gravity moments rise → acceleration must drop → speed drops → capacity drops - **Increase capacity** → must reduce cycle time → must either shorten path OR increase speed → payload drops The **performance envelope** of a robot is therefore a curve in 3D space: {Payload, Speed, Reach} = constant motor power. KTU questions test the student's ability to identify which constraint binds and which lever to pull. --- # 4.
